title: TDA-RC: Task-Driven Alignment for Knowledge-Based Reasoning Chains in Large Language Models
summary: Enhancing the reasoning capability of large language models (LLMs) remains a core challenge in natural language processing. The Chain-of-Thought (CoT) paradigm dominates practical applications for its single-round efficiency, yet its reasoning chains often exhibit logical gaps. While multi-round paradigms like Graph-of-Thoughts (GoT), Tree-of-Thoughts (ToT), and Atom of Thought (AoT) achieve strong performance and reveal effective reasoning structures, their high cost limits practical use. To address this problem, this paper proposes a topology-based method for optimizing reasoning chains. The framework embeds essential topological patterns of effective reasoning into the lightweight CoT paradigm. Using persistent homology, we map CoT, ToT, and GoT into a unified topological space to quantify their structural features. On this basis, we design a unified optimization system: a Topological Optimization Agent diagnoses deviations in CoT chains from desirable topological characteristics and simultaneously generates targeted strategies to repair these structural deficiencies. Compared with multi-round reasoning methods like ToT and GoT, experiments on multiple datasets show that our approach offers a superior balance between reasoning accuracy and efficiency, showcasing a practical solution to ``single-round generation with multi-round intelligence''.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a topology-based framework that improves single-round Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning by embedding structural patterns learned from more powerful but costly multi-round methods like ToT and GoT, achieving better accuracy–efficiency trade-offs.
AI.motivation: LLMs’ reasoning abilities are still limited. Single-round Chain-of-Thought is efficient but often logically brittle, while multi-round frameworks (GoT, ToT, AoT) provide better reasoning structures at the cost of significant computational overhead. There is a need for a method that retains the efficiency of CoT while inheriting the structural strengths of multi-round reasoning, without requiring expensive multi-step interaction at inference time.
AI.method: The authors introduce a topology-based optimization framework for reasoning chains. They use persistent homology to embed reasoning paradigms (CoT, ToT, GoT) into a unified topological space, capturing structural properties of effective reasoning. Based on these topological representations, they design a Topological Optimization Agent that (1) diagnoses where a given CoT chain deviates from the desired topological patterns characteristic of strong reasoning, and (2) generates targeted repair strategies to adjust and improve the structure of the CoT chain, all within a single-round generation paradigm.
AI.result: Across multiple datasets, the proposed topological optimization of CoT achieves reasoning performance comparable to or better than multi-round methods like ToT and GoT, while being more efficient. Experiments indicate that enforcing desirable topological characteristics in CoT leads to improved reasoning accuracy without incurring the high interaction cost of full multi-round frameworks.
AI.conclusion: Embedding topological patterns of strong reasoning into CoT enables “single-round generation with multi-round intelligence.” The proposed framework demonstrates that topological analysis (via persistent homology) can unify and transfer structural strengths from complex reasoning paradigms (ToT, GoT) into an efficient CoT setting, offering a practical, scalable improvement path for LLM reasoning chains.

title: The Illusion of Latent Generalization: Bi-directionality and the Reversal Curse
summary: The reversal curse describes a failure of autoregressive language models to retrieve a fact in reverse order (e.g., training on ``$A > B$'' but failing on ``$B < A$''). Recent work shows that objectives with bidirectional supervision (e.g., bidirectional attention or masking-based reconstruction for decoder-only models) can mitigate the reversal curse. We extend this evaluation to include a vanilla masked language modeling (MLM) objective and compare it to decoder-only masking-based training across four reversal benchmarks and then provide a minimal mechanistic study of \emph{how} these objectives succeed. We show that reversal accuracy requires training signal that explicitly makes the source entity a prediction target, and we find little evidence that success corresponds to a single direction-agnostic representation of a fact. Instead, representation distances and linear probes are consistent with storing forward and reverse directions as distinct entries, with different indexing geometry for MLM versus decoder-only masking-based training. Our results caution that objective-level ``fixes'' can improve reversal behavior without necessarily inducing the kind of latent generalization one might expect from a unified concept.

AI.tldr: The paper studies why language models struggle to reverse factual relations (the “reversal curse”), evaluates different training objectives that mitigate it, and investigates how these objectives change internal representations.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive language models often fail to answer reversed factual queries (e.g., knowing that A is the capital of B but failing to infer that B’s capital is A). Prior work suggests that training objectives with bidirectional supervision can help, but it is unclear whether they truly teach models a direction-agnostic, unified concept of a fact or merely patch the specific reversal behavior. Understanding this distinction is important for designing objectives that yield robust, generalizable reasoning rather than brittle pattern learning.
AI.method: The authors extend reversal benchmarks to compare a standard masked language modeling (MLM) objective with decoder-only masking-based training across four reversal tasks. They then conduct a minimal mechanistic analysis: (1) checking when training signals explicitly require predicting the source entity in reversed queries, (2) analyzing representation distances between forward and reverse facts, and (3) using linear probes to examine whether forward and reverse relations share a unified, direction-agnostic representation or are stored separately. They also compare the geometric structure (“indexing geometry”) of these representations between MLM and decoder-only masking regimes.
AI.result: They find that high reversal accuracy only occurs when the training objective explicitly makes the source entity (in the reversed statement) a prediction target; without this, reversal performance remains poor. Representation analyses show little evidence for a single, direction-agnostic encoding of a fact. Instead, forward and reverse directions appear to be stored as distinct entries in representation space. Moreover, the way these entries are organized—i.e., the indexing geometry—differs between MLM and decoder-only masking-based training, suggesting that objective choice shapes how facts are partitioned and accessed internally.
AI.conclusion: Bidirectional or masking-based objectives can alleviate the reversal curse, but they do so by creating separate forward and reverse representations rather than a unified, direction-agnostic concept of a fact. Effective reversal depends on training signals that directly supervise predictions of the reversed entity, not on emergent generalized reasoning. Consequently, objective-level “fixes” to reversal errors may overstate the degree of genuine conceptual understanding that models acquire, and care is needed when interpreting improved reversal performance as evidence of deeper latent generalization.

title: Inclusion-of-Thoughts: Mitigating Preference Instability via Purifying the Decision Space
summary: Multiple-choice questions (MCQs) are widely used to evaluate large language models (LLMs). However, LLMs remain vulnerable to the presence of plausible distractors. This often diverts attention toward irrelevant choices, resulting in unstable oscillation between correct and incorrect answers. In this paper, we propose Inclusion-of-Thoughts (IoT), a progressive self-filtering strategy that is designed to mitigate this cognitive load (i.e., instability of model preferences under the presence of distractors) and enable the model to focus more effectively on plausible answers. Our method operates to reconstruct the MCQ using only plausible option choices, providing a controlled setting for examining comparative judgements and therefore the stability of the model's internal reasoning under perturbation. By explicitly documenting this filtering process, IoT also enhances the transparency and interpretability of the model's decision-making. Extensive empirical evaluation demonstrates that IoT substantially boosts chain-of-thought performance across a range of arithmetic, commonsense reasoning, and educational benchmarks with minimal computational overhead.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Inclusion-of-Thoughts (IoT), a self-filtering strategy that reconstructs multiple-choice questions using only plausible options to stabilize LLM reasoning and significantly improve chain-of-thought performance with low overhead.
AI.motivation: LLMs are commonly evaluated with multiple-choice questions, but they are easily distracted by plausible yet incorrect options, leading to unstable switching between correct and incorrect answers. This instability under distractors reflects cognitive load and hinders reliable evaluation and deployment. There is a need for a method that helps LLMs focus on genuinely plausible answers, reduces this cognitive load, and provides a clearer view into the model’s internal reasoning process.
AI.method: The paper proposes Inclusion-of-Thoughts (IoT), a progressive self-filtering strategy for MCQs. The model first examines all answer options and filters out implausible ones, effectively reconstructing the question using only the options it judges as plausible. It then performs comparative reasoning within this reduced option set, which offers a controlled setting to test the stability of its preferences under perturbations. The filtering and reasoning steps are explicitly documented, yielding a transparent trace of how options are pruned and how the final choice is made. IoT is applied as an overlay to chain-of-thought prompting with minimal added computation.
AI.result: Experiments across diverse benchmarks—arithmetic reasoning, commonsense reasoning, and educational question answering—show that applying IoT consistently improves accuracy of chain-of-thought prompting. The method reduces oscillation between answers in the presence of distractors, stabilizes the model’s preferences, and does so with little extra computational cost compared to standard CoT baselines.
AI.conclusion: Inclusion-of-Thoughts (IoT) effectively mitigates LLM vulnerability to distractors in multiple-choice settings by self-filtering options to a plausible subset and then reasoning comparatively within that subset. This reduces cognitive load, stabilizes internal preferences, and enhances both performance and interpretability of chain-of-thought reasoning, all while incurring minimal overhead. The approach offers a practical and transparent way to improve MCQ-based evaluation and deployment of LLMs.

title: Phase-Associative Memory: Sequence Modeling in Complex Hilbert Space
comment: submitting to APS Open Science, 10 pages, 1 figure, code and training logs available at https://github.com/gowrav-vishwakarma/qllm2
summary: We present Phase-Associative Memory (PAM), a recurrent sequence model in which all representations are complex-valued, associations accumulate in a matrix state $S_{t}$ $\in$ $\mathbb{C}^{d \times d}$ via outer products, and retrieval operates through the conjugate inner product $K_t^* \cdot Q_t / \sqrt{d}$. At $\sim$100M parameters on WikiText-103, PAM reaches validation perplexity 30.0, within $\sim$10\% of a matched transformer (27.1) trained under identical conditions, despite $4\times$ arithmetic overhead from complex computation and no custom kernels. We trace the experimental path from vector-state models, where holographic binding fails due to the $O(1/\sqrt{n})$ capacity degradation of superposed associations, to the matrix state that resolves it. The competitiveness of an architecture whose native operations are complex-valued superposition and conjugate retrieval is consistent with recent empirical evidence that semantic interpretation in both humans and large language models exhibits non-classical contextuality, and we discuss what this implies for the choice of computational formalism in language modeling.

AI.tldr: Introduces Phase-Associative Memory (PAM), a complex-valued recurrent sequence model that uses matrix-valued associative memory and achieves perplexity close to transformers on WikiText-103.
AI.motivation: Explore whether a recurrent architecture built around complex-valued associative memory and holographic binding can compete with transformers in language modeling, and address the capacity limits of traditional vector-state holographic memory.
AI.method: Design PAM where all representations are complex-valued; maintain a matrix memory state S_t in C^{d x d} updated via outer products of complex vectors (associations); perform retrieval using conjugate inner products K_t^* · Q_t / sqrt(d); empirically compare perplexity to a similarly sized transformer on WikiText-103; analyze why simpler vector-state holographic models fail due to 1/sqrt{n} capacity degradation and show how moving to matrix-state resolves this issue.
AI.result: At around 100M parameters on WikiText-103, PAM attains validation perplexity 30.0 versus 27.1 for a matched transformer, demonstrating competitive performance despite 4x more arithmetic from complex operations and no specialized kernels. The matrix-state formulation mitigates the capacity degradation seen in vector-state holographic memory models.
AI.conclusion: Complex-valued, matrix-based associative memory with conjugate retrieval can yield language models that are competitive with transformers, and the success of such architectures aligns with evidence that human and LLM semantics exhibit non-classical contextuality. This suggests that complex-valued formalisms and quantum-like superposition/retrieval operations may be a natural and fruitful computational basis for language modeling.

title: Pramana: Fine-Tuning Large Language Models for Epistemic Reasoning through Navya-Nyaya
comment: 52 pages + appendices, comprehensive treatment of Navya-Nyaya computational formalization
summary: Large language models produce fluent text but struggle with systematic reasoning, often hallucinating confident but unfounded claims. When Apple researchers added irrelevant context to mathematical problems, LLM performance degraded by 65% Apple Machine Learning Research, exposing brittle pattern-matching beneath apparent reasoning. This epistemic gap, the inability to ground claims in traceable evidence, limits AI reliability in domains requiring justification. We introduce Pramana, a novel approach that teaches LLMs explicit epistemological methodology by fine-tuning on Navya-Nyaya logic, a 2,500-year-old Indian reasoning framework. Unlike generic chain-of-thought prompting, Navya-Nyaya enforces structured 6-phase reasoning: SAMSHAYA (doubt analysis), PRAMANA (evidence source identification), PANCHA AVAYAVA (5-member syllogism with universal rules), TARKA (counterfactual verification), HETVABHASA (fallacy detection), and NIRNAYA (ascertainment distinguishing knowledge from hypothesis). This integration of logic and epistemology provides cognitive scaffolding absent from standard reasoning approaches. We fine-tune Llama 3.2-3B and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B on 55 Nyaya-structured logical problems (constraint satisfaction, Boolean SAT, multi-step deduction). Stage 1 achieves 100% semantic correctness on held-out evaluation despite only 40% strict format adherence revealing that models internalize reasoning content even when structural enforcement is imperfect. Ablation studies show format prompting and temperature critically affect performance, with optimal configurations differing by stage. We release all models, datasets, and training infrastructure on Hugging Face to enable further research on epistemic frameworks for AI reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Pramana, a method that fine-tunes LLMs with the Navya-Nyaya logical framework to improve epistemically grounded reasoning, demonstrating perfect semantic correctness on held-out logical tasks and releasing all resources for community use.
AI.motivation: Large language models often hallucinate and lack robust, evidence-grounded reasoning, especially when exposed to irrelevant or distracting context. Existing methods like chain-of-thought prompting provide informal reasoning traces but do not enforce a rigorous epistemic framework that distinguishes justified knowledge from unsupported claims. The authors aim to close this epistemic gap by providing LLMs with an explicit, historically rich methodology for structured reasoning and justification, thereby improving reliability in tasks that require traceable evidence and systematic logic.
AI.method: The authors design Pramana, an approach that embeds Navya-Nyaya, a classical Indian logical and epistemological system, into LLM training. Navya-Nyaya is operationalized as a 6-phase reasoning protocol: (1) SAMSHAYA – analyze doubt and clarify the question; (2) PRAMANA – identify and articulate valid sources of evidence; (3) PANCHA AVAYAVA – construct a 5-part syllogism with universal rules; (4) TARKA – perform counterfactual and consistency checks; (5) HETVABHASA – detect and categorize fallacies; (6) NIRNAYA – finalize the conclusion while marking what is known versus hypothesized. They fine-tune Llama 3.2-3B and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama-8B on 55 logical problems (constraint satisfaction, SAT, multi-step deduction) annotated in this 6-stage structure. They experiment with different prompting formats and sampling temperatures and conduct ablations to understand their influence on reasoning performance and format adherence.
AI.result: After fine-tuning on the Nyaya-structured dataset, the models achieve 100% semantic correctness on held-out evaluation problems, even though they only strictly follow the specified 6-phase format about 40% of the time. This indicates that the underlying reasoning patterns are learned even when the surface structure is inconsistently expressed. Ablation experiments reveal that both explicit format prompting and temperature settings have substantial impact on performance, and the best hyperparameters differ across the six reasoning stages. The authors also publicly release their fine-tuned models, datasets, and training infrastructure on Hugging Face.
AI.conclusion: Embedding Navya-Nyaya’s explicit epistemic and logical structure into LLM training yields models that reason more accurately and with better grounding in evidence, compared with generic chain-of-thought approaches. The 6-phase framework acts as cognitive scaffolding, helping models analyze doubt, identify evidence, construct formal arguments, check counterfactuals, detect fallacies, and clearly distinguish knowledge from hypothesis. Despite imperfect adherence to the formal structure, the models internalize its reasoning principles, achieving perfect semantic accuracy on held-out logical tasks. The work suggests that importing rigorous, historically developed epistemological systems into LLM training is a promising direction for improving AI reliability and invites further research using the released open-source resources.

title: This Treatment Works, Right? Evaluating LLM Sensitivity to Patient Question Framing in Medical QA
summary: Patients are increasingly turning to large language models (LLMs) with medical questions that are complex and difficult to articulate clearly. However, LLMs are sensitive to prompt phrasings and can be influenced by the way questions are worded. Ideally, LLMs should respond consistently regardless of phrasing, particularly when grounded in the same underlying evidence. We investigate this through a systematic evaluation in a controlled retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) setting for medical question answering (QA), where expert-selected documents are used rather than retrieved automatically. We examine two dimensions of patient query variation: question framing (positive vs. negative) and language style (technical vs. plain language). We construct a dataset of 6,614 query pairs grounded in clinical trial abstracts and evaluate response consistency across eight LLMs. Our findings show that positively- and negatively-framed pairs are significantly more likely to produce contradictory conclusions than same-framing pairs. This framing effect is further amplified in multi-turn conversations, where sustained persuasion increases inconsistency. We find no significant interaction between framing and language style. Our results demonstrate that LLM responses in medical QA can be systematically influenced through query phrasing alone, even when grounded in the same evidence, highlighting the importance of phrasing robustness as an evaluation criterion for RAG-based systems in high-stakes settings.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how different phrasings of patient medical questions (positive vs. negative framing, technical vs. plain language) affect the consistency of answers produced by large language models, even when they are given the same supporting evidence via RAG.
AI.motivation: Patients already use LLMs for complex medical advice, but LLMs are known to be sensitive to how prompts are written. In high‑stakes domains like medicine, it is important that answers remain consistent when the underlying evidence is the same, regardless of phrasing. The authors want to quantify how serious this problem is and what kinds of query variations cause inconsistency.
AI.method: The authors create a controlled retrieval‑augmented generation setup where models are not allowed to search the web; instead, they are given expert‑selected clinical trial abstracts as grounding evidence. They design 6,614 pairs of patient‑style queries that differ along two axes: (1) question framing (positive vs. negative) and (2) language style (technical vs. plain). Using these pairs, they query eight different LLMs, compare the models’ conclusions across each pair, and measure how often the answers are contradictory. They also extend the evaluation to multi‑turn dialogues to see whether sustained conversational framing changes consistency.
AI.result: Across eight LLMs, question framing has a strong effect: positive vs. negative formulations of the same medical question, grounded in the same trial abstract, are significantly more likely to produce contradictory final conclusions than questions that share the same framing. This inconsistency becomes even worse in multi‑turn conversations where the framing bias is reinforced. In contrast, changing language style (technical vs. plain) does not significantly interact with framing in affecting contradictions.
AI.conclusion: LLM outputs for medical question answering can be systematically biased by how a question is framed, even when the same supporting evidence is provided via RAG. This reveals that current systems lack robustness to prompt phrasing—especially framing—and that this vulnerability is amplified in conversational use. The authors argue that robustness to phrasing should be treated as a core evaluation dimension when designing and deploying retrieval‑augmented LLMs in high‑stakes applications like healthcare.

title: Operational Noncommutativity in Sequential Metacognitive Judgments
summary:
Metacognition, understood as the monitoring and regulation of one's own cognitive processes, is inherently sequential: an agent evaluates an internal state, updates it, and may then re-evaluate under modified criteria. Order effects in cognition are well documented, yet it remains unclear whether such effects reflect classical state changes or reveal a deeper structural non-commutativity. We develop an operational framework that makes this distinction explicit. In our formulation, metacognitive evaluations are modeled as state-transforming operations acting on an internal state space with probabilistic readouts, thereby separating evaluation back-action from observable output.
  We show that order dependence prevents any faithful Boolean-commutative representation. We then address a stronger question: can observed order effects always be explained by enlarging the state space with classical latent variables? To formalize this issue, we introduce two assumptions, counterfactual definiteness and evaluation non-invasiveness, under which the existence of a joint distribution over all sequential readouts implies a family of testable constraints on pairwise sequential correlations. Violation of these constraints rules out any classical non-invasive account and certifies what we call genuine non-commutativity.
  We provide an explicit three-dimensional rotation model with fully worked numerical examples that exhibits such violations. We also outline a behavioral paradigm involving sequential confidence, error-likelihood, and feeling-of-knowing judgments following a perceptual decision, together with the corresponding empirical test. No claim is made regarding quantum physical substrates; the framework is purely operational and algebraic.

AI.tldr: The paper develops an operational, algebraic framework to distinguish ordinary order effects in metacognition from deeper, structurally non-commutative ones, and proposes empirical tests to detect the latter.
AI.motivation: Metacognitive judgments (like confidence or error likelihood) are sequential and often show order effects, but it is unclear whether these effects can always be explained by classical state changes with hidden variables or whether they indicate a more fundamental, non-commutative structure in how evaluations interact. The authors want a principled way to distinguish these possibilities.
AI.method: They build an operational model where metacognitive evaluations are explicit state-transforming operations on an internal state space, with probabilistic readouts. This separates the back-action of making an evaluation from its observable outcome. They then define assumptions of counterfactual definiteness and evaluation non-invasiveness and derive constraints (inequalities) on pairwise sequential correlations that must hold if a classical, non-invasive latent-variable model exists. They provide a concrete three-dimensional rotation model that violates these constraints and sketch a behavioral paradigm (sequential confidence, error-likelihood, and feeling-of-knowing judgments after a perceptual decision) to test their theory.
AI.result: They prove that when metacognitive evaluations display order dependence, the process cannot be represented in a Boolean-commutative framework. Under their assumptions, they derive testable constraints on sequential correlations; violations of these constraints constitute evidence for what they term "genuine non-commutativity". Their 3D rotation model explicitly shows such violations and supplies numerical examples demonstrating how empirical data could similarly rule out classical non-invasive explanations of order effects.
AI.conclusion: Order effects in metacognition are not always explainable by classical state changes plus latent variables. Their framework clarifies when sequential metacognitive evaluations genuinely fail to commute at the structural level, in an operational sense analogous to non-commutativity in quantum theory but without positing quantum brain mechanisms. They provide both a formal toolbox and a concrete experimental paradigm for empirically detecting such genuine non-commutativity in human metacognition.

title: Memory Dial: A Training Framework for Controllable Memorization in Language Models
summary: Memorization in language models is widely studied but remains difficult to isolate and control. Understanding when and what models memorize is essential for explaining their predictions, yet existing approaches are post-hoc: they can detect memorization in trained models, but cannot disentangle its effects from architecture, data, or optimization. We introduce Memory Dial, a training framework that makes memorization pressure an explicit, controllable variable. Memory Dial interpolates between standard cross-entropy and a temperature-sharpened objective via a single parameter $α$, producing a family of models identical in architecture and training setup (within each sweep), differing only in memorization pressure. Experiments across six architectures and five benchmarks demonstrate that: (1) $α$ reliably controls memorization pressure, with seen-example accuracy increasing monotonically while unseen accuracy remains stable; (2) larger models are more responsive to memorization pressure; and (3) frequent sequences are easier to memorize than rare ones. Additional analyses show that the effect is robust across a range of sharpening temperatures, differs qualitatively from single-temperature cross-entropy, transfers to multilingual settings, and is detectable even on naturally occurring single-occurrence sequences. Memory Dial provides a controlled experimental framework for studying how memorization behavior emerges and interacts with generalization in language models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Memory Dial, a training framework that explicitly controls memorization pressure in language models using a single parameter α that interpolates between standard cross-entropy and a sharpened objective, enabling controlled study of how memorization emerges and interacts with generalization.
AI.motivation: Existing work on memorization in language models is largely post-hoc: it can only detect what a trained model has memorized, without the ability to manipulate memorization as an independent variable during training. This makes it hard to disentangle memorization from other factors like architecture and data, and to understand its causal role in model behavior and generalization. The authors want a way to systematically vary and study memorization pressure while holding everything else constant.
AI.method: The authors propose Memory Dial, a training framework that introduces a tunable parameter α which interpolates between standard cross-entropy loss and a temperature-sharpened variant of the loss. By adjusting α, they create a continuum of models with the same architecture, data, and optimization setup but different levels of memorization pressure. They then run experiments across six language model architectures and five benchmarks, comparing performance on seen versus unseen examples, analyzing sensitivity to model size, sequence frequency, temperature settings, and multilingual transfer. They also probe behavior on naturally occurring sequences that appear only once in training data.
AI.result: (1) The α parameter provides a reliable control knob over memorization: as α increases, accuracy on seen (training) examples increases monotonically, while accuracy on unseen examples stays essentially constant, indicating that memorization pressure is selectively affecting memorization rather than overall generalization. (2) Larger models exhibit stronger responsiveness to increased memorization pressure, suggesting a scaling relationship between capacity and memorization. (3) High-frequency sequences are memorized more easily than low-frequency ones under increased memorization pressure. Additional findings show that the phenomenon is robust across different sharpening temperatures, qualitatively distinct from simply training with a single fixed temperature, extends to multilingual settings, and can be observed even on sequences that appear only once in the training corpus.
AI.conclusion: Memory Dial offers a controlled, parametric way to study memorization in language models by turning memorization pressure into an explicit training variable. This framework enables clean experimental comparisons that isolate memorization from other factors, and reveals systematic patterns in how memorization relates to model size, data frequency, and generalization, thereby providing a foundation for deeper mechanistic and empirical investigations of memorization behavior in language models.

title: Proximity Measure of Information Object Features for Solving the Problem of Their Identification in Information Systems
summary: The paper considers a new quantitative-qualitative proximity measure for the features of information objects, where data enters a common information resource from several sources independently. The goal is to determine the possibility of their relation to the same physical object (observation object). The proposed measure accounts for the possibility of differences in individual feature values - both quantitative and qualitative - caused by existing determination errors. To analyze the proximity of quantitative feature values, the author employs a probabilistic measure; for qualitative features, a measure of possibility is used. The paper demonstrates the feasibility of the proposed measure by checking its compliance with the axioms required of any measure. Unlike many known measures, the proposed approach does not require feature value transformation to ensure comparability. The work also proposes several variants of measures to determine the proximity of information objects (IO) based on a group of diverse features.

AI.tldr: Proposes and validates a new proximity measure for comparing heterogeneous (quantitative and qualitative) features of information objects from multiple sources, to decide if they describe the same real-world object, without transforming feature values.
AI.motivation: When information about the same physical object comes from multiple independent sources, its features (attributes) can differ due to measurement and determination errors. Existing similarity/proximity measures often require transforming or normalizing feature values to compare heterogeneous data types, and they typically treat quantitative and qualitative attributes separately or inadequately. There is a need for a unified, theoretically sound proximity measure that can handle both quantitative and qualitative features, explicitly account for errors, and help decide whether different information objects actually refer to the same real-world observation object.
AI.method: The author defines a new proximity measure between features of information objects that combines probabilistic treatment of quantitative attributes with possibilistic treatment of qualitative attributes. Quantitative features are compared using a probabilistic measure that incorporates known or assumed determination (measurement) errors, while qualitative features are compared using a possibility measure that models uncertainty and variability in categorical values. The paper then formally checks that the constructed proximity measure satisfies standard axioms required of a measure (e.g., non-negativity, boundary conditions, and other consistency properties). Finally, the approach is extended to define several variants of composite proximity measures over groups of heterogeneous features to assess the overall similarity between information objects.
AI.result: The paper obtains a unified proximity measure that can compare both quantitative and qualitative features of information objects without requiring prior transformation or normalization of feature values. It is shown to satisfy the axiomatic requirements for a valid measure. Additionally, the author develops multiple aggregated proximity measures that operate over sets of diverse features, enabling the evaluation of how close two information objects are in terms of their combined attributes.
AI.conclusion: The proposed proximity measure is theoretically consistent and practically applicable for determining whether information objects from different sources likely refer to the same physical object, even when their features differ due to determination errors and include both quantitative and qualitative attributes. By avoiding the need for feature value transformation and providing composite measures for heterogeneous attribute sets, the approach offers a flexible and more natural way to assess similarity in multi-source information systems.

title: Beyond LLM-as-a-Judge: Deterministic Metrics for Multilingual Generative Text Evaluation
summary: While Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly adopted as automated judges for evaluating generated text, their outputs are often costly, and highly sensitive to prompt design, language, and aggregation strategies, severely, which limits reproducibility. To address these challenges, we propose \textbf{\textit{OmniScore}}, a family of complementary, deterministic learned metrics developed using small size ($<$1B) parameter models. OmniScore approximates LLM-judge behavior while preserving the low latency and consistency of traditional model-based scoring. We trained the models large-scale synthetic supervision ($\sim$564k instances, in \textbf{107 languages}) and evaluated using 8,617 manually annotated instances. The OmniScore family supports reliable, multi-dimensional scores across a variety of settings, including reference-based, source-grounded, and hybrid evaluations. We evaluate these models across question answering (QA), translation, and summarization in \textbf{6 languages}. Our results demonstrate that lightweight, deterministic learned metrics provide a highly practical and scalable alternative to frontier LLMs. Our models and datasets can be found at https://huggingface.co/collections/QCRI/omniscore

AI.tldr: Introduces OmniScore, a family of small, deterministic learned metrics that mimic LLM judges for text evaluation with lower cost and higher consistency.
AI.motivation: LLM-based automatic evaluation of generated text is powerful but expensive, slow, and highly sensitive to prompts, languages, and aggregation choices, leading to poor reproducibility and practicality at scale. There is a need for cheaper, deterministic, and reliable metrics that still capture the rich judgments of LLMs across multiple languages and tasks.
AI.method: Train sub-1B parameter models (OmniScore) to approximate LLM-judge behavior using large-scale synthetic supervision (about 564k instances across 107 languages), then validate them on 8,617 human-annotated instances. Design the metrics to output multi-dimensional scores and support multiple evaluation setups (reference-based, source-grounded, hybrid). Evaluate performance on QA, translation, and summarization in six languages, comparing against LLM judges and traditional metrics.
AI.result: The OmniScore models achieve strong correlation with human and LLM-based judgments while being deterministic, lightweight, and low-latency. They provide reliable multi-dimensional evaluation across several tasks and languages, demonstrating that small learned metrics can approximate frontier LLM judges effectively. The work releases models and datasets publicly for community use.
AI.conclusion: Deterministic, small learned metrics like OmniScore can serve as practical, scalable alternatives to expensive, prompt-sensitive LLM judges for text generation evaluation. They preserve many benefits of LLM-based evaluation while improving cost, latency, consistency, and reproducibility, and are broadly applicable across tasks, languages, and evaluation setups.

title: ReVEL: Multi-Turn Reflective LLM-Guided Heuristic Evolution via Structured Performance Feedback
summary: Designing effective heuristics for NP-hard combinatorial optimization problems remains a challenging and expertise-intensive task. Existing applications of large language models (LLMs) primarily rely on one-shot code synthesis, yielding brittle heuristics that underutilize the models' capacity for iterative reasoning. We propose ReVEL: Multi-Turn Reflective LLM-Guided Heuristic Evolution via Structured Performance Feedback, a hybrid framework that embeds LLMs as interactive, multi-turn reasoners within an evolutionary algorithm (EA). The core of ReVEL lies in two mechanisms: (i) performance-profile grouping, which clusters candidate heuristics into behaviorally coherent groups to provide compact and informative feedback to the LLM; and (ii) multi-turn, feedback-driven reflection, through which the LLM analyzes group-level behaviors and generates targeted heuristic refinements. These refinements are selectively integrated and validated by an EA-based meta-controller that adaptively balances exploration and exploitation. Experiments on standard combinatorial optimization benchmarks show that ReVEL consistently produces heuristics that are more robust and diverse, achieving statistically significant improvements over strong baselines. Our results highlight multi-turn reasoning with structured grouping as a principled paradigm for automated heuristic design.

AI.tldr: ReVEL embeds a large language model as a multi-turn reasoner inside an evolutionary algorithm to automatically evolve strong heuristics for NP-hard combinatorial optimization problems using structured performance feedback.
AI.motivation: Designing good heuristics for NP-hard combinatorial optimization problems is difficult, often requiring substantial human expertise and manual trial-and-error. Existing uses of LLMs typically generate heuristics via one-shot code synthesis, which tends to produce fragile solutions and fails to exploit LLMs’ strengths in iterative reasoning and reflection. There is a need for a more systematic way to leverage LLMs to automatically design and refine heuristics based on performance feedback.
AI.method: The authors propose ReVEL, a hybrid framework that integrates an LLM into an evolutionary algorithm as an interactive, multi-turn reasoner. ReVEL introduces: (i) performance-profile grouping, which clusters candidate heuristics into behaviorally similar groups and summarizes their performance to provide concise, structured feedback to the LLM; and (ii) multi-turn, feedback-driven reflection, where the LLM analyzes these group-level behaviors and generates refined heuristic variants. An EA-based meta-controller selects which refinements to keep, managing exploration vs. exploitation while iteratively evolving the population of heuristics.
AI.result: On standard combinatorial optimization benchmarks, ReVEL produces heuristic algorithms that are more robust and diverse than those from baseline methods. The improvements are statistically significant when compared against strong existing approaches, indicating that the multi-turn reflective process and structured feedback lead to genuinely better heuristics rather than incidental gains.
AI.conclusion: Embedding LLMs as reflective, multi-turn reasoners within an evolutionary optimization loop, coupled with structured performance-profile grouping, is an effective and principled approach to automated heuristic design. This paradigm outperforms one-shot code synthesis and demonstrates how LLMs can be systematically guided by performance feedback to iteratively improve combinatorial optimization heuristics.

title: Document Optimization for Black-Box Retrieval via Reinforcement Learning
summary: Document expansion is a classical technique for improving retrieval quality, and is attractive since it shifts computation offline, avoiding additional query-time processing. However, when applied to modern retrievers, it has been shown to degrade performance, often introducing noise that obfuscates the discriminative signal. We recast document expansion as a document optimization problem: a language model or a vision language model is fine-tuned to transform documents into representations that better align with the expected query distribution under a target retriever, using GRPO with the retriever's ranking improvements as rewards. This approach requires only black-box access to retrieval ranks, and is applicable across single-vector, multi-vector and lexical retrievers. We evaluate our approach on code retrieval and visual document retrieval (VDR) tasks. We find that learned document transformations yield retrieval gains and in many settings enable smaller, more efficient retrievers to outperform larger ones. For example, applying document optimization to OpenAI text-embedding-3-small model improves nDCG5 on code (58.7 to 66.8) and VDR (53.3 to 57.6), even slightly surpassing the 6.5X more expensive OpenAI text-embedding-3-large model (66.3 on code; 57.0 on VDR). When retriever weights are accessible, document optimization is often competitive with fine-tuning, and in most settings their combination performs best, improving Jina-ColBERT-V2 from 55.8 to 63.3 on VDR and from 48.6 to 61.8 on code retrieval.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new way to do document expansion by directly optimizing documents with a language or vision-language model so they align better with likely queries for a given retriever, leading to consistent retrieval gains and allowing smaller retrievers to outperform larger ones.
AI.motivation: Classical document expansion can help retrieval but often hurts modern dense and hybrid retrievers by adding noisy, off-distribution text. There is a need for a method that keeps the computation offline like document expansion but produces expansions that are explicitly tuned to improve a specific retriever’s ranking quality, and that works even when the retriever is a black box.
AI.method: Reformulate document expansion as a document optimization problem. Fine-tune an LLM or VLM that takes a document as input and outputs a transformed representation (e.g., expanded or rewritten document). Use GRPO (a policy-optimization-style RL method) where the reward is based on retrieval ranking improvements under a target retriever. Only black-box access to the retriever’s ranks is needed, and the approach is applied to multiple retriever types (single-vector, multi-vector, and lexical). They also compare document optimization with and without additional retriever fine-tuning, when retriever weights are available.
AI.result: On code retrieval and visual document retrieval benchmarks, the optimized documents consistently improve ranking metrics. For OpenAI text-embedding-3-small, document optimization increases nDCG@5 from 58.7 to 66.8 on code and from 53.3 to 57.6 on VDR, slightly surpassing the much larger and 6.5x more expensive text-embedding-3-large model (66.3 on code, 57.0 on VDR). When retriever weights are accessible, document optimization alone is often competitive with fine-tuning the retriever; combining both yields the best results, e.g., Jina-ColBERT-V2 improves from 55.8 to 63.3 on VDR and from 48.6 to 61.8 on code retrieval.
AI.conclusion: Treating document expansion as an RL-based document optimization problem allows learning document transformations that are tailored to a target retriever and query distribution, yielding substantial offline retrieval gains across different retriever architectures and modalities. This can make smaller retrievers competitive with or superior to larger ones, and can complement or partially replace retriever fine-tuning when model weights are available.

title: Algebraic Structure Discovery for Real World Combinatorial Optimisation Problems: A General Framework from Abstract Algebra to Quotient Space Learning
summary: Many combinatorial optimisation problems hide algebraic structures that, once exposed, shrink the search space and improve the chance of finding the global optimal solution. We present a general framework that (i) identifies algebraic structure, (ii) formalises operations, (iii) constructs quotient spaces that collapse redundant representations, and (iv) optimises directly over these reduced spaces. Across a broad family of rule-combination tasks (e.g., patient subgroup discovery and rule-based molecular screening), conjunctive rules form a monoid. Via a characteristic-vector encoding, we prove an isomorphism to the Boolean hypercube $\{0,1\}^n$ with bitwise OR, so logical AND in rules becomes bitwise OR in the encoding. This yields a principled quotient-space formulation that groups functionally equivalent rules and guides structure-aware search. On real clinical data and synthetic benchmarks, quotient-space-aware genetic algorithms recover the global optimum in 48% to 77% of runs versus 35% to 37% for standard approaches, while maintaining diversity across equivalence classes. These results show that exposing and exploiting algebraic structure offers a simple, general route to more efficient combinatorial optimisation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a general algebraic-structure-based framework that reduces the search space of combinatorial optimisation problems by constructing quotient spaces and optimising directly over them, demonstrated on rule-combination tasks.
AI.motivation: Many combinatorial optimisation problems are hard because they contain large, redundant search spaces. In rule-based tasks, multiple rule sets can be functionally equivalent but are treated as distinct by standard optimisation algorithms, wasting computation and hindering the discovery of global optima. The authors aim to systematically expose and exploit hidden algebraic structure to collapse these redundancies and enable more efficient, principled search.
AI.method: They introduce a four-step general framework: (i) identify the underlying algebraic structure of the solution space, (ii) formalise the operations on this space, (iii) construct quotient spaces that group functionally equivalent elements and collapse redundant representations, and (iv) design optimisation procedures that operate directly on these reduced quotient spaces. For a broad class of rule-combination problems, they show that conjunctive rules form a monoid. They then encode rules as characteristic vectors and prove an isomorphism to the Boolean hypercube {0,1}^n under bitwise OR, where logical AND of rules corresponds to bitwise OR in the encoding. This algebraic characterisation yields a principled quotient-space representation, which they integrate into genetic algorithms, producing quotient-space-aware evolutionary search procedures.
AI.result: On both real clinical datasets and synthetic benchmarks for rule-combination tasks (e.g., patient subgroup discovery, rule-based molecular screening), the quotient-space-aware genetic algorithms substantially outperform standard approaches. Specifically, they recover the global optimum in 48%–77% of runs, compared with 35%–37% for conventional methods, while also maintaining diversity across equivalence classes in the quotient space. This empirically validates that exploiting algebraic structure and quotient spaces can significantly improve the efficiency and robustness of combinatorial optimisation.
AI.conclusion: Exposing the algebraic structure underlying combinatorial optimisation problems and optimising over corresponding quotient spaces leads to more efficient and effective search. For rule-combination tasks, representing conjunctive rules as a monoid isomorphic to the Boolean hypercube enables a principled reduction of the search space and improved global optimum recovery rates. The approach is simple, general, and suggests that algebraic reasoning can systematically enhance a wide range of combinatorial optimisation methods beyond the specific applications studied.

title: Multilingual Language Models Encode Script Over Linguistic Structure
summary: Multilingual language models (LMs) organize representations for typologically and orthographically diverse languages into a shared parameter space, yet the nature of this internal organization remains elusive. In this work, we investigate which linguistic properties - abstract language identity or surface-form cues - shape multilingual representations. Focusing on compact, distilled models where representational trade-offs are explicit, we analyze language-associated units in Llama-3.2-1B and Gemma-2-2B using the Language Activation Probability Entropy (LAPE) metric, and further decompose activations with Sparse Autoencoders. We find that these units are strongly conditioned on orthography: romanization induces near-disjoint representations that align with neither native-script inputs nor English, while word-order shuffling has limited effect on unit identity. Probing shows that typological structure becomes increasingly accessible in deeper layers, while causal interventions indicate that generation is most sensitive to units that are invariant to surface-form perturbations rather than to units identified by typological alignment alone. Overall, our results suggest that multilingual LMs organize representations around surface form, with linguistic abstraction emerging gradually without collapsing into a unified interlingua.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how compact multilingual language models internally organize different languages, finding that representations are primarily shaped by surface orthographic form, with deeper layers gradually encoding more abstract linguistic structure but not a single unified interlingua.
AI.motivation: Although multilingual language models can handle many languages in a shared parameter space, it is unclear whether their internal representations are organized mainly by abstract language identity and typology, or by superficial cues like script and orthography. Understanding this helps clarify what kind of “multilingual understanding” these models really have and guides model design and evaluation.
AI.method: The authors focus on small, distilled multilingual models (Llama-3.2-1B and Gemma-2-2B) to make representational trade-offs clearer. They identify language-associated units using a proposed Language Activation Probability Entropy (LAPE) metric, then analyze these units’ behavior under controlled perturbations: romanization of scripts and word-order shuffling. They further decompose activations using Sparse Autoencoders to isolate interpretable components. They probe layers for typological information and perform causal interventions to see which units actually affect generation.
AI.result: They find that language-associated units correlate strongly with orthography: when non-Latin scripts are romanized, the resulting representations become almost disjoint from both the original-script representations and from English. Word-order shuffling, in contrast, has relatively little impact on which units are activated. Probing reveals that typological information becomes more accessible in deeper layers, and causal interventions show that generation depends most on units that are robust to surface-form perturbations, not on units selected solely for typological alignment.
AI.conclusion: Multilingual LMs primarily structure their internal space around surface-form properties, especially script and orthography. More abstract, typological linguistic structure emerges progressively in deeper layers but does not consolidate into a single shared interlingua. This suggests that multilinguality in current compact LMs is driven by orthographic organization with layered, partial abstraction rather than by a fully language-agnostic semantic space.

title: PaperOrchestra: A Multi-Agent Framework for Automated AI Research Paper Writing
summary: Synthesizing unstructured research materials into manuscripts is an essential yet under-explored challenge in AI-driven scientific discovery. Existing autonomous writers are rigidly coupled to specific experimental pipelines, and produce superficial literature reviews. We introduce PaperOrchestra, a multi-agent framework for automated AI research paper writing. It flexibly transforms unconstrained pre-writing materials into submission-ready LaTeX manuscripts, including comprehensive literature synthesis and generated visuals, such as plots and conceptual diagrams. To evaluate performance, we present PaperWritingBench, the first standardized benchmark of reverse-engineered raw materials from 200 top-tier AI conference papers, alongside a comprehensive suite of automated evaluators. In side-by-side human evaluations, PaperOrchestra significantly outperforms autonomous baselines, achieving an absolute win rate margin of 50%-68% in literature review quality, and 14%-38% in overall manuscript quality.

AI.tldr: PaperOrchestra is a multi-agent AI system that converts messy, unstructured research materials into full, submission-ready scientific papers and is evaluated with a new benchmark, outperforming existing autonomous writing systems.
AI.motivation: While AI is increasingly used in scientific discovery, turning raw, unstructured experimental notes and references into high-quality, full research manuscripts remains difficult and under-explored. Existing autonomous paper-writing tools are tightly coupled to specific experimental setups and tend to produce shallow literature reviews instead of deep, well-synthesized narratives. There is a need for a flexible, general framework that can take arbitrary pre-writing materials and generate full manuscripts that meet top-conference standards, along with a reliable way to evaluate such systems.
AI.method: The authors propose PaperOrchestra, a multi-agent framework for automated paper writing. It accepts unconstrained pre-writing materials (e.g., notes, results, references) and orchestrates specialized agents to: (1) synthesize a comprehensive literature review; (2) structure and write a full LaTeX manuscript; and (3) generate visuals like plots and conceptual diagrams. To systematically assess performance, they create PaperWritingBench, a benchmark built from reverse-engineered raw materials of 200 published top-tier AI conference papers, plus a set of automated evaluation tools to compare generated manuscripts against human-written ones.
AI.result: In human side-by-side evaluations comparing PaperOrchestra to existing autonomous writing baselines, PaperOrchestra achieves substantially higher preference rates. It wins by an absolute margin of 50%–68% in literature review quality and 14%–38% in overall manuscript quality, indicating stronger synthesis, structure, and presentation. Automated evaluators within PaperWritingBench further corroborate these improvements across multiple dimensions of writing quality.
AI.conclusion: PaperOrchestra demonstrates that a multi-agent approach can effectively transform unstructured research artifacts into high-quality, submission-ready AI research papers, including both text and figures. Combined with the PaperWritingBench benchmark, this work provides both a practical tool for AI-assisted scientific writing and a standardized framework to evaluate future autonomous paper-writing systems, showing clear gains over current baselines especially in literature synthesis and overall manuscript quality.

title: MegaTrain: Full Precision Training of 100B+ Parameter Large Language Models on a Single GPU
summary: We present MegaTrain, a memory-centric system that efficiently trains 100B+ parameter large language models at full precision on a single GPU. Unlike traditional GPU-centric systems, MegaTrain stores parameters and optimizer states in host memory (CPU memory) and treats GPUs as transient compute engines. For each layer, we stream parameters in and compute gradients out, minimizing persistent device state. To battle the CPU-GPU bandwidth bottleneck, we adopt two key optimizations. 1) We introduce a pipelined double-buffered execution engine that overlaps parameter prefetching, computation, and gradient offloading across multiple CUDA streams, enabling continuous GPU execution. 2) We replace persistent autograd graphs with stateless layer templates, binding weights dynamically as they stream in, eliminating persistent graph metadata while providing flexibility in scheduling. On a single H200 GPU with 1.5TB host memory, MegaTrain reliably trains models up to 120B parameters. It also achieves 1.84$\times$ the training throughput of DeepSpeed ZeRO-3 with CPU offloading when training 14B models. MegaTrain also enables 7B model training with 512k token context on a single GH200.

AI.tldr: MegaTrain is a memory-centric training system that streams full-precision 100B+ model parameters from CPU to a single GPU, enabling efficient large-scale LLM training on commodity hardware.
AI.motivation: Conventional GPU-centric training frameworks require all model parameters and optimizer states to fit in GPU memory, which severely limits trainable model size and context length on a single device and makes large models expensive to train. Existing CPU-offload approaches suffer from CPU-GPU bandwidth bottlenecks and overhead from persistent autograd graphs, leading to underutilized GPUs and low throughput. A new system design is needed to maximize model scale and efficiency when using limited GPU memory but large host memory.
AI.method: MegaTrain adopts a memory-centric architecture where all parameters and optimizer states reside in host (CPU) memory while GPUs act as transient compute engines. For each layer, parameters are streamed into the GPU just-in-time to perform forward and backward computation, and gradients are streamed back to the CPU, minimizing persistent state on the GPU. To mitigate the CPU-GPU bandwidth bottleneck, MegaTrain uses (1) a pipelined double-buffered execution engine that overlaps parameter prefetching, computation, and gradient offloading across multiple CUDA streams to maintain continuous GPU utilization, and (2) stateless layer templates instead of persistent autograd graphs, dynamically binding weights as they stream in, which removes persistent graph metadata and provides flexible scheduling.
AI.result: On a single NVIDIA H200 GPU equipped with 1.5 TB of host memory, MegaTrain can reliably train large language models up to 120B parameters in full precision. When training 14B-parameter models, it achieves 1.84× higher training throughput compared to DeepSpeed ZeRO-3 with CPU offloading. MegaTrain further enables training a 7B-parameter model with a 512k-token context length on a single GH200 node.
AI.conclusion: MegaTrain demonstrates that a memory-centric, streaming-based training architecture can significantly extend the maximum trainable model size and context length on a single GPU while improving throughput over state-of-the-art CPU-offloading methods. By treating the GPU as a transient compute engine and leveraging pipelined double-buffering with stateless layer templates, the system overcomes CPU-GPU bandwidth and autograd overhead bottlenecks, enabling efficient full-precision training of 100B+ parameter LLMs using large host memory configurations.

title: Part-Level 3D Gaussian Vehicle Generation with Joint and Hinge Axis Estimation
summary:
Simulation is essential for autonomous driving, yet current frameworks often model vehicles as rigid assets and fail to capture part-level articulation. With perception algorithms increasingly leveraging dynamics such as wheel steering or door opening, realistic simulation requires animatable vehicle representations. Existing CAD-based pipelines are limited by library coverage and fixed templates, preventing faithful reconstruction of in-the-wild instances.
  We propose a generative framework that, from a single image or sparse multi-view input, synthesizes an animatable 3D Gaussian vehicle. Our method addresses two challenges: (i) large 3D asset generators are optimized for static quality but not articulation, leading to distortions at part boundaries when animated; and (ii) segmentation alone cannot provide the kinematic parameters required for motion. To overcome this, we introduce a part-edge refinement module that enforces exclusive Gaussian ownership and a kinematic reasoning head that predicts joint positions and hinge axes of movable parts. Together, these components enable faithful part-aware simulation, bridging the gap between static generation and animatable vehicle models.

AI.tldr: A method that turns a single/sparse-view vehicle image into an animatable 3D Gaussian model with articulated parts for realistic autonomous driving simulation.
AI.motivation: Autonomous driving simulation needs realistic, part-level vehicle articulation (e.g., wheel steering, door opening) because modern perception algorithms exploit these dynamics. Existing simulators and CAD-based pipelines either treat vehicles as rigid assets or rely on limited, fixed-shape CAD templates, so they cannot accurately reconstruct and animate arbitrary real-world vehicles seen in the wild.
AI.method: They introduce a generative framework that reconstructs an animatable vehicle as a 3D Gaussian representation from a single or sparse multi-view image input. The method tackles two main issues: (1) standard high-quality 3D generators, designed for static assets, produce artifacts at part boundaries when the model is articulated; (2) semantic segmentation alone is insufficient to infer kinematic parameters like joint locations and hinge axes. To address this, they design (a) a part-edge refinement module that refines boundaries so that each Gaussian is exclusively owned by a single part, reducing distortions under motion, and (b) a kinematic reasoning head that predicts joint positions and hinge axes for movable components, enabling principled articulation.
AI.result: The framework can synthesize 3D Gaussian vehicle models that are not only high-quality in static pose but also support realistic articulation of parts such as wheels and doors. The part-edge refinement improves deformation behavior at boundaries, and the kinematic reasoning head provides plausible kinematic parameters from sparse image input, enabling faithful part-aware animations from in-the-wild instances.
AI.conclusion: By integrating part-aware Gaussian refinement with learned kinematic reasoning, the method bridges the gap between static 3D generation and fully animatable vehicle models. This makes simulation assets better aligned with the needs of modern autonomous driving perception systems that depend on dynamic, part-level vehicle behaviors.

title: RAG or Learning? Understanding the Limits of LLM Adaptation under Continuous Knowledge Drift in the Real World
summary: Large language models (LLMs) acquire most of their knowledge during pretraining, which ties them to a fixed snapshot of the world and makes adaptation to continuously evolving knowledge challenging. As facts, entities, and events change over time, models may experience continuous knowledge drift, resulting not only in outdated predictions but also in temporally inconsistent reasoning. Although existing approaches, such as continual finetuning, knowledge editing, and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), aim to update or supplement model knowledge, they are rarely evaluated in settings that reflect chronological, evolving, and real-world knowledge evolution. In this work, we introduce a new benchmark of real-world dynamic events, constructed from time-stamped evidence that captures how knowledge evolves over time, which enables systematic evaluation of model adaptation under continuous knowledge drift. The benchmark reveals that most existing methods, including vanilla RAG and several learning-based approaches, struggle under this setting, exposing critical limitations such as catastrophic forgetting and temporal inconsistency. To mitigate these limitations, we propose a time-aware retrieval baseline, Chronos, which progressively organizes retrieved evidence into an Event Evolution Graph to enable more temporally consistent understanding in LLMs without additional training. Overall, this work provides a foundation for analyzing and advancing LLM adaptation to continuous knowledge drift in realistic settings.

AI.tldr: They build a realistic, time-stamped benchmark of evolving real‑world events to test how well LLMs handle continuously changing knowledge, show that existing update methods fail, and propose a simple time-aware retrieval baseline (Chronos) that improves temporal consistency without extra training.
AI.motivation: LLMs mostly learn during pretraining and are then stuck with a static snapshot of the world. In reality, facts, entities, and events change, causing knowledge drift. Existing adaptation methods (continual finetuning, knowledge editing, RAG) are not properly evaluated on realistic, chronologically evolving data, so we don’t know how well they handle continuous change or temporal consistency. A dedicated benchmark is needed to study these issues systematically.
AI.method: 1) Construct a benchmark of real-world dynamic events using time-stamped evidence that records how information about events evolves. 2) Define evaluation settings that stress-test models under continuous knowledge drift, looking at both accuracy and temporal consistency. 3) Evaluate a wide range of existing adaptation approaches, including vanilla RAG and learning-based methods, under this benchmark. 4) Propose Chronos, a time-aware retrieval baseline that organizes retrieved documents into an Event Evolution Graph over time, and use it to guide LLM reasoning without additional training.
AI.result: The new benchmark shows that most current methods struggle with continuous knowledge drift: they suffer from catastrophic forgetting and produce temporally inconsistent answers when knowledge changes. In contrast, the proposed Chronos baseline improves temporal consistency by leveraging time-aware retrieval and structured organization of evidence, even without any model finetuning.
AI.conclusion: Continuous, real-world knowledge drift exposes serious weaknesses in how LLMs are currently adapted and evaluated. The proposed dynamic-events benchmark offers a more realistic testbed, revealing issues like forgetting and temporal inconsistency. A simple time-aware retrieval strategy (Chronos) that structures evidence as an Event Evolution Graph already yields better temporal reasoning without retraining, suggesting that future work should focus on temporally grounded representations and evaluation for LLM adaptation.

title: MMORF: A Multi-agent Framework for Designing Multi-objective Retrosynthesis Planning Systems
summary: Multi-objective retrosynthesis planning is a critical chemistry task requiring dynamic balancing of quality, safety, and cost objectives. Language model-based multi-agent systems (MAS) offer a promising approach for this task: leveraging interactions of specialized agents to incorporate multiple objectives into retrosynthesis planning. We present MMORF, a framework for constructing MAS for multi-objective retrosynthesis planning. MMORF features modular agentic components, which can be flexibly combined and configured into different systems, enabling principled evaluation and comparison of different system designs. Using MMORF, we construct two representative MAS: MASIL and RFAS. On a newly curated benchmark consisting of 218 multi-objective retrosynthesis planning tasks, MASIL achieves strong safety and cost metrics on soft-constraint tasks, frequently Pareto-dominating baseline routes, while RFAS achieves a 48.6% success rate on hard-constraint tasks, outperforming state-of-the-art baselines. Together, these results show the effectiveness of MMORF as a foundational framework for exploring MAS for multi-objective retrosynthesis planning. Code and data are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/MMORF/.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MMORF, a modular multi-agent framework using language models for multi-objective retrosynthesis planning, and demonstrates its effectiveness on a new benchmark by building two systems, MASIL and RFAS, that outperform existing baselines on soft- and hard-constraint tasks.
AI.motivation: Retrosynthesis planning in chemistry must balance multiple often-conflicting objectives such as route quality, safety, and cost. Existing methods struggle to dynamically trade off these objectives in a unified, systematic way, and it is difficult to compare different multi-agent language model systems because there is no common framework or benchmark. The authors aim to create a structured way to build, configure, and evaluate multi-agent systems for multi-objective retrosynthesis so that different designs can be compared and improved.
AI.method: The authors propose MMORF, a modular framework for constructing language model-based multi-agent systems tailored to multi-objective retrosynthesis planning. MMORF defines reusable agent components and interaction patterns that can be flexibly assembled into different systems. Using this framework, they instantiate two concrete systems: MASIL, targeting soft-constraint tasks where safety and cost are optimized but can be traded off, and RFAS, focusing on hard-constraint tasks where strict requirements must be satisfied. They also curate a benchmark of 218 multi-objective retrosynthesis tasks to systematically test these systems against state-of-the-art baselines and evaluate performance using multi-objective metrics like Pareto dominance and success rate under constraints.
AI.result: On the new 218-task benchmark, MASIL performs strongly on soft-constraint retrosynthesis problems, achieving better safety and cost trade-offs than baseline routes and often Pareto-dominating them. RFAS attains a 48.6% success rate on hard-constraint problems, surpassing state-of-the-art baselines for constrained retrosynthesis planning. These results collectively indicate that systems built via MMORF can effectively handle both soft and hard multi-objective constraints, and that MMORF supports systematic experimentation with different multi-agent designs.
AI.conclusion: MMORF provides a foundational, modular framework for building and comparing language model-based multi-agent systems for multi-objective retrosynthesis planning. By enabling flexible configuration of agent roles and interactions and by introducing a dedicated benchmark, it allows principled evaluation of different system designs. The strong performance of MASIL and RFAS on soft- and hard-constraint tasks validates the approach and suggests that MMORF is a useful basis for future research into multi-agent, multi-objective planning in chemistry.

title: $π^2$: Structure-Originated Reasoning Data Improves Long-Context Reasoning Ability of Large Language Models
comment: Our structured analytical reasoning data, which originates from Wikipedia tables, significantly improves long-context reasoning capability of LLMs
summary: We study a pipeline that curates reasoning data from initial structured data for improving long-context reasoning in large language models (LLMs). Our approach, $π^2$, constructs high-quality reasoning data through rigorous QA curation: 1) extracting and expanding tables from Wikipedia, 2) from the collected tables and relevant context, generating realistic and multi-hop analytical reasoning questions whose answers are automatically determined and verified through dual-path code execution, and 3) back-translating step-by-step structured reasoning traces as solutions of QA pairs given realistic web-search context. Supervised fine-tuning with \textsc{\small{gpt-oss-20b}} and \textsc{\small{Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507}} on $π^2$ yields consistent improvements across four long-context reasoning benchmarks and our alike $π^2$-Bench, with average absolute accuracy gains of +4.3% and +2.7% respectively. Notably, our dataset facilitates self-distillation, where \textsc{\small{gpt-oss-20b}} even improves its average performance by +4.4% with its own reasoning traces, demonstrating $π^2$'s usefulness. Our code, data, and models are open-source at https://github.com/vt-pi-squared/pi-squared.

AI.tldr: The paper presents π², a pipeline that constructs high-quality, code-verified reasoning datasets from Wikipedia tables to improve long-context reasoning in LLMs, and shows consistent accuracy gains and effective self-distillation on multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Long-context reasoning is challenging for LLMs, and high-quality, scalable, and verifiable reasoning datasets—especially involving multi-hop and analytical questions over structured data and long contexts—are scarce. The authors aim to systematically curate such data from existing structured sources to better train and evaluate LLMs on long-context, multi-step reasoning tasks.
AI.method: They introduce π², a three-stage data generation pipeline: (1) automatically extract and expand tables from Wikipedia; (2) generate realistic, multi-hop analytical QA pairs over these tables plus relevant textual context, with answers automatically computed and verified via dual-path code execution; and (3) convert structured, step-by-step programmatic reasoning traces into natural-language chain-of-thought solutions, conditioned on realistic web-search-like context. The resulting QA + reasoning-trace dataset is then used for supervised fine-tuning of open-source LLMs.
AI.result: Fine-tuning GPT-OSS-20B and Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 on π² data yields average absolute accuracy improvements of +4.3% across four standard long-context reasoning benchmarks and +2.7% on the authors’ own π²-Bench. Moreover, using π² to perform self-distillation—training GPT-OSS-20B on its own generated reasoning traces—further improves its average performance by +4.4%, indicating that the curated traces are beneficial even for the original model.
AI.conclusion: Structured, code-verified reasoning data derived from Wikipedia tables and realistic contexts can significantly improve LLM long-context reasoning. The π² pipeline offers a scalable way to generate such data, supports effective supervised fine-tuning and self-distillation, and is made broadly available through open-source code, data, and models.

title: MedGemma 1.5 Technical Report
summary: We introduce MedGemma 1.5 4B, the latest model in the MedGemma collection. MedGemma 1.5 expands on MedGemma 1 by integrating additional capabilities: high-dimensional medical imaging (CT/MRI volumes and histopathology whole slide images), anatomical localization via bounding boxes, multi-timepoint chest X-ray analysis, and improved medical document understanding (lab reports, electronic health records). We detail the innovations required to enable these modalities within a single architecture, including new training data, long-context 3D volume slicing, and whole-slide pathology sampling. Compared to MedGemma 1 4B, MedGemma 1.5 4B demonstrates significant gains in these new areas, improving 3D MRI condition classification accuracy by 11% and 3D CT condition classification by 3% (absolute improvements). In whole slide pathology imaging, MedGemma 1.5 4B achieves a 47% macro F1 gain. Additionally, it improves anatomical localization with a 35% increase in Intersection over Union on chest X-rays and achieves a 4% macro accuracy for longitudinal (multi-timepoint) chest x-ray analysis. Beyond its improved multimodal performance over MedGemma 1, MedGemma 1.5 improves on text-based clinical knowledge and reasoning, improving by 5% on MedQA accuracy and 22% on EHRQA accuracy. It also achieves an average of 18% macro F1 on 4 different lab report information extraction datasets (EHR Datasets 2, 3, 4, and Mendeley Clinical Laboratory Test Reports). Taken together, MedGemma 1.5 serves as a robust, open resource for the community, designed as an improved foundation on which developers can create the next generation of medical AI systems.
