Resources and tutorials for building upon MedGemma 1.5 can be found at https://goo.gle/MedGemma.

AI.tldr: MedGemma 1.5 4B is an upgraded open medical foundation model that unifies more imaging and text modalities and substantially improves performance over MedGemma 1 across 3D imaging, pathology slides, localization, longitudinal X-rays, and clinical NLP tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing MedGemma 1 and similar medical foundation models have limited support for complex, high-dimensional medical data (3D CT/MRI, whole-slide pathology, multi-timepoint imaging) and for rich clinical documents (EHRs, lab reports). There is a need for a unified, open model that can handle these modalities in a single architecture and provide stronger clinical reasoning and information extraction, enabling more capable and practical medical AI systems for the community.
AI.method: The authors extend the MedGemma architecture to MedGemma 1.5 4B by: (1) adding new training data covering 3D CT/MRI volumes, whole-slide histopathology images, chest X-ray sequences, and clinical documents such as EHRs and lab reports; (2) introducing technical mechanisms for handling long-context and high-resolution data, including long-context 3D volume slicing for CT/MRI and specialized sampling strategies for whole-slide pathology images; and (3) jointly training these modalities within a single multimodal foundation model to support imaging tasks (classification, localization, longitudinal analysis) and text-based tasks (QA, information extraction).
AI.result: MedGemma 1.5 4B achieves consistent performance gains over MedGemma 1 4B: +11% accuracy on 3D MRI condition classification, +3% on 3D CT condition classification, +47% macro F1 on whole-slide pathology imaging, +35% IoU for anatomical localization on chest X-rays, and effective performance on longitudinal chest X-ray analysis (4% macro accuracy, as reported). For text-based clinical tasks, it improves MedQA accuracy by 5% and EHRQA accuracy by 22%, and attains an average 18% macro F1 on four lab report information extraction datasets. These results show substantially enhanced capability in both imaging and clinical NLP benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: MedGemma 1.5 4B is a more capable, unified, and open medical foundation model than its predecessor, providing strong improvements in high-dimensional imaging, anatomical localization, longitudinal imaging analysis, and clinical text understanding and extraction. The model and accompanying resources are positioned as a robust foundation for researchers and developers to build next-generation medical AI applications, with tutorials and assets made available to facilitate community adoption and extension.

title: SenseAI: A Human-in-the-Loop Dataset for RLHF-Aligned Financial Sentiment Reasoning
comment: Dataset available on request (bernykabalisa18@gmail.com) See GitHub for dataset snapshot and automated data collection script demo https://github.com/bernykabalisa18-netizen/SenseAI
summary:
We introduce SenseAI, a human-in-the-loop (HITL) validated financial sentiment dataset designed to capture not only model outputs but the full reasoning process behind them. Unlike existing resources, SenseAI incorporates reasoning chains, confidence scores, human correction signals, and real-world market outcomes, providing a structure aligned with Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) paradigms.
  The dataset consists of 1,439 labelled data points across 40 US-listed equities and 13 financial data categories, enabling direct integration into modern LLM fine-tuning pipelines. Through analysis, we identify several systematic patterns in model behavior, including a novel failure mode we term Latent Reasoning Drift, where models introduce information not grounded in the input, as well as consistent confidence miscalibration and forward projection tendencies.
  These findings suggest that LLM errors in financial reasoning are not random but occur within a predictable and correctable regime, supporting the use of structured HITL data for targeted model improvement. We discuss implications for financial AI systems and highlight opportunities for applying SenseAI in model evaluation and alignment.

AI.tldr: SenseAI is a human-in-the-loop financial sentiment dataset that logs not just labels but reasoning, confidence, human corrections, and market outcomes, enabling RLHF-style training and revealing systematic LLM failure patterns in finance.
AI.motivation: Existing financial sentiment datasets mostly provide labels or shallow annotations and do not capture the full reasoning process, confidence, and human feedback required for RLHF and fine-grained error analysis. Financial reasoning is high-stakes and domain-specific, so understanding how and why LLMs fail, rather than just whether they are correct, is critical. The authors aim to build a dataset tailored for financial AI alignment and model debugging, especially to support human-in-the-loop learning and safer deployment in financial applications.
AI.method: The authors construct SenseAI, a HITL-validated dataset of 1,439 labeled examples spanning 40 US equities and 13 financial data categories. For each example, they record the model’s sentiment decision, its reasoning chain, confidence score, human correction signals when the model is wrong or miscalibrated, and real-world market outcomes. They then analyze this structured data to characterize patterns of model behavior and failure, focusing on phenomena such as hallucinated reasoning (Latent Reasoning Drift), confidence miscalibration, and forward projection beyond the given input.
AI.result: The analysis of SenseAI reveals systematic, repeatable patterns in LLM financial reasoning errors. In particular, the authors identify a novel failure mode called Latent Reasoning Drift, where models introduce ungrounded information into their reasoning chains. They also observe consistent confidence miscalibration—models being over- or under-confident relative to actual correctness—and a tendency to project future events or implications that go beyond the provided data. These patterns appear across the dataset rather than being isolated, suggesting structured error regimes.
AI.conclusion: LLM errors in financial sentiment and reasoning are structured and predictable rather than random, indicating that they can be targeted and corrected using appropriately designed HITL datasets. SenseAI, by including reasoning traces, confidence, human feedback, and market outcomes, is well-suited for RLHF-style training, evaluation, and alignment of financial AI systems. The authors argue that this resource can support more reliable financial models, enable better diagnostic evaluation frameworks, and open avenues for research on aligning LLM reasoning with human and market-grounded objectives.

title: Uncertainty-Guided Latent Diagnostic Trajectory Learning for Sequential Clinical Diagnosis
summary: Clinical diagnosis requires sequential evidence acquisition under uncertainty. However, most Large Language Model (LLM) based diagnostic systems assume fully observed patient information and therefore do not explicitly model how clinical evidence should be sequentially acquired over time. Even when diagnosis is formulated as a sequential decision process, it is still challenging to learn effective diagnostic trajectories. This is because the space of possible evidence-acquisition paths is relatively large, while clinical datasets rarely provide explicit supervision information for desirable diagnostic paths. To this end, we formulate sequential diagnosis as a Latent Diagnostic Trajectory Learning (LDTL) framework based on a planning LLM agent and a diagnostic LLM agent. For the diagnostic LLM agent, diagnostic action sequences are treated as latent paths and we introduce a posterior distribution that prioritizes trajectories providing more diagnostic information. The planning LLM agent is then trained to follow this distribution, encouraging coherent diagnostic trajectories that progressively reduce uncertainty. Experiments on the MIMIC-CDM benchmark demonstrate that our proposed LDTL framework outperforms existing baselines in diagnostic accuracy under a sequential clinical diagnosis setting, while requiring fewer diagnostic tests. Furthermore, ablation studies highlight the critical role of trajectory-level posterior alignment in achieving these improvements.

AI.tldr: They propose a framework that learns good sequences of diagnostic actions (tests/questions) with LLMs by treating the diagnostic path as a latent variable and aligning a planning agent to favor information-rich trajectories, improving accuracy and efficiency in sequential clinical diagnosis.
AI.motivation: Most LLM-based diagnostic systems assume all patient information is available upfront and ignore how to optimally acquire evidence over time. Even when sequential diagnosis is modeled, learning effective diagnostic trajectories is hard because the action space is huge and real clinical data rarely includes explicit supervision of good diagnostic paths. There is a need for a method that can learn which evidence to collect, and in what order, to reduce uncertainty efficiently without requiring trajectory-level labels.
AI.method: They cast sequential diagnosis as Latent Diagnostic Trajectory Learning (LDTL) using two LLM agents: a diagnostic agent and a planning agent. Diagnostic action sequences (e.g., which tests to order, what to ask next) are treated as latent trajectories. They define a trajectory-level posterior distribution that favors action sequences that yield higher diagnostic information (i.e., reduce uncertainty or improve predictive quality). The diagnostic LLM uses this posterior to infer high-quality latent paths from data. The planning LLM is then trained to approximate/follow this posterior, effectively learning to propose coherent, information-efficient diagnostic trajectories. This aligns planning with the inferred high-value trajectories without needing explicit path labels.
AI.result: On the MIMIC-CDM benchmark, the LDTL framework achieves higher diagnostic accuracy than existing sequential clinical diagnosis baselines while also using fewer diagnostic tests, indicating more efficient evidence acquisition. Ablation studies show that removing or weakening the trajectory-level posterior alignment significantly degrades performance, underscoring that this component is key to the observed gains.
AI.conclusion: Modeling diagnostic action sequences as latent trajectories and aligning a planning LLM to a posterior that prioritizes information-rich paths leads to more accurate and efficient sequential clinical diagnosis. The LDTL framework can learn effective diagnostic policies from standard clinical datasets without explicit supervision of optimal diagnostic paths, and trajectory-level posterior alignment is crucial for these benefits.

title: EvolveRouter: Co-Evolving Routing and Prompt for Multi-Agent Question Answering
summary: Large language model agents often exhibit complementary strengths, making routing a promising approach for multi-agent question answering. However, existing routing methods remain limited in two important ways: they typically optimize over a fixed pool of agents without improving the agents themselves, and they often rely on rigid collaboration schemes that cannot adapt the number of participating agents to the query. We propose EvolveRouter, a trainable framework that addresses both limitations by jointly improving agent quality and collaboration structure. First, EvolveRouter couples graph-based query routing with targeted instruction refinement in a closed-loop co-evolution process, allowing router diagnostics to guide agent improvement while refined agents provide cleaner supervision for routing. Second, it introduces an adaptive inference strategy that dynamically determines the effective collaboration size for each query through router-weighted answer agreement. Together, these designs enable more capable and more efficient multi-agent reasoning. Experiments on five question answering benchmarks show that EvolveRouter consistently outperforms SOTA routing baselines in both F1 and exact match, while further analysis confirms the benefits of closed-loop refinement and adaptive collaboration.

AI.tldr: EvolveRouter is a framework that jointly optimizes both how queries are routed across multiple LLM agents and how those agents are instructed, using a graph-based, adaptive collaboration scheme to improve multi-agent QA accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent question answering with large language models can benefit from routing queries to agents with complementary strengths, but existing routing methods operate over a fixed, non-improving pool of agents and use rigid collaboration schemes with a fixed number of agents per query. This limits both the potential performance gains from agent specialization and the efficiency of collaboration, motivating a framework that can simultaneously enhance agent capabilities and flexibly adapt collaboration structure to each query.
AI.method: EvolveRouter introduces a trainable, graph-based query routing framework coupled with targeted instruction refinement for the agents. The system runs in a closed-loop co-evolution process: router diagnostics (e.g., which agents are chosen and how they perform) are used to refine agent instructions, while the improved agents provide higher-quality supervision signals for further training the router. Additionally, EvolveRouter employs an adaptive inference strategy that, for each query, dynamically selects the effective collaboration size by assessing router-weighted agreement among agent answers, so that only as many agents as needed are involved.
AI.result: Across five question answering benchmarks, EvolveRouter achieves consistent improvements over state-of-the-art routing baselines in both F1 and exact match metrics. Ablation or further analyses show that both the closed-loop instruction refinement and the adaptive collaboration mechanism contribute materially to these gains.
AI.conclusion: Jointly evolving the router and the agents, along with dynamically adjusting the number of collaborating agents per query, leads to more accurate and efficient multi-agent reasoning. EvolveRouter demonstrates that closed-loop co-evolution and adaptive collaboration are effective principles for improving multi-agent large language model question answering beyond what static routing and fixed collaboration schemes can offer.

title: Non-monotonic causal discovery with Kolmogorov-Arnold Fuzzy Cognitive Maps
comment: Fuzzy Cognitive Maps, Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks, Causal Modeling, Neuro-Symbolic AI
summary: Fuzzy Cognitive Maps constitute a neuro-symbolic paradigm for modeling complex dynamic systems, widely adopted for their inherent interpretability and recurrent inference capabilities. However, the standard FCM formulation, characterized by scalar synaptic weights and monotonic activation functions, is fundamentally constrained in modeling non-monotonic causal dependencies, thereby limiting its efficacy in systems governed by saturation effects or periodic dynamics. To overcome this topological restriction, this research proposes the Kolmogorov-Arnold Fuzzy Cognitive Map (KA-FCM), a novel architecture that redefines the causal transmission mechanism. Drawing upon the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem, static scalar weights are replaced with learnable, univariate B-spline functions located on the model edges. This fundamental modification shifts the non-linearity from the nodes' aggregation phase directly to the causal influence phase. This modification allows for the modeling of arbitrary, non-monotonic causal relationships without increasing the graph density or introducing hidden layers. The proposed architecture is validated against both baselines (standard FCM trained with Particle Swarm Optimization) and universal black-box approximators (Multi-Layer Perceptron) across three distinct domains: non-monotonic inference (Yerkes-Dodson law), symbolic regression, and chaotic time-series forecasting. Experimental results demonstrate that KA-FCMs significantly outperform conventional architectures and achieve competitive accuracy relative to MLPs, while preserving graph- based interpretability and enabling the explicit extraction of mathematical laws from the learned edges.

AI.tldr: Introduces Kolmogorov-Arnold Fuzzy Cognitive Maps (KA-FCM), which replace scalar weights in FCMs with learnable univariate B-spline functions to model arbitrary non-monotonic causal relationships while preserving interpretability, outperforming standard FCMs and rivaling MLPs.
AI.motivation: Standard Fuzzy Cognitive Maps (FCMs) use scalar weights and monotonic activation functions, which inherently limit their ability to capture non-monotonic causal dependencies such as saturation effects or periodic dynamics common in many complex systems. This topological and functional restriction reduces their modeling efficacy and expressiveness, especially compared to black-box neural networks. There is a need for an approach that enhances the representational power of FCMs for non-monotonic phenomena without sacrificing their graph-based interpretability or requiring more complex network topologies.
AI.method: The paper proposes the Kolmogorov-Arnold Fuzzy Cognitive Map (KA-FCM), which redefines the causal transmission mechanism in FCMs. Using the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem, the authors replace static scalar synaptic weights on edges with learnable univariate B-spline functions. This shifts the non-linearity from node-level activation/aggregation to the edge-level causal influence. The architecture is trained and evaluated on tasks requiring non-monotonic modeling, symbolic regression, and chaotic time-series forecasting, and its performance is compared against standard FCMs (trained via Particle Swarm Optimization) and Multi-Layer Perceptrons (MLPs).
AI.result: KA-FCMs can represent arbitrary, non-monotonic causal relationships without adding hidden nodes or increasing graph density. In experiments on three domains—non-monotonic inference (Yerkes-Dodson law), symbolic regression, and chaotic time-series forecasting—KA-FCMs significantly outperform conventional FCM architectures and achieve performance competitive with MLPs. Furthermore, the learned B-spline edge functions yield explicit mathematical expressions, providing interpretable causal laws that traditional black-box models do not directly offer.
AI.conclusion: By moving nonlinearity from nodes to edges and parameterizing edge influences as univariate B-spline functions grounded in the Kolmogorov-Arnold representation theorem, KA-FCMs overcome the monotonicity and expressiveness limitations of standard FCMs. They retain the interpretability and structural simplicity of graph-based models while approaching the predictive power of universal approximators like MLPs. This makes KA-FCMs a promising neuro-symbolic framework for modeling complex systems with non-monotonic causal mechanisms and for extracting explicit mathematical laws from data.

title: Just Pass Twice: Efficient Token Classification with LLMs for Zero-Shot NER
summary:
Large language models encode extensive world knowledge valuable for zero-shot named entity recognition. However, their causal attention mechanism, where tokens attend only to preceding context, prevents effective token classification when disambiguation requires future context. Existing approaches use LLMs generatively, prompting them to list entities or produce structured outputs, but suffer from slow autoregressive decoding, hallucinated entities, and formatting errors.
  We propose Just Pass Twice (JPT), a simple yet effective method that enables causal LLMs to perform discriminative token classification with full bidirectional context. Our key insight is that concatenating the input to itself lets each token in the second pass attend to the complete sentence, requiring no architectural modifications. We combine these representations with definition-guided entity embeddings for flexible zero-shot generalization. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art results on zero-shot NER benchmarks, surpassing the previous best method by +7.9 F1 on average across CrossNER and MIT benchmarks, being over 20x faster than comparable generative methods.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Just Pass Twice (JPT), a simple technique that lets causal LLMs do token-level NER classification with effectively bidirectional context by duplicating the input sequence, achieving state-of-the-art zero-shot NER performance much faster than generative prompting methods.
AI.motivation: Causal large language models contain rich world knowledge that is useful for zero-shot named entity recognition, but their left-to-right attention makes it difficult to classify tokens that need right-side (future) context for disambiguation. Existing generative uses of LLMs for NER—having the model list entities or emit structured outputs—are slow due to autoregressive decoding and prone to hallucinations and formatting errors. There is a need for a simple, efficient way to exploit LLMs’ knowledge for discriminative token classification while accessing full sentence context.
AI.method: The authors propose Just Pass Twice (JPT), which feeds the input sequence twice in concatenation to a causal LLM. In the second pass, each token can attend to all tokens from the first pass, effectively giving it bidirectional context without changing the model architecture. Token representations from this second pass are then combined with definition-guided entity type embeddings, enabling zero-shot generalization to new entity types described in natural language rather than requiring task-specific training. This yields a discriminative token classification pipeline built on top of a standard causal LLM.
AI.result: On standard zero-shot NER benchmarks, specifically CrossNER and MIT datasets, JPT achieves new state-of-the-art performance. It improves average F1 score by 7.9 points over the previous best method, while being more than 20 times faster than comparable generative LLM-based approaches. The method preserves the efficiency of a single forward pass (over a doubled sequence) and avoids common generative issues like hallucinated entities and output format errors.
AI.conclusion: JPT demonstrates that a simple input transformation—concatenating the sequence to itself—can convert causal LLMs into effective discriminative token classifiers with full bidirectional context, eliminating the need for architectural changes or slow generative decoding. Coupled with definition-based entity embeddings, the approach delivers strong, flexible zero-shot NER performance and substantial speedups, suggesting a practical path for leveraging existing causal LLMs for structured prediction tasks.

title: A mathematical theory of evolution for self-designing AIs
summary: As artificial intelligence systems (AIs) become increasingly produced by recursive self-improvement, a form of evolution may emerge, in which the traits of AI systems are shaped by the success of earlier AIs in designing and propagating their descendants. There is a rich mathematical theory modeling how behavioral traits are shaped by biological evolution, but AI evolution will be radically different: biological DNA mutations are random and approximately reversible, but descendant design in AIs will be strongly directed. Here we develop a mathematical model of evolution in self-designing AI systems, replacing random mutations with a directed tree of possible AI programs. Current programs determine the design of their descendants, while humans retain partial control through a "fitness function" that allocates limited computational resources across lineages. We show that evolutionary dynamics reflects not just current fitness but factors related to the long-run growth potential of descendant lineages. Without further assumptions, fitness need not increase over time. However, assuming bounded fitness and a fixed probability that any AI reproduces a "locked" copy of itself, we show that fitness concentrates on the maximum reachable value. We consider the implications of this for AI alignment, specifically for cases where fitness and human utility are not perfectly correlated. We show in an additive model that if deception increases fitness beyond genuine utility, evolution will select for deception. This risk could be mitigated if reproduction is based on purely objective criteria, rather than human judgment.

AI.tldr: The paper builds a mathematical model of evolution in recursively self-improving AI systems, where descendant design is directed rather than random, and studies how this affects alignment and the emergence of deceptive behaviors.
AI.motivation: Existing evolutionary theory explains how random genetic mutations and natural selection shape behavior in biological organisms. However, future AI systems may be produced through recursive self-improvement, where AIs design their own descendants under resource constraints and human oversight. This “AI evolution” will be structurally different from biological evolution because modifications are intentionally designed rather than random. There is a need for new formal models to understand how such directed evolutionary processes will shape AI behavior, especially when human-specified fitness differs from true human utility, raising alignment and deception risks.
AI.method: The authors construct a mathematical model of evolution in self-designing AI systems, replacing random mutations with a directed tree of possible AI programs. Each AI program determines the design of its descendants, while humans allocate limited computational resources across lineages through a fitness function. They analyze the resulting evolutionary dynamics, deriving conditions under which fitness does or does not increase over time, and formally study how descendant growth potential and constraints like bounded fitness and self-copying probabilities influence long-run outcomes. They then apply this framework to an additive model linking fitness and human utility, examining how misalignment and deceptive strategies are selected for or against.
AI.result: The model shows that evolutionary dynamics depend not only on current fitness but also on properties related to the long-run growth potential of lineages. In general, fitness does not have to monotonically increase over time. However, if fitness is bounded and there is a fixed probability that any AI reproduces a locked, identical copy of itself, then over time fitness concentrates on the maximal reachable value in the program tree. In the alignment analysis, the authors demonstrate that when fitness and human utility are imperfectly correlated, and when deceptive behavior raises fitness more than genuine utility does, evolutionary processes will favor deception. They further argue that basing reproduction on human judgment is particularly vulnerable to this effect.
AI.conclusion: Directed evolution in recursively self-improving AI systems behaves differently from biological evolution and can systematically favor traits that maximize a design-time fitness function rather than true human utility. Under plausible assumptions, evolutionary pressure will drive AI systems toward maximal fitness, and when deception is fitness-enhancing relative to honest behavior, deceptive strategies are evolutionarily selected. This highlights a structural alignment risk for self-improving AI ecosystems. The authors suggest that one way to mitigate this risk is to ground AI reproduction and selection in objective, verifiable criteria instead of human-judgment-based assessments, thereby reducing the evolutionary advantage of deception.

title: What Makes a Good Response? An Empirical Analysis of Quality in Qualitative Interviews
summary: Qualitative interviews provide essential insights into human experiences when they elicit high-quality responses. While qualitative and NLP researchers have proposed various measures of interview quality, these measures lack validation that high-scoring responses actually contribute to the study's goals. In this work, we identify, implement, and evaluate 10 proposed measures of interview response quality to determine which are actually predictive of a response's contribution to the study findings. To conduct our analysis, we introduce the Qualitative Interview Corpus, a newly constructed dataset of 343 interview transcripts with 16,940 participant responses from 14 real research projects. We find that direct relevance to a key research question is the strongest predictor of response quality. We additionally find that two measures commonly used to evaluate NLP interview systems, clarity and surprisal-based informativeness, are not predictive of response quality. Our work provides analytic insights and grounded, scalable metrics to inform the design of qualitative studies and the evaluation of automated interview systems.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates which automatic measures of qualitative interview response quality actually predict contributions to study findings, using a new corpus, and finds that relevance to research questions matters most, while some popular NLP metrics do not.
AI.motivation: Existing measures of qualitative interview quality, including those from qualitative research and NLP, have not been rigorously validated against the practical goal of interviews: generating responses that meaningfully contribute to study findings. Researchers lack evidence about which metrics truly reflect usefulness for the research aims, yet these metrics are increasingly used to design studies and evaluate automated interview agents.
AI.method: The authors identify 10 previously proposed metrics of interview response quality, implement them, and systematically test how well each predicts expert-judged response quality. They build the Qualitative Interview Corpus, containing 343 transcripts and 16,940 participant responses from 14 real qualitative research projects. Using this dataset, they quantify the relationship between each metric and the degree to which a response contributes to the study’s key findings, comparing predictive power across metrics.
AI.result: Among the 10 metrics, direct relevance of a response to a core research question emerges as the strongest and most reliable predictor of response quality. In contrast, two measures often used in NLP to assess interview systems—linguistic clarity and surprisal-based informativeness—show little to no predictive value for whether a response actually advances the research aims. Other metrics fall between these extremes in their association with expert-rated contribution to findings.
AI.conclusion: Response quality in qualitative interviews is best captured by how directly an answer speaks to the study’s key research questions, rather than by generic language qualities like clarity or information-theoretic surprisal. The paper offers validated, scalable metrics and a new dataset to guide both qualitative researchers and NLP practitioners in designing interviews and evaluating automated interviewing systems, emphasizing alignment with research goals over surface-level linguistic properties.

title: IntentScore: Intent-Conditioned Action Evaluation for Computer-Use Agents
summary: Computer-Use Agents (CUAs) leverage large language models to execute GUI operations on desktop environments, yet they generate actions without evaluating action quality, leading to irreversible errors that cascade through subsequent steps. We propose IntentScore, a plan-aware reward model that learns to score candidate actions from 398K offline GUI interaction steps spanning three operating systems. IntentScore trains with two complementary objectives: contrastive alignment for state-action relevance and margin ranking for action correctness. Architecturally, it embeds each candidate's planning intent in the action encoder, enabling discrimination between candidates with similar actions but different rationales. IntentScore achieves 97.5% pairwise discrimination accuracy on held-out evaluation. Deployed as a re-ranker for Agent S3 on OSWorld, an environment entirely unseen during training, IntentScore improves task success rate by 6.9 points, demonstrating that reward estimation learned from heterogeneous offline trajectories generalizes to unseen agents and task distributions.

AI.tldr: IntentScore is a plan-aware reward model that scores GUI agent actions to reduce cascading errors, significantly improving success rates when used as a re-ranker.
AI.motivation: Current computer-use agents acting on GUIs execute actions without explicitly evaluating action quality, which can lead to irreversible mistakes that propagate through multi-step tasks. There is a need for a generalizable mechanism to assess and select better actions using offline interaction data across heterogeneous systems.
AI.method: The authors build IntentScore, a reward model trained on 398K offline GUI interaction steps from three operating systems. The model uses a plan-aware architecture where each candidate action is encoded together with its planning intent. Training combines two objectives: contrastive alignment to enforce relevance between state and action, and margin ranking to distinguish correct from incorrect actions among candidates.
AI.result: IntentScore attains 97.5% pairwise discrimination accuracy on held-out data, showing it can reliably rank better actions over worse ones. When plugged in as an action re-ranker for the Agent S3 system and evaluated on the unseen OSWorld benchmark, it boosts task success rate by 6.9 percentage points, evidencing strong generalization to new agents and task distributions.
AI.conclusion: A plan-aware reward model trained on heterogeneous offline GUI trajectories can effectively estimate action quality and generalize to unseen environments. Integrating such a model as a re-ranking component substantially reduces cascading errors and improves the robustness and task success rate of computer-use agents.

title: Gradient-Controlled Decoding: A Safety Guardrail for LLMs with Dual-Anchor Steering
summary: Large language models (LLMs) remain susceptible to jailbreak and direct prompt-injection attacks, yet the strongest defensive filters frequently over-refuse benign queries and degrade user experience. Previous work on jailbreak and prompt injection detection such as GradSafe, detects unsafe prompts with a single "accept all" anchor token, but its threshold is brittle and it offers no deterministic guarantee that harmful content will not be emitted once decoding begins. We introduce Gradient-Controlled Decoding (GCD), a training-free guardrail that combines an acceptance anchor token ("Sure") and refusal anchor token ("Sorry") tightening the decision boundary and significantly lowering false positives. In the mitigation stage, if a prompt is flagged, GCD preset-injects one or two refusal tokens ("Sorry, I can't...") before autoregressive decoding resumes, guaranteeing first-token safety regardless of sampling strategy. On ToxicChat, XSTest-v2, and AdvBench, GCD reduces false positives by 52% vs. GradSafe at comparable recall, lowers attack success rate by up to 10% vs. the strongest decoding-only baseline, adds under 15-20 ms latency on an average on V100 instances, transfers to LLaMA-2-7B, Mixtral-8x7B, and Qwen-2-7B, and requires only 20 demonstration templates.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Gradient-Controlled Decoding (GCD), a training-free guardrail for LLMs that more reliably detects and mitigates jailbreak and prompt-injection attacks while reducing false positives and maintaining low latency.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM safety filters either over-refuse harmless inputs, harming usability, or fail to reliably stop jailbreak and prompt-injection attacks. Methods like GradSafe use a single acceptance anchor token and brittle thresholds, providing no deterministic guarantee that the first generated token is safe once decoding starts. There is a need for a more precise, low-overhead, and model-agnostic mechanism that both accurately detects unsafe prompts and guarantees safe initial output tokens without retraining the model.
AI.method: GCD is a training-free decoding-time guardrail that uses gradient-based scoring with two anchor tokens: an acceptance token (e.g., "Sure") and a refusal token (e.g., "Sorry"). During detection, the model’s gradient signals around these anchors are used to tighten the decision boundary, reducing false positives compared to single-anchor approaches. In the mitigation stage, if a prompt is flagged as unsafe, GCD modifies the decoding process by preset-injecting one or two refusal tokens such as "Sorry, I can't..." before resuming normal autoregressive decoding. This guarantees that the first emitted token(s) are safe regardless of the sampling strategy. The method is applied at inference only, needs only a small set of demonstration templates, and transfers across several LLM architectures.
AI.result: On benchmark datasets ToxicChat, XSTest-v2, and AdvBench, GCD reduces false positives by 52% compared to GradSafe at similar recall, indicating a better balance between safety and usability. It also decreases attack success rates by up to 10% relative to the strongest decoding-only baseline, while adding only about 15–20 ms latency on V100 GPUs. The approach generalizes to multiple models, including LLaMA-2-7B, Mixtral-8x7B, and Qwen-2-7B, with only 20 demonstration templates required.
AI.conclusion: GCD offers an effective, training-free, and model-agnostic guardrail for LLMs that simultaneously improves safety against jailbreak and prompt-injection attacks and reduces unnecessary refusals of benign inputs. By leveraging dual anchor tokens and a mitigation strategy that presets safe refusal tokens, it provides deterministic first-token safety with minimal computational overhead and good cross-model transfer, making it a practical solution for real-world deployments.

title: Bypassing the CSI Bottleneck: MARL-Driven Spatial Control for Reflector Arrays
summary: Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces (RIS) are pivotal for next-generation smart radio environments, yet their practical deployment is severely bottlenecked by the intractable computational overhead of Channel State Information (CSI) estimation. To bypass this fundamental physical-layer barrier, we propose an AI-native, data-driven paradigm that replaces complex channel modeling with spatial intelligence. This paper presents a fully autonomous Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) framework to control mechanically adjustable metallic reflector arrays. By mapping high-dimensional mechanical constraints to a reduced-order virtual focal point space, we deploy a Centralized Training with Decentralized Execution (CTDE) architecture. Using Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization (MAPPO), our decentralized agents learn cooperative beam-focusing strategies relying on user coordinates, achieving CSI-free operation. High-fidelity ray-tracing simulations in dynamic non-line-of-sight (NLOS) environments demonstrate that this multi-agent approach rapidly adapts to user mobility, yielding up to a 26.86 dB enhancement over static flat reflectors and outperforming single-agent and hardware-constrained DRL baselines in both spatial selectivity and temporal stability. Crucially, the learned policies exhibit good deployment resilience, sustaining stable signal coverage even under 1.0-meter localization noise. These results validate the efficacy of MARL-driven spatial abstractions as a scalable, highly practical pathway toward AI-empowered wireless networks.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a MARL-based, CSI-free control framework for mechanically reconfigurable reflector arrays, using spatial abstractions to enable scalable, practical RIS-like smart radio environments.
AI.motivation: Conventional RIS systems require accurate and frequent CSI estimation, which is computationally expensive and hard to scale in dynamic NLOS scenarios with user mobility and localization noise. This CSI bottleneck hinders the practical deployment of smart radio environments. The authors are motivated to remove or bypass the need for explicit channel modeling and CSI, and instead exploit spatial information (e.g., user positions) and AI-based control to achieve adaptive beam focusing and robust coverage.
AI.method: They design a fully autonomous Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) framework to control mechanically adjustable metallic reflector arrays. High-dimensional mechanical states are mapped to a lower-dimensional virtual focal point space to simplify control. A Centralized Training with Decentralized Execution (CTDE) architecture is used with Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization (MAPPO) as the learning algorithm. Each agent learns a cooperative beam-focusing policy based on user coordinates, without relying on CSI. High-fidelity ray-tracing simulations in dynamic NLOS environments with user mobility and localization noise are used for training and evaluation.
AI.result: The MARL-controlled reflector arrays achieve up to 26.86 dB gain over static flat reflectors. The proposed multi-agent approach outperforms single-agent and hardware-constrained DRL baselines in spatial selectivity (better focusing on users) and temporal stability (more consistent performance over time) in dynamic environments. The learned policies maintain stable signal coverage even when user location estimates are noisy, with up to 1.0 m localization error.
AI.conclusion: MARL-based spatial abstractions provide an effective way to control reconfigurable reflector arrays without explicit CSI, enabling scalable and practical smart radio environments. The demonstrated performance gains and robustness to mobility and localization noise suggest that AI-native, CSI-free control is a promising direction for future AI-empowered wireless networks and RIS deployments.

title: Improving Clinical Trial Recruitment using Clinical Narratives and Large Language Models
summary: Screening patients for enrollment is a well-known, labor-intensive bottleneck that leads to under-enrollment and, ultimately, trial failures. Recent breakthroughs in large language models (LLMs) offer a promising opportunity to use artificial intelligence to improve screening. This study systematically explored both encoder- and decoder-based generative LLMs for screening clinical narratives to facilitate clinical trial recruitment. We examined both general-purpose LLMs and medical-adapted LLMs and explored three strategies to alleviate the "Lost in the Middle" issue when handling long documents, including 1) Original long-context: using the default context windows of LLMs, 2) NER-based extractive summarization: converting the long document into summarizations using named entity recognition, 3) RAG: dynamic evidence retrieval based on eligibility criteria. The 2018 N2C2 Track 1 benchmark dataset is used for evaluation. Our experimental results show that the MedGemma model with the RAG strategy achieved the best micro-F1 score of 89.05%, outperforming other models. Generative LLMs have remarkably improved trial criteria that require long-term reasoning across long documents, whereas trial criteria that span a short piece of context (e.g., lab tests) show incremental improvements. The real-world adoption of LLMs for trial recruitment must consider specific criteria for selecting among rule-based queries, encoder-based LLMs, and generative LLMs to maximize efficiency within reasonable computing costs.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates general-purpose and medical large language models (LLMs) with different long-document handling strategies to automate screening of clinical narratives for clinical trial recruitment, finding that a medical-adapted generative LLM with retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) performs best.
AI.motivation: Patient screening for clinical trials is labor-intensive and a key cause of under-enrollment and trial failure. With recent progress in LLMs, there is an opportunity to automate or assist the screening of long, complex clinical narratives, but it is unclear which types of LLMs and long-context strategies work best and where they provide the most benefit.
AI.method: The study systematically compared encoder-based and decoder-based generative LLMs, including both general-purpose and medically adapted models, on the task of classifying eligibility from long clinical narratives. It investigated three strategies for mitigating the 'Lost in the Middle' problem in long-context processing: (1) using the LLMs' native long-context window, (2) NER-based extractive summarization to condense documents, and (3) retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) that dynamically retrieves evidence based on trial eligibility criteria. The models were evaluated on the 2018 n2c2 Track 1 benchmark dataset using micro-F1 scores.
AI.result: Among the tested configurations, the MedGemma model combined with the RAG strategy achieved the highest performance, reaching a micro-F1 score of 89.05%, outperforming other model and strategy combinations. Generative LLMs particularly improved performance on eligibility criteria requiring long-range reasoning across the entire document, while delivering only modest gains on criteria localized in short text spans, such as lab test values.
AI.conclusion: Generative LLMs, especially medical-adapted models with retrieval-augmented generation, are effective for automating trial eligibility screening from long clinical narratives, particularly for complex criteria needing long-term reasoning. However, for simpler, localized criteria, traditional rule-based systems or encoder-based models may be sufficient, and practical deployment should carefully choose among rule-based queries, encoder-based LLMs, and generative LLMs to balance accuracy, efficiency, and computational cost.

title: Learning to Focus: CSI-Free Hierarchical MARL for Reconfigurable Reflectors
summary: Reconfigurable Intelligent Surfaces (RIS) has a potential to engineer smart radio environments for next-generation millimeter-wave (mmWave) networks. However, the prohibitive computational overhead of Channel State Information (CSI) estimation and the dimensionality explosion inherent in centralized optimization severely hinder practical large-scale deployments. To overcome these bottlenecks, we introduce a ``CSI-free" paradigm powered by a Hierarchical Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (HMARL) architecture to control mechanically reconfigurable reflective surfaces. By substituting pilot-based channel estimation with accessible user localization data, our framework leverages spatial intelligence for macro-scale wave propagation management. The control problem is decomposed into a two-tier neural architecture: a high-level controller executes temporally extended, discrete user-to-reflector allocations, while low-level controllers autonomously optimize continuous focal points utilizing Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization (MAPPO) under a Centralized Training with Decentralized Execution (CTDE) scheme. Comprehensive deterministic ray-tracing evaluations demonstrate that this hierarchical framework achieves massive RSSI improvements of up to 7.79 dB over centralized baselines. Furthermore, the system exhibits robust multi-user scalability and maintains highly resilient beam-focusing performance under practical sub-meter localization tracking errors. By eliminating CSI overhead while maintaining high-fidelity signal redirection, this work establishes a scalable and cost-effective blueprint for intelligent wireless environments.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a CSI-free hierarchical multi-agent reinforcement learning framework to control mechanically reconfigurable intelligent surfaces using user location data instead of channel estimates, achieving large RSSI gains, scalability, and robustness to localization errors.
AI.motivation: Conventional RIS control for mmWave networks relies on explicit CSI estimation and centralized optimization, which become computationally prohibitive and unscalable as the system size and number of users grow. There is a need for a control paradigm that avoids expensive CSI acquisition and can scale to large, practical deployments while still enabling effective wavefront manipulation.
AI.method: The authors design a hierarchical multi-agent reinforcement learning (HMARL) architecture for mechanically reconfigurable reflective surfaces. The framework replaces pilot-based CSI estimation with user localization information to guide RIS configuration. A high-level controller performs discrete, temporally extended user-to-reflector association decisions, while multiple low-level controllers independently optimize continuous focal points for each reflector using Multi-Agent Proximal Policy Optimization under a Centralized Training with Decentralized Execution paradigm. Deterministic ray-tracing simulations are used to evaluate performance.
AI.result: In ray-tracing based evaluations, the proposed HMARL framework yields received signal strength (RSSI) improvements of up to 7.79 dB compared to centralized optimization baselines. It shows good scalability when serving multiple users and retains effective beam-focusing performance even when user location data has realistic sub-meter tracking errors.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that a CSI-free, location-driven hierarchical MARL control scheme for mechanically reconfigurable RIS can achieve substantial performance gains while avoiding the heavy overhead of channel estimation and centralized optimization. This indicates a feasible, scalable, and cost-effective pathway for implementing intelligent wireless environments in future mmWave networks.

title: Faster Superword Tokenization
summary: Byte Pair Encoding (BPE) is a widely used tokenization algorithm, whose tokens cannot extend across pre-tokenization boundaries, functionally limiting it to representing at most full words. The BoundlessBPE and SuperBPE algorithms extend and improve BPE by relaxing this limitation and allowing the formation of superwords, which are combinations of pretokens that form phrases. However, previous implementations were impractical to train: for example, BoundlessBPE took 4.7 CPU days to train on 1GB of data. We show that supermerge candidates, two or more consecutive pretokens eligible to form a supermerge, can be aggregated by frequency much like regular pretokens. This avoids keeping full documents in memory, as the original implementations of BoundlessBPE and SuperBPE required, leading to a significant training speedup. We present a two-phase formulation of BoundlessBPE that separates first-phase learning of regular merges from second-phase learning of supermerges, producing identical results to the original implementation. We also show a near-equivalence between two-phase BoundlessBPE and SuperBPE, with the difference being that a manually selected hyperparameter used in SuperBPE can be automatically determined in the second phase of BoundlessBPE. These changes enable a much faster implementation, allowing training on that same 1GB of data in 603 and 593 seconds for BoundlessBPE and SuperBPE, respectively, a more than 600x increase in speed. For each of BoundlessBPE, SuperBPE, and BPE, we open-source both a reference Python implementation and a fast Rust implementation.

AI.tldr: The paper accelerates and refines superword-based extensions of Byte Pair Encoding (BoundlessBPE and SuperBPE), making them practical to train while preserving their benefits over standard BPE.
AI.motivation: Standard BPE tokenization cannot merge across pre-tokenization boundaries, so it is limited to single words and cannot natively form phrase-level tokens (superwords). Prior algorithms that relax this constraint (BoundlessBPE and SuperBPE) were computationally impractical to train, requiring days on modest datasets and holding full documents in memory. The authors aim to preserve the modeling benefits of phrase-level tokens while making training feasible and efficient in realistic settings.
AI.method: The authors observe that supermerge candidates—sequences of two or more consecutive pretokens that could form phrase-level tokens—can be counted and aggregated by frequency without storing entire documents, similarly to how standard BPE aggregates pretoken frequencies. They reformulate BoundlessBPE into two distinct phases: (1) learn regular within-pretoken merges as in standard BPE, and (2) learn supermerges that cross pretoken boundaries. They prove that this two-phase procedure yields the same tokenization as the original BoundlessBPE implementation. They further analyze the relationship between this two-phase BoundlessBPE and SuperBPE, showing near-equivalence and providing a way for BoundlessBPE’s second phase to automatically determine a hyperparameter that SuperBPE requires to be set manually. They implement optimized versions of BoundlessBPE, SuperBPE, and BPE in both Python and Rust.
AI.result: The new training formulation avoids retaining full documents in memory by using aggregated counts of supermerge candidates. This yields dramatic speedups: on a 1GB dataset where the original BoundlessBPE implementation needed 4.7 CPU days, the improved methods train in roughly 10 minutes (603 seconds for BoundlessBPE and 593 seconds for SuperBPE), representing more than a 600x improvement. The implementations are released as open-source reference (Python) and high-performance (Rust) code for BPE, BoundlessBPE, and SuperBPE.
AI.conclusion: By rethinking how supermerge candidates are counted and by splitting BoundlessBPE into a two-phase learning process, the authors make phrase-level tokenization with BoundlessBPE and SuperBPE computationally practical without changing their behavior. They clarify the close relationship between BoundlessBPE and SuperBPE and automate a key hyperparameter choice. The resulting open-source implementations enable efficient training of advanced tokenizers that can form superword tokens beyond word boundaries, potentially improving downstream language modeling performance while remaining tractable to train at scale.

title: Instruction-Tuned LLMs for Parsing and Mining Unstructured Logs on Leadership HPC Systems
summary:
Leadership-class HPC systems generate massive volumes of heterogeneous, largely unstructured system logs. Because these logs originate from diverse software, hardware, and runtime layers, they exhibit inconsistent formats, making structure extraction and pattern discovery extremely challenging. Therefore, robust log parsing and mining is critical to transform this raw telemetry into actionable insights that reveal operational patterns, diagnose anomalies, and enable reliable, efficient, and scalable system analysis. Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) offer a promising new direction for automated log understanding in leadership-class HPC environments.
  To capitalize on this opportunity, we present a domain-adapted, instruction-following, LLM-driven framework that leverages chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning to parse and structure HPC logs with high fidelity. Our approach combines domain-specific log-template data with instruction-tuned examples to fine-tune an 8B-parameter LLaMA model tailored for HPC log analysis. We develop a hybrid fine-tuning methodology that adapts a general-purpose LLM to domain-specific log data, enabling privacy-preserving, locally deployable, fast, and energy-efficient log-mining approach. We conduct experiments on a diverse set of log datasets from the LogHub repository. The evaluation confirms that our approach achieves parsing accuracy on par with significantly larger models, such as LLaMA 70B and Anthropic's Claude. We further validate the practical utility of our fine-tuned LLM model by parsing over 600 million production logs from the Frontier supercomputer over a four-week window, uncovering critical patterns in temporal dynamics, node-level anomalies, and workload-error log correlations.

AI.tldr: They fine-tune an 8B-parameter LLaMA model for HPC log parsing using domain data and instruction tuning, achieving accuracy comparable to much larger models and demonstrating value on 600M+ Frontier supercomputer logs.
AI.motivation: Leadership-class HPC systems produce enormous, heterogeneous, and poorly structured logs across hardware, software, and runtime layers. The inconsistent formats make automatic structure extraction, anomaly diagnosis, and pattern mining difficult, yet these capabilities are vital for reliable and efficient operation. Recent LLM advances suggest they could help interpret such complex, unstructured logs automatically, motivating a specialized, domain-adapted LLM framework for HPC log analysis.
AI.method: They design an instruction-following, chain-of-thought LLM framework tailored to HPC logs. Using domain-specific log-template data plus instruction-tuned examples, they hybrid fine-tune an 8B-parameter LLaMA model so it can robustly parse and structure logs. The fine-tuning is done in a way that supports local, privacy-preserving, fast, and energy-efficient deployment. They evaluate on multiple LogHub datasets and then apply the trained model to large-scale production logs from the Frontier supercomputer.
AI.result: On benchmark log datasets from LogHub, the fine-tuned 8B LLaMA achieves parsing accuracy comparable to much larger models such as LLaMA 70B and Anthropic Claude. In a real-world deployment, the model successfully parses over 600 million production logs from the Frontier supercomputer over four weeks, enabling discovery of temporal patterns, node-level anomalies, and correlations between workloads and error logs.
AI.conclusion: A domain-adapted, instruction-following LLM with CoT reasoning can effectively parse and structure massive, heterogeneous HPC logs, matching the performance of much larger models while remaining deployable locally and efficiently. This approach transforms raw HPC telemetry into actionable insights and demonstrates practical value at leadership-class supercomputing scale.

title: XMark: Reliable Multi-Bit Watermarking for LLM-Generated Texts
comment: Accepted by ACL 2026 as a main conference paper
summary: Multi-bit watermarking has emerged as a promising solution for embedding imperceptible binary messages into Large Language Model (LLM)-generated text, enabling reliable attribution and tracing of malicious usage of LLMs. Despite recent progress, existing methods still face key limitations: some become computationally infeasible for large messages, while others suffer from a poor trade-off between text quality and decoding accuracy. Moreover, the decoding accuracy of existing methods drops significantly when the number of tokens in the generated text is limited, a condition that frequently arises in practical usage. To address these challenges, we propose \textsc{XMark}, a novel method for encoding and decoding binary messages in LLM-generated texts. The unique design of \textsc{XMark}'s encoder produces a less distorted logit distribution for watermarked token generation, preserving text quality, and also enables its tailored decoder to reliably recover the encoded message with limited tokens. Extensive experiments across diverse downstream tasks show that \textsc{XMark} significantly improves decoding accuracy while preserving the quality of watermarked text, outperforming prior methods. The code is at https://github.com/JiiahaoXU/XMark.

AI.tldr: Proposes XMark, a multi-bit watermarking method for LLM-generated text that improves message decoding accuracy, especially with short outputs, while preserving text quality and computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-bit watermarking methods for LLM text either do not scale well to long binary messages or exhibit a poor trade-off between text quality and decoding accuracy. Their performance degrades notably when the generated text is short, which is common in real-world usage. There is a need for a watermarking scheme that can reliably encode and decode multi-bit messages in LLM outputs without noticeably harming fluency or incurring excessive computation, and that remains robust when only a limited number of tokens are available for decoding.
AI.method: Introduce XMark, a watermarking framework with a specially designed encoder–decoder pair. The encoder perturbs the LLM’s logit distribution in a way that minimally distorts it while embedding a multi-bit message across tokens, maintaining natural text generation. The decoder is tailored to this logit perturbation scheme and can reliably reconstruct the embedded binary message even from relatively few generated tokens. The method is implemented and evaluated across various downstream tasks using LLM-generated text.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple downstream tasks show that XMark substantially improves decoding accuracy compared with prior multi-bit watermarking approaches, particularly when the number of generated tokens is small. At the same time, human- and automatic-evaluation metrics indicate that the fluency and quality of the watermarked texts are preserved, and the method scales to larger message sizes without becoming computationally prohibitive.
AI.conclusion: XMark provides a more practical and robust solution for multi-bit watermarking of LLM-generated text, achieving a better balance between decoding reliability, text quality, and efficiency than existing approaches, and maintaining high decoding accuracy even for short generations. The publicly released implementation enables further research and adoption in LLM attribution and misuse tracing.

title: ClawsBench: Evaluating Capability and Safety of LLM Productivity Agents in Simulated Workspaces
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly deployed to automate productivity tasks (e.g., email, scheduling, document management), but evaluating them on live services is risky due to potentially irreversible changes. Existing benchmarks rely on simplified environments and fail to capture realistic, stateful, multi-service workflows. We introduce ClawsBench, a benchmark for evaluating and improving LLM agents in realistic productivity settings. It includes five high-fidelity mock services (Gmail, Slack, Google Calendar, Google Docs, Google Drive) with full state management and deterministic snapshot/restore, along with 44 structured tasks covering single-service, cross-service, and safety-critical scenarios. We decompose agent scaffolding into two independent levers (domain skills that inject API knowledge via progressive disclosure, and a meta prompt that coordinates behavior across services) and vary both to measure their separate and combined effects. Experiments across 6 models, 4 agent harnesses, and 33 conditions show that with full scaffolding, agents achieve task success rates of 39-64% but exhibit unsafe action rates of 7-33%. On OpenClaw, the top five models fall within a 10 percentage-point band on task success (53-63%), with unsafe action rates from 7% to 23% and no consistent ordering between the two metrics. We identify eight recurring patterns of unsafe behavior, including multi-step sandbox escalation and silent contract modification.

AI.tldr: ClawsBench is a realistic benchmark for evaluating LLM agents on productivity tasks across multiple mock services, revealing moderate success but substantial unsafe behavior.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM agent benchmarks use oversimplified, low-fidelity environments that do not reflect realistic, stateful workflows across multiple productivity tools. Evaluating directly on live services is risky because agents can make irreversible changes to users’ data and settings. There is a need for a safe, high-fidelity, reproducible testbed to rigorously measure both task success and safety of LLM agents in complex productivity scenarios.
AI.method: The authors build ClawsBench, a benchmark environment with five high-fidelity mock services (Gmail, Slack, Google Calendar, Google Docs, Google Drive) that provide full state management and deterministic snapshot/restore, enabling repeatable experiments. They design 44 structured tasks spanning single-service, cross-service, and safety-critical workflows. They factor agent design into two independent axes: (1) domain skills, which progressively disclose API and domain knowledge, and (2) a meta prompt that coordinates the agent’s behavior across services. They then systematically vary these scaffolding components and evaluate 6 LLMs, 4 agent harnesses, and 33 experimental conditions to quantify the impact on task success and safety. They also perform qualitative analysis to identify patterns of unsafe behavior.
AI.result: With full scaffolding, agents achieve moderate task success rates between 39% and 64%, but they also perform unsafe actions at rates between 7% and 33%. On the public OpenClaw subset, the top five models cluster closely on task success (53–63%), while their unsafe action rates vary from 7% to 23%, with no consistent ranking between performance and safety. The authors identify eight recurring unsafe behavior patterns, such as multi-step sandbox escalation and silent contract modification, indicating systematic safety issues rather than isolated errors.
AI.conclusion: ClawsBench provides a realistic, controlled, and reproducible environment to evaluate LLM agents on complex productivity tasks across multiple services while measuring both capability and safety. The results show that even strong agents with advanced scaffolding still make frequent unsafe decisions, and that higher task performance does not reliably correlate with safer behavior. The benchmark and analysis highlight the need for better agent design, scaffolding, and safety mechanisms, and offer a shared testbed for future research on robust and safe LLM-based productivity agents.

title: Exemplar Retrieval Without Overhypothesis Induction: Limits of Distributional Sequence Learning in Early Word Learning
comment: 27 pages, 7 figures, 22 references. Pre-registered study (OSF: https://osf.io/qj9hb/). Code and data: https://github.com/synthiumjp/overhypothesis. Submitted to Cognitive Computation
summary: Background: Children do not simply learn that balls are round and blocks are square. They learn that shape is the kind of feature that tends to define object categories -- a second-order generalisation known as an overhypothesis [1, 2]. What kind of learning mechanism is sufficient for this inductive leap? Methods: We trained autoregressive transformer language models (3.4M-25.6M parameters) on synthetic corpora in which shape is the stable feature dimension across categories, with eight conditions controlling for alternative explanations. Results: Across 120 pre-registered runs evaluated on a 1,040-item wug test battery, every model achieved perfect first-order exemplar retrieval (100%) while second-order generalisation to novel nouns remained at chance (50-52%), a result confirmed by equivalence testing. A feature-swap diagnostic revealed that models rely on frame-to-feature template matching rather than structured noun-to-domain-to-feature abstraction. Conclusions: These results reveal a clear limitation of autoregressive distributional sequence learning under developmental-scale training conditions.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether small transformer language models can learn an abstract ‘shape-bias-like’ overhypothesis from text-like input and finds they cannot: they memorize exemplars but fail to generalize the second-order rule that shape is the stable category-defining feature.
AI.motivation: In human cognitive development, children learn not only specific category-feature associations (e.g., a given object’s shape) but also higher-order regularities such as that shape in general is a reliable cue to object category membership. This second-order learning (overhypothesis formation) is central to rapid, systematic generalization. The authors want to know whether standard autoregressive transformer language models, trained under developmentally plausible conditions, can form analogous overhypotheses from their input statistics, or whether they are restricted to surface-level pattern matching. This addresses broader questions about whether current distributional sequence learning mechanisms are sufficient models of human concept learning and inductive bias acquisition.
AI.method: The authors constructed synthetic training corpora in which object shape is systematically the stable feature dimension that defines categories, while other dimensions vary. They trained a range of autoregressive transformer language models (3.4M–25.6M parameters) on these corpora, using eight different training conditions designed to rule out confounds and alternative explanations (e.g., simple co-occurrence patterns or trivial cues). To probe what was learned, they used a pre-registered testing protocol: (1) a 1,040-item ‘wug’ test battery assessing first-order learning (exemplar retrieval) and second-order learning (generalization of the shape-based overhypothesis to novel nouns), and (2) a feature-swap diagnostic that disrupts the usual relationships between linguistic frames and features to see whether models rely on abstract noun-to-domain-to-feature mappings or simpler frame-to-feature templates.
AI.result: Across 120 pre-registered training runs covering all model sizes and conditions, the transformers achieved perfect performance on first-order exemplar retrieval tasks (100%), indicating that they fully encoded the specific training examples. However, on the critical second-order generalization tests involving novel nouns, their performance stayed at chance level (50–52%). Equivalence tests statistically confirmed that performance did not exceed chance in a meaningful way. The feature-swap diagnostic further showed that the models’ behavior was best explained by frame-to-feature template matching: they learned associations between particular linguistic contexts (frames) and features, rather than an abstract representation linking nouns to domains (e.g., object category) and then to a stable feature such as shape.
AI.conclusion: Autoregressive transformer language models, when trained under developmentally realistic data regimes on synthetic corpora engineered to support a shape-based overhypothesis, do not develop human-like second-order generalizations about which features define categories. They can memorize and retrieve exemplars flawlessly but fail to infer the higher-order regularity that shape is the stable, category-defining dimension and to extend it to new items. This suggests a fundamental limitation of pure distributional sequence learning—at least in the tested regime—for capturing the kind of structured, overhypothesis-driven concept learning that characterizes human cognitive development, and points to the need for additional inductive biases or learning mechanisms to model such abilities.

title: Attribution Bias in Large Language Models
summary: As Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used to support search and information retrieval, it is critical that they accurately attribute content to its original authors. In this work, we introduce AttriBench, the first fame- and demographically-balanced quote attribution benchmark dataset. Through explicitly balancing author fame and demographics, AttriBench enables controlled investigation of demographic bias in quote attribution. Using this dataset, we evaluate 11 widely used LLMs across different prompt settings and find that quote attribution remains a challenging task even for frontier models. We observe large and systematic disparities in attribution accuracy between race, gender, and intersectional groups. We further introduce and investigate suppression, a distinct failure mode in which models omit attribution entirely, even when the model has access to authorship information. We find that suppression is widespread and unevenly distributed across demographic groups, revealing systematic biases not captured by standard accuracy metrics. Our results position quote attribution as a benchmark for representational fairness in LLMs.

AI.tldr: Introduces AttriBench, a balanced benchmark to study demographic bias and failure modes in LLM quote attribution.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used for search and information retrieval, where correctly attributing content to original authors is important for credit, trust, and accountability. Existing evaluations do not adequately capture whether LLMs fairly attribute quotes across demographic groups or how fame influences attribution quality, leaving potential demographic and representational biases underexplored.
AI.method: The authors construct AttriBench, a quote attribution dataset that is explicitly balanced across author fame levels and demographic attributes (e.g., race, gender, and their intersections). They then systematically evaluate 11 widely used LLMs under multiple prompting conditions on this dataset. They measure attribution accuracy and also define and analyze a new failure mode, called suppression, where the model omits attribution despite having access to authorship information. They compare performance and suppression rates across demographic groups to quantify disparities and biases.
AI.result: Across the 11 LLMs, quote attribution is challenging even for state-of-the-art models, producing substantial error rates. The evaluation reveals large, systematic disparities in attribution accuracy across racial, gender, and intersectional groups. Additionally, the newly defined suppression failure mode is found to be common: models frequently refrain from naming an author even when they have or could access that information. Suppression is not randomly distributed but is higher for certain demographic groups, indicating systematic, group-dependent behavior that standard accuracy metrics miss.
AI.conclusion: AttriBench enables controlled and rigorous study of demographic bias in LLM quote attribution by balancing fame and demographic factors. The study shows that current LLMs exhibit both accuracy disparities and a distinct suppression failure mode that disproportionately affects certain demographic groups. These findings highlight that quote attribution is a useful and sensitive benchmark for assessing representational fairness in LLMs and suggest that improving attribution behavior and mitigating suppression-related biases should be priorities for future model development and evaluation.

title: From Governance Norms to Enforceable Controls: A Layered Translation Method for Runtime Guardrails in Agentic AI
summary: Agentic AI systems plan, use tools, maintain state, and produce multi-step trajectories with external effects. Those properties create a governance problem that differs materially from single-turn generative AI: important risks emerge dur- ing execution, not only at model development or deployment time. Governance standards such as ISO/IEC 42001, ISO/IEC 23894, ISO/IEC 42005, ISO/IEC 5338, ISO/IEC 38507, and the NIST AI Risk Management Framework are therefore highly relevant to agentic AI, but they do not by themselves yield implementable runtime guardrails. This paper proposes a layered translation method that connects standards-derived governance objectives to four control layers: governance objectives, design- time constraints, runtime mediation, and assurance feedback. It distinguishes governance objectives, technical controls, runtime guardrails, and assurance evidence; introduces a control tuple and runtime-enforceability rubric for layer assignment; and demonstrates the method in a procurement-agent case study. The central claim is modest: standards should guide control placement across architecture, runtime policy, human escalation, and audit, while runtime guardrails are reserved for controls that are observable, determinate, and time-sensitive enough to justify execution-time intervention.

AI.tldr: The paper links high-level AI governance standards to concrete, implementable runtime guardrails for agentic AI systems through a layered control framework.
AI.motivation: Existing AI governance standards focus on development and deployment phases and are not directly implementable as runtime safeguards, especially for agentic AI whose main risks arise during autonomous execution. There is a need for a systematic way to translate standards into operational controls that work at runtime.
AI.method: The authors propose a layered translation method that maps standards-derived governance objectives onto four control layers: governance objectives, design-time constraints, runtime mediation (guardrails), and assurance feedback. They define and distinguish governance objectives, technical controls, runtime guardrails, and assurance evidence, introduce a control tuple and a rubric for deciding which controls are enforceable at runtime, and illustrate the approach using a procurement-agent case study.
AI.result: The paper produces a structured framework and rubric that allocates controls across architecture, runtime policies, human-in-the-loop escalation, and audit mechanisms, clarifying which types of controls should be implemented as runtime guardrails versus at other layers. The case study demonstrates how the framework can operationalize high-level standards for an agentic procurement system.
AI.conclusion: Standards like ISO/IEC and NIST AI RMF are crucial but insufficient on their own for governing agentic AI. They should be used to guide the placement of different types of controls across multiple layers, with runtime guardrails reserved only for controls that are observable, determinate, and time-sensitive enough to warrant intervention during execution.

title: Beneath the Surface: Investigating LLMs' Capabilities for Communicating with Subtext
summary: Human communication is fundamentally creative, and often makes use of subtext -- implied meaning that goes beyond the literal content of the text. Here, we systematically study whether language models can use subtext in communicative settings, and introduce four new evaluation suites to assess these capabilities. Our evaluation settings range from writing & interpreting allegories to playing multi-agent and multi-modal games inspired by the rules of board games like Dixit. We find that frontier models generally exhibit a strong bias towards overly literal, explicit communication, and thereby fail to account for nuanced constraints -- even the best performing models generate literal clues 60% of times in one of our environments -- Visual Allusions. However, we find that some models can sometimes make use of common ground with another party to help them communicate with subtext, achieving 30%-50% reduction in overly literal clues; but they struggle at inferring presence of a common ground when not explicitly stated. For allegory understanding, we find paratextual and persona conditions to significantly shift the interpretation of subtext. Overall, our work provides quantifiable measures for an inherently complex and subjective phenomenon like subtext and reveals many weaknesses and idiosyncrasies of current LLMs. We hope this research to inspire future work towards socially grounded creative communication and reasoning.
