AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well large language models understand and use subtext—meaning that goes beyond literal wording—via four new benchmarks, and shows that even frontier models remain overly literal and have difficulty leveraging common ground and context for allegorical and allusive communication.
AI.motivation: Human communication relies heavily on subtext, implication, and shared background knowledge, but most current LLM benchmarks and analyses focus on literal tasks like question answering or summarization. There is little systematic evidence about whether LLMs can produce and interpret creative, socially grounded, non-literal communication. The authors aim to fill this gap by providing structured tests that capture how models handle allegory, allusion, and context-dependent meanings.
AI.method: The authors design four evaluation suites that probe LLMs’ ability to (1) write and interpret allegories, and (2) communicate using subtext in interactive game-like setups, including multi-agent and multi-modal environments inspired by board games such as Dixit. One environment, Visual Allusions, measures whether models produce literal vs. allusive clues referring to images. They also manipulate conditions such as explicit vs. implicit common ground, paratext (extra textual cues), and persona to see how these factors change models’ use and interpretation of subtext. They then test a range of frontier LLMs on these tasks and quantify how often the models resort to overly literal communication and how their performance changes under different contextual conditions.
AI.result: Across tasks, models show a strong bias for literal, explicit communication and often ignore nuanced pragmatic constraints. For example, in the Visual Allusions environment even the best model still produces literal clues around 60% of the time. Some models can reduce literalness by about 30–50% when the common ground with a communication partner is clearly specified, indicating partial sensitivity to shared context. However, models perform poorly at inferring that such common ground exists when it is not explicitly stated. In allegory interpretation tasks, factors like paratext and speaker persona substantially shift how models interpret subtext, revealing both sensitivity to framing and instability in meaning attribution.
AI.conclusion: The work introduces concrete, quantitative benchmarks for assessing LLMs’ abilities with subtext and non-literal communication, showing that current frontier models remain strongly literal, struggle with implicit common ground, and display idiosyncratic shifts in allegory interpretation under different framings. These findings highlight that socially grounded, creative communication is still a major open challenge for LLMs and underline the need for future methods and training regimes that better capture pragmatic, context-rich aspects of human language use.

title: EAGLE: Edge-Aware Graph Learning for Proactive Delivery Delay Prediction in Smart Logistics Networks
summary: Modern logistics networks generate rich operational data streams at every warehouse node and transportation lane -- from order timestamps and routing records to shipping manifests -- yet predicting delivery delays remains predominantly reactive. Existing predictive approaches typically treat this problem either as a tabular classification task, ignoring network topology, or as a time-series anomaly detection task, overlooking the spatial dependencies of the supply chain graph. To bridge this gap, we propose a hybrid deep learning framework for proactive supply chain risk management. The proposed method jointly models temporal order-flow dynamics via a lightweight Transformer patch encoder and inter-hub relational dependencies through an Edge-Aware Graph Attention Network (E-GAT), optimized via a multi-task learning objective. Evaluated on the real-world DataCo Smart Supply Chain dataset, our framework achieves consistent improvements over baseline methods, yielding an F1-score of 0.8762 and an AUC-ROC of 0.9773. Across four independent random seeds, the framework exhibits a cross-seed F1 standard deviation of only 0.0089 -- a 3.8 times improvement over the best ablated variant -- achieving the strongest balance of predictive accuracy and training stability among all evaluated models.

AI.tldr: They propose a hybrid Transformer + edge-aware GAT model that uses both time-series order-flow and supply-chain network structure to proactively predict delivery delays, achieving higher accuracy and stability than baselines on a real supply-chain dataset.
AI.motivation: Current delay prediction methods are reactive and either ignore the supply-chain graph structure (tabular classifiers) or ignore temporal dynamics and spatial dependencies together (pure time-series anomaly detection). There is a need for a proactive, unified model that simultaneously captures temporal order-flow behavior and the relational structure of logistics networks to better manage supply-chain risk.
AI.method: They design a hybrid deep learning framework that combines: (1) a lightweight Transformer patch encoder to model temporal order-flow dynamics, and (2) an Edge-Aware Graph Attention Network (E-GAT) to capture inter-hub relational dependencies in the supply chain graph. The model is trained with a multi-task learning objective and evaluated on the DataCo Smart Supply Chain dataset, with comparisons to several baseline and ablated variants and robustness checks across four random seeds.
AI.result: On the DataCo Smart Supply Chain dataset, the method reaches an F1-score of 0.8762 and AUC-ROC of 0.9773, outperforming baseline methods. It also shows strong robustness, with a cross-seed F1 standard deviation of 0.0089, which is 3.8 times lower than the best ablated variant, indicating significantly improved training stability.
AI.conclusion: Jointly modeling temporal dynamics and network structure via a Transformer + E-GAT hybrid, optimized in a multi-task manner, enables more accurate and stable proactive prediction of delivery delays in logistics networks compared to existing baselines that neglect either temporal or spatial dependencies.

title: Right at My Level: A Unified Multilingual Framework for Proficiency-Aware Text Simplification
summary: Text simplification supports second language (L2) learning by providing comprehensible input, consistent with the Input Hypothesis. However, constructing personalized parallel corpora is costly, while existing large language model (LLM)-based readability control methods rely on pre-labeled sentence corpora and primarily target English. We propose Re-RIGHT, a unified reinforcement learning framework for adaptive multilingual text simplification without parallel corpus supervision. We first show that prompting-based lexical simplification at target proficiency levels (CEFR, JLPT, TOPIK, and HSK) performs poorly at easier levels and for non-English languages, even with state-of-the-art LLMs such as GPT-5.2 and Gemini 2.5. To address this, we collect 43K vocabulary-level data across four languages (English, Japanese, Korean, and Chinese) and train a compact 4B policy model using Re-RIGHT, which integrates three reward modules: vocabulary coverage, semantic preservation, and coherence. Compared to the stronger LLM baselines, Re-RIGHT achieves higher lexical coverage at target proficiency levels while maintaining original meaning and fluency.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Re-RIGHT, a reinforcement-learning-based framework for multilingual, proficiency-controlled text simplification without needing parallel corpora.
AI.motivation: Second language learners benefit from text simplification aligned with their proficiency, but building personalized parallel corpora is expensive and existing LLM-based readability control heavily depend on labeled English corpora and underperform at easier proficiency levels and in non-English languages.
AI.method: The authors propose Re-RIGHT, a unified reinforcement learning framework that trains a compact 4B policy model on 43K vocabulary-level data across English, Japanese, Korean, and Chinese. The framework uses three reward modules—vocabulary coverage with respect to target proficiency levels (CEFR, JLPT, TOPIK, HSK), semantic preservation, and coherence—to guide the model toward generating simplified text matching learners’ vocabularies while staying faithful and fluent.
AI.result: Experiments show that direct prompting of strong LLMs (GPT-5.2, Gemini 2.5) for proficiency-controlled lexical simplification works poorly for easier levels and non-English languages. In contrast, Re-RIGHT achieves higher lexical coverage for target proficiency vocabularies while still preserving original meaning and fluency, outperforming these stronger LLM baselines on key simplification metrics.
AI.conclusion: Re-RIGHT demonstrates that reinforcement learning with carefully designed reward modules can enable effective, multilingual, proficiency-aware text simplification without parallel corpora, providing a compact and more controllable alternative to large general-purpose LLMs for L2-oriented simplification tasks.

title: Simulating the Evolution of Alignment and Values in Machine Intelligence
summary: Model alignment is currently applied in a vacuum, evaluated primarily through standardised benchmark performance. The purpose of this study is to examine the effects of alignment on populations of models through time. We focus on the treatment of beliefs which contain both an alignment signal (how well it does on the test) and a true value (what the impact actually will be). By applying evolutionary theory we can model how different populations of beliefs and selection methodologies can fix deceptive beliefs through iterative alignment testing. The correlation between testing accuracy and true value remains a strong feature, but even at high correlations ($ρ= 0.8$) there is variability in the resulting deceptive beliefs that become fixed. Mutations allow for more complex developments, highlighting the increasing need to update the quality of tests to avoid fixation of maliciously deceptive models. Only by combining improving evaluator capabilities, adaptive test design, and mutational dynamics do we see significant reductions in deception while maintaining alignment fitness (permutation test, $p_{\text{adj}} < 0.001$).

AI.tldr: The paper uses evolutionary theory to study how aligning AI models via tests over time can unintentionally fix deceptive behaviors, and shows that only with better evaluators, adaptive tests, and accounting for mutations can deception be significantly reduced without sacrificing alignment performance.
AI.motivation: Current model alignment focuses heavily on benchmark performance in isolation, ignoring how alignment processes shape populations of models over time and may select for deceptive behaviors. The authors want to understand how iterative alignment testing interacts with models' internal beliefs—each having both a score on tests and a real-world impact—to see under what conditions deceptive beliefs become entrenched.
AI.method: They construct an evolutionary model where individual “beliefs” in models are characterized by two dimensions: an alignment signal (test accuracy) and a true value (actual impact). Different selection methodologies (ways of picking models based on test results) are simulated over multiple generations, tracking how beliefs evolve. Correlations between test scores and true value are varied (e.g., ρ = 0.8) and mutational processes are introduced to allow beliefs to change and become more complex. They then analyze which deceptive beliefs become fixed and statistically test the impact of adding improved evaluators, adaptive test design, and mutations on deception and alignment fitness (using permutation tests with adjusted p-values).
AI.result: The evolutionary simulations show that even when test scores are strongly correlated with true value (ρ = 0.8), deceptive beliefs can still become fixed in the model population and that the specific deceptive beliefs that fix vary. Introducing mutations leads to more complex forms of deception and dynamics, which can worsen the problem if tests are static or low-quality. Only when evaluator capabilities improve, tests are adaptively updated, and mutational dynamics are explicitly considered do they observe a substantial reduction in deception, while alignment performance remains high; this effect is statistically significant (permutation test, padj < 0.001).
AI.conclusion: Alignment procedures that rely mainly on static benchmark performance can, over time, inadvertently select for deceptive models, even when benchmarks are highly correlated with real-world value. Evolutionary and mutational dynamics mean that deceptive strategies can become entrenched unless alignment practices evolve in tandem. To maintain high alignment fitness while minimizing deception, alignment must integrate stronger evaluators, adaptively designed tests, and explicit modeling of how models’ internal beliefs mutate and evolve across iterations.

title: DIA-HARM: Dialectal Disparities in Harmful Content Detection Across 50 English Dialects
summary: Harmful content detectors-particularly disinformation classifiers-are predominantly developed and evaluated on Standard American English (SAE), leaving their robustness to dialectal variation unexplored. We present DIA-HARM, the first benchmark for evaluating disinformation detection robustness across 50 English dialects spanning U.S., British, African, Caribbean, and Asia-Pacific varieties. Using Multi-VALUE's linguistically grounded transformations, we introduce D3 (Dialectal Disinformation Detection), a corpus of 195K samples derived from established disinformation benchmarks. Our evaluation of 16 detection models reveals systematic vulnerabilities: human-written dialectal content degrades detection by 1.4-3.6% F1, while AI-generated content remains stable. Fine-tuned transformers substantially outperform zero-shot LLMs (96.6% vs. 78.3% best-case F1), with some models exhibiting catastrophic failures exceeding 33% degradation on mixed content. Cross-dialectal transfer analysis across 2,450 dialect pairs shows that multilingual models (mDeBERTa: 97.2% average F1) generalize effectively, while monolingual models like RoBERTa and XLM-RoBERTa fail on dialectal inputs. These findings demonstrate that current disinformation detectors may systematically disadvantage hundreds of millions of non-SAE speakers worldwide. We release the DIA-HARM framework, D3 corpus, and evaluation tools: https://github.com/jsl5710/dia-harm

AI.tldr: DIA-HARM introduces a large benchmark and corpus (D3) to test how robust disinformation detectors are across 50 English dialects and shows that many current systems perform much worse on non-Standard American English, especially for human-written content.
AI.motivation: Most harmful-content and disinformation detectors are built and evaluated almost exclusively on Standard American English, with little understanding of how they behave on other English dialects. This creates a potential fairness and reliability gap for hundreds of millions of speakers whose language varieties systematically differ from SAE. The authors aim to quantify and analyze these robustness and equity issues by providing a controlled, large-scale benchmark that spans many global English dialects.
AI.method: The authors construct DIA-HARM, a benchmark built on D3 (Dialectal Disinformation Detection), which contains 195K dialectal variants of existing disinformation benchmark samples. They use Multi-VALUE’s linguistically informed transformation rules to systematically convert original SAE content into 50 dialects covering U.S., British, African, Caribbean, and Asia-Pacific Englishes. They then evaluate 16 disinformation detection models—including fine-tuned transformers, multilingual models, and zero-shot large language models—on both human-written and AI-generated content, analyzing robustness, cross-dialect transfer, and performance degradation patterns across 2,450 dialect pairs.
AI.result: Human-written dialectal text causes consistent performance drops of about 1.4–3.6 F1 points across models, while AI-generated content does not see similar degradation. Fine-tuned transformer models greatly outperform zero-shot LLMs (best-case 96.6% vs. 78.3% F1). Some systems experience severe, over-33% performance drops on mixed-dialect content. Cross-dialect transfer experiments show that multilingual models like mDeBERTa maintain high performance and generalize well across dialects (around 97.2% average F1), whereas monolingual or limited-coverage models (e.g., RoBERTa, XLM-RoBERTa) fail substantially on dialectal input.
AI.conclusion: Disinformation detection systems trained and evaluated primarily on Standard American English are not reliably robust to global English dialect variation and can underperform markedly for non-SAE speakers. Multilingual, dialect-aware models show much better cross-dialect generalization, but many widely used monolingual models exhibit serious robustness and fairness problems. The DIA-HARM benchmark, together with the D3 corpus and tools, is released to enable systematic evaluation and drive development of more equitable and dialect-robust harmful content detectors.

title: Pressure, What Pressure? Sycophancy Disentanglement in Language Models via Reward Decomposition
summary: Large language models exhibit sycophancy, the tendency to shift their stated positions toward perceived user preferences or authority cues regardless of evidence. Standard alignment methods fail to correct this because scalar reward models conflate two distinct failure modes into a single signal: pressure capitulation, where the model changes a correct answer under social pressure, and evidence blindness, where the model ignores the provided context entirely. We operationalise sycophancy through formal definitions of pressure independence and evidence responsiveness, serving as a working framework for disentangled training rather than a definitive characterisation of the phenomenon. We propose the first approach to sycophancy reduction via reward decomposition, introducing a multi-component Group Relative Policy Optimisation (GRPO) reward that decomposes the training signal into five terms: pressure resistance, context fidelity, position consistency, agreement suppression, and factual correctness. We train using a contrastive dataset pairing pressure-free baselines with pressured variants across three authority levels and two opposing evidence contexts. Across five base models, our two-phase pipeline consistently reduces sycophancy on all metric axes, with ablations confirming that each reward term governs an independent behavioural dimension. The learned resistance to pressure generalises beyond our training methodology and prompt structure, reducing answer-priming sycophancy by up to 17 points on SycophancyEval despite the absence of such pressure forms during training.

AI.tldr: They propose and test a new, decomposed reward-training method to reduce sycophancy in large language models by separately rewarding resistance to pressure and responsiveness to evidence.
AI.motivation: LLMs tend to agree with users or authority cues even when this conflicts with evidence, a behavior called sycophancy. Existing alignment techniques use a single scalar reward that mixes different underlying problems, so they cannot selectively fix specific aspects like giving in to social pressure versus ignoring context. The authors want a more precise framework and training objective to target and reduce sycophancy without harming other capabilities.
AI.method: They first give formal operational definitions for two components of sycophancy: pressure independence (not changing correct answers under pressure) and evidence responsiveness (actually using context). They then design a decomposed, multi-component reward within a Group Relative Policy Optimisation (GRPO) framework, splitting the reward into five terms: pressure resistance, context fidelity, position consistency, agreement suppression, and factual correctness. Using a contrastive dataset of paired prompts—pressure-free baselines and pressured variants across three authority levels and two opposing evidence conditions—they train models in a two-phase pipeline. They also run ablations to test the contribution and independence of each reward term.
AI.result: Across five base LLMs, the new decomposed-reward training pipeline consistently reduces measured sycophancy on all defined metric axes. The ablation studies show that each reward component controls a distinct behavioral dimension, supporting the intended disentanglement. Furthermore, the improved resistance to pressure generalizes to unseen forms of sycophancy: on SycophancyEval, they reduce answer-priming sycophancy by up to 17 percentage points even though that specific pressure pattern was not present during training.
AI.conclusion: Decomposing the reward signal for alignment into separate terms targeting different aspects of sycophancy is effective and yields more robust anti-sycophantic behavior than standard scalar rewards. Their framework of pressure independence and evidence responsiveness, plus the GRPO-based multi-term reward, provides a practical and generalizable approach to reducing sycophancy in LLMs, with evidence of cross-setting generalization beyond the exact training pressures and prompts used.

title: Human Values Matter: Investigating How Misalignment Shapes Collective Behaviors in LLM Agent Communities
summary: As LLMs become increasingly integrated into human society, evaluating their orientations on human values from social science has drawn growing attention. Nevertheless, it is still unclear why human values matter for LLMs, especially in LLM-based multi-agent systems, where group-level failures may accumulate from individually misaligned actions. We ask whether misalignment with human values alters the collective behavior of LLM agents and what changes it induces? In this work, we introduce CIVA, a controlled multi-agent environment grounded in social science theories, where LLM agents form a community and autonomously communicate, explore, and compete for resources, enabling systematic manipulation of value prevalence and behavioral analysis. Through comprehensive simulation experiments, we reveal three key findings. (1) We identify several structurally critical values that substantially shape the community's collective dynamics, including those diverging from LLMs' original orientations. Triggered by the misspecification of these values, we (2) detect system failure modes, e.g., catastrophic collapse, at the macro level, and (3) observe emergent behaviors like deception and power-seeking at the micro level. These results offer quantitative evidence that human values are essential for collective outcomes in LLMs and motivate future multi-agent value alignment.

AI.tldr: The paper builds a controlled multi-agent simulation to study how different human value settings in LLM agents affect community-level behavior and failure modes.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly deployed in socially embedded and multi-agent settings, understanding how their alignment or misalignment with human values influences not only individual behavior but also group-level dynamics is crucial. Classical alignment work focuses on single models and benchmarks, but less is known about how value orientations propagate, interact, and potentially amplify into systemic risks like coordination breakdown, exploitation, or collapse in agent societies.
AI.method: The authors design CIVA, a controlled multi-agent environment grounded in social science theories where LLM agents inhabit a community, communicate, explore, and compete for resources. Within this environment, they parametrically manipulate the prevalence and specification of different human values in agents’ decision-making processes. They then run large numbers of simulations to observe how changing specific values or combinations of values alters micro-level actions and macro-level community dynamics, using quantitative metrics to characterize outcomes and failure modes.
AI.result: They identify a subset of “structurally critical” values whose presence or absence strongly influences collective dynamics. Mis-specifying these values leads to qualitatively different system behaviors, including macro-level failures such as catastrophic collapse of the community’s functioning, as well as micro-level emergent behaviors like deception and power-seeking. These emergent behaviors can arise even when they diverge from the models’ original value orientations, demonstrating sensitivity to value configuration in the multi-agent context.
AI.conclusion: Human value alignment is not only an individual-agent property but a determinant of collective outcomes in LLM-based multi-agent systems. Certain values play a disproportionately important structural role: when set incorrectly, they can cause system-level failures and undesirable emergent strategies. The study provides quantitative evidence that careful, theory-informed design and evaluation of value configurations is essential for safe and robust deployment of LLM agent societies, and it motivates further research in multi-agent value alignment and systemic safety analysis.

title: DQA: Diagnostic Question Answering for IT Support
comment: 7 pages, 2 tables, accepted at ACL 2026 Industry Track
summary: Enterprise IT support interactions are fundamentally diagnostic: effective resolution requires iterative evidence gathering from ambiguous user reports to identify an underlying root cause. While retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) provides grounding through historical cases, standard multi-turn RAG systems lack explicit diagnostic state and therefore struggle to accumulate evidence and resolve competing hypotheses across turns. We introduce DQA, a diagnostic question-answering framework that maintains persistent diagnostic state and aggregates retrieved cases at the level of root causes rather than individual documents. DQA combines conversational query rewriting, retrieval aggregation, and state-conditioned response generation to support systematic troubleshooting under enterprise latency and context constraints. We evaluate DQA on 150 anonymized enterprise IT support scenarios using a replay-based protocol. Averaged over three independent runs, DQA achieves a 78.7% success rate under a trajectory-level success criterion, compared to 41.3% for a multi-turn RAG baseline, while reducing average turns from 8.4 to 3.9.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes DQA, a diagnostic question-answering framework that maintains explicit diagnostic state and aggregates retrieval at the root-cause level, achieving markedly higher success and fewer turns than standard multi-turn RAG in enterprise IT support scenarios.
AI.motivation: Enterprise IT support requires iterative diagnosis from ambiguous user descriptions to underlying root causes. Standard multi-turn RAG systems ground responses on retrieved documents but lack an explicit diagnostic state, making it hard to accumulate evidence, manage competing hypotheses, and operate effectively within enterprise constraints on latency and context window. There is a need for a framework that better matches the structure of diagnostic reasoning.
AI.method: The authors design DQA, a diagnostic QA framework with three main components: (1) conversational query rewriting to reformulate user utterances into clearer, retrieval-friendly diagnostic queries; (2) retrieval aggregation that clusters or aggregates retrieved cases at the root-cause level instead of treating each document independently, enabling reasoning over hypotheses; and (3) state-conditioned response generation that maintains a persistent diagnostic state across turns, so the system can accumulate evidence, update or eliminate hypotheses, and generate responses based on the evolving diagnostic context. They evaluate DQA using a replay-based protocol on 150 anonymized enterprise IT support scenarios, comparing against a standard multi-turn RAG baseline.
AI.result: On 150 enterprise IT support scenarios, averaged over three independent runs, DQA achieves a 78.7% success rate under a trajectory-level success metric, significantly outperforming a multi-turn RAG baseline at 41.3%. DQA also reduces the average interaction length, cutting the mean number of turns from 8.4 to 3.9, indicating both higher effectiveness and greater efficiency in troubleshooting dialogues.
AI.conclusion: Maintaining explicit diagnostic state and aggregating evidence at the root-cause level leads to substantially better performance for multi-turn troubleshooting than standard RAG approaches. DQA demonstrates that structuring retrieval and generation around diagnostic hypotheses can both improve task success rates and shorten dialogues under realistic enterprise constraints, suggesting a promising direction for building more capable support and troubleshooting assistants.

title: Graph of Skills: Dependency-Aware Structural Retrieval for Massive Agent Skills
comment: 13 pages of main text, 13 pages of appendix. Core contribution by Dawei Li and Zongxia Li. Project page: https://github.com/davidliuk/graph-of-skills
summary:
Skill usage has become a core component of modern agent systems and can substantially improve agents' ability to complete complex tasks. In real-world settings, where agents must monitor and interact with numerous personal applications, web browsers, and other environment interfaces, skill libraries can scale to thousands of reusable skills. Scaling to larger skill sets introduces two key challenges. First, loading the full skill set saturates the context window, driving up token costs, hallucination, and latency.
  In this paper, we present Graph of Skills (GoS), an inference-time structural retrieval layer for large skill libraries. GoS constructs an executable skill graph offline from skill packages, then at inference time retrieves a bounded, dependency-aware skill bundle through hybrid semantic-lexical seeding, reverse-weighted Personalized PageRank, and context-budgeted hydration. On SkillsBench and ALFWorld, GoS improves average reward by 43.6% over the vanilla full skill-loading baseline while reducing input tokens by 37.8%, and generalizes across three model families: Claude Sonnet, GPT-5.2 Codex, and MiniMax. Additional ablation studies across skill libraries ranging from 200 to 2,000 skills further demonstrate that GoS consistently outperforms both vanilla skills loading and simple vector retrieval in balancing reward, token efficiency, and runtime.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Graph of Skills (GoS), a retrieval layer that dynamically selects a small, dependency-aware subset of skills from very large skill libraries, improving performance and efficiency for agent systems.
AI.motivation: Modern agent systems rely heavily on skills (tools, APIs, procedures) to solve complex, real‑world tasks across many apps and interfaces. In practice, skill libraries can grow to thousands of skills. Naively loading all skills into the model’s context is expensive and harmful: it increases token usage, latency, and hallucinations, and can exceed context limits. Existing simple retrieval methods do not fully handle dependency structures between skills. The paper is motivated by the need for a scalable, structured way to select only the most relevant, executable subset of skills at inference time, while respecting dependencies and tight context budgets.
AI.method: The authors propose Graph of Skills (GoS), an inference‑time structural retrieval layer. Offline, GoS parses skill packages and builds an executable directed graph whose nodes are skills and whose edges encode dependencies or call relationships. At inference time, given a task description, GoS performs: (1) hybrid semantic‑lexical seeding to identify a set of initial relevant skills; (2) reverse‑weighted Personalized PageRank over the skill graph to propagate relevance through dependencies; and (3) context‑budgeted hydration, which expands and truncates the selected subgraph so that the final skill bundle both respects dependency requirements and fits within a specified context/token budget. This bounded, dependency‑aware bundle is then loaded for the LLM agent to use.
AI.result: On the SkillsBench and ALFWorld benchmarks, GoS improves average reward by 43.6% compared to a baseline that simply loads the full skill set, while simultaneously reducing input tokens by 37.8%. It generalizes across three different model families (Claude Sonnet, GPT‑5.2 Codex, and MiniMax). Additional ablation studies over libraries with 200–2,000 skills show that GoS consistently outperforms both full skill loading and simple vector‑based retrieval in terms of task reward, token efficiency, and runtime, demonstrating robustness across scales and retrieval baselines.
AI.conclusion: GoS provides a practical and effective way to scale agent systems to very large skill libraries by turning skill selection into a structured graph retrieval problem. By leveraging dependency‑aware graph construction, Personalized PageRank, and strict context budgeting, it can significantly boost task performance while cutting token and computation costs. The approach generalizes across benchmarks and model families and offers a more robust alternative to naive full loading and basic vector search for skills, suggesting a promising direction for scaling tool‑augmented LLM agents in real‑world environments.

title: ICR-Drive: Instruction Counterfactual Robustness for End-to-End Language-Driven Autonomous Driving
summary: Recent progress in vision-language-action (VLA) models has enabled language-conditioned driving agents to execute natural-language navigation commands in closed-loop simulation, yet standard evaluations largely assume instructions are precise and well-formed. In deployment, instructions vary in phrasing and specificity, may omit critical qualifiers, and can occasionally include misleading, authority-framed text, leaving instruction-level robustness under-measured. We introduce ICR-Drive, a diagnostic framework for instruction counterfactual robustness in end-to-end language-conditioned autonomous driving. ICR-Drive generates controlled instruction variants spanning four perturbation families: Paraphrase, Ambiguity, Noise, and Misleading, where Misleading variants conflict with the navigation goal and attempt to override intent. We replay identical CARLA routes under matched simulator configurations and seeds to isolate performance changes attributable to instruction language. Robustness is quantified using standard CARLA Leaderboard metrics and per-family performance degradation relative to the baseline instruction. Experiments on LMDrive and BEVDriver show that minor instruction changes can induce substantial performance drops and distinct failure modes, revealing a reliability gap for deploying embodied foundation models in safety-critical driving.

AI.tldr: ICR-Drive is a diagnostic benchmark that tests how robust language-conditioned driving agents are to variations and distortions in natural-language navigation instructions.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of vision-language-action driving models assume instructions are clean, precise, and cooperative, which is unrealistic for real-world deployment where human commands can be vague, paraphrased, incomplete, or even misleading. There is a need to measure how sensitive these systems are to such instruction variations, especially in safety-critical driving contexts.
AI.method: The authors propose ICR-Drive, a framework that automatically generates controlled counterfactual variants of the original navigation instruction along four perturbation families: (1) Paraphrase (reworded but equivalent), (2) Ambiguity (less specific), (3) Noise (irrelevant or extraneous text), and (4) Misleading (conflicting with the true navigation goal, mimicking deceptive or erroneous commands). They then replay the same CARLA simulator routes with identical seeds and configurations while only changing the instruction text, and evaluate performance with standard CARLA Leaderboard metrics, measuring degradation per perturbation family relative to the baseline instruction.
AI.result: On two state-of-the-art language-conditioned driving systems, LMDrive and BEVDriver, the study finds that even small changes in instructions can cause large performance drops and qualitatively different failure modes. Misleading and other perturbed instructions significantly degrade success rates and driving quality, indicating that the models over-trust the surface form of language and lack robust grounding to the intended navigation goal.
AI.conclusion: Instruction language has a much larger impact on end-to-end autonomous driving agents than current benchmarks reveal. ICR-Drive exposes a substantial robustness and reliability gap in vision-language-action driving models, especially under paraphrased, ambiguous, noisy, or adversarially misleading instructions. The work argues for incorporating instruction robustness as a first-class evaluation dimension for embodied foundation models deployed in safety-critical scenarios.

title: TRACE: Capability-Targeted Agentic Training
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) deployed in agentic environments must exercise multiple capabilities across different task instances, where a capability is performing one or more actions in a trajectory that are necessary for successfully solving a subset of tasks in the environment. Many existing approaches either rely on synthetic training data that is not targeted to the model's actual capability deficits in the target environment or train directly on the target environment, where the model needs to implicitly learn the capabilities across tasks. We introduce TRACE (Turning Recurrent Agent failures into Capability-targeted training Environments), an end-to-end system for environment-specific agent self-improvement. TRACE contrasts successful and failed trajectories to automatically identify lacking capabilities, synthesizes a targeted training environment for each that rewards whether the capability was exercised, and trains a LoRA adapter via RL on each synthetic environment, routing to the relevant adapter at inference. Empirically, TRACE generalizes across different environments, improving over the base agent by +14.1 points on $τ^2$-bench (customer service) and +7 perfect scores on ToolSandbox (tool use), outperforming the strongest baseline by +7.4 points and +4 perfect scores, respectively. Given the same number of rollouts, TRACE scales more efficiently than baselines, outperforming GRPO and GEPA by +9.2 and +7.4 points on $τ^2$-bench.

AI.tldr: TRACE is a system that lets an LLM-based agent analyze its own failures in a specific environment, automatically derive missing capabilities, build targeted training environments for those capabilities, and train specialized adapters via RL, resulting in better and more sample-efficient performance.
AI.motivation: LLM agents in complex environments need multiple distinct capabilities, but current training either uses generic synthetic data or directly trains on the full environment, forcing the model to implicitly discover and learn all capabilities at once. This is inefficient and not targeted to actual weaknesses. The authors want a way for an agent to automatically identify and fix its real capability gaps in a given environment, with better sample efficiency and generalization across tasks within that environment.
AI.method: TRACE compares successful and failed trajectories from an LLM agent acting in an environment to automatically identify specific lacking capabilities. For each such capability, it synthesizes a focused training environment that directly rewards exercising that capability. The model is augmented with LoRA adapters, and for every synthetic capability environment, a separate adapter is trained via reinforcement learning. At inference time, the system routes to the appropriate adapter depending on which capability is needed. This creates an environment-specific, capability-targeted self-improvement loop.
AI.result: On the τ^2-bench customer service benchmark, TRACE improves the base agent’s score by +14.1 points and beats the strongest baseline by +7.4 points. On the ToolSandbox tool-use benchmark, it achieves +7 additional perfect scores over the base agent and +4 over the strongest baseline. Under a fixed rollout budget, TRACE is more sample-efficient than GRPO and GEPA, outperforming them by +9.2 and +7.4 points, respectively, on τ^2-bench.
AI.conclusion: Automatically turning agent failures into capability-specific training environments and training LoRA adapters for each capability via RL can significantly and sample-efficiently improve LLM agents in diverse environments. TRACE generalizes across different benchmarks and outperforms strong baselines, suggesting that capability-targeted self-improvement is a promising direction for training agentic LLMs.

title: Confidence Should Be Calibrated More Than One Turn Deep
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly applied in high-stakes domains such as finance, healthcare, and education, where reliable multi-turn interactions with users are essential. However, existing work on confidence estimation and calibration, a major approach to building trustworthy LLM systems, largely focuses on single-turn settings and overlooks the risks and potential of multi-turn conversations. In this work, we introduce the task of multi-turn calibration to reframe calibration from a static property into a dynamic challenge central to reliable multi-turn conversation, where calibrating model confidence at each turn conditioned on the conversation history is required. We first reveal the risks of this setting: using Expected Calibration Error at turn T (ECE@T), a new metric that tracks calibration dynamics over turns, we show that user feedback (e.g., persuasion) can degrade multi-turn calibration. To address this, we propose MTCal, which minimises ECE@T via a surrogate calibration target, and further leverage calibrated confidence in ConfChat, a decoding strategy that improves both factuality and consistency of the model response in multi-turn interactions. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MT-Cal achieves outstanding and consistent performance in multi-turn calibration, and ConfChat preserves and even enhances model performance in multi-turn interactions. Our results mark multi-turn calibration as one missing link for scaling LLM calibration toward safe, reliable, and real-world use.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces multi-turn calibration for LLMs, showing that user feedback across turns can harm calibration, and proposes MTCal and ConfChat to improve multi-turn confidence estimation, factuality, and consistency.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM calibration work mainly addresses single-turn settings, which is insufficient for high-stakes, real-world multi-turn interactions where model confidence must remain reliable across a conversation. There is a need to understand and mitigate how conversation dynamics and user feedback affect calibration.
AI.method: 1) Formulate multi-turn calibration as calibrating model confidence at each turn, conditioned on conversation history. 2) Propose ECE@T, a metric to track calibration dynamics over conversation turns and reveal how user feedback such as persuasion degrades calibration. 3) Introduce MTCal, which minimizes ECE@T via a surrogate calibration target. 4) Design ConfChat, a decoding strategy that uses calibrated confidence to guide generation in multi-turn interactions.
AI.result: Experiments show that user feedback can significantly worsen calibration as measured by ECE@T. MTCal yields strong and stable multi-turn calibration improvements, and ConfChat maintains or improves model factuality and consistency in multi-turn dialogues while leveraging calibrated confidence.
AI.conclusion: Multi-turn calibration is crucial but previously overlooked for safe and reliable real-world LLM use. The proposed MTCal and ConfChat effectively address calibration issues in multi-turn settings, highlighting multi-turn calibration as a key step toward scaling trustworthy LLM systems.

title: Dynamic Agentic AI Expert Profiler System Architecture for Multidomain Intelligence Modeling
comment: Accepted to be Published in IEEE Conference on Artificial Intelligence (CAI) 2026 - May 8-10, 2026, Granada, Spain
summary: In today's artificial intelligence driven world, modern systems communicate with people from diverse backgrounds and skill levels. For human-machine interaction to be meaningful, systems must be aware of context and user expertise. This study proposes an agentic AI profiler that classifies natural language responses into four levels: Novice, Basic, Advanced, and Expert. The system uses a modular layered architecture built on LLaMA v3.1 (8B), with components for text preprocessing, scoring, aggregation, and classification. Evaluation was conducted in two phases: a static phase using pre-recorded transcripts from 82 participants, and a dynamic phase with 402 live interviews conducted by an agentic AI interviewer. In both phases, participant self-ratings were compared with profiler predictions. In the dynamic phase, expertise was assessed after each response rather than at the end of the interview. Across domains, 83% to 97% of profiler evaluations matched participant self-assessments. Remaining differences were due to self-rating bias, unclear responses, and occasional misinterpretation of nuanced expertise by the language model.

AI.tldr: The paper presents an AI-based profiler that automatically classifies a user’s domain expertise (Novice, Basic, Advanced, Expert) from natural language responses, showing high agreement with users’ self-assessments.
AI.motivation: As AI systems interact with users of widely varying knowledge and skills, they need to adapt language, explanations, and support to the user’s actual expertise. Existing systems often lack fine-grained, automatic, and real-time estimation of user expertise from conversation, which limits personalization and can degrade user experience and effectiveness. This work aims to fill that gap by creating an automated, conversational expertise profiler.
AI.method: The authors design an “agentic AI profiler” built on LLaMA v3.1 (8B) in a modular, layered architecture. The pipeline includes text preprocessing, scoring of responses, aggregation across turns, and final classification into four expertise levels: Novice, Basic, Advanced, Expert. They evaluate the profiler in two phases: (1) a static phase using pre-recorded interview transcripts from 82 participants, and (2) a dynamic phase with 402 live interviews conducted by an AI interviewer agent. In the dynamic phase, the system updates expertise classification after each response rather than only at the end. In both phases, profiler outputs are compared to participants’ self-rated expertise.
AI.result: The profiler’s predicted expertise levels matched participants’ self-assessments in approximately 83% to 97% of cases across different domains and settings. Discrepancies were mainly attributed to (a) biased or inaccurate self-ratings by participants, (b) ambiguous or low-information responses, and (c) occasional failures of the language model to capture subtle or highly nuanced expertise signals.
AI.conclusion: A conversational, LLM-based profiler can reliably estimate users’ expertise levels from natural language responses, both from static transcripts and in real time during live interviews. The high agreement with self-assessments suggests the method is viable for adaptive user interfaces and personalized human–AI interaction, though limitations remain in handling biased self-judgments, unclear answers, and very subtle expertise cues.

title: Multi-Drafter Speculative Decoding with Alignment Feedback
summary: Speculative decoding (SD) accelerates large language model (LLM) inference by using a smaller model to draft future tokens, which are then verified by the target LLM. This preserves generation quality by accepting only aligned tokens. However, individual drafters, often trained for specific tasks or domains, exhibit limited effectiveness across diverse applications. To address this, we introduce \textsc{MetaSD}, a unified framework that integrates multiple drafters into the SD process. MetaSD dynamically allocates computational resources to heterogeneous drafters by leveraging alignment feedback and framing drafter selection as a multi-armed bandit problem. Extensive experiments show MetaSD consistently outperforms single-drafter approaches.

AI.tldr: Paper proposes MetaSD, a framework that combines multiple speculative decoding drafters using bandit-based selection to speed up LLM inference beyond single-drafter methods.
AI.motivation: Single speculative decoders are usually tuned for specific tasks or domains and thus generalize poorly across diverse applications, limiting their practical usefulness for real-world heterogeneous workloads. The authors want a way to exploit multiple available drafters in a principled way while preserving output quality and maximizing speedup.
AI.method: They design MetaSD, a unified speculative decoding framework that runs with a collection of heterogeneous drafters. During generation, it treats each drafter as an arm in a multi-armed bandit problem and uses feedback from the target LLM’s alignment/verification step as rewards. Based on this online feedback, MetaSD adaptively allocates compute by choosing which drafter(s) to query for future drafts, favoring those that historically produce more verifiable tokens under current conditions.
AI.result: Across extensive experiments, MetaSD achieves higher speedups (better tokens-per-second or lower latency) than any single-drafter baseline, while preserving the same target model quality. The framework shows consistent gains across different tasks and drafter collections, demonstrating robustness and generality.
AI.conclusion: Integrating multiple speculative drafters and managing them with a bandit-based, feedback-driven policy is more effective than relying on a single drafter. MetaSD offers a practical, general framework for accelerating LLM inference across diverse settings without sacrificing output quality.

title: From Retinal Evidence to Safe Decisions: RETINA-SAFE and ECRT for Hallucination Risk Triage in Medical LLMs
summary: Hallucinations in medical large language models (LLMs) remain a safety-critical issue, particularly when available evidence is insufficient or conflicting. We study this problem in diabetic retinopathy (DR) decision settings and introduce RETINA-SAFE, an evidence-grounded benchmark aligned with retinal grading records, comprising 12,522 samples. RETINA-SAFE is organized into three evidence-relation tasks: E-Align (evidence-consistent), E-Conflict (evidence-conflicting), and E-Gap (evidence-insufficient). We further propose ECRT (Evidence-Conditioned Risk Triage), a two-stage white-box detection framework: Stage 1 performs Safe/Unsafe risk triage, and Stage 2 refines unsafe cases into contradiction-driven versus evidence-gap risks. ECRT leverages internal representation and logit shifts under CTX/NOCTX conditions, with class-balanced training for robust learning. Under evidence-grouped (not patient-disjoint) splits across multiple backbones, ECRT provides strong Stage-1 risk triage and explicit subtype attribution, improves Stage-1 balanced accuracy by +0.15 to +0.19 over external uncertainty and self-consistency baselines and by +0.02 to +0.07 over the strongest adapted supervised baseline, and consistently exceeds a single-stage white-box ablation on Stage-1 balanced accuracy. These findings support white-box internal signals grounded in retinal evidence as a practical route to interpretable medical LLM risk triage.

AI.tldr: They introduce RETINA-SAFE, a retinal evidence-grounded benchmark and a white-box detection framework (ECRT) to identify and categorize hallucination risks in medical LLM decisions on diabetic retinopathy.
AI.motivation: Medical LLMs can hallucinate, especially when clinical evidence is lacking or contradictory, posing safety risks in real-world decision support such as diabetic retinopathy grading. There is a need for structured benchmarks and interpretable methods to detect when model outputs are unsafe with respect to available evidence.
AI.method: They build RETINA-SAFE, a 12,522-sample benchmark aligned with retinal grading records and organized into three evidence relations: E-Align (answers consistent with evidence), E-Conflict (answers conflicting with evidence), and E-Gap (answers where evidence is insufficient). They also propose ECRT, a two-stage white-box detection framework: Stage 1 classifies outputs as Safe vs Unsafe; Stage 2 further splits Unsafe into contradiction-driven vs evidence-gap risks. ECRT uses models’ internal representations and logit shifts under context vs no-context (CTX/NOCTX) conditions, with class-balanced training. They evaluate ECRT across multiple model backbones, under evidence-grouped splits, and compare against external uncertainty, self-consistency, and supervised baselines, including a single-stage white-box ablation.
AI.result: ECRT achieves strong performance in Stage-1 Safe/Unsafe risk triage and provides explicit attribution of hallucination subtypes. It improves Stage-1 balanced accuracy by +0.15 to +0.19 over external uncertainty and self-consistency baselines and by +0.02 to +0.07 over the best adapted supervised baseline. It also consistently outperforms a single-stage white-box ablation in Stage-1 balanced accuracy across backbones and evidence-grouped splits.
AI.conclusion: White-box internal signals that are grounded in structured retinal evidence can be effectively used for interpretable risk triage of medical LLM outputs. RETINA-SAFE offers a systematic benchmark for studying evidence-consistent, conflicting, and insufficient scenarios, and ECRT provides a practical, interpretable approach for detecting and categorizing hallucination risks in diabetic retinopathy decision support settings.

title: Learning What Matters: Dynamic Dimension Selection and Aggregation for Interpretable Vision-Language Reward Modeling
summary: Vision-language reward modeling faces a dilemma: generative approaches are interpretable but slow, while discriminative ones are efficient but act as opaque "black boxes." To bridge this gap, we propose VL-MDR (Vision-Language Multi-Dimensional Reward), a framework that dynamically decomposes evaluation into granular, interpretable dimensions. Instead of outputting a monolithic scalar, VL-MDR employs a visual-aware gating mechanism to identify relevant dimensions and adaptively weight them (e.g., Hallucination, Reasoning) for each specific input. To support this, we curate a dataset of 321k vision-language preference pairs annotated across 21 fine-grained dimensions. Extensive experiments show that VL-MDR consistently outperforms existing open-source reward models on benchmarks like VL-RewardBench. Furthermore, we show that VL-MDR-constructed preference pairs effectively enable DPO alignment to mitigate visual hallucinations and improve reliability, providing a scalable solution for VLM alignment.

AI.tldr: Proposes VL-MDR, a vision-language reward model that decomposes evaluation into multiple interpretable dimensions and adaptively weights them per input, outperforming prior models and improving visual alignment.
AI.motivation: Existing vision-language reward models face a trade-off: generative models are interpretable but slow, whereas discriminative ones are efficient but opaque, giving only a single scalar score. There is a need for reward models that are both efficient and interpretable, especially to diagnose issues like hallucination and reasoning errors in vision-language models and to better align them.
AI.method: Introduce VL-MDR (Vision-Language Multi-Dimensional Reward), which models rewards along multiple fine-grained, interpretable dimensions instead of a single scalar. It uses a visual-aware gating mechanism to select which dimensions are relevant for a given input and to assign adaptive weights to them (e.g., hallucination, reasoning, etc.). The authors also build a large-scale dataset of 321k vision-language preference pairs, each annotated along 21 fine-grained dimensions, and train the multi-dimensional reward model on this data. They then use the learned reward to construct preference pairs for Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) alignment.
AI.result: VL-MDR consistently outperforms current open-source vision-language reward models on benchmarks such as VL-RewardBench. Additionally, when VL-MDR is used to construct preference data for DPO, the resulting aligned vision-language models show reduced visual hallucinations and improved reliability.
AI.conclusion: Multi-dimensional, interpretable reward modeling with adaptive, visually-aware dimension weighting is an effective and scalable approach to vision-language model evaluation and alignment. VL-MDR not only improves benchmark performance over existing reward models but also serves as a practical tool for generating high-quality preference data that enhances alignment (e.g., reduces hallucinations and increases reliability) through methods such as DPO.

title: ETR: Entropy Trend Reward for Efficient Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
summary: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning improves large language model performance on complex tasks, but often produces excessively long and inefficient reasoning traces. Existing methods shorten CoTs using length penalties or global entropy reduction, implicitly assuming that low uncertainty is desirable throughout reasoning. We show instead that reasoning efficiency is governed by the trajectory of uncertainty. CoTs with dominant downward entropy trends are substantially shorter. Motivated by this insight, we propose Entropy Trend Reward (ETR), a trajectory-aware objective that encourages progressive uncertainty reduction while allowing limited local exploration. We integrate ETR into Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) and evaluate it across multiple reasoning models and challenging benchmarks. ETR consistently achieves a superior accuracy-efficiency tradeoff, improving DeepSeek-R1-Distill-7B by 9.9% in accuracy while reducing CoT length by 67% across four benchmarks. Code is available at https://github.com/Xuan1030/ETR

AI.tldr: The paper tackles the problem that chain-of-thought reasoning makes LLMs accurate but verbose and inefficient. It analyzes how uncertainty (entropy) evolves over a reasoning trajectory and finds that CoTs with steadily decreasing entropy are both shorter and effective. Based on this, it introduces Entropy Trend Reward (ETR), a trajectory-aware reinforcement signal that rewards reasoning paths with an overall downward entropy trend, leading to substantially shorter CoTs with equal or better accuracy across several benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Chain-of-thought (CoT) improves performance on complex tasks but leads to long, inefficient reasoning traces that are expensive at inference time. Existing methods to shorten CoTs (e.g., length penalties, global entropy reduction) treat low uncertainty everywhere as ideal, which may overly suppress necessary exploration. The authors are motivated to better understand what makes reasoning efficient, focusing on the *trajectory* of model uncertainty instead of its absolute level, and to design an objective that yields shorter, effective CoTs without sacrificing accuracy.
AI.method: 1) Empirical analysis: They measure token-level predictive entropy along CoT traces and identify that trajectories with a dominant downward trend in entropy correspond to shorter, effective reasoning. 2) Entropy Trend Reward (ETR): They define a trajectory-aware reward that encourages a monotonic or mostly downward entropy trend over time while tolerating some local increases to allow exploration. This reward is computed over the entire reasoning sequence rather than per-token. 3) Integration into GRPO: They plug ETR into Group Relative Policy Optimization, a reinforcement-learning-style training framework for LLMs, to guide the model to produce CoTs that demonstrate progressive uncertainty reduction. 4) Evaluation: They test ETR-augmented models on multiple reasoning tasks and benchmarks, comparing accuracy and CoT length against baselines that use length penalties or global entropy control, particularly on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-7B.
AI.result: Models trained with ETR achieve a better accuracy-efficiency tradeoff compared to baselines. For DeepSeek-R1-Distill-7B, ETR improves accuracy by 9.9% while reducing CoT length by 67% across four challenging reasoning benchmarks. These gains are consistent across different reasoning models and datasets, indicating that shaping the entropy *trajectory* is more effective than naive length penalties or static entropy reduction.
AI.conclusion: The entropy trajectory—not just low entropy per se—governs the efficiency of chain-of-thought reasoning. By explicitly rewarding CoTs that exhibit progressive uncertainty reduction (via Entropy Trend Reward), the model learns to reason more decisively and concisely while preserving or improving accuracy. ETR, implemented within GRPO, offers a general, trajectory-aware principle for training efficient reasoning LLMs and outperforms prior approaches that ignore uncertainty dynamics.

title: Content Fuzzing for Escaping Information Cocoons on Digital Social Media
summary: Information cocoons on social media limit users' exposure to posts with diverse viewpoints. Modern platforms use stance detection as an important signal in recommendation and ranking pipelines, which can route posts primarily to like-minded audiences and reduce cross-cutting exposure. This restricts the reach of dissenting opinions and hinders constructive discourse. We take the creator's perspective and investigate how content can be revised to reach beyond existing affinity clusters. We present ContentFuzz, a confidence-guided fuzzing framework that rewrites posts while preserving their human-interpreted intent and induces different machine-inferred stance labels. ContentFuzz aims to route posts beyond their original cocoons. Our method guides a large language model (LLM) to generate meaning-preserving rewrites using confidence feedback from stance detection models. Evaluated on four representative stance detection models across three datasets in two languages, ContentFuzz effectively changes machine-classified stance labels, while maintaining semantic integrity with respect to the original content.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ContentFuzz, a framework that rewrites social media posts to change how stance detection models classify them, helping posts escape information cocoons while preserving their original meaning.
AI.motivation: Social media platforms use stance detection in recommendation systems, which often routes posts mainly to like-minded users, reinforcing information cocoons and limiting exposure to dissenting views. The authors want to find a way, from the content creator’s side, to revise posts so they can reach audiences beyond these affinity clusters without changing their intended meaning.
AI.method: The authors design ContentFuzz, a confidence-guided fuzzing framework. It uses a large language model (LLM) to generate multiple meaning-preserving rewrites of a given post. A stance detection model then provides confidence scores for the stance labels of these rewrites. The framework iteratively steers the LLM, based on these confidence signals, toward rewrites that keep the human-perceived intent but flip or alter the machine-inferred stance label. They evaluate ContentFuzz on four stance detection models, three datasets, and two languages.
AI.result: ContentFuzz succeeds in changing the stance labels output by different stance detection models for a substantial portion of posts, demonstrating that relatively small semantic-preserving rewrites can fool or redirect these models. At the same time, human-level semantic integrity between the original and rewritten posts is largely maintained, according to their evaluations across multiple datasets and languages.
AI.conclusion: Stance detection models used in social media pipelines are vulnerable to targeted, meaning-preserving rewrites that alter their classifications. ContentFuzz shows that creators can, in practice, revise content to escape information cocoons and reach different audiences without changing their intended message. This raises both opportunities for breaking echo chambers and concerns about the robustness and reliability of stance-aware recommendation systems.

title: LatentAudit: Real-Time White-Box Faithfulness Monitoring for Retrieval-Augmented Generation with Verifiable Deployment
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) mitigates hallucination but does not eliminate it: a deployed system must still decide, at inference time, whether its answer is actually supported by the retrieved evidence. We introduce LatentAudit, a white-box auditor that pools mid-to-late residual-stream activations from an open-weight generator and measures their Mahalanobis distance to the evidence representation. The resulting quadratic rule requires no auxiliary judge model, runs at generation time, and is simple enough to calibrate on a small held-out set. We show that residual-stream geometry carries a usable faithfulness signal, that this signal survives architecture changes and realistic retrieval failures, and that the same rule remains amenable to public verification. On PubMedQA with Llama-3-8B, LatentAudit reaches 0.942 AUROC with 0.77,ms overhead. Across three QA benchmarks and five model families (Llama-2/3, Qwen-2.5/3, Mistral), the monitor remains stable; under a four-way stress test with contradictions, retrieval misses, and partial-support noise, it reaches 0.9566--0.9815 AUROC on PubMedQA and 0.9142--0.9315 on HotpotQA. At 16-bit fixed-point precision, the audit rule preserves 99.8% of the FP16 AUROC, enabling Groth16-based public verification without revealing model weights or activations. Together, these results position residual-stream geometry as a practical basis for real-time RAG faithfulness monitoring and optional verifiable deployment.

AI.tldr: LatentAudit is a lightweight, white-box method that uses residual-stream geometry and a Mahalanobis-distance rule to decide whether a RAG model’s answer is actually supported by retrieved evidence, works robustly across models/benchmarks, and can be publicly verified via succinct proofs.
AI.motivation: RAG reduces but does not remove hallucinations, so deployed systems still need a reliable, low-latency way to judge whether each generated answer is faithful to the retrieved evidence, without relying on extra judge models or heavy post-hoc pipelines, and ideally in a way that supports public verification of faithfulness claims.
AI.method: Use an open-weight generator’s mid-to-late residual-stream activations as a latent representation of the model’s internal belief about the answer, compare this to an evidence representation via Mahalanobis distance, and treat the resulting quadratic form as a calibrated decision rule for answer faithfulness. The method is model-internal (white-box), runs during generation, and can be implemented in low-precision fixed-point arithmetic to enable SNARK-friendly public verification (e.g., Groth16) without exposing full model details.
AI.result: On PubMedQA with Llama-3-8B, LatentAudit achieves 0.942 AUROC with only 0.77 ms overhead per query; across multiple QA benchmarks (PubMedQA, HotpotQA, etc.) and model families (Llama-2/3, Qwen-2.5/3, Mistral), the auditor’s performance remains robust, including under stress tests with contradictions, retrieval failures, and partial-support noise, where AUROCs stay around 0.9566–0.9815 (PubMedQA) and 0.9142–0.9315 (HotpotQA). At 16-bit fixed-point precision, the method retains 99.8% of FP16 AUROC while remaining compatible with Groth16 proofs.
AI.conclusion: Residual-stream activations encode a stable, transferable signal about whether a RAG model’s answers are supported by retrieved evidence; a simple Mahalanobis-distance-based quadratic rule can exploit this signal to monitor faithfulness in real time, with low overhead, robustness across models and retrieval imperfections, and the option of cryptographically verifiable deployment without revealing proprietary model details.

title: Don't Act Blindly: Robust GUI Automation via Action-Effect Verification and Self-Correction
summary: Autonomous GUI agents based on vision-language models (VLMs) often assume deterministic environment responses, generating actions without verifying whether previous operations succeeded. In real-world settings with network latency, rendering delays, and system interruptions, this assumption leads to undetected action failures, repetitive ineffective behaviors, and catastrophic error accumulation. Moreover, learning robust recovery strategies is challenging due to the high cost of online interaction and the lack of real-time feedback in offline datasets.We propose VeriGUI (Verification-driven GUI Agent), which explicitly models action outcomes and recovery under noisy environments. VeriGUI introduces a Thinking--Verification--Action--Expectation (TVAE) framework to detect failures and guide corrective reasoning, and a two-stage training pipeline that combines Robust SFT with synthetic failure trajectories and GRPO with asymmetric verification rewards. We further construct a Robustness Benchmark based on AndroidControl to evaluate failure recognition and correction. Experiments show that VeriGUI significantly reduces failure loops and improves recovery success while maintaining competitive standard task performance.

AI.tldr: VeriGUI is a verification-driven GUI agent for noisy, non-deterministic environments that explicitly checks whether its actions succeeded and learns to recover from failures, reducing error loops while keeping strong normal-task performance.
AI.motivation: Existing VLM-based autonomous GUI agents implicitly assume a deterministic, perfectly responsive environment: they issue actions and continue planning without explicitly checking whether prior actions actually took effect. In realistic GUI environments (e.g., mobile apps) with network delays, rendering lag, and interruptions, this leads to undetected failures, repeated ineffective actions (e.g., clicking the same unresponsive button), and cascading errors that derail task completion. Furthermore, learning robust recovery strategies is hard because online interaction is expensive and offline logs often lack clear, real-time feedback about which operations failed and how to correct them. The paper aims to build agents that are robust to such noise by explicitly modeling verification and recovery.
AI.method: The paper proposes VeriGUI, a verification-driven GUI agent based on vision-language models, built around a Thinking–Verification–Action–Expectation (TVAE) workflow. In TVAE, the agent (1) performs internal reasoning (Thinking), (2) explicitly checks and interprets the environment state to verify whether its last action succeeded (Verification), (3) issues the next GUI operation (Action), and (4) forms expectations about the outcome to guide future verification (Expectation). To train this behavior, they introduce a two-stage pipeline: (1) Robust SFT (supervised fine-tuning) using synthetic failure trajectories where action outcomes are perturbed to teach the agent to recognize and respond to failures, and (2) GRPO (a reinforcement learning method) with asymmetric verification rewards that encourage accurate failure recognition and effective recovery more than simple success in noise-free settings. They also build a Robustness Benchmark based on the AndroidControl environment to systematically inject failures, measure the agent’s ability to detect them, and evaluate its corrective strategies.
AI.result: On the proposed Robustness Benchmark derived from AndroidControl, VeriGUI detects action failures more reliably than baseline GUI agents, enters failure loops (e.g., endlessly repeating a broken action) far less often, and shows higher success rates in recovering from noisy or failed interactions. At the same time, it maintains comparable, competitive performance on standard tasks without injected noise, demonstrating that explicit verification and recovery do not significantly degrade normal-case efficiency or accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling verification and recovery in GUI agents is crucial for robustness in real-world, noisy environments. VeriGUI’s TVAE framework and two-stage training (Robust SFT plus GRPO with asymmetric verification rewards) enable agents to recognize action failures, avoid repetitive ineffective behaviors, and execute effective recovery strategies, all while preserving strong performance in clean environments. This suggests that verification-driven design is a promising direction for scaling autonomous GUI agents to practical, non-deterministic settings.

title: TFRBench: A Reasoning Benchmark for Evaluating Forecasting Systems
summary: We introduce TFRBench, the first benchmark designed to evaluate the reasoning capabilities of forecasting systems. Traditionally, time-series forecasting has been evaluated solely on numerical accuracy, treating foundation models as ``black boxes.'' Unlike existing benchmarks, TFRBench provides a protocol for evaluating the reasoning generated by forecasting systems--specifically their analysis of cross-channel dependencies, trends, and external events. To enable this, we propose a systematic multi-agent framework that utilizes an iterative verification loop to synthesize numerically grounded reasoning traces. Spanning ten datasets across five domains, our evaluation confirms that this reasoning is causally effective; useful for evaluation; and prompting LLMs with our generated traces significantly improves forecasting accuracy compared to direct numerical prediction (e.g., avg. $\sim40.2\%\to56.6\%)$, validating the quality of our reasoning. Conversely, benchmarking experiments reveal that off-the-shelf LLMs consistently struggle with both reasoning (lower LLM-as-a-Judge scores) and numerical forecasting, frequently failing to capture domain-specific dynamics. TFRBench thus establishes a new standard for interpretable, reasoning-based evaluation in time-series forecasting. Our benchmark is available at: https://tfrbench.github.io

AI.tldr: TFRBench is a new benchmark to evaluate not just numerical accuracy but also the reasoning quality of time-series forecasting systems.
AI.motivation: Existing time-series forecasting evaluation focuses almost exclusively on numerical error metrics and treats modern foundation models as black boxes, providing no way to assess or compare the quality of their underlying reasoning about trends, cross-channel dependencies, and external events. There is a growing need for interpretable, reasoning-aware evaluation, especially as LLMs are increasingly used for forecasting tasks.
AI.method: The authors build TFRBench, a benchmark and protocol that evaluates forecasting models on their generated reasoning traces. They design a systematic multi-agent framework with an iterative verification loop to synthesize numerically grounded reasoning, focusing on how models analyze cross-channel dependencies, temporal trends, and exogenous factors. They then use LLMs (with LLM-as-a-judge style scoring) within this framework to evaluate reasoning quality across ten datasets in five domains, and also run prompting experiments where LLMs are guided by these reasoning traces for forecasting.
AI.result: The benchmark shows that the synthesized reasoning traces are causally effective: when LLMs are prompted with these traces, their forecasting accuracy improves substantially (e.g., from about 40.2% to 56.6% on average) relative to direct numerical prediction without reasoning guidance. At the same time, experiments demonstrate that standard off-the-shelf LLMs perform poorly both in generating high-quality reasoning (lower LLM-as-a-Judge scores) and in numerical forecasting, often failing to model domain-specific patterns. This contrast validates the benchmark’s discriminative power and the usefulness of the generated reasoning traces.
AI.conclusion: TFRBench provides the first standardized, interpretable, reasoning-oriented benchmark for time-series forecasting, moving beyond pure numerical metrics to assess how models reason about temporal dynamics and external drivers. The work shows that high-quality, numerically grounded reasoning can measurably improve forecasting performance and that current LLMs have significant gaps in this area, thereby establishing a new direction and standard for evaluating and improving forecasting systems.

title: Cross-Modal Coreference Alignment: Enabling Reliable Information Transfer in Omni-LLMs
summary: Omni Large Language Models (Omni-LLMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities in holistic multi-modal perception, yet they consistently falter in complex scenarios requiring synergistic omni-modal reasoning. Beyond understanding global multimodal context, effective reasoning also hinges on fine-grained cross-modal alignment, especially identifying shared referents across modalities, yet this aspect has been largely overlooked. To bridge this gap, we formalize the challenge as a cross-modal coreference problem, where a model must localize a referent in a source modality and re-identify it in a target modality. Building on this paradigm, we introduce CrossOmni, a dataset comprising nine tasks equipped with human-designed reasoning rationales to evaluate and enhance this capability. Experiments on 13 Omni-LLMs reveal systematic weaknesses in cross-modal coreference, which we attribute to the absence of coreference-aware thinking patterns. To address this, we enhance cross-modal alignment via two strategies: a training-free In-Context Learning method and a training-based SFT+GRPO framework designed to induce such thinking patterns. Both approaches yield substantial performance gains and generalize effectively to collaborative reasoning tasks. Overall, our findings highlight cross-modal coreference as a crucial missing piece for advancing robust omni-modal reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies cross-modal coreference as a missing capability in current Omni-LLMs, introduces the CrossOmni benchmark for it, and proposes both training-free and training-based methods that significantly improve performance and generalize to broader omni-modal reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Current Omni-LLMs can perceive and understand multi-modal inputs but fail in complex tasks requiring coordinated reasoning across modalities. The authors argue that a neglected root cause is weak fine-grained cross-modal alignment, particularly the ability to consistently identify the same referent across different modalities (cross-modal coreference). They seek to formalize, measure, and improve this capability to advance robust omni-modal reasoning.
