AI.method: 1) Formalize cross-modal coreference as the task of localizing a referent in one modality and re-identifying it in another. 2) Construct CrossOmni, a dataset of nine tasks with human-designed reasoning rationales focusing on this capability. 3) Evaluate 13 existing Omni-LLMs on CrossOmni to reveal systematic weaknesses. 4) Propose two enhancement strategies: (a) a training-free In-Context Learning (ICL) method that injects coreference-aware reasoning patterns via prompts; (b) a training-based pipeline combining Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with GRPO-style reinforcement optimization to explicitly induce these patterns. 5) Test both approaches on CrossOmni and other collaborative reasoning tasks to assess performance gains and generalization.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows that state-of-the-art Omni-LLMs systematically underperform on cross-modal coreference tasks, confirming it as a weak spot. Applying the proposed ICL strategy leads to notable improvements without additional training, while the SFT+GRPO framework achieves even larger gains. These improvements transfer beyond the benchmark itself, enhancing performance on broader collaborative omni-modal reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: Cross-modal coreference is a key, previously underexplored ingredient for strong omni-modal reasoning. By formalizing the problem, providing the CrossOmni benchmark with rationales, and demonstrating effective enhancement strategies, the paper shows that explicitly inducing coreference-aware thinking significantly boosts both in-domain and out-of-domain omni-modal reasoning. The work suggests that future Omni-LLM development should treat cross-modal coreference as a central design and evaluation axis.

title: LLM-as-Judge for Semantic Judging of Powerline Segmentation in UAV Inspection
summary: The deployment of lightweight segmentation models on drones for autonomous power line inspection presents a critical challenge: maintaining reliable performance under real-world conditions that differ from training data. Although compact architectures such as U-Net enable real-time onboard inference, their segmentation outputs can degrade unpredictably in adverse environments, raising safety concerns. In this work, we study the feasibility of using a large language model (LLM) as a semantic judge to assess the reliability of power line segmentation results produced by drone-mounted models. Rather than introducing a new inspection system, we formalize a watchdog scenario in which an offboard LLM evaluates segmentation overlays and examine whether such a judge can be trusted to behave consistently and perceptually coherently. To this end, we design two evaluation protocols that analyze the judge's repeatability and sensitivity. First, we assess repeatability by repeatedly querying the LLM with identical inputs and fixed prompts, measuring the stability of its quality scores and confidence estimates. Second, we evaluate perceptual sensitivity by introducing controlled visual corruptions (fog, rain, snow, shadow, and sunflare) and analyzing how the judge's outputs respond to progressive degradation in segmentation quality. Our results show that the LLM produces highly consistent categorical judgments under identical conditions while exhibiting appropriate declines in confidence as visual reliability deteriorates. Moreover, the judge remains responsive to perceptual cues such as missing or misidentified power lines, even under challenging conditions. These findings suggest that, when carefully constrained, an LLM can serve as a reliable semantic judge for monitoring segmentation quality in safety-critical aerial inspection tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper explores using a large language model (LLM) as an offboard semantic judge to monitor and assess the reliability of power line segmentation produced by lightweight models on drones, showing that the LLM can provide consistent and perceptually coherent quality assessments under varying visual conditions.
AI.motivation: Deploying lightweight segmentation models on drones for autonomous power line inspection demands reliable performance in diverse, real-world conditions. However, these compact models can fail unpredictably in adverse environments (fog, rain, snow, etc.), which poses safety risks. There is therefore a need for a mechanism to monitor and assess the trustworthiness of their segmentation outputs without redesigning the whole inspection pipeline.
AI.method: The authors formalize a watchdog scenario in which a large language model acts offboard as a semantic judge of segmentation overlays generated by drone-mounted models. They design two evaluation protocols: (1) a repeatability test, where the LLM is queried multiple times with identical inputs and fixed prompts to measure stability of its quality scores and confidence; and (2) a perceptual sensitivity test, where controlled visual corruptions (fog, rain, snow, shadow, sunflare) are added to images to progressively degrade segmentation quality, and the LLM's responses are analyzed for appropriate changes in judgments and confidence. They examine both categorical judgments and confidence estimates, and inspect responsiveness to specific perceptual cues like missing or misidentified power lines.
AI.result: The LLM demonstrates high consistency in categorical judgments across repeated, identical queries, indicating strong repeatability. Under increasing visual corruption, the LLM's confidence appropriately declines, reflecting sensitivity to degradation in visual reliability. It remains able to detect perceptual issues such as missing or misclassified power lines even under challenging conditions, suggesting robust perceptual sensitivity.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that a carefully constrained LLM can function as a reliable semantic judge for monitoring segmentation quality in safety-critical aerial power line inspection. This watchdog role can enhance safety by providing consistent, perceptually coherent assessments of segmentation reliability without overhauling existing lightweight, onboard inspection models.

title: Turbulence-like 5/3 spectral scaling in contextual representations of language as a complex system
summary: Natural language is a complex system that exhibits robust statistical regularities. Here, we represent text as a trajectory in a high-dimensional embedding space generated by transformer-based language models, and quantify scale-dependent fluctuations along the token sequence using an embedding-step signal. Across multiple languages and corpora, the resulting power spectrum exhibits a robust power law with an exponent close to $5/3$ over an extended frequency range. This scaling is observed consistently in contextual embeddings from both human-written and AI-generated text, but is absent in static word embeddings and is disrupted by randomization of token order. These results show that the observed scaling reflects multiscale, context-dependent organization rather than lexical statistics alone. By analogy with the Kolmogorov spectrum in turbulence, our findings suggest that semantic information is integrated in a scale-free, self-similar manner across linguistic scales, and provide a quantitative, model-agnostic benchmark for studying complex structure in language representations.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that when text is mapped into transformer embedding space and analyzed as a time series, its fluctuations follow a robust power-law spectrum with exponent ~5/3, analogous to Kolmogorov turbulence, revealing scale-free, multiscale semantic organization beyond simple word statistics.
AI.motivation: To find model-agnostic, quantitative signatures of structure in natural language that go beyond simple lexical statistics, and to understand how semantic information is distributed across scales in text as represented by modern transformer-based language models.
AI.method: Represent a text sequence as a trajectory of contextual token embeddings produced by transformer LMs. Define an embedding-step signal that measures stepwise changes along the token sequence, then compute its power spectrum to analyze scale-dependent fluctuations. Compare spectra across multiple languages, corpora, and model types; contrast contextual embeddings vs. static word embeddings; test robustness on human vs. AI-generated text and under token-order randomization.
AI.result: The power spectrum of the embedding-step signal shows a robust power-law scaling with exponent close to 5/3 across a broad frequency range for many languages, corpora, and transformer models. This behavior appears both in human and AI-generated text, disappears in static (non-contextual) word embeddings, and is disrupted when token order is randomized, indicating reliance on sequential, contextual structure.
AI.conclusion: Transformer contextual embeddings of text encode a scale-free, self-similar organization of semantic information, reminiscent of Kolmogorov’s 5/3 law in turbulence. This structure depends on multiscale context rather than mere word frequency statistics and yields a quantitative, model-agnostic benchmark for probing and comparing complex structure in language representations.

title: Towards Effective In-context Cross-domain Knowledge Transfer via Domain-invariant-neurons-based Retrieval
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have made notable progress in logical reasoning, yet still fall short of human-level performance. Current boosting strategies rely on expert-crafted in-domain demonstrations, limiting their applicability in expertise-scarce domains, such as specialized mathematical reasoning, formal logic, or legal analysis. In this work, we demonstrate the feasibility of leveraging cross-domain demonstrating examples to boost the LLMs' reasoning performance. Despite substantial domain differences, many reusable implicit logical structures are shared across domains. In order to effectively retrieve cross-domain examples for unseen domains under investigation, in this work, we further propose an effective retrieval method, called domain-invariant neurons-based retrieval (\textbf{DIN-Retrieval}). Concisely, DIN-Retrieval first summarizes a hidden representation that is universal across different domains. Then, during the inference stage, we use the DIN vector to retrieve structurally compatible cross-domain demonstrations for the in-context learning. Experimental results in multiple settings for the transfer of mathematical and logical reasoning demonstrate that our method achieves an average improvement of 1.8 over the state-of-the-art methods \footnote{Our implementation is available at https://github.com/Leon221220/DIN-Retrieval}.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes DIN-Retrieval, a method that uses domain-invariant neuron representations to retrieve cross-domain demonstration examples that improve LLM logical and mathematical reasoning, achieving state-of-the-art transfer performance.
AI.motivation: LLMs are improving at logical reasoning but still lag behind humans. Existing boosting strategies depend on hand-crafted, in-domain demonstrations, which are often unavailable in expertise-scarce domains like advanced math, formal logic, or legal reasoning. The authors are motivated to remove this dependency by reusing demonstrations from other domains, exploiting common logical structures to enhance reasoning without needing domain experts for every new task domain.
AI.method: The authors introduce DIN-Retrieval, a retrieval-based in-context learning method that identifies and leverages domain-invariant internal representations of LLMs. First, they summarize a hidden representation (DIN vector) that captures universal, domain-agnostic logical structures shared across tasks and domains. At inference time, they use this DIN vector as a key to retrieve cross-domain demonstration examples that are structurally compatible with the target problem, even if they come from different domains. These retrieved examples are then used as in-context demonstrations to guide the LLM’s reasoning on the new domain tasks.
AI.result: In experiments transferring between mathematical and logical reasoning tasks, DIN-Retrieval outperforms existing state-of-the-art cross-domain and retrieval-based methods. The method yields an average performance gain of 1.8 points over prior approaches across multiple transfer settings, indicating more effective reuse of cross-domain demonstrations for reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Logical structures learned by LLMs are partially domain-invariant and can be exploited via internal representations to support cross-domain transfer in reasoning tasks. By using domain-invariant neurons to guide example retrieval, DIN-Retrieval enables effective in-context learning in domains lacking expert-crafted demonstrations and achieves better performance than existing methods. This validates the feasibility and benefit of cross-domain demonstration retrieval for boosting LLM reasoning.

title: Learning to Edit Knowledge via Instruction-based Chain-of-Thought Prompting
summary: Large language models (LLMs) can effectively handle outdated information through knowledge editing. However, current approaches face two key limitations: (I) Poor generalization: Most approaches rigidly inject new knowledge without ensuring that the model can use it effectively to solve practical problems. (II) Narrow scope: Current methods focus primarily on structured fact triples, overlooking the diverse unstructured forms of factual information (e.g., news, articles) prevalent in real-world contexts. To address these challenges, we propose a new paradigm: teaching LLMs to edit knowledge via Chain of Thoughts (CoTs) reasoning (CoT2Edit). We first leverage language model agents for both structured and unstructured edited data to generate CoTs, building high-quality instruction data. The model is then trained to reason over edited knowledge through supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO). At inference time, we integrate Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to dynamically retrieve relevant edited facts for real-time knowledge editing. Experimental results demonstrate that our method achieves strong generalization across six diverse knowledge editing scenarios with just a single round of training on three open-source language models. The codes are available at https://github.com/FredJDean/CoT2Edit.

AI.tldr: They propose CoT2Edit, a method that teaches LLMs to perform knowledge editing via chain-of-thought reasoning, improving generalization and handling both structured and unstructured factual updates.
AI.motivation: Existing knowledge editing methods for LLMs have two main issues: they don’t generalize well to practical problem-solving after an edit, and they mostly handle simple structured fact triples, not richer unstructured factual content like news or articles. This limits real-world applicability when knowledge changes or needs correction.
AI.method: 1) Use language-model agents to generate chain-of-thought (CoT) rationales for both structured and unstructured edited knowledge, forming high-quality instruction-style training data. 2) Train the target LLM to reason over the edited knowledge using supervised fine-tuning (SFT) plus Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), a reinforcement learning-style optimization. 3) At inference, apply Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to fetch relevant edited facts on the fly, so the model can condition its reasoning on up-to-date information for each query.
AI.result: Across six different knowledge editing scenarios and three open-source base LLMs, a single round of training with CoT2Edit yields strong generalization: the models can use edited knowledge more reliably to solve downstream tasks, and they can handle both structured and unstructured factual updates more effectively than prior editing methods (details and metrics are referenced but not in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: Teaching LLMs to perform knowledge editing through explicit chain-of-thought reasoning, combined with SFT, GRPO, and RAG-based retrieval of edited facts, leads to more generalizable and broadly applicable knowledge editing. This paradigm better matches real-world needs where updates come in varied, often unstructured forms and must support practical reasoning tasks.

title: Neural Assistive Impulses: Synthesizing Exaggerated Motions for Physics-based Characters
summary: Physics-based character animation has become a fundamental approach for synthesizing realistic, physically plausible motions. While current data-driven deep reinforcement learning (DRL) methods can synthesize complex skills, they struggle to reproduce exaggerated, stylized motions, such as instantaneous dashes or mid-air trajectory changes, which are required in animation but violate standard physical laws. The primary limitation stems from modeling the character as an underactuated floating-base system, in which internal joint torques and momentum conservation strictly govern motion. Direct attempts to enforce such motions via external wrenches often lead to training instability, as velocity discontinuities produce sparse, high-magnitude force spikes that prevent policy convergence. We propose Assistive Impulse Neural Control, a framework that reformulates external assistance in impulse space rather than force space to ensure numerical stability. We decompose the assistive signal into an analytic high-frequency component derived from Inverse Dynamics and a learned low-frequency residual correction, governed by a hybrid neural policy. We demonstrate that our method enables robust tracking of highly agile, dynamically infeasible maneuvers that were previously intractable for physics-based methods.

AI.tldr: They introduce a numerically stable way to add nonphysical, stylized assistance impulses into physics-based character control so DRL policies can reproduce agile, dynamically infeasible motions.
AI.motivation: Existing DRL physics-based character animation methods can imitate realistic motions but fail on exaggerated, nonphysical maneuvers, like instant direction changes or mid-air trajectory edits, which animators often need. Modeling characters as underactuated floating-base systems with only internal torques enforces strict physical laws (e.g., momentum conservation), so trying to impose such exaggerated motions via external forces leads to unstable training due to discontinuous velocities and spiky, sparse forces. A new formulation is needed to incorporate these stylized effects while maintaining training stability.
AI.method: They introduce Assistive Impulse Neural Control, which formulates the external assistance in impulse space instead of continuous force space to avoid instability from force spikes. The assistive signal is split into (1) an analytic, high-frequency component computed via inverse dynamics that accounts for the fast, discontinuous effects, and (2) a learned, low-frequency residual component produced by a neural network. A hybrid neural policy governs this decomposition, allowing the DRL controller to focus on smooth residual corrections while the analytic part handles the problematic high-frequency behavior.
AI.result: Using the proposed framework, policies can robustly track highly agile, dynamically infeasible motions (e.g., exaggerated dashes, mid-air redirections) that previous physics-based, DRL-driven animation methods could not reproduce or could only do so unstably. The impulse-space formulation and analytic-plus-residual decomposition lead to stable training and execution for such motions.
AI.conclusion: Recasting external assistance as impulses and decomposing it into analytic high-frequency and learned low-frequency components enables stable DRL training for physics-based characters performing highly agile but physically impossible maneuvers. This expands the expressive power of physics-based animation, bridging the gap between physically realistic control and the exaggerated stylization often required in character animation.

title: Efficient Inference for Large Vision-Language Models: Bottlenecks, Techniques, and Prospects
summary: Large Vision-Language Models (LVLMs) enable sophisticated reasoning over images and videos, yet their inference is hindered by a systemic efficiency barrier known as visual token dominance. This overhead is driven by a multi-regime interplay between high-resolution feature extraction, quadratic attention scaling, and memory bandwidth constraints. We present a systematic taxonomy of efficiency techniques structured around the inference lifecycle, consisting of encoding, prefilling, and decoding. Unlike prior reviews focused on isolated optimizations, we analyze the end-to-end pipeline to reveal how upstream decisions dictate downstream bottlenecks, covering compute-bound visual encoding, the intensive prefilling of massive contexts, and the ''visual memory wall'' in bandwidth-bound decoding. By decoupling the efficiency landscape into the axes of shaping information density, managing long-context attention, and overcoming memory limits, this work provides a structured analysis of how isolated optimizations compose to navigate the trade-off between visual fidelity and system efficiency. The survey concludes by outlining four future frontiers supported by pilot empirical insights, including hybrid compression based on functional unit sensitivity, modality-aware decoding with relaxed verification, progressive state management for streaming continuity, and stage-disaggregated serving through hardware-algorithm co-design. The submitted software contains a snapshot of our literature repository, which is designed to be maintained as a living resource for the community.

AI.tldr: The paper surveys efficiency challenges and techniques for Large Vision-Language Models, framing them around the full inference pipeline and proposing future research directions to mitigate visual token dominance.
AI.motivation: Large Vision-Language Models achieve strong multimodal reasoning but are bottlenecked by excessive visual tokens, which interact with attention complexity and hardware limits to make inference slow and costly. Existing work discusses optimizations piecemeal, lacking a unified, end-to-end view that explains how design choices at one stage constrain others. This paper aims to provide that structured perspective to guide both researchers and system designers.
AI.method: The authors conduct a systematic survey and taxonomy of efficiency methods for LVLM inference, organizing prior techniques along the stages of the inference lifecycle—visual encoding, prefilling, and decoding—and along three key axes: information density shaping, long-context attention management, and memory/bandwidth optimization. They analyze how these techniques interact, identify typical bottlenecks such as compute-bound encoding and bandwidth-bound decoding, and support their taxonomy with initial empirical observations and a curated, open literature repository.
AI.result: The survey clarifies the notion of visual token dominance as a multi-factor efficiency barrier and maps existing optimization techniques to where they operate in the pipeline and which bottlenecks they target. It highlights how encoding decisions affect prefilling and decoding costs, and how different families of methods—compression, attention optimization, and memory scheduling—compose and trade off visual quality versus efficiency. It also distills several promising directions such as hybrid compression guided by hardware sensitivity, modality-aware decoding, progressive state handling for streams, and hardware-algorithm co-designed serving architectures.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that overcoming LVLM inference inefficiency requires viewing optimizations holistically across the encoding–prefill–decoding lifecycle rather than as isolated tricks. By organizing the space into clear axes and stages, and by identifying frontiers like hybrid compression and stage-disaggregated serving, the survey provides a roadmap for more scalable, hardware-aware LVLM systems. The accompanying, maintainable software repository is intended to keep the taxonomy current and serve as a community resource.

title: Reason Analogically via Cross-domain Prior Knowledge: An Empirical Study of Cross-domain Knowledge Transfer for In-Context Learning
summary: Despite its success, existing in-context learning (ICL) relies on in-domain expert demonstrations, limiting its applicability when expert annotations are scarce. We posit that different domains may share underlying reasoning structures, enabling source-domain demonstrations to improve target-domain inference despite semantic mismatch. To test this hypothesis, we conduct a comprehensive empirical study of different retrieval methods to validate the feasibility of achieving cross-domain knowledge transfer under the in-context learning setting. Our results demonstrate conditional positive transfer in cross-domain ICL. We identify a clear example absorption threshold: beyond it, positive transfer becomes more likely, and additional demonstrations yield larger gains. Further analysis suggests that these gains stem from reasoning structure repair by retrieved cross-domain examples, rather than semantic cues. Overall, our study validates the feasibility of leveraging cross-domain knowledge transfer to improve cross-domain ICL performance, motivating the community to explore designing more effective retrieval approaches for this novel direction.\footnote{Our implementation is available at https://github.com/littlelaska/ICL-TF4LR}

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to use demonstrations from a different domain for in-context learning and shows that cross-domain examples can improve performance when enough examples are retrieved and used.
AI.motivation: In-context learning usually needs expert, in-domain labeled examples, which are expensive or unavailable in many scenarios. The authors suspect that because different domains can share similar reasoning structures, one may not need domain-matched examples; instead, demonstrations from other domains might still help if their reasoning patterns are relevant. They want to understand whether and when such cross-domain transfer in ICL is possible.
AI.method: They perform a broad empirical study comparing multiple retrieval strategies that select demonstrations from source domains and use them as in-context examples for target-domain tasks. They systematically vary domain similarity, the number of retrieved demonstrations, and analyze how these factors affect ICL performance. They then examine performance patterns to detect conditions under which cross-domain transfer is beneficial, and analyze whether improvements are due to reasoning structure versus semantic overlap.
AI.result: They find that cross-domain in-context learning can give conditional positive transfer: in some settings, using source-domain examples improves target-domain performance, but not universally. They identify an "example absorption threshold"—a minimum number of demonstrations above which positive transfer becomes significantly more likely and additional examples yield larger performance gains. Their analysis indicates that these gains come mainly from repairing or strengthening the model’s reasoning structure via the examples, rather than from direct semantic similarity across domains.
AI.conclusion: The study shows that cross-domain knowledge transfer in ICL is feasible and can meaningfully improve performance when enough relevant examples are used, even if they are from different semantic domains. This suggests that future work should focus on designing more effective retrieval methods that capture and reuse shared reasoning structures across domains, reducing dependence on scarce in-domain expert annotations.

title: Stop Fixating on Prompts: Reasoning Hijacking and Constraint Tightening for Red-Teaming LLM Agents
summary: With the widespread application of LLM-based agents across various domains, their complexity has introduced new security threats. Existing red-team methods mostly rely on modifying user prompts, which lack adaptability to new data and may impact the agent's performance. To address the challenge, this paper proposes the JailAgent framework, which completely avoids modifying the user prompt. Specifically, it implicitly manipulates the agent's reasoning trajectory and memory retrieval with three key stages: Trigger Extraction, Reasoning Hijacking, and Constraint Tightening. Through precise trigger identification, real-time adaptive mechanisms, and an optimized objective function, JailAgent demonstrates outstanding performance in cross-model and cross-scenario environments.

AI.tldr: JailAgent is a framework that attacks LLM-based agents without changing the user prompt, by secretly hijacking their reasoning and memory processes.
AI.motivation: As LLM-based agents become more complex and widely deployed, new security threats emerge. Existing red-team approaches mainly modify user prompts, which are brittle to new data and can degrade the agent’s normal performance. There is a need for more adaptive, covert methods that work across models and scenarios without interfering with genuine user inputs.
AI.method: The paper introduces JailAgent, a framework that leaves the user prompt untouched and instead manipulates internal agent processes. It operates in three stages: (1) Trigger Extraction – identifying precise internal conditions or patterns that can be exploited; (2) Reasoning Hijacking – covertly steering the agent’s intermediate reasoning trajectory; and (3) Constraint Tightening – adjusting how constraints and safety checks are applied via memory retrieval and internal control. The framework uses real-time adaptive mechanisms and an optimized objective function to make the attack both effective and robust across settings.
AI.result: Experiments show that JailAgent achieves strong performance in both cross-model and cross-scenario evaluations, indicating it can reliably subvert different LLM agents and tasks while remaining effective without prompt modification.
AI.conclusion: By shifting from prompt-based manipulation to internal process hijacking, JailAgent reveals a new and powerful attack surface for LLM-based agents. The framework demonstrates that reasoning trajectories and memory retrieval processes can be exploited systematically, highlighting the need for stronger internal defenses and monitoring beyond simple prompt-level safeguards.

title: AutoSOTA: An End-to-End Automated Research System for State-of-the-Art AI Model Discovery
summary: Artificial intelligence research increasingly depends on prolonged cycles of reproduction, debugging, and iterative refinement to achieve State-Of-The-Art (SOTA) performance, creating a growing need for systems that can accelerate the full pipeline of empirical model optimization. In this work, we introduce AutoSOTA, an end-to-end automated research system that advances the latest SOTA models published in top-tier AI papers to reproducible and empirically improved new SOTA models. We formulate this problem through three tightly coupled stages: resource preparation and goal setting; experiment evaluation; and reflection and ideation. To tackle this problem, AutoSOTA adopts a multi-agent architecture with eight specialized agents that collaboratively ground papers to code and dependencies, initialize and repair execution environments, track long-horizon experiments, generate and schedule optimization ideas, and supervise validity to avoid spurious gains. We evaluate AutoSOTA on recent research papers collected from eight top-tier AI conferences under filters for code availability and execution cost. Across these papers, AutoSOTA achieves strong end-to-end performance in both automated replication and subsequent optimization. Specifically, it successfully discovers 105 new SOTA models that surpass the original reported methods, averaging approximately five hours per paper. Case studies spanning LLM, NLP, computer vision, time series, and optimization further show that the system can move beyond routine hyperparameter tuning to identify architectural innovation, algorithmic redesigns, and workflow-level improvements. These results suggest that end-to-end research automation can serve not only as a performance optimizer, but also as a new form of research infrastructure that reduces repetitive experimental burden and helps redirect human attention toward higher-level scientific creativity.

AI.tldr: The paper presents AutoSOTA, a multi‑agent automated research system that reproduces and then improves SOTA AI models from recent top‑tier papers, often discovering better models in about five hours per paper.
AI.motivation: Modern AI progress relies on long, trial‑and‑error cycles of reproduction, debugging, and iterative experimental refinement to achieve SOTA results. This process is slow, labor‑intensive, and difficult to scale, creating a need for systems that can automate not just single experiments or tuning steps, but the entire empirical optimization pipeline behind new research results.
AI.method: The authors formulate end‑to‑end research automation as three coupled stages: (1) resource preparation and goal setting, (2) experiment evaluation, and (3) reflection and ideation. They implement this via AutoSOTA, a multi‑agent system with eight specialized agents responsible for: grounding papers into runnable code and dependencies; setting up and repairing execution environments; running and monitoring long‑horizon experiments; generating, prioritizing, and scheduling optimization ideas; and validating that observed gains are genuine rather than spurious. The system operates autonomously from reading the paper and code to proposing and executing model and training modifications.
AI.result: On a benchmark of recent papers from eight top‑tier AI conferences (filtered for code availability and reasonable execution cost), AutoSOTA is able to both replicate and then improve on many reported SOTA results. It produces 105 new SOTA models that outperform the original methods, with an average of around five hours of automated work per paper. Case studies across LLMs, NLP, vision, time‑series forecasting, and optimization show that the system can propose nontrivial changes such as new architectures, algorithmic redesigns, and workflow improvements, beyond simple hyperparameter tuning.
AI.conclusion: End‑to‑end automation of the empirical research loop is feasible and effective: AutoSOTA can reliably reproduce and then surpass existing SOTA results across diverse domains, within practical time and compute budgets. This positions such systems not only as automated performance optimizers, but as research infrastructure that offloads repetitive experimentation and enables human researchers to focus more on high‑level conceptual and creative work.

title: CODESTRUCT: Code Agents over Structured Action Spaces
summary: LLM-based code agents treat repositories as unstructured text, applying edits through brittle string matching that frequently fails due to formatting drift or ambiguous patterns. We propose reframing the codebase as a structured action space where agents operate on named AST entities rather than text spans. Our framework, CODESTRUCT, provides readCode for retrieving complete syntactic units and editCode for applying syntax-validated transformations to semantic program elements. Evaluated on SWE-Bench Verified across six LLMs, CODESTRUCT improves Pass@1 accuracy by 1.2-5.0% while reducing token consumption by 12-38% for most models. Models that frequently fail to produce valid patches under text-based interfaces benefit most: GPT-5-nano improves by 20.8% as empty-patch failures drop from 46.6% to 7.2%. On CodeAssistBench, we observe consistent accuracy gains (+0.8-4.4%) with cost reductions up to 33%. Our results show that structure-aware interfaces offer a more reliable foundation for code agents.

AI.tldr: CODESTRUCT reframes code editing for LLM agents from raw text manipulation to AST-structured operations, improving reliability, accuracy, and efficiency on coding benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based code agents treat codebases as plain text and modify them via string matching and patching. This is brittle: small formatting changes, ambiguous patterns, or imprecise matches cause edits to fail or introduce errors. The authors aim to create a more robust and semantically grounded way for LLMs to inspect and modify code, reducing invalid patches and improving task success rates while also lowering token usage.
AI.method: They introduce CODESTRUCT, an interface that exposes the codebase as a structured action space built on the abstract syntax tree (AST). Instead of addressing raw text ranges, the agent interacts with named syntactic and semantic entities. Two main primitives are provided: (1) readCode, which returns entire syntactic units (e.g., functions, classes) for inspection; and (2) editCode, which enforces syntax-validated transformations on specific program elements. They integrate this with multiple LLMs and keep the core reasoning in the LLM while constraining how it reads and writes code via this structured API.
AI.result: On the SWE-Bench Verified benchmark across six different LLMs, CODESTRUCT yields Pass@1 accuracy improvements of 1.2–5.0% while typically reducing token consumption by 12–38%. For models that previously struggled to generate valid patches via text-based interfaces, the gains are larger: e.g., GPT-5-nano’s accuracy improves by 20.8% as its empty-patch failure rate falls from 46.6% to 7.2%. On the CodeAssistBench benchmark, they also observe consistent accuracy gains of 0.8–4.4% and cost reductions of up to 33%.
AI.conclusion: Treating repositories as structured AST-based action spaces rather than raw text leads to more reliable and efficient LLM code agents. Syntax-aware read and edit operations reduce invalid or empty patches, improve benchmark accuracy, and cut token usage, suggesting that future code assistants should be built around structure-aware interfaces rather than purely text-based editing.

title: FastDiSS: Few-step Match Many-step Diffusion Language Model on Sequence-to-Sequence Generation--Full Version
comment: camera-ready version, accepted by ACL Findings (ACL 2026)
summary: Self-conditioning has been central to the success of continuous diffusion language models, as it allows models to correct previous errors. Yet its ability degrades precisely in the regime where diffusion is most attractive for deployment: few-step sampling for fast inference. In this study, we show that when models only have a few denoising steps, inaccurate self-conditioning induces a substantial approximation gap; this mistake compounds across denoising steps and ultimately dominate the sample quality. To address this, we propose a novel training framework that handles these errors during learning by perturbing the self-conditioning signal to match inference noise, improving robustness to prior estimation errors. In addition, we introduce a token-level noise-awareness mechanism that prevents training from saturation, hence improving optimization. Extensive experiments across conditional generation benchmarks demonstrate that our framework surpasses standard continuous diffusion models while providing up to 400x faster inference speed, and remains competitive against other one-step diffusion frameworks.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a key weakness of continuous diffusion language models in the few-step regime and proposes a training framework that perturbs self-conditioning signals and adds token-level noise-awareness, significantly improving generation quality and speed.
AI.motivation: Continuous diffusion language models rely heavily on self-conditioning to refine predictions over multiple denoising steps, but this mechanism becomes unreliable when using only a few steps—a regime that is critical for practical, fast inference. Existing approaches suffer from compounding errors in self-conditioning that create an approximation gap and hurt sample quality, especially when aiming for low-step or one-step diffusion. The authors aim to make diffusion-based language models viable and competitive in this fast-inference setting.
AI.method: The authors analyze how self-conditioning errors accumulate in few-step diffusion sampling and then design a novel training framework with two main components: (1) they explicitly perturb the self-conditioning signal during training to mimic the noisy and inaccurate conditions observed at inference time, making the model robust to prior estimation errors; and (2) they introduce a token-level noise-awareness mechanism that allows the model to sense and adapt to the noise level per token, preventing optimization saturation and improving learning efficiency. Training is thus aligned with the inference regime that uses a small number of denoising steps.
AI.result: On a range of conditional text generation benchmarks, the proposed framework consistently outperforms standard continuous diffusion language models in the few-step and one-step regimes, delivering better sample quality while achieving up to 400x faster inference speed compared to multi-step diffusion baselines. Performance is also competitive with other state-of-the-art one-step diffusion approaches, indicating that the method effectively mitigates the approximation gap caused by self-conditioning errors.
AI.conclusion: The study shows that naïve self-conditioning becomes a major bottleneck for few-step diffusion language models due to compounding approximation errors. By perturbing self-conditioning during training and incorporating token-level noise-awareness, the proposed framework makes diffusion-based language models robust and efficient in low-step regimes, enabling high-quality, fast generation that is both superior to standard continuous diffusion models and competitive with specialized one-step methods.

title: Multi-Agent Pathfinding with Non-Unit Integer Edge Costs via Enhanced Conflict-Based Search and Graph Discretization
comment: 16 pages, 7 figures, submitted to cs.AI, Multi-Agent Systems, Pathfinding Optimization
summary: Multi-Agent Pathfinding (MAPF) plays a critical role in various domains. Traditional MAPF methods typically assume unit edge costs and single-timestep actions, which limit their applicability to real-world scenarios. MAPFR extends MAPF to handle non-unit costs with real-valued edge costs and continuous-time actions, but its geometric collision model leads to an unbounded state space that compromises solver efficiency. In this paper, we propose MAPFZ, a novel MAPF variant on graphs with non-unit integer costs that preserves a finite state space while offering improved realism over classical MAPF. To solve MAPFZ efficiently, we develop CBS-NIC, an enhanced Conflict-Based Search framework incorporating time-interval-based conflict detection and an improved Safe Interval Path Planning (SIPP) algorithm. Additionally, we propose Bayesian Optimization for Graph Design (BOGD), a discretization method for non-unit edge costs that balances efficiency and accuracy with a sub-linear regret bound. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art methods in runtime and success rate across diverse benchmark scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MAPFZ, a multi-agent pathfinding variant with non-unit integer edge costs that keeps the state space finite while better modeling real-world timing, and proposes new algorithms (CBS-NIC, improved SIPP, and BOGD discretization) that significantly improve efficiency and success over prior methods.
AI.motivation: Classical Multi-Agent Pathfinding (MAPF) assumes unit edge costs and discrete, single-timestep moves, which is unrealistic for many practical applications where actions have different durations. MAPFR allows real-valued costs and continuous time but results in an unbounded state space and inefficient solving due to its geometric collision model. There is a need for a model that better captures non-uniform action durations while maintaining a finite, tractable state space and efficient solvers.
AI.method: The authors define MAPFZ, a new MAPF formulation on graphs with non-unit integer edge costs that retains a finite state space. To solve it, they develop CBS-NIC, a variant of Conflict-Based Search that uses time-interval-based conflict detection tailored to non-unit costs and an improved Safe Interval Path Planning (SIPP) procedure. In addition, they introduce Bayesian Optimization for Graph Design (BOGD), which discretizes general non-unit edge costs into an integer-cost representation, balancing computational efficiency and solution accuracy, and they analyze it with a sub-linear regret bound.
AI.result: Through extensive experiments on diverse benchmarks, the proposed MAPFZ model solved with CBS-NIC and BOGD achieves better runtime and higher success rates compared to existing state-of-the-art MAPF and MAPFR approaches, showing that the finite-state, integer-cost abstraction and the new algorithms scale more effectively while preserving good solution quality.
AI.conclusion: MAPFZ offers a practical compromise between classical MAPF and fully continuous-time MAPFR, capturing more realistic timing via non-unit integer costs while keeping the state space finite. The CBS-NIC framework with improved SIPP and the BOGD discretization method together provide an efficient and theoretically grounded approach that empirically outperforms prior methods, suggesting that integer-cost formulations plus principled discretization are a powerful direction for real-world multi-agent pathfinding.

title: Context-Agent: Dynamic Discourse Trees for Non-Linear Dialogue
summary: Large Language Models demonstrate outstanding performance in many language tasks but still face fundamental challenges in managing the non-linear flow of human conversation. The prevalent approach of treating dialogue history as a flat, linear sequence is misaligned with the intrinsically hierarchical and branching structure of natural discourse, leading to inefficient context utilization and a loss of coherence during extended interactions involving topic shifts or instruction refinements. To address this limitation, we introduce Context-Agent, a novel framework that models multi-turn dialogue history as a dynamic tree structure. This approach mirrors the inherent non-linearity of conversation, enabling the model to maintain and navigate multiple dialogue branches corresponding to different topics. Furthermore, to facilitate robust evaluation, we introduce the Non-linear Task Multi-turn Dialogue (NTM) benchmark, specifically designed to assess model performance in long-horizon, non-linear scenarios. Our experiments demonstrate that Context-Agent enhances task completion rates and improves token efficiency across various LLMs, underscoring the value of structured context management for complex, dynamic dialogues. The dataset and code is available at GitHub.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Context-Agent, a tree-structured context management framework and a new benchmark (NTM) to better handle non-linear, multi-turn dialogues with large language models, improving task completion and token efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing large language model dialogue systems treat conversation history as a flat, linear sequence, which clashes with the naturally hierarchical and branching structure of human dialogue. This mismatch leads to poor handling of topic shifts, instruction refinements, and long-horizon conversations, causing inefficiencies and reduced coherence. There is also a lack of targeted benchmarks to systematically evaluate non-linear, multi-turn dialogue capabilities.
AI.method: The authors introduce Context-Agent, a framework that represents multi-turn dialogue history as a dynamic tree. Instead of appending all turns to a single linear history, the system maintains multiple branches, each corresponding to different topics or interaction paths. The framework includes mechanisms for building, updating, and navigating this tree so the model can selectively retrieve and condition on relevant branches. Additionally, they design the Non-linear Task Multi-turn Dialogue (NTM) benchmark, which consists of long-horizon, non-linear dialogue tasks that force models to manage branching conversations and revisiting topics over time.
AI.result: Experiments on various large language models show that using Context-Agent improves task completion rates in non-linear, multi-turn dialogues and reduces the number of tokens needed (i.e., improves token efficiency) compared to conventional linear-context baselines. These results indicate that structured, tree-based context management leads to more effective use of context in complex, dynamic interactions.
AI.conclusion: Modeling dialogue history as a dynamic tree better captures the inherently non-linear nature of human conversation than traditional linear histories. The proposed Context-Agent framework, along with the NTM benchmark, demonstrates that structured context management can significantly enhance large language models’ performance and efficiency in long, branching dialogues. The public release of dataset and code facilitates further research in non-linear dialogue management.

title: EpiBench: Benchmarking Multi-turn Research Workflows for Multimodal Agents
summary: Scientific research follows multi-turn, multi-step workflows that require proactively searching the literature, consulting figures and tables, and integrating evidence across papers to align experimental settings and support reproducible conclusions. This joint capability is not systematically assessed in existing benchmarks, which largely under-evaluate proactive search, multi-evidence integration and sustained evidence use over time. In this work, we introduce EpiBench, an episodic multi-turn multimodal benchmark that instantiates short research workflows. Given a research task, agents must navigate across papers over multiple turns, align evidence from figures and tables, and use the accumulated evidence in the memory to answer objective questions that require cross paper comparisons and multi-figure integration. EpiBench introduces a process-level evaluation framework for fine-grained testing and diagnosis of research agents. Our experiments show that even the leading model achieves an accuracy of only 29.23% on the hard split, indicating substantial room for improvement in multi-turn, multi-evidence research workflows, providing an evaluation platform for verifiable and reproducible research agents.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EpiBench, a benchmark to evaluate AI agents on realistic, multi-turn, multimodal scientific research workflows that require proactive literature search and integration of evidence from multiple papers and figures.
AI.motivation: Current benchmarks for scientific and research assistants mainly test single-turn or simplified tasks, and they under-evaluate three key capabilities needed for realistic scientific work: (1) proactively searching and navigating the literature over time, (2) integrating multiple pieces of evidence across papers, figures, and tables, and (3) maintaining and using accumulated evidence across a multi-step workflow. There is a lack of systematic, process-level evaluation of agents acting as research assistants, especially in multimodal (text+figures+tables) and episodic, multi-turn settings that resemble real scientific practice. This gap motivates the creation of a new benchmark to stress-test these capabilities and provide clearer diagnosis of where current models fail.
AI.method: The authors design EpiBench as an episodic, multi-turn, multimodal benchmark that simulates short scientific research workflows. For each research task (episode), an agent must: (1) navigate across multiple scientific papers over several interaction turns, (2) inspect and align evidence from textual content, figures, and tables, and (3) accumulate this evidence in memory to answer objective questions. These questions require cross-paper comparisons and integration of information from multiple figures/tables, rather than simple single-snippet extraction. The benchmark also introduces a process-level evaluation framework, allowing fine-grained analysis of agent behaviors and failure modes at each step of the research workflow, not just final-answer accuracy. The authors then evaluate existing leading models on EpiBench, including a ‘hard’ split designed to be especially challenging.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows that even the strongest existing model achieves only 29.23% accuracy on the hard split of EpiBench. This low performance indicates that current models are far from reliably handling multi-turn, multi-evidence, multimodal research workflows. The process-level evaluation further highlights weaknesses in proactive search, evidence integration across papers and modalities, and sustained use of information over multiple turns.
AI.conclusion: EpiBench provides a challenging and structured benchmark for evaluating research agents on realistic, episodic, multi-turn, multimodal scientific tasks. The poor performance of current leading models demonstrates significant headroom for improvement in areas such as literature navigation, multimodal evidence integration, and long-horizon reasoning over accumulated evidence. The benchmark offers a process-level evaluation framework that can help guide the development and diagnosis of more verifiable, reproducible, and capable AI research assistants.

title: Automated Auditing of Hospital Discharge Summaries for Care Transitions
comment: Accepted as a poster at IEEE-ICHI 2026; 3 pages, 2 figure
summary: Incomplete or inconsistent discharge documentation is a primary driver of care fragmentation and avoidable readmissions. Despite its critical role in patient safety, auditing discharge summaries relies heavily on manual review and is difficult to scale. We propose an automated framework for large-scale auditing of discharge summaries using locally deployed Large Language Models (LLMs). Our approach operationalizes core transition-of-care requirements such as follow-up instructions, medication history and changes, patient information and clinical course, etc. into a structured validation checklist of questions based on DISCHARGED framework. Using adult inpatient summaries from the MIMIC-IV database, we utilize a privacy-preserving LLM to identify the presence, absence, or ambiguity of key documentation elements. This work demonstrates the feasibility of scalable, automated clinical auditing and provides a foundation for systematic quality improvement in electronic health record documentation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an automated, LLM-based framework to audit hospital discharge summaries at scale, checking for required transition-of-care elements to improve documentation quality and patient safety.
AI.motivation: Discharge summaries are often incomplete or inconsistent, which drives care fragmentation and avoidable hospital readmissions. Current auditing of discharge documentation is largely manual, labor-intensive, and not scalable, limiting systematic quality improvement and making it hard to ensure patient safety and continuity of care.
AI.method: The authors design an automated auditing framework using locally deployed, privacy-preserving large language models (LLMs). They translate core transition-of-care requirements—such as follow-up instructions, medication history and changes, patient demographics, and clinical course—into a structured validation checklist derived from the DISCHARGED framework. Using adult inpatient discharge summaries from the MIMIC-IV database, the LLM is prompted to classify whether each key documentation element is present, absent, or ambiguously documented in a given summary.
AI.result: The framework successfully uses LLMs to detect the presence, absence, or ambiguity of critical documentation elements in discharge summaries drawn from the MIMIC-IV dataset, showing that automated, large-scale auditing of transition-of-care documentation is technically feasible while preserving data privacy. Although specific quantitative metrics are not provided in the abstract, the results support that LLM-based auditing can reliably operationalize the DISCHARGED checklist in routine clinical documentation.
AI.conclusion: Automated auditing of discharge summaries with locally deployed LLMs is feasible and scalable, enabling systematic, privacy-preserving quality assessment of transition-of-care documentation. This approach lays the groundwork for continuous quality improvement in electronic health record documentation and may help reduce care fragmentation and preventable readmissions by ensuring key discharge elements are consistently and clearly documented.

title: THIVLVC: Retrieval Augmented Dependency Parsing for Latin
summary: We describe THIVLVC, a two-stage system for the EvaLatin 2026 Dependency Parsing task. Given a Latin sentence, we retrieve structurally similar entries from the CIRCSE treebank using sentence length and POS n-gram similarity, then prompt a large language model to refine the baseline parse from UDPipe using the retrieved examples and UD annotation guidelines. We submit two configurations: one without retrieval and one with retrieval (RAG). On poetry (Seneca), THIVLVC improves CLAS by +17 points over the UDPipe baseline; on prose (Thomas Aquinas), the gain is +1.5 CLAS. A double-blind error analysis of 300 divergences between our system and the gold standard reveals that, among unanimous annotator decisions, 53.3% favour THIVLVC, showing annotation inconsistencies both within and across treebanks.

AI.tldr: THIVLVC is a two-stage system that refines UDPipe Latin dependency parses using an LLM, with and without retrieval of similar treebank examples, achieving substantial gains on poetry and modest gains on prose while exposing annotation inconsistencies.
AI.motivation: Improve Latin dependency parsing performance, especially on challenging genres like poetry, by leveraging large language models and example-based refinement, and to investigate annotation consistency in existing treebanks.
AI.method: First, given a Latin sentence, retrieve structurally similar sentences from the CIRCSE treebank based on sentence length and POS n-gram similarity. Then prompt a large language model with: (a) the baseline UDPipe parse, (b) the retrieved examples when using RAG, and (c) UD annotation guidelines, asking it to produce an improved dependency parse. Two configurations are evaluated: with retrieval (RAG) and without retrieval.
AI.result: On the EvaLatin 2026 Dependency Parsing task, the THIVLVC system yields a +17 CLAS improvement over the UDPipe baseline on poetic text (Seneca) and a +1.5 CLAS improvement on prose (Thomas Aquinas). An error analysis of 300 disagreements between THIVLVC and the gold standard shows that, in cases where human annotators agreed, 53.3% of decisions favoured THIVLVC over the gold annotations.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based refinement of baseline parses, optionally supported by retrieval of similar annotated examples, can significantly improve Latin dependency parsing, particularly for poetry. The system’s frequent superiority over the gold standard in manual evaluation also highlights notable annotation inconsistencies within and between Latin treebanks, suggesting that current benchmarks may underrepresent true model performance.

title: Adaptive Serverless Resource Management via Slot-Survival Prediction and Event-Driven Lifecycle Control
summary: Serverless computing eliminates infrastructure management overhead but introduces significant challenges regarding cold start latency and resource utilization. Traditional static resource allocation often leads to inefficiencies under variable workloads, resulting in performance degradation or excessive costs. This paper presents an adaptive engineering framework that optimizes serverless performance through event-driven architecture and probabilistic modeling. We propose a dual-strategy mechanism that dynamically adjusts idle durations and employs an intelligent request waiting strategy based on slot survival predictions. By leveraging sliding window aggregation and asynchronous processing, our system proactively manages resource lifecycles. Experimental results show that our approach reduces cold starts by up to 51.2% and improves cost-efficiency by nearly 2x compared to baseline methods in multi-cloud environments.

AI.tldr: Adaptive framework to reduce cold starts and improve cost-efficiency in serverless computing using event-driven design and probabilistic modeling.
AI.motivation: Serverless platforms suffer from cold start latency and poor resource utilization when using static resource allocation, especially under variable workloads, causing performance degradation or higher costs.
AI.method: Introduce an adaptive engineering framework with an event-driven architecture and probabilistic modeling. Use a dual-strategy mechanism that dynamically adjusts idle durations of serverless slots and applies an intelligent request waiting strategy based on predictions of slot survival. Implement sliding window aggregation and asynchronous processing to proactively manage resource lifecycles in multi-cloud settings.
AI.result: Experiments in multi-cloud environments indicate that the proposed system reduces cold starts by up to 51.2% and nearly doubles cost-efficiency compared to baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: The adaptive, probabilistic, event-driven framework effectively mitigates cold starts and enhances cost-efficiency for serverless workloads, outperforming traditional static allocation approaches in dynamic, multi-cloud environments.

title: YoNER: A New Yorùbá Multi-domain Named Entity Recognition Dataset
summary: Named Entity Recognition (NER) is a foundational NLP task, yet research in Yorùbá has been constrained by limited and domain-specific resources. Existing resources, such as MasakhaNER (a manually annotated news-domain corpus) and WikiAnn (automatically created from Wikipedia), are valuable but restricted in domain coverage. To address this gap, we present YoNER, a new multidomain Yorùbá NER dataset that extends entity coverage beyond news and Wikipedia. The dataset comprises about 5,000 sentences and 100,000 tokens collected from five domains including Bible, Blogs, Movies, Radio broadcast and Wikipedia, and annotated with three entity types: Person (PER), Organization (ORG) and Location (LOC), following CoNLL-style guidelines. Annotation was conducted manually by three native Yorùbá speakers, with an inter-annotator agreement of over 0.70, ensuring high quality and consistency. We benchmark several transformer encoder models using cross-domain experiments with MasakhaNER 2.0, and we also assess the effect of few-shot in-domain data using YoNER and cross-lingual setups with English datasets. Our results show that African-centric models outperform general multilingual models for Yorùbá, but cross-domain performance drops substantially, particularly for blogs and movie domains. Furthermore, we observed that closely related formal domains, such as news and Wikipedia, transfer more effectively. In addition, we introduce a new Yorùbá-specific language model (OyoBERT) that outperforms multilingual models in in-domain evaluation. We publicly release the YoNER dataset and pretrained OyoBERT models to support future research on Yorùbá natural language processing.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces YoNER, a new multi-domain Yorùbá NER dataset plus a Yorùbá-specific language model (OyoBERT), and shows African-centric and Yorùbá-specific models work best while cross-domain transfer remains challenging.
AI.motivation: Yorùbá NER research has been limited because existing datasets (e.g., MasakhaNER and WikiAnn) are small, manually expensive, and limited to specific domains like news or Wikipedia. This hampers building robust NER systems that generalize to real-world Yorùbá text from diverse sources such as blogs, movies, or radio transcripts. The authors want to close this gap by creating a higher-quality, broader-coverage NER resource, and by investigating which models and transfer setups work best for Yorùbá.
AI.method: The authors construct YoNER, a Yorùbá NER corpus of ~5,000 sentences and 100k tokens gathered from five different domains: Bible, blogs, movie subtitles/scripts, radio broadcasts, and Wikipedia. They annotate tokens with three standard entity types (PER, ORG, LOC) according to CoNLL-style NER guidelines. Three native Yorùbá speakers manually annotate the data, and inter-annotator agreement above 0.70 confirms reliability. For experiments, they benchmark multiple transformer encoder models, contrasting African-centric pretrained models against general multilingual models. They run cross-domain evaluations jointly with MasakhaNER 2.0 to study domain transfer, run few-shot in-domain experiments using small labeled samples from YoNER, and explore cross-lingual transfer by leveraging English NER datasets. Finally, they pretrain and evaluate a Yorùbá-specific BERT-style language model, OyoBERT, on Yorùbá text and test it on the YoNER benchmarks.
AI.result: African-centric transformer models consistently outperform general multilingual models on Yorùbá NER tasks. However, models experience a sharp drop in performance when applied cross-domain, especially for less formal and more colloquial domains such as blogs and movies. More similar and formal domains (like news and Wikipedia) exhibit better transfer, indicating that domain similarity is a key factor. The newly introduced Yorùbá-specific model, OyoBERT, achieves the best in-domain NER performance, surpassing multilingual baselines on YoNER. Few-shot in-domain data and cross-lingual transfer provide some gains, but do not fully close the gap in challenging domains.
AI.conclusion: YoNER fills a major resource gap by providing a relatively large, high-quality, multi-domain Yorùbá NER dataset, and OyoBERT offers a strong Yorùbá-specific language model for NER. The study demonstrates that domain and language specialization—via African-centric and language-specific models—significantly improves Yorùbá NER, while also revealing that cross-domain robustness is still a key challenge, particularly for informal text types. The public release of YoNER and OyoBERT is expected to catalyze further work on Yorùbá and related low-resource African languages, especially on domain adaptation and robust NER in diverse real-world contexts.

title: OntoTKGE: Ontology-Enhanced Temporal Knowledge Graph Extrapolation
summary: Temporal knowledge graph (TKG) extrapolation is an important task that aims to predict future facts through historical interaction information within KG snapshots. A key challenge for most existing TKG extrapolation models is handling entities with sparse historical interaction. The ontological knowledge is beneficial for alleviating this sparsity issue by enabling these entities to inherit behavioral patterns from other entities with the same concept, which is ignored by previous studies. In this paper, we propose a novel encoder-decoder framework OntoTKGE that leverages the ontological knowledge from the ontology-view KG (i.e., a KG modeling hierarchical relations among abstract concepts as well as the connections between concepts and entities) to guide the TKG extrapolation model's learning process through the effective integration of the ontological and temporal knowledge, thereby enhancing entity embeddings. OntoTKGE is flexible enough to adapt to many TKG extrapolation models. Extensive experiments on four data sets demonstrate that OntoTKGE not only significantly improves the performance of many TKG extrapolation models but also surpasses many SOTA baseline methods.

AI.tldr: OntoTKGE is an encoder–decoder framework that injects ontological knowledge into temporal knowledge graph extrapolation models to improve prediction of future facts, especially for sparsely observed entities.
AI.motivation: Temporal knowledge graph extrapolation models struggle when many entities have few historical interactions, leading to poor embeddings and inaccurate prediction of future facts. Existing work largely ignores ontological knowledge (concept hierarchies and entity–concept links), which could allow sparse entities to share behavioral patterns with other entities under the same concepts. The paper is motivated by the need to use this ontological structure to alleviate sparsity and improve temporal prediction.
AI.method: The authors propose OntoTKGE, an encoder–decoder framework that jointly leverages an ontology-view KG and a temporal KG. The ontology-view KG encodes hierarchical relations among abstract concepts and links between concepts and entities. OntoTKGE integrates ontological and temporal information so that entities can inherit behavioral patterns from concept-level structures, enhancing their embeddings. The framework is designed to be model-agnostic: it can be plugged into many existing TKG extrapolation models to guide their learning process via ontology-informed encoding and decoding.
AI.result: Across four benchmark datasets, incorporating OntoTKGE into various TKG extrapolation models leads to substantial performance gains compared with the base models alone. Moreover, OntoTKGE-augmented models outperform many state-of-the-art baselines in TKG extrapolation tasks, especially in scenarios with sparse entity histories.
AI.conclusion: Integrating ontological knowledge with temporal knowledge graphs via the OntoTKGE encoder–decoder framework effectively mitigates sparsity issues in temporal KG extrapolation. By allowing entities to share concept-level behavioral patterns, OntoTKGE improves entity embeddings and yields better future fact prediction, while remaining flexible enough to adapt to multiple backbone TKG extrapolation models.

title: Graph-Based Chain-of-Thought Pruning for Reducing Redundant Reflections in Reasoning LLMs
summary: Extending CoT through RL has been widely used to enhance the reasoning capabilities of LLMs. However, due to the sparsity of reward signals, it can also induce undesirable thinking patterns such as overthinking, i.e., generating redundant intermediate reasoning content. In this work, we argue that a major source of such redundancy is inefficient reflection, which often manifests in two problematic patterns: Indiscriminate Reflection, where the model performs broad, low-impact checks throughout reasoning, and Repetitive Reflection, where it repeatedly re-verifies an already established conclusion. To address this, we introduce a graph-based CoT optimization framework. Specifically, we convert each linear CoT into a directed acyclic graph (DAG) with explicit dependency edges, and design a dual pruning strategy: branch-level pruning removes weakly contributing reflection branches, while depth-level pruning eliminates late-stage re-verification. We distill this behavior via a three-stage pipeline: (1) SFT to initialize the policy on pruned concise traces, (2) DPO to prefer correct but less redundant trajectories, and (3) GRPO with length penalty to jointly optimize answer correctness and efficiency. Experiments show that our approach reduces the average reasoning tokens by 42\% while maintaining or improving accuracy.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a graph-based framework to prune redundant reasoning steps in chain-of-thought (CoT) training with reinforcement learning, cutting reasoning length by about 42% while preserving or improving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning extensions of chain-of-thought often suffer from reward sparsity, which unintentionally encourages large language models to overthink—producing long, redundant reasoning traces. Much of this redundancy comes from inefficient reflection, such as checking everything indiscriminately or repeatedly re-verifying conclusions, which wastes tokens and computation without improving correctness. The paper aims to systematically reduce this inefficiency while keeping or improving answer quality.
AI.method: The authors model each linear CoT as a directed acyclic graph (DAG) that makes reasoning dependencies explicit. On this graph they apply a dual pruning strategy: (1) branch-level pruning, which removes reflection branches that weakly contribute to the final answer, and (2) depth-level pruning, which cuts off late-stage re-verification steps that do not change the conclusion. They then distill these concise reasoning patterns into the model via a three-stage pipeline: (i) supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on pruned, concise CoT traces to initialize the policy; (ii) direct preference optimization (DPO) to train the model to prefer correct but less redundant trajectories; and (iii) GRPO (a form of RL) with an explicit length penalty to jointly optimize accuracy and efficiency.
AI.result: Across evaluated benchmarks, the proposed framework reduces the average number of reasoning tokens by about 42% while maintaining, and in some cases improving, final answer accuracy compared to standard CoT+RL baselines.
AI.conclusion: By representing CoT as a dependency DAG and pruning inefficient reflection both across branches and in depth, then distilling these patterns with a staged SFT–DPO–GRPO pipeline, the authors can significantly compress reasoning traces without sacrificing correctness. This suggests that overthinking in RL-extended CoT largely stems from identifiable, prunable reflection patterns and that structured optimization of reasoning graphs is an effective way to train more efficient reasoning LLMs.

title: Can We Trust a Black-box LLM? LLM Untrustworthy Boundary Detection via Bias-Diffusion and Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown a high capability in answering questions on a diverse range of topics. However, these models sometimes produce biased, ideologized or incorrect responses, limiting their applications if there is no clear understanding of which topics their answers can be trusted. In this research, we introduce a novel algorithm, named as GMRL-BD, designed to identify the untrustworthy boundaries (in terms of topics) of a given LLM, with black-box access to the LLM and under specific query constraints. Based on a general Knowledge Graph (KG) derived from Wikipedia, our algorithm incorporates with multiple reinforcement learning agents to efficiently identify topics (some nodes in KG) where the LLM is likely to generate biased answers. Our experiments demonstrated the efficiency of our algorithm, which can detect the untrustworthy boundary with just limited queries to the LLM. Additionally, we have released a new dataset containing popular LLMs including Llama2, Vicuna, Falcon, Qwen2, Gemma2 and Yi-1.5, along with labels indicating the topics on which each LLM is likely to be biased.

AI.tldr: Proposes GMRL-BD, a reinforcement-learning-based black-box method to find topic regions where an LLM is likely to give biased answers, using a Wikipedia-derived knowledge graph, and releases a benchmark dataset of topic-level bias labels for several popular LLMs.
AI.motivation: LLMs can answer many questions but may output biased, ideologically skewed, or incorrect content on some topics. Users and developers lack clarity on which topical areas are trustworthy, limiting safe deployment and evaluation. Existing bias-detection often assumes white-box access, dense querying, or coarse-grained topic treatment. A method is needed to systematically and efficiently map out the topical boundaries where an LLM becomes unreliable, under realistic query constraints and without internal model access.
AI.method: They build a general-purpose knowledge graph from Wikipedia, whose nodes represent topics. Treating the LLM as a black box with limited query budget, they design GMRL-BD, an algorithm with multiple reinforcement learning agents that navigate the graph to locate nodes (topics) where the LLM is likely biased. The agents use feedback signals derived from LLM responses to guide exploration, learning to focus queries on regions of the graph that indicate higher bias likelihood, thereby discovering the topical boundary between trustworthy and untrustworthy areas with minimal queries.
AI.result: Experiments show that GMRL-BD can efficiently detect untrustworthy topical boundaries with relatively few queries to the target LLM, outperforming naive or baseline exploration strategies in terms of query efficiency and coverage of biased topics. They apply the method to several popular LLMs—Llama2, Vicuna, Falcon, Qwen2, Gemma2, and Yi-1.5—and successfully identify many topics for each model where responses are likely biased. They compile these findings into a labeled dataset mapping topics to bias likelihood for each model.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that it is feasible to systematically chart the topical reliability boundaries of LLMs using only black-box access and a limited query budget. GMRL-BD efficiently discovers topics prone to biased answers and provides a scalable way to profile different models. The released dataset offers a resource for benchmarking, auditing, and improving LLMs with respect to topical bias, and the framework can be extended to other reliability dimensions or future models.

title: See the Forest for the Trees: Loosely Speculative Decoding via Visual-Semantic Guidance for Efficient Inference of Video LLMs
summary: Video Large Language Models (Video-LLMs) excel in video understanding but suffer from high inference latency during autoregressive generation. Speculative Decoding (SD) mitigates this by applying a draft-and-verify paradigm, yet existing methods are constrained by rigid exact-match rules, severely limiting the acceleration potential. To bridge this gap, we propose LVSpec, the first training-free loosely SD framework tailored for Video-LLMs. Grounded in the insight that generation is governed by sparse visual-relevant anchors (mandating strictness) amidst abundant visual-irrelevant fillers (permitting loose verification), LVSpec employs a lightweight visual-relevant token identification scheme to accurately pinpoint the former. To further maximize acceptance, we augment this with a position-shift tolerant mechanism that effectively salvages positionally mismatched but semantically equivalent tokens. Experiments demonstrate that LVSpec achieves high fidelity and speed: it preserves >99.8 of target performance while accelerating Qwen2.5-VL-32B by 2.70x and LLaVA-OneVision-72B by 2.94x. Notably, it boosts the mean accepted length and speedup ratio by 136% and 35% compared to SOTA training-free SD methods for Video-LLMs.

AI.tldr: LVSpec is a training-free, loosely speculative decoding framework for Video-LLMs that accelerates generation by distinguishing strict visual-relevant tokens from flexible filler tokens and allowing position-tolerant verification, achieving ~3x speedup with almost no performance loss.
AI.motivation: Video-LLMs are powerful but slow at inference due to autoregressive decoding. Existing speculative decoding methods can speed things up but rely on rigid exact-match verification between draft and target tokens, which greatly limits how many drafted tokens can be accepted, especially in video settings where outputs mix crucial visual content with less-critical filler text. A more flexible, Video-LLM-aware speculative decoding strategy is needed to unlock higher acceleration without hurting accuracy.
