AI.method: LVSpec introduces a training-free, loosely constrained speculative decoding framework specialized for Video-LLMs. It first identifies visual-relevant tokens in the generated sequence using a lightweight scheme, treating these as critical anchors that still require strict verification. Remaining tokens are considered visual-irrelevant fillers and are verified more loosely. Additionally, a position-shift tolerant mechanism is applied so that tokens that are semantically equivalent but appear in slightly different positions can still be accepted, rather than rejected by exact positional matching. This combination maximizes the number of drafted tokens that pass verification without retraining the underlying Video-LLM.
AI.result: On two strong Video-LLMs, Qwen2.5-VL-32B and LLaVA-OneVision-72B, LVSpec preserves more than 99.8% of the original model performance while substantially reducing inference latency, achieving 2.70x and 2.94x speedup respectively. Compared to the state-of-the-art training-free speculative decoding baselines for Video-LLMs, LVSpec increases the mean accepted token length by 136% and the overall speedup ratio by 35%.
AI.conclusion: LVSpec demonstrates that speculative decoding for Video-LLMs can be made both faster and highly faithful by exploiting the structural difference between visual-relevant anchors and filler tokens and by relaxing rigid positional constraints. Its training-free design, large speedups, and minimal performance degradation suggest it is a practical drop-in solution for accelerating existing Video-LLMs and that loosely verified, position-tolerant speculative decoding is a promising direction for future multimodal generation systems.

title: Auditable Agents
summary:
LLM agents call tools, query databases, delegate tasks, and trigger external side effects. Once an agent system can act in the world, the question is no longer only whether harmful actions can be prevented--it is whether those actions remain answerable after deployment. We distinguish accountability (the ability to determine compliance and assign responsibility), auditability (the system property that makes accountability possible), and auditing (the process of reconstructing behavior from trustworthy evidence). Our claim is direct: no agent system can be accountable without auditability.
  To make this operational, we define five dimensions of agent auditability, i.e., action recoverability, lifecycle coverage, policy checkability, responsibility attribution, and evidence integrity, and identify three mechanism classes (detect, enforce, recover) whose temporal information-and-intervention constraints explain why, in practice, no single approach suffices. We support the position with layered evidence rather than a single benchmark: lower-bound ecosystem measurements suggest that even basic security prerequisites for auditability are widely unmet (617 security findings across six prominent open-source projects); runtime feasibility results show that pre-execution mediation with tamper-evident records adds only 8.3 ms median overhead; and controlled recovery experiments show that responsibility-relevant information can be partially recovered even when conventional logs are missing. We propose an Auditability Card for agent systems and identify six open research problems organized by mechanism class.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that LLM-based agent systems cannot be truly accountable unless they are explicitly designed to be auditable, and it defines concrete dimensions, mechanisms, evidence, and open problems around auditability for such agents.
AI.motivation: As LLM agents gain the power to act in the real world—calling tools, accessing databases, and triggering external side effects—risks shift from only preventing harmful actions to also ensuring that any actions taken can later be explained, scrutinized, and traced back to responsible parties. Existing work focuses heavily on safety and alignment during model design and deployment, but much less on whether post‑hoc investigation, compliance checking, and responsibility assignment are actually possible once agents are operating. There is a gap in conceptual clarity (e.g., conflating safety with accountability) and in practical mechanisms to reconstruct and evaluate an agent’s behavior after the fact. The authors aim to clarify key concepts (accountability, auditability, auditing), argue that accountability requires auditability, and provide a structured framework and empirical evidence to guide the design and assessment of auditable agent systems.
AI.method: The authors develop a conceptual and operational framework for auditability in LLM agent systems. First, they define and distinguish three core notions: accountability, auditability, and auditing. Then they introduce five dimensions of agent auditability—action recoverability, lifecycle coverage, policy checkability, responsibility attribution, and evidence integrity—to specify what an auditable system must support. They further characterize three classes of mechanisms (detect, enforce, recover) and analyze their temporal constraints on information availability and intervention, showing why no single mechanism class can achieve full auditability. Empirically, they gather layered evidence: (1) ecosystem measurements of six prominent open‑source agent projects to identify unmet security and auditability prerequisites, reporting 617 security findings; (2) a runtime feasibility study showing that pre‑execution mediation plus tamper‑evident logging introduces modest latency overhead (8.3 ms median); and (3) controlled recovery experiments demonstrating that some responsibility‑relevant information can be reconstructed even when standard logs are absent. Finally, they propose an “Auditability Card” as a practical documentation and evaluation tool for agent systems and outline open research directions structured by mechanism class.
AI.result: Conceptually, the paper delivers a clear argument that accountability in LLM agent systems is impossible without auditability and formally articulates what auditability entails via five dimensions. It shows that existing approaches that focus only on detection, enforcement, or post‑hoc recovery are each temporally constrained and therefore insufficient alone; practical systems need combined mechanisms. Empirically, their ecosystem analysis uncovers 617 security and auditability‑relevant issues across six widely used open‑source agent frameworks, indicating that current practice falls far short of even baseline requirements. Their runtime experiments demonstrate that integrating pre‑execution mediation and tamper‑evident logging is technically feasible with only an 8.3 ms median performance overhead, mitigating common concerns that strong audit mechanisms are too costly. Their recovery experiments further show that, even when traditional logs are missing, it is still possible to partially reconstruct responsibility‑relevant information, highlighting both the potential and the limits of post‑hoc reconstruction. Together, these results substantiate the claim that auditability is both necessary and practically attainable, while exposing concrete gaps in present‑day agent ecosystems.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that any LLM agent system capable of real‑world actions must be designed with auditability as a first‑class property, because accountability is unattainable without it. Auditability is multi‑dimensional—requiring reliable reconstruction of actions, coverage across the agent lifecycle, the ability to check policies, clear responsibility attribution, and tamper‑resistant evidence—and cannot be achieved by a single class of mechanisms. Instead, effective accountability will require a combination of detection, enforcement, and recovery techniques, each respecting their temporal limits on information and intervention. The authors’ empirical findings reveal that current open‑source agent frameworks lack many fundamental prerequisites for auditability, but also that robust, low‑overhead audit mechanisms are feasible. They advocate for tools like the proposed Auditability Card to standardize how systems disclose and evaluate their auditability properties and highlight six open research challenges aligned with the three mechanism classes. Overall, they argue that to responsibly deploy powerful agentic LLM systems, the research and practitioner community must treat auditability—not just safety or robustness—as a core design goal.

title: LLM Reasoning as Trajectories: Step-Specific Representation Geometry and Correctness Signals
summary: This work characterizes large language models' chain-of-thought generation as a structured trajectory through representation space. We show that mathematical reasoning traverses functionally ordered, step-specific subspaces that become increasingly separable with layer depth. This structure already exists in base models, while reasoning training primarily accelerates convergence toward termination-related subspaces rather than introducing new representational organization. While early reasoning steps follow similar trajectories, correct and incorrect solutions diverge systematically at late stages. This late-stage divergence enables mid-reasoning prediction of final-answer correctness with ROC-AUC up to 0.87. Furthermore, we introduce trajectory-based steering, an inference-time intervention framework that enables reasoning correction and length control based on derived ideal trajectories. Together, these results establish reasoning trajectories as a geometric lens for interpreting, predicting, and controlling LLM reasoning behavior.

AI.tldr: The paper studies chain-of-thought in large language models as geometric trajectories in representation space and uses this structure to interpret, predict, and steer reasoning.
AI.motivation: To better understand how large language models perform step-by-step reasoning and to gain tools for predicting and controlling their reasoning behavior, beyond just looking at input-output text.
AI.method: Analyze internal activations during chain-of-thought reasoning as trajectories through high-dimensional representation space; identify step-specific subspaces and their separability across layers; compare base vs reasoning-trained models; measure divergence between correct and incorrect solution trajectories; evaluate mid-reasoning correctness prediction (ROC-AUC); and propose trajectory-based steering interventions using idealized trajectories to correct or adjust reasoning length at inference time.
AI.result: Reasoning steps occupy ordered, step-specific subspaces that become more separable in deeper layers; this structure exists even in base models, and reasoning training mainly speeds convergence to termination-related subspaces; correct and incorrect trajectories are similar early but diverge reliably in later steps, enabling mid-reasoning prediction of final correctness with ROC-AUC up to 0.87; trajectory-based steering can modify reasoning behavior, including correcting reasoning paths and controlling chain length.
AI.conclusion: Chain-of-thought generation in LLMs can be viewed as a structured geometric trajectory in representation space. This perspective provides a powerful interpretability tool and enables practical applications like predicting whether a model’s final answer will be correct partway through reasoning and steering its reasoning during inference to improve reliability and control length.

title: SCMAPR: Self-Correcting Multi-Agent Prompt Refinement for Complex-Scenario Text-to-Video Generation
summary: Text-to-Video (T2V) generation has benefited from recent advances in diffusion models, yet current systems still struggle under complex scenarios, which are generally exacerbated by the ambiguity and underspecification of text prompts. In this work, we formulate complex-scenario prompt refinement as a stage-wise multi-agent refinement process and propose SCMAPR, i.e., a scenario-aware and Self-Correcting Multi-Agent Prompt Refinement framework for T2V prompting. SCMAPR coordinates specialized agents to (i) route each prompt to a taxonomy-grounded scenario for strategy selection, (ii) synthesize scenario-aware rewriting policies and perform policy-conditioned refinement, and (iii) conduct structured semantic verification that triggers conditional revision when violations are detected. To clarify what constitutes complex scenarios in T2V prompting, provide representative examples, and enable rigorous evaluation under such challenging conditions, we further introduce {T2V-Complexity}, which is a complex-scenario T2V benchmark consisting exclusively of complex-scenario prompts. Extensive experiments on 3 existing benchmarks and our T2V-Complexity benchmark demonstrate that SCMAPR consistently improves text-video alignment and overall generation quality under complex scenarios, achieving up to 2.67\% and 3.28 gains in average score on VBench and EvalCrafter, and up to 0.028 improvement on T2V-CompBench over 3 State-Of-The-Art baselines.

AI.tldr: They propose a multi-agent, self-correcting prompt refinement framework and a new benchmark to improve and evaluate text-to-video generation in complex scenarios.
AI.motivation: Existing text-to-video diffusion models perform poorly on complex scenarios, mainly because natural language prompts are often ambiguous, underspecified, or structurally complicated. There is no clear definition or benchmark focusing specifically on such complex scenarios, making it hard to systematically improve or evaluate T2V models in these conditions.
AI.method: They introduce SCMAPR, a scenario-aware, self-correcting multi-agent prompt refinement framework. First, an agent routes each input prompt to a taxonomy-defined scenario to choose an appropriate refinement strategy. Second, specialized agents synthesize scenario-aware rewriting policies and refine the prompt conditioned on these policies. Third, a structured semantic verification agent checks whether the refined prompt satisfies constraints and desired semantics, and if it detects violations, it triggers conditional revisions, making the system self-correcting. In addition, they build T2V-Complexity, a benchmark consisting only of complex-scenario prompts, based on a clear taxonomy and representative examples of complex T2V conditions.
AI.result: On three existing T2V benchmarks (including VBench and EvalCrafter) and the new T2V-Complexity benchmark, SCMAPR improves text-video alignment and overall generation quality in complex scenarios compared with three state-of-the-art baselines. It yields up to 2.67% and 3.28 average-score gains on VBench and EvalCrafter, and up to 0.028 improvement on T2V-CompBench.
AI.conclusion: Scenario-aware, multi-agent, and self-correcting prompt refinement substantially strengthens T2V generation under complex prompts. By formalizing complex scenarios via a taxonomy and providing a dedicated benchmark, the work both advances practical T2V prompting (through SCMAPR) and establishes a more rigorous evaluation setting (T2V-Complexity) for future methods targeting complex text-to-video generation.

title: Attention Editing: A Versatile Framework for Cross-Architecture Attention Conversion
summary: Key-Value (KV) cache memory and bandwidth increasingly dominate large language model inference cost in long-context and long-generation regimes. Architectures such as multi-head latent attention (MLA) and hybrid sliding-window attention (SWA) can alleviate this bound, but integrating them into existing models remains difficult. Prior methods impose fine-grained structural requirements on both source and target attention modules, which cannot meet the feasible requirement in practical deployment. We present Attention Editing, a practical framework for converting already-trained large language models (LLMs) with new attention architectures without re-pretraining from scratch. Attention editing replaces the original attention with a learnable target module and trains it using progressive distillation, consisting of (1) layer-wise teacher-forced optimization with intermediate activation supervision to prevent cold-start error accumulation, and (2) model-level distillation on next-token distributions, optionally regularized by weak feature matching. We instantiate the framework on two different target--MLA and GateSWA, a gated hybrid SWA design, and apply it to Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-30B-A3B. The resulting models maintain competitive performance while delivering substantial efficiency improvements, demonstrating that large-scale attention conversion is both feasible and robust. Notably, experiments are conducted on an Ascend 910B clusters, offering a practical training case study on domestic hardware.

AI.tldr: They propose a practical way (“attention editing”) to swap in more efficient attention mechanisms into already-trained LLMs to cut KV-cache cost without re-pretraining, and show it works on Qwen models with MLA and a new GateSWA design.
AI.motivation: KV-cache memory and bandwidth dominate inference cost for LLMs in long-context/long-generation settings. New attention architectures like MLA and SWA can reduce this, but are hard to retrofit into existing pretrained models because prior conversion methods require strict structural alignment between old and new attention, making large-scale deployment impractical.
AI.method: Introduce Attention Editing, a framework to replace an LLM’s existing attention block with a learnable target attention module and train it via progressive knowledge distillation. Step (1) performs layer-wise, teacher-forced optimization using intermediate activation supervision to avoid error accumulation during early training. Step (2) performs full-model distillation on next-token predictions, optionally adding weak feature-matching regularization. They instantiate this with two target attention designs—multi-head latent attention (MLA) and GateSWA, a gated hybrid sliding-window attention—then apply the conversion to Qwen3-8B and Qwen3-30B-A3B models, training on Ascend 910B clusters.
AI.result: Converted Qwen models using MLA and GateSWA retain competitive task performance compared to the originals while significantly reducing KV-cache memory/bandwidth requirements and improving inference efficiency in long-context scenarios. The experiments also validate that the progressive distillation procedure is stable for large models and can be executed effectively on Ascend 910B hardware.
AI.conclusion: Large-scale attention conversion in pretrained LLMs is feasible and robust when using the proposed Attention Editing framework. It enables retrofitting more efficient attention mechanisms such as MLA and GateSWA into existing models without full re-pretraining, yielding substantial inference-efficiency gains while largely preserving model quality, and it works in practical deployments on Ascend 910B clusters.

title: Thinking Diffusion: Penalize and Guide Visual-Grounded Reasoning in Diffusion Multimodal Language Models
summary: Diffusion large language models (dLLMs) are emerging as promising alternatives to autoregressive (AR) LLMs. Recently, this paradigm has been extended to multimodal tasks, leading to the development of diffusion multimodal large language models (dMLLMs). These models are expected to retain the reasoning capabilities of LLMs while enabling faster inference through parallel generation. However, when combined with Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning, dMLLMs exhibit two critical issues. First, we observe that dMLLMs often generate the final answer token at a very early timestep. This trend indicates that the model determines the answer before sufficient reasoning, leading to degraded reasoning performance. Second, during the initial timesteps, dMLLMs show minimal dependency on visual prompts, exhibiting a fundamentally different pattern of visual information utilization compared to AR vision-language models. In summary, these findings indicate that dMLLMs tend to generate premature final answers without sufficiently grounding on visual inputs. To address these limitations, we propose Position and Step Penalty (PSP) and Visual Reasoning Guidance (VRG). PSP penalizes tokens in later positions during early timesteps, delaying premature answer generation and encouraging progressive reasoning across timesteps. VRG, inspired by classifier-free guidance, amplifies visual grounding signals to enhance the model's alignment with visual evidence. Extensive experiments across various dMLLMs demonstrate that our method achieves up to 7.5% higher accuracy while delivering more than 3x speedup compared to reasoning with four times more diffusion steps.

AI.tldr: The paper studies why diffusion-based multimodal LLMs reason poorly with chain-of-thought and proposes two techniques, PSP and VRG, to delay premature answers and strengthen visual grounding, improving accuracy and speed.
AI.motivation: Diffusion large language models have emerged as an alternative to autoregressive LLMs, offering potential speed advantages through parallel token generation. This diffusion paradigm has been extended to multimodal (vision-language) settings, creating diffusion multimodal LLMs that should, in principle, combine strong reasoning with fast inference. However, when chain-of-thought reasoning is applied, these models exhibit degraded performance. Understanding why this happens and how to fix it is crucial for making dMLLMs practical for reasoning-intensive visual tasks.
AI.method: The authors first empirically analyze the behavior of diffusion multimodal LLMs during chain-of-thought generation. They discover that (1) the final answer token tends to be produced at very early diffusion timesteps, before sufficient reasoning tokens, and (2) at these early timesteps, the model shows weak dependence on visual inputs, unlike autoregressive vision-language models. To remedy this, they introduce two training/inference strategies: (a) Position and Step Penalty (PSP), which penalizes generating tokens in later positions (such as the final answer) too early in the diffusion process, encouraging a more stepwise buildup of reasoning tokens; and (b) Visual Reasoning Guidance (VRG), a guidance mechanism inspired by classifier-free guidance that amplifies visual grounding signals so that the denoising process is more strongly conditioned on visual evidence throughout reasoning.
AI.result: Across multiple diffusion multimodal LLMs and benchmarks, incorporating PSP and VRG leads to improvements of up to 7.5 percentage points in accuracy. Moreover, their method allows using fewer diffusion steps while maintaining or surpassing baseline performance, resulting in more than 3x inference speedup compared to running chain-of-thought with four times more diffusion steps.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion multimodal LLMs, when naively used with chain-of-thought prompting, tend to shortcut reasoning by emitting final answers too early and insufficiently relying on visual inputs. By reshaping the temporal structure of token generation (PSP) and strengthening visual conditioning (VRG), the authors restore more progressive, visually grounded reasoning. Their approach substantially boosts both accuracy and efficiency, suggesting that careful control of diffusion timesteps and conditioning is key to unlocking effective chain-of-thought reasoning in diffusion-based multimodal language models.

title: Dialogue Act Patterns in GenAI-Mediated L2 Oral Practice: A Sequential Analysis of Learner-Chatbot Interactions
comment: Accepted for publication as a full paper (Main Track) at the 27th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Education (AIED 2026)
summary: While generative AI (GenAI) voice chatbots offer scalable opportunities for second language (L2) oral practice, the interactional processes related to learners' gains remain underexplored. This study investigates dialogue act (DA) patterns in interactions between Grade 9 Chinese English as a foreign language (EFL) learners and a GenAI voice chatbot over a 10-week intervention. Seventy sessions from 12 students were annotated by human coders using a pedagogy-informed coding scheme, yielding 6,957 coded DAs. DA distributions and sequential patterns were compared between high- and low-progress sessions. At the DA level, high-progress sessions showed more learner-initiated questions, whereas low-progress sessions exhibited higher rates of clarification-seeking, indicating greater comprehension difficulty. At the sequential level, high-progress sessions were characterised by more frequent prompting-based corrective feedback sequences, consistently positioned after learner responses, highlighting the role of feedback type and timing in effective interaction. Overall, these findings underscore the value of a dialogic lens in GenAI chatbot design, contribute a pedagogy-informed DA coding framework, and inform the design of adaptive GenAI chatbots for L2 education.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how Grade 9 Chinese EFL learners interact with a GenAI voice chatbot, showing that specific dialogue act patterns and feedback sequences are associated with higher language progress, and proposing implications for adaptive chatbot design.
AI.motivation: Although GenAI voice chatbots are increasingly used for scalable L2 oral practice, we know little about the fine‑grained interactional processes—especially dialogue act patterns and feedback sequences—that actually relate to learners’ progress. Existing work tends to focus on outcomes or broad interaction metrics rather than pedagogy-informed conversational structures. This paper addresses that gap.
AI.method: Over a 10‑week intervention, interactions between Grade 9 Chinese EFL learners and a GenAI voice chatbot were collected. From 12 students, 70 sessions were sampled and manually annotated by trained human coders using a pedagogy‑informed dialogue act (DA) coding scheme, yielding 6,957 DA instances. The authors then compared DA distributions (what kinds of moves occurred and how often) and sequential patterns (how DAs followed one another, especially around feedback) between sessions classified as high‑progress and low‑progress, using quantitative pattern analysis of both frequencies and sequences.
AI.result: High‑progress sessions featured more learner‑initiated questions, suggesting greater engagement and proactive language use. Low‑progress sessions contained higher rates of clarification‑seeking DAs, indicating more comprehension difficulty. At the sequential level, high‑progress sessions were marked by more frequent prompting‑based corrective feedback sequences from the chatbot, systematically occurring right after learner responses. These sequences appear to scaffold learning more effectively than other interactional patterns observed in low‑progress sessions.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that specific dialogic patterns—especially learner initiative (asking questions) and well‑timed, prompting‑based corrective feedback after learner turns—are associated with better learning progress in GenAI‑mediated L2 oral practice. It emphasizes the importance of viewing GenAI chatbots through a dialogic, pedagogy‑informed lens rather than just as content generators, offers a DA coding framework tailored to educational interactions, and provides concrete design implications for adaptive GenAI chatbots that can adjust feedback type and timing to support more effective L2 learning.

title: OmniDiagram: Advancing Unified Diagram Code Generation via Visual Interrogation Reward
summary: The paradigm of programmable diagram generation is evolving rapidly, playing a crucial role in structured visualization. However, most existing studies are confined to a narrow range of task formulations and language support, constraining their applicability to diverse diagram types. In this work, we propose OmniDiagram, a unified framework that incorporates diverse diagram code languages and task definitions. To address the challenge of aligning code logic with visual fidelity in Reinforcement Learning (RL), we introduce a novel visual feedback strategy named Visual Interrogation Verifies All (\textsc{Viva}). Unlike brittle syntax-based rules or pixel-level matching, \textsc{Viva} rewards the visual structure of rendered diagrams through a generative approach. Specifically, \textsc{Viva} actively generates targeted visual inquiries to scrutinize diagram visual fidelity and provides fine-grained feedback for optimization. This mechanism facilitates a self-evolving training process, effectively obviating the need for manually annotated ground truth code. Furthermore, we construct M3$^2$Diagram, the first large-scale diagram code generation dataset, containing over 196k high-quality instances. Experimental results confirm that the combination of SFT and our \textsc{Viva}-based RL allows OmniDiagram to establish a new state-of-the-art (SOTA) across diagram code generation benchmarks.

AI.tldr: OmniDiagram is a unified framework for generating diagram code in multiple languages and tasks, trained with a new visual-feedback RL method (Viva) and a large dataset, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Existing programmable diagram generation systems support only limited task formats and a small set of diagram code languages, which restricts their use across diverse diagram types and real-world applications. Moreover, aligning generated code with the correct visual output during RL training is difficult: syntax checks or pixel-level comparisons are brittle and require ground-truth code, limiting scalability.
AI.method: The authors propose OmniDiagram, a unified framework that supports multiple diagram code languages and task definitions. To train it effectively, they introduce Viva (Visual Interrogation Verifies All), a visual-feedback strategy for reinforcement learning. Instead of relying on syntax rules or raw pixel similarity, Viva uses a generative approach to actively query the rendered diagrams, generate targeted visual questions, and evaluate the visual structure. The fine-grained feedback from these interrogations serves as the reward signal in RL, enabling self-evolving training without manual ground-truth code. They also build M3^2Diagram, a large-scale diagram code generation dataset with over 196k examples, used for supervised fine-tuning and RL.
AI.result: Using supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on M3^2Diagram followed by Viva-based RL, OmniDiagram achieves superior performance compared to previous methods on standard diagram code generation benchmarks. It establishes new state-of-the-art results across multiple diagram languages and task settings, demonstrating both improved accuracy and generality.
AI.conclusion: A unified, multi-language framework combined with a generative visual feedback RL strategy can significantly advance programmable diagram generation. Viva enables alignment with visual fidelity without ground-truth code, while the large M3^2Diagram dataset supports broad generalization. Together, these components let OmniDiagram outperform prior approaches and provide a scalable path for future diagram code generation systems.

title: MedLayBench-V: A Large-Scale Benchmark for Expert-Lay Semantic Alignment in Medical Vision Language Models
comment: Accepted at ACL 2026 Findings (Oral). 9 pages, 5 figures, 11 tables, plus appendix
summary: Medical Vision-Language Models (Med-VLMs) have achieved expert-level proficiency in interpreting diagnostic imaging. However, current models are predominantly trained on professional literature, limiting their ability to communicate findings in the lay register required for patient-centered care. While text-centric research has actively developed resources for simplifying medical jargon, there is a critical absence of large-scale multimodal benchmarks designed to facilitate lay-accessible medical image understanding. To bridge this resource gap, we introduce MedLayBench-V, the first large-scale multimodal benchmark dedicated to expert-lay semantic alignment. Unlike naive simplification approaches that risk hallucination, our dataset is constructed via a Structured Concept-Grounded Refinement (SCGR) pipeline. This method enforces strict semantic equivalence by integrating Unified Medical Language System (UMLS) Concept Unique Identifiers (CUIs) with micro-level entity constraints. MedLayBench-V provides a verified foundation for training and evaluating next-generation Med-VLMs capable of bridging the communication divide between clinical experts and patients.

AI.tldr: Introduces MedLayBench-V, the first large-scale multimodal benchmark to align expert medical image interpretations with layperson-understandable language using a structured, concept-grounded pipeline.
AI.motivation: Medical vision-language models can interpret diagnostic images at expert level but are trained mainly on professional literature, so they struggle to explain findings in language accessible to patients. Existing work mostly targets text-only medical simplification and lacks multimodal resources to ensure that visual diagnostics can be communicated clearly and accurately to laypeople.
AI.method: The authors build MedLayBench-V, a multimodal benchmark for expert-lay semantic alignment, created via a Structured Concept-Grounded Refinement (SCGR) pipeline. This pipeline links content to Unified Medical Language System (UMLS) Concept Unique Identifiers (CUIs) and imposes fine-grained, micro-level entity constraints to maintain strict semantic equivalence while transforming expert descriptions into lay language and preventing hallucinated or altered medical meaning.
AI.result: They obtain a large-scale, verified dataset where expert medical image interpretations are tightly aligned with corresponding lay-friendly descriptions, all grounded in standardized UMLS concepts and entity-level checks, enabling reliable evaluation and training of Med-VLMs on lay-accessible communication tasks.
AI.conclusion: MedLayBench-V offers the first systematic multimodal resource for ensuring that medical vision-language models can preserve clinical accuracy while communicating in patient-friendly language, laying groundwork for future Med-VLMs that better bridge the communication gap between clinicians and patients.

title: UniCreative: Unifying Long-form Logic and Short-form Sparkle via Reference-Free Reinforcement Learning
summary: A fundamental challenge in creative writing lies in reconciling the inherent tension between maintaining global coherence in long-form narratives and preserving local expressiveness in short-form texts. While long-context generation necessitates explicit macroscopic planning, short-form creativity often demands spontaneous, constraint-free expression. Existing alignment paradigms, however, typically employ static reward signals and rely heavily on high-quality supervised data, which is costly and difficult to scale. To address this, we propose \textbf{UniCreative}, a unified reference-free reinforcement learning framework. We first introduce \textbf{AC-GenRM}, an adaptive constraint-aware reward model that dynamically synthesizes query-specific criteria to provide fine-grained preference judgments. Leveraging these signals, we propose \textbf{ACPO}, a policy optimization algorithm that aligns models with human preferences across both content quality and structural paradigms without supervised fine-tuning and ground-truth references. Empirical results demonstrate that AC-GenRM aligns closely with expert evaluations, while ACPO significantly enhances performance across diverse writing tasks. Crucially, our analysis reveals an emergent meta-cognitive ability: the model learns to autonomously differentiate between tasks requiring rigorous planning and those favoring direct generation, validating the effectiveness of our direct alignment approach.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces UniCreative, a reinforcement learning framework that aligns language models for both long-form and short-form creative writing without needing supervised fine-tuning or reference texts.
AI.motivation: Creative writing models face a trade-off: long-form narratives require global coherence and explicit planning, while short-form texts benefit from local, unconstrained expressiveness. Existing alignment methods depend on static reward signals and expensive high-quality supervised data, limiting scalability and flexibility. The authors aim to build a scalable, reference-free alignment method that can adapt to different creative writing demands and better reflect human preferences.
AI.method: The authors propose UniCreative, a unified reinforcement learning-based alignment framework. They first design AC-GenRM, an adaptive constraint-aware reward model that generates query-specific evaluation criteria and provides fine-grained preference judgments without ground-truth references. Using these dynamic rewards, they introduce ACPO, a policy optimization algorithm that directly optimizes the model to align with human preferences over both content quality and structural style (e.g., planned long-form vs. direct short-form) without supervised fine-tuning. The framework uses reference-free RL instead of relying on curated labeled datasets.
AI.result: Empirical experiments show that AC-GenRM’s judgments are highly correlated with expert human evaluations. Applying ACPO to language models leads to substantial improvements on a range of creative writing tasks, covering both long-context narrative generation and short-form creative outputs. The trained models exhibit better alignment with human notions of quality and structure compared to baselines using static rewards or supervised fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that UniCreative successfully unifies alignment for both long-form and short-form creative writing in a reference-free RL framework. The adaptive reward model and policy optimization algorithm reduce dependence on expensive supervised data while achieving strong agreement with human experts. An important emergent property is a meta-cognitive behavior: the model learns to decide when to engage in detailed planning versus when to generate directly, suggesting that the direct alignment approach effectively internalizes task-dependent structural strategies.

title: Controlling Distributional Bias in Multi-Round LLM Generation via KL-Optimized Fine-Tuning
summary: While the real world is inherently stochastic, Large Language Models (LLMs) are predominantly evaluated on single-round inference against fixed ground truths. In this work, we shift the lens to distribution alignment: assessing whether LLMs, when prompted repeatedly, can generate outputs that adhere to a desired target distribution, e.g. reflecting real-world statistics or a uniform distribution. We formulate distribution alignment using the attributes of gender, race, and sentiment within occupational contexts. Our empirical analysis reveals that off-the-shelf LLMs and standard alignment techniques, including prompt engineering and Direct Preference Optimization, fail to reliably control output distributions. To bridge this gap, we propose a novel fine-tuning framework that couples Steering Token Calibration with Semantic Alignment. We introduce a hybrid objective function combining Kullback-Leibler divergence to anchor the probability mass of latent steering tokens and Kahneman-Tversky Optimization to bind these tokens to semantically consistent responses. Experiments across six diverse datasets demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms baselines, achieving precise distributional control in attribute generation tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how well LLMs can match desired output distributions over attributes (like gender, race, sentiment in occupations) and proposes a new fine-tuning framework to achieve precise distributional control, outperforming existing methods.
AI.motivation: Current LLM evaluation and alignment mostly consider single-answer correctness relative to fixed ground truth, ignoring that many tasks require generating outputs that follow specific statistical distributions (e.g., real-world demographics or balanced/uniform settings). Existing alignment methods (prompting, DPO) are not sufficient to control such distributions, especially for socially salient attributes like gender, race, and sentiment in occupational descriptions. The paper aims to formally study and improve LLMs’ ability to align with target distributions.
AI.method: The authors define distribution alignment as repeatedly prompting an LLM and checking whether the empirical distribution of generated attributes (gender, race, sentiment within occupations) matches a target distribution. They evaluate off-the-shelf LLMs and standard alignment strategies, finding they fail to reliably match target distributions. They then introduce a fine-tuning framework that uses "Steering Token Calibration" to allocate probability mass to special steering tokens according to a Kullback-Leibler divergence objective, and "Semantic Alignment" to ensure these tokens correspond to semantically appropriate outputs via Kahneman-Tversky Optimization. The training objective is a hybrid that jointly optimizes KL divergence over steering tokens and KTO-based preference alignment over responses.
AI.result: Experiments on six varied datasets involving attribute generation tasks show that their method yields much tighter adherence to target distributions than baselines, including unmodified LLMs, prompt engineering, and DPO. The approach achieves precise control over generated attribute frequencies (gender, race, sentiment) in occupational contexts while maintaining semantic coherence.
AI.conclusion: Distribution alignment is a distinct and challenging problem for LLMs that is not solved by existing alignment techniques. The proposed steering-token-based fine-tuning with a combined KL and KTO loss provides a practical way to control output attribute distributions accurately. This leads to more reliable modeling of desired or real-world distributions in LLM generations, opening paths for fairer or more controlled behavior in downstream applications.

title: Market-Bench: Benchmarking Large Language Models on Economic and Trade Competition
summary: The ability of large language models (LLMs) to manage and acquire economic resources remains unclear. In this paper, we introduce \textbf{Market-Bench}, a comprehensive benchmark that evaluates the capabilities of LLMs in economically-relevant tasks through economic and trade competition. Specifically, we construct a configurable multi-agent supply chain economic model where LLMs act as retailer agents responsible for procuring and retailing merchandise. In the \textbf{procurement} stage, LLMs bid for limited inventory in budget-constrained auctions. In the \textbf{retail} stage, LLMs set retail prices, generate marketing slogans, and provide them to buyers through a role-based attention mechanism for purchase. Market-Bench logs complete trajectories of bids, prices, slogans, sales, and balance-sheet states, enabling automatic evaluation with economic, operational, and semantic metrics. Benchmarking on 20 open- and closed-source LLM agents reveals significant performance disparities and winner-take-most phenomenon, \textit{i.e.}, only a small subset of LLM retailers can consistently achieve capital appreciation, while many hover around the break-even point despite similar semantic matching scores. Market-Bench provides a reproducible testbed for studying how LLMs interact in competitive markets.

AI.tldr: They build a benchmark (Market-Bench) to test how well LLMs can act as retailers in a simulated market and manage money/resources.
AI.motivation: Although LLMs are powerful at language tasks, we do not yet understand whether they can effectively manage and grow economic resources, particularly in competitive, market-like environments. The authors want a systematic, reproducible way to measure LLMs’ economic decision-making and interaction in markets rather than using only static or single-agent tests.
AI.method: They design a configurable multi-agent supply chain simulation in which LLMs play the role of retailer agents. The environment has two main stages: (1) procurement—LLM retailers participate in budget-constrained auctions to bid for limited inventory; (2) retail—LLM retailers set product prices, generate marketing slogans, and present them to buyers within a role-based attention mechanism that mediates how buyers see the messages. The system records full trajectories of each agent’s bids, prices, slogans, sales, and financial states, and derives economic (e.g., profit, capital growth), operational (e.g., inventory usage), and semantic (e.g., marketing-message quality) metrics for automatic evaluation. They benchmark 20 different open- and closed-source LLMs as retailer agents in this setup.
AI.result: Across the 20 LLM agents, they observe large performance gaps in economic outcomes. A small subset of LLM retailers consistently grows their capital, while many others only break even or fail to appreciate capital, even though their semantic metrics (e.g., alignment between slogans and products) are similar. This indicates that better economic performance is not simply explained by better language or semantic matching alone. They also observe a “winner-take-most” dynamic in the simulated market, where the successful agents capture a disproportionate share of gains.
AI.conclusion: Market-Bench offers a reproducible environment for studying LLMs as economic actors in competitive markets, revealing that only some models can reliably translate linguistic capabilities into effective resource management and profit generation. The benchmark can be used to investigate mechanisms behind economic performance differences and to guide future development of LLMs for economically-relevant, multi-agent settings.

title: Identifying Influential N-grams in Confidence Calibration via Regression Analysis
summary: While large language models (LLMs) improve performance by explicit reasoning, their responses are often overconfident, even though they include linguistic expressions demonstrating uncertainty. In this work, we identify what linguistic expressions are related to confidence by applying the regression method. Specifically, we predict confidence of those linguistic expressions in the reasoning parts of LLMs as the dependent variables and analyze the relationship between a specific $n$-gram and confidence. Across multiple models and QA benchmarks, we show that LLMs remain overconfident when reasoning is involved and attribute this behavior to specific linguistic information. Interestingly, several of the extracted expressions coincide with cue phrases intentionally inserted on test-time scaling to improve reasoning performance. Through our test on causality and verification that the extracted linguistic information truly affects confidence, we reveal that confidence calibration is possible by simply suppressing those overconfident expressions without drops in performance.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes how specific linguistic expressions in LLMs’ reasoning relate to miscalibrated confidence and shows that suppressing overconfident phrases can improve calibration without hurting accuracy.
AI.motivation: LLMs increasingly use explicit reasoning (e.g., chain-of-thought), but their answers are often miscalibrated: they sound highly confident even when wrong. Existing work focuses on numeric confidence scores, not on the role of language itself. Understanding which linguistic patterns signal or induce overconfidence could enable simple, non-invasive ways to calibrate LLMs’ behavior.
AI.method: The authors extract reasoning segments from LLM outputs on multiple QA benchmarks and identify $n$-gram expressions within these segments. They then use a regression framework where the dependent variable is the model’s confidence and the independent variables indicate the presence of specific $n$-grams. By studying coefficients and performing causal/verification tests, they isolate which linguistic expressions are associated with higher or lower confidence and test interventions that suppress certain phrases.
AI.result: They find that LLMs remain systematically overconfident when they engage in explicit reasoning. The regression analysis links certain $n$-grams and cue phrases to elevated confidence, some of which overlap with phrases previously hand-crafted for test-time scaling to enhance reasoning. Intervention experiments show that modifying or suppressing these overconfident expressions can reduce overconfidence while maintaining task performance.
AI.conclusion: Linguistic form is tightly linked to LLM confidence: particular phrases in reasoning both reflect and affect overconfidence. By identifying and suppressing these expressions, one can calibrate LLM confidence without sacrificing accuracy, suggesting that simple, language-level interventions are a promising avenue for safer and more reliable LLM deployment.

title: ActivityEditor: Learning to Synthesize Physically Valid Human Mobility
summary: Human mobility modeling is indispensable for diverse urban applications. However, existing data-driven methods often suffer from data scarcity, limiting their applicability in regions where historical trajectories are unavailable or restricted. To bridge this gap, we propose \textbf{ActivityEditor}, a novel dual-LLM-agent framework designed for zero-shot cross-regional trajectory generation. Our framework decomposes the complex synthesis task into two collaborative stages. Specifically, an intention-based agent, which leverages demographic-driven priors to generate structured human intentions and coarse activity chains to ensure high-level socio-semantic coherence. These outputs are then refined by editor agent to obtain mobility trajectories through iteratively revisions that enforces human mobility law. This capability is acquired through reinforcement learning with multiple rewards grounded in real-world physical constraints, allowing the agent to internalize mobility regularities and ensure high-fidelity trajectory generation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that \textbf{ActivityEditor} achieves superior zero-shot performance when transferred across diverse urban contexts. It maintains high statistical fidelity and physical validity, providing a robust and highly generalizable solution for mobility simulation in data-scarce scenarios. Our code is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/ActivityEditor-066B.

AI.tldr: They propose ActivityEditor, a dual-LLM-agent system that can generate realistic human mobility trajectories in new cities without local trajectory data, by first generating intentions and then editing them into trajectories using RL-guided physical constraints.
AI.motivation: Realistic human mobility data is crucial for many urban applications (e.g., planning, transport, simulation), but in many regions historical trajectory data is scarce, unavailable, or restricted due to privacy or access issues. Existing data-driven models rely heavily on local data and thus fail to generalize or operate in zero-shot cross-regional settings. There is therefore a need for a model that can simulate human mobility patterns in new urban contexts without requiring local trajectory histories while still respecting human behavior regularities and physical constraints.
AI.method: They design ActivityEditor, a dual large-language-model (LLM) agent framework that decomposes trajectory synthesis into two stages. First, an intention-based agent uses demographic priors to generate structured human intentions and coarse activity chains, ensuring socio-semantic coherence at a high level. Second, an editor agent refines these coarse plans into detailed mobility trajectories via iterative revisions. The editor is trained with reinforcement learning using multiple reward signals grounded in real-world physical and mobility constraints, enabling it to internalize human mobility laws and correct trajectories towards physically valid, realistic patterns. The approach is evaluated in zero-shot cross-regional transfer scenarios.
AI.result: In extensive experiments across diverse urban contexts, ActivityEditor shows superior zero-shot performance compared to baselines when transferred to new cities or regions without local trajectory data. It produces mobility simulations with high statistical fidelity (matching empirical mobility patterns) and strong physical validity (respecting spatial-temporal constraints and real-world feasibility).
AI.conclusion: ActivityEditor offers a robust, generalizable solution for human mobility simulation in data-scarce regions by combining intention generation with RL-trained trajectory editing. Its dual-agent LLM design enables zero-shot cross-regional trajectory generation that preserves socio-semantic coherence and physical realism, making it suitable as a practical tool for urban applications where historical trajectory data are limited or inaccessible.

title: PhageBench: Can LLMs Understand Raw Bacteriophage Genomes?
summary: Bacteriophages, often referred to as the dark matter of the biosphere, play a critical role in regulating microbial ecosystems and in antibiotic alternatives. Thus, accurate interpretation of their genomes holds significant scientific and practical value. While general-purpose Large Language Models (LLMs) excel at understanding biological texts, their ability to directly interpret raw nucleotide sequences and perform biological reasoning remains underexplored. To address this, we introduce PhageBench, the first benchmark designed to evaluate phage genome understanding by mirroring the workflow of bioinformatics experts. The dataset contains 5,600 high-quality samples covering five core tasks across three stages: Screening, Quality Control, and Phenotype Annotation. Our evaluation of eight LLMs reveals that general-purpose reasoning models significantly outperform random baselines in phage contig identification and host prediction, demonstrating promising potential for genomic understanding. However, they exhibit significant limitations in complex reasoning tasks involving long-range dependencies and fine-grained functional localization. These findings highlight the necessity of developing next-generation models with enhanced reasoning capabilities for biological sequences.

AI.tldr: PhageBench is a new benchmark to test how well large language models can understand and reason over bacteriophage genomes, revealing strengths in simple tasks but weaknesses in complex, fine-grained genomic reasoning.
AI.motivation: Bacteriophages are crucial for microbial ecosystem regulation and as potential antibiotic alternatives, so interpreting their genomes is scientifically and clinically important. Current large language models are good at biological text but it is unclear how well they handle raw DNA sequences and associated biological reasoning. There is a need for a systematic way to evaluate LLMs on phage genome understanding, aligned with real expert bioinformatics workflows.
AI.method: The authors build PhageBench, a benchmark dataset of 5,600 carefully curated phage-related genomic samples. It is structured to mimic three main stages of expert phage genome analysis: Screening, Quality Control, and Phenotype Annotation. Across these stages they define five core tasks, including phage contig identification, host prediction, and more complex reasoning tasks like functional localization. They then systematically evaluate eight general-purpose LLMs on these tasks, comparing performance against random baselines and examining how models handle long-range dependencies and detailed functional reasoning in sequences.
AI.result: General-purpose reasoning LLMs clearly outperform random baselines on simpler sequence understanding tasks such as identifying phage contigs and predicting phage hosts, suggesting they do capture some biologically relevant patterns from nucleotide input. However, their performance drops substantially on more demanding tasks that require complex reasoning across long genomic regions and precise localization of functional elements, indicating current models are not yet capable of expert-level phage genome interpretation.
AI.conclusion: PhageBench reveals that current LLMs have promising but limited capabilities for phage genome understanding: they can handle basic identification and prediction tasks but struggle with complex, fine-grained reasoning over long DNA sequences. This underscores the need for next-generation models and training strategies specifically tailored to biological sequences, with stronger long-range reasoning and functional understanding, and establishes PhageBench as a standardized testbed for future progress in this area.

title: Inventory of the 12 007 Low-Dimensional Pseudo-Boolean Landscapes Invariant to Rank, Translation, and Rotation
summary: Many randomized optimization algorithms are rank-invariant, relying solely on the relative ordering of solutions rather than absolute fitness values. We introduce a stronger notion of rank landscape invariance: two problems are equivalent if their ranking, but also their neighborhood structure and symmetries (translation and rotation), induce identical landscapes. This motivates the study of rank landscapes rather than individual functions. While prior work analyzed the rankings of injective function classes in isolation, we provide an exhaustive inventory of the invariant landscape classes for pseudo-Boolean functions of dimensions 1, 2, and 3, including non-injective cases. Our analysis reveals 12,007 classes in total, a significant reduction compared to rank-invariance alone. We find that non-injective functions yield far more invariant landscape classes than injective ones. In addition, complex combinations of topological landscape properties and algorithm behaviors emerge, particularly regarding deceptiveness, neutrality, and the performance of hill-climbing strategies. The inventory serves as a resource for pedagogical purposes and benchmark design, offering a foundation for constructing larger problems with controlled hardness and advancing our understanding of landscape difficulty and algorithm performance.

AI.tldr: The paper classifies and studies “rank landscape invariant” classes of pseudo-Boolean optimization problems, where rankings, neighborhood structure, and symmetries define equivalence of problems, yielding a comprehensive catalog for small dimensions and insights into algorithm behavior and landscape difficulty.
AI.motivation: Many randomized optimization algorithms use only the ranking of candidate solutions, not their exact objective values, making it natural to study problems up to rank-based equivalence. However, prior work has largely ignored neighborhood structure, symmetries, and non-injective functions, limiting understanding of how landscapes influence algorithm performance. A more structured, landscape-level view can better explain deceptiveness, neutrality, and search difficulty, and support principled benchmark design.
AI.method: The authors define a stronger equivalence relation on optimization problems: two pseudo-Boolean functions are equivalent if they induce the same ranking over bitstrings, the same neighborhood structure, and are related by translations and rotations (symmetries). They then conduct an exhaustive combinatorial and computational enumeration of all such invariant landscape classes for pseudo-Boolean functions in dimensions 1, 2, and 3, explicitly including non-injective functions. For each equivalence class, they analyze topological properties (e.g., deceptiveness, neutrality) and study the behavior of local search (especially hill-climbing) on these landscapes.
AI.result: They obtain a complete catalog of 12,007 invariant landscape classes for pseudo-Boolean functions of up to 3 variables, showing this is a substantial reduction from considering rank invariance alone. The study reveals that non-injective functions account for many more distinct invariant landscape classes than injective ones. They also uncover rich patterns between landscape properties (like deceptiveness and neutrality) and algorithm behavior, including diverse performance profiles of hill-climbing methods across different classes.
AI.conclusion: Rank landscape invariance, which respects ranking, neighborhood, and symmetry, provides a more informative and compact way to categorize optimization problems than rank invariance alone. The resulting inventory of invariant landscape classes for low-dimensional pseudo-Boolean functions is both a conceptual and practical resource: it clarifies how structural features of landscapes affect search difficulty and algorithm behavior, and it offers a principled basis for creating educational examples and scalable benchmark problems with controlled hardness.

title: What Models Know, How Well They Know It: Knowledge-Weighted Fine-Tuning for Learning When to Say "I Don't Know"
summary: While large language models (LLMs) demonstrate strong capabilities across diverse user queries, they still suffer from hallucinations, often arising from knowledge misalignment between pre-training and fine-tuning. To address this misalignment, we reliably estimate a fine-grained, instance-level knowledge score via multi-sampled inference. Using the knowledge score, we scale the learning signal according to the model's existing knowledge, while encouraging explicit "I don't know" responses for out-of-scope queries. Experimental results show that this approach allows the model to explicitly express uncertainty when it lacks knowledge, while maintaining accuracy on questions it can answer. Furthermore, we propose evaluation metrics for uncertainty, showing that accurate discrimination between known and unknown instances consistently improves performance.

AI.tldr: They reduce LLM hallucinations by estimating how well the model already knows each instance, scaling training accordingly, and promoting "I don't know" answers for unknown cases, with new uncertainty metrics to evaluate this behavior.
AI.motivation: LLMs hallucinate because there's a mismatch between what they learned in pre-training and how they're supervised in fine-tuning, and current methods do not carefully account for what the model already knows on each specific query or promote honest uncertainty expression.
AI.method: They run multi-sampled inference to compute an instance-level "knowledge score" that quantifies how confidently and consistently the model already knows the answer. This knowledge score is then used during fine-tuning to scale the learning signal—strong updates where knowledge is weak, weaker updates where it's strong—and to explicitly train the model to respond "I don't know" when its knowledge score indicates the query is out of scope or unknown. They also define new metrics to measure how well the model distinguishes between queries it knows and doesn't know.
AI.result: Models trained with this knowledge-aware scaling and "I don't know" objective better express uncertainty on queries where they lack knowledge, while preserving or improving accuracy on queries they can answer, and they show better discrimination between known and unknown instances under the proposed uncertainty metrics.
AI.conclusion: Instance-level knowledge estimation plus adaptive training and explicit uncertainty responses can reduce hallucinations, yield more honest LLM behavior, and improve performance by helping models distinguish between questions they know and don't know.

title: Experience Transfer for Multimodal LLM Agents in Minecraft Game
summary: Multimodal LLM agents operating in complex game environments must continually reuse past experience to solve new tasks efficiently. In this work, we propose Echo, a transfer-oriented memory framework that enables agents to derive actionable knowledge from prior interactions rather than treating memory as a passive repository of static records. To make transfer explicit, Echo decomposes reusable knowledge into five dimensions: structure, attribute, process, function, and interaction. This formulation allows the agent to identify recurring patterns shared across different tasks and infer what prior experience remains applicable in new situations. Building on this formulation, Echo leverages In-Context Analogy Learning (ICAL) to retrieve relevant experiences and adapt them to unseen tasks through contextual examples. Experiments in Minecraft show that, under a from-scratch learning setting, Echo achieves a 1.3x to 1.7x speed-up on object-unlocking tasks. Moreover, Echo exhibits a burst-like chain-unlocking phenomenon, rapidly unlocking multiple similar items within a short time interval after acquiring transferable experience. These results suggest that experience transfer is a promising direction for improving the efficiency and adaptability of multimodal LLM agents in complex interactive environments.

AI.tldr: Echo is a memory framework for multimodal LLM agents in games that explicitly decomposes and transfers prior experience to speed up learning on new tasks.
AI.motivation: Multimodal LLM agents in complex game environments need to efficiently reuse prior experience instead of relearning from scratch for each new task. Existing memory mechanisms often store past interactions as static logs, without explicit mechanisms for extracting and transferring actionable knowledge to new situations. This limits efficiency, adaptability, and the ability to quickly generalize across related tasks.
AI.method: The paper introduces Echo, a transfer-oriented memory framework. Echo represents reusable knowledge along five dimensions: structure, attribute, process, function, and interaction. This structured representation makes it easier to detect recurring patterns across tasks and decide which parts of prior experience can be transferred. Echo uses In-Context Analogy Learning (ICAL) to retrieve relevant past experiences and embed them as contextual examples for solving new tasks, effectively adapting old solutions to new but related problems in a multimodal game environment (Minecraft).
AI.result: In Minecraft object-unlocking tasks under a from-scratch learning setting, Echo yields 1.3x–1.7x speed-up compared to baselines without such transfer-focused memory. It also exhibits a burst-like chain-unlocking effect, where once transferable experience is acquired for a type of item, the agent rapidly unlocks multiple similar items in a short time window.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling and transferring experience through a structured memory framework like Echo significantly improves learning speed and adaptability of multimodal LLM agents in complex interactive environments. The observed chain-unlocking behavior suggests that systematic experience transfer can induce rapid generalization across related tasks, making this a promising direction for future agent design.

title: Measuring What Matters!! Assessing Therapeutic Principles in Mental-Health Conversation
summary: The increasing use of large language models in mental health applications calls for principled evaluation frameworks that assess alignment with psychotherapeutic best practices beyond surface-level fluency. While recent systems exhibit conversational competence, they lack structured mechanisms to evaluate adherence to core therapeutic principles. In this paper, we study the problem of evaluating AI-generated therapist-like responses for clinically grounded appropriateness and effectiveness. We assess each therapists utterance along six therapeutic principles: non-judgmental acceptance, warmth, respect for autonomy, active listening, reflective understanding, and situational appropriateness using a fine-grained ordinal scale. We introduce FAITH-M, a benchmark annotated with expert-assigned ordinal ratings, and propose CARE, a multi-stage evaluation framework that integrates intra-dialogue context, contrastive exemplar retrieval, and knowledge-distilled chain-of-thought reasoning. Experiments show that CARE achieves an F-1 score of 63.34 versus the strong baseline Qwen3 F-1 score of 38.56 which is a 64.26 improvement, which also serves as its backbone, indicating that gains arise from structured reasoning and contextual modeling rather than backbone capacity alone. Expert assessment and external dataset evaluations further demonstrate robustness under domain shift, while highlighting challenges in modelling implicit clinical nuance. Overall, CARE provides a clinically grounded framework for evaluating therapeutic fidelity in AI mental health systems.

AI.tldr: The paper presents FAITH-M, a benchmark and CARE, a structured evaluation framework to assess whether AI therapist-like responses align with core psychotherapeutic principles using expert-annotated ordinal ratings.
AI.motivation: Large language models are increasingly used in mental health contexts, but current evaluations mostly focus on surface-level fluency and general conversational quality. There is a lack of clinically grounded, principled frameworks to assess whether AI responses adhere to established therapeutic principles. This gap raises concerns about the safety, appropriateness, and true therapeutic value of AI systems used in mental health support.
AI.method: The authors define six core therapeutic principles—non-judgmental acceptance, warmth, respect for autonomy, active listening, reflective understanding, and situational appropriateness—and annotate therapist-like utterances with expert-assigned ordinal ratings for each principle, creating the FAITH-M benchmark. They then propose CARE, a multi-stage evaluation framework that: (1) models intra-dialogue context, (2) retrieves contrastive exemplars to compare good vs. poor responses, and (3) uses knowledge-distilled chain-of-thought reasoning on top of a strong base LLM (Qwen3) to predict fine-grained ratings for each principle.
AI.result: CARE achieves an F1 score of 63.34 on the FAITH-M benchmark, substantially outperforming the strong backbone model Qwen3 used in a straightforward manner, which attains an F1 of 38.56, corresponding to a 64.26% relative improvement. Experiments on external datasets and expert assessments show that CARE’s performance is robust to domain shifts, though it still struggles with subtle and implicit clinical nuances that are difficult even for experts to capture consistently.
AI.conclusion: Structured evaluation that encodes therapeutic principles, uses rich conversational context, and incorporates explicit reasoning can significantly enhance the assessment of AI therapist-like responses beyond what model capacity alone provides. CARE, together with the FAITH-M benchmark, offers a clinically grounded framework for measuring therapeutic fidelity in AI mental health systems, though further work is needed to better model implicit clinical subtleties.

title: SignalClaw: LLM-Guided Evolutionary Synthesis of Interpretable Traffic Signal Control Skills
summary: Traffic signal control TSC requires strategies that are both effective and interpretable for deployment, yet reinforcement learning produces opaque neural policies while program synthesis depends on restrictive domain-specific languages. We present SIGNALCLAW, a framework that uses large language models LLMs as evolutionary skill generators to synthesize and refine interpretable control skills for adaptive TSC. Each skill includes rationale, selection guidance, and executable code, making policies human-inspectable and self-documenting. At each generation, evolution signals from simulation metrics such as queue percentiles, delay trends, and stagnation are translated into natural language feedback to guide improvement. SignalClaw also introduces event-driven compositional evolution: an event detector identifies emergency vehicles, transit priority, incidents, and congestion via TraCI, and a priority dispatcher selects specialized skills. Each skill is evolved independently, and a priority chain enables runtime composition without retraining. We evaluate SignalClaw on routine and event-injected SUMO scenarios against four baselines. On routine scenarios, it achieves average delay of 7.8 to 9.2 seconds, within 3 to 10 percent of the best method, with low variance across random seeds. Under event scenarios, it yields the lowest emergency delay 11.2 to 18.5 seconds versus 42.3 to 72.3 for MaxPressure and 78.5 to 95.3 for DQN, and the lowest transit person delay 9.8 to 11.5 seconds versus 38.7 to 45.2 for MaxPressure. In mixed events, the dispatcher composes skills effectively while maintaining stable overall delay. The evolved skills progress from simple linear rules to conditional strategies with multi-feature interactions, while remaining fully interpretable and directly modifiable by traffic engineers.

AI.tldr: SIGNALCLAW uses large language models as evolutionary generators to synthesize human-readable, event-aware traffic signal control skills that approach RL performance while remaining interpretable and composable without retraining.
AI.motivation: Existing traffic signal control methods face a tradeoff between performance and interpretability: reinforcement learning can yield high-performing but opaque neural policies, while program synthesis via domain-specific languages is interpretable but rigid and hard to scale to complex situations and events. There is a need for adaptive TSC strategies that are both effective in dynamic, eventful environments and transparent enough for deployment and inspection by traffic engineers.
AI.method: The paper proposes SIGNALCLAW, a framework that treats large language models as evolutionary skill generators. Starting from initial interpretable control skills (with natural language rationale, guidance on when to apply them, and executable code), the framework evaluates their performance in SUMO simulations. It extracts rich evolution signals from traffic metrics (queue length percentiles, delay trends, stagnation indicators) and converts these into natural language feedback, which is fed back to the LLM to iteratively refine and synthesize improved skills across generations. Additionally, the system defines an event-driven compositional evolution approach: a TraCI-based event detector recognizes emergency vehicles, transit priority needs, incidents, and congestion; a priority dispatcher then selects among specialized skills evolved independently for each event type. Skills are thus modular, composable at runtime via a priority chain, and do not require joint retraining.
AI.result: In routine (non-event) SUMO scenarios, SIGNALCLAW attains average delays of 7.8–9.2 seconds, performing within 3–10% of the best baseline and showing low variance across random seeds. In event-injected scenarios, it substantially outperforms strong baselines: emergency vehicle delays are reduced to 11.2–18.5 seconds compared to 42.3–72.3 seconds for MaxPressure and 78.5–95.3 seconds for DQN; transit person delays drop to 9.8–11.5 seconds versus 38.7–45.2 seconds for MaxPressure. In mixed-event settings, the dispatcher successfully composes multiple skills while keeping overall delay stable. Qualitatively, evolved skills become more sophisticated over generations, moving from simple linear heuristics to conditional, multi-feature decision rules while remaining interpretable.
AI.conclusion: SIGNALCLAW demonstrates that large language models can serve as evolutionary generators of modular, interpretable traffic signal control skills that approach or surpass traditional methods in complex, event-rich environments. By coupling metric-based feedback in natural language with event-driven skill composition, the framework yields policies that are both high-performing and transparent, allowing traffic engineers to inspect, understand, and directly modify the resulting control logic without retraining monolithic models.

title: CLEAR: Cross-Lingual Enhancement in Alignment via Reverse-training
summary: Existing multilingual embedding models often encounter challenges in cross-lingual scenarios due to imbalanced linguistic resources and less consideration of cross-lingual alignment during training. Although standardized contrastive learning approaches for cross-lingual adaptation are widely adopted, they may struggle to capture fundamental alignment between languages and degrade performance in well-aligned languages such as English. To address these challenges, we propose Cross-Lingual Enhancement in Retrieval via Reverse-training (CLEAR), a novel loss function utilizing a reverse training scheme to improve retrieval performance across diverse cross-lingual retrieval scenarios. CLEAR leverages an English passage as a bridge to strengthen alignments between the target language and English, ensuring robust performance in the cross-lingual retrieval task. Our extensive experiments demonstrate that CLEAR achieves notable improvements in cross-lingual scenarios, with gains up to 15%, particularly in low-resource languages, while minimizing performance degradation in English. Furthermore, our findings highlight that CLEAR offers promising effectiveness even in multilingual training, suggesting its potential for broad application and scalability. We release the code at https://github.com/dltmddbs100/CLEAR.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CLEAR, a reverse-training–based loss to better align multilingual embeddings for cross-lingual retrieval, significantly improving low-resource languages while preserving English performance.
AI.motivation: Multilingual embedding models underperform in cross-lingual retrieval, especially for low-resource languages, because (1) training data and resources across languages are imbalanced and (2) standard contrastive learning doesn’t explicitly enforce strong cross-lingual alignment. Moreover, methods that adapt to low-resource languages can inadvertently hurt already well-aligned languages like English. The authors aim to improve cross-lingual alignment without sacrificing English performance.
AI.method: They propose CLEAR (Cross-Lingual Enhancement in Retrieval via Reverse-training), a new loss function that introduces a reverse training scheme for cross-lingual retrieval. Instead of only aligning a query in a target language with passages in that language, CLEAR uses an English passage as an intermediary bridge: it jointly optimizes representations so that target-language queries and documents are well aligned with corresponding English passages. This reverse training loss is plugged into standard retrieval training, leveraging English as a hub language to enforce consistent cross-lingual alignment.
AI.result: Across diverse cross-lingual retrieval benchmarks, CLEAR yields substantial gains, up to 15% improvement, especially for low-resource languages that previously suffered from poor alignment. At the same time, it limits or avoids degradation on English retrieval performance, which is a common side effect of prior cross-lingual adaptation methods. Experiments also show that the approach remains effective under multilingual training settings, not just in pairwise or bilingual configurations.
AI.conclusion: CLEAR is an effective, scalable way to enhance cross-lingual retrieval by using English as a bridging language within a reverse-training loss. It improves alignment and retrieval quality in low-resource languages while preserving performance in high-resource languages like English, and it integrates smoothly with multilingual training setups, indicating broad applicability.
