The authors also release their code to encourage further use and extension.

title: A canonical generalization of OBDD
summary: We introduce Tree Decision Diagrams (TDD) as a model for Boolean functions that generalizes OBDD. They can be seen as a restriction of structured d-DNNF; that is, d-DNNF that respect a vtree $T$. We show that TDDs enjoy the same tractability properties as OBDD, such as model counting, enumeration, conditioning, and apply, and are more succinct. In particular, we show that CNF formulas of treewidth $k$ can be represented by TDDs of FPT size, which is known to be impossible for OBDD. We study the complexity of compiling CNF formulas into deterministic TDDs via bottom-up compilation and relate the complexity of this approach with the notion of factor width introduced by Bova and Szeider.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Tree Decision Diagrams (TDDs), a more succinct generalization of OBDDs constrained by a vtree, retaining OBDD-like tractability for key reasoning tasks and enabling fixed-parameter tractable representations for CNFs of bounded treewidth, while analyzing the compilation complexity via factor width.
AI.motivation: OBDDs provide strong tractability for Boolean reasoning tasks but can be exponentially large for some structured CNFs, even when those CNFs have small treewidth. Meanwhile, structured d-DNNFs are more succinct but less constrained. The authors want a representation that preserves OBDD-style algorithmic tractability yet is more succinct on structured instances, especially those of bounded treewidth, and to understand the compilation complexity of such representations.
AI.method: They define Tree Decision Diagrams (TDDs) as a representation for Boolean functions that generalizes OBDDs and is a restricted form of structured d-DNNF respecting a vtree T. They then prove that TDDs support the same polynomial-time operations as OBDDs (model counting, enumeration, conditioning, apply). They give size upper bounds showing that CNF formulas of treewidth k can be compiled into TDDs of size FPT in k, and compare this to known lower bounds for OBDDs. Finally, they analyze the bottom-up compilation of CNFs into deterministic TDDs and connect its complexity to factor width, a structural parameter defined by Bova and Szeider.
AI.result: The paper shows that TDDs inherit all major tractability properties of OBDDs while being strictly more succinct in general. In particular, any CNF of treewidth k admits a TDD representation whose size is fixed-parameter tractable in k, unlike OBDDs for which such succinct representations are impossible in general. Furthermore, the authors establish a theoretical link between the complexity of bottom-up compilation of CNFs into deterministic TDDs and the factor width parameter, clarifying when such compilation is efficient.
AI.conclusion: Tree Decision Diagrams provide a robust middle ground between OBDDs and structured d-DNNFs: they generalize OBDDs, match their algorithmic tractability, and offer better succinctness, especially for bounded-treewidth CNFs. The analysis via factor width gives insight into the structural conditions under which bottom-up compilation into deterministic TDDs is feasible, thus advancing the understanding of knowledge compilation for structured Boolean formulas.

title: "OK Aura, Be Fair With Me": Demographics-Agnostic Training for Bias Mitigation in Wake-up Word Detection
comment: Accepted at Speech Language Models in Low-Resource Settings: Performance, Evaluation, and Bias Analysis (SPEAKABLE) - LREC2026 Workshops
summary: Voice-based interfaces are widely used; however, achieving fair Wake-up Word detection across diverse speaker populations remains a critical challenge due to persistent demographic biases. This study evaluates the effectiveness of demographics-agnostic training techniques in mitigating performance disparities among speakers of varying sex, age, and accent. We utilize the OK Aura database for our experiments, employing a training methodology that excludes demographic labels, which are reserved for evaluation purposes. We explore (i) data augmentation techniques to enhance model generalization and (ii) knowledge distillation of pre-trained foundational speech models. The experimental results indicate that these demographics-agnostic training techniques markedly reduce demographic bias, leading to a more equitable performance profile across different speaker groups. Specifically, one of the evaluated techniques achieves a Predictive Disparity reduction of 39.94\% for sex, 83.65\% for age, and 40.48\% for accent when compared to the baseline. This study highlights the effectiveness of label-agnostic methodologies in fostering fairness in Wake-up Word detection.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates demographics-agnostic training strategies—data augmentation and knowledge distillation from foundational speech models—to reduce demographic bias in wake-up word detection, demonstrating large reductions in predictive disparity across sex, age, and accent without using demographic labels during training.
AI.motivation: Voice assistants rely on wake-up word detection, but these systems often perform unevenly across demographic groups (sex, age, accent), causing unfair user experiences and potential exclusion. Existing fairness methods frequently require demographic labels at training time, which are costly, incomplete, or raise privacy concerns. The paper aims to determine whether we can significantly improve fairness in wake-word detection using training strategies that do not rely on demographic labels, while still rigorously evaluating impact across demographics.
AI.method: Using the OK Aura wake-word dataset, the authors train wake-word detection models with a demographics-agnostic approach: demographic attributes are never used during training and are only employed for evaluation. They investigate (i) data augmentation schemes to broaden acoustic variability and improve generalization, and (ii) knowledge distillation from large pre-trained foundational speech models into smaller wake-word detectors. They then measure model performance across demographic slices (sex, age groups, and accent types) and compute Predictive Disparity as the fairness metric, comparing various techniques against a baseline model.
AI.result: Both data augmentation and knowledge distillation significantly reduce demographic performance gaps relative to the baseline. One of the proposed techniques yields large reductions in Predictive Disparity: about 40% for sex, nearly 84% for age, and about 40% for accent. Overall, these demographics-agnostic approaches lead to more similar error rates across different speaker groups without explicit demographic supervision during training.
AI.conclusion: Demographics-agnostic training strategies—particularly appropriate augmentation and distillation from strong pre-trained speech models—can substantially mitigate demographic bias in wake-word detection systems, even when demographic labels are unavailable or withheld during training. The findings support the use of label-agnostic fairness techniques in practical voice interfaces to improve equity across user groups while avoiding the costs and privacy issues of demographic annotation.

title: From Large Language Model Predicates to Logic Tensor Networks: Neurosymbolic Offer Validation in Regulated Procurement
summary: We present a neurosymbolic approach, i.e., combining symbolic and subsymbolic artificial intelligence, to validating offer documents in regulated public institutions. We employ a language model to extract information and then aggregate with an LTN (Logic Tensor Network) to make an auditable decision. In regulated public institutions, decisions must be made in a manner that is both factually correct and legally verifiable. Our neurosymbolic approach allows existing domain-specific knowledge to be linked to the semantic text understanding of language models. The decisions resulting from our pipeline can be justified by predicate values, rule truth values, and corresponding text passages, which enables rule checking based on a real corpus of offer documents. Our experiments on a real corpus show that the proposed pipeline achieves performance comparable to existing models, while its key advantage lies in its interpretability, modular predicate extraction, and explicit support for XAI (Explainable AI).

AI.tldr: Neurosymbolic pipeline that combines language models with Logic Tensor Networks to validate offer documents in regulated public institutions, yielding interpretable, legally verifiable decisions with performance comparable to existing models.
AI.motivation: Regulated public institutions must make decisions that are both factually correct and legally verifiable. Traditional subsymbolic AI (e.g., deep learning models) can extract information from text but are often black boxes and difficult to justify in legal or audit settings. Purely symbolic approaches, while interpretable, struggle with the semantic richness and ambiguity of natural language in real offer documents. There is a need for a method that can leverage domain-specific legal and regulatory knowledge while still benefiting from modern language understanding capabilities, and that can provide transparent, auditable explanations for each decision.
AI.method: The authors propose a neurosymbolic pipeline that first uses a language model to extract structured information and predicates from offer documents. These extracted predicates are then fed into a Logic Tensor Network (LTN), a differentiable first-order logic framework that integrates logical rules with continuous-valued truth degrees. Domain-specific rules and constraints from the regulatory context are encoded in the LTN. The LTN aggregates the predicate values according to these rules to arrive at a final validation decision for each offer document. The system also tracks which predicates, rule truth values, and text spans contributed to each decision, enabling explicit explanation and rule checking.
AI.result: On a real corpus of offer documents from regulated public institutions, the proposed neurosymbolic pipeline achieves performance comparable to existing baseline models in terms of validation accuracy or similar metrics. Although it may not significantly surpass state-of-the-art purely subsymbolic methods in raw performance, it remains competitive while simultaneously providing richer interpretability. The experiments demonstrate that the combined language model + LTN architecture can effectively capture both the textual semantics and the domain-specific logical constraints required for document validation.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that neurosymbolic AI, specifically the combination of language models with Logic Tensor Networks, is well-suited for validating offer documents in regulated domains that require legal verifiability and transparency. The approach offers a practical trade-off: performance comparable to existing methods, but with substantial gains in interpretability, modular predicate extraction, and built-in support for explainable AI. This makes it a promising direction for deploying AI in high-stakes, regulation-heavy public sector applications, and suggests future work in extending the rule base, improving predicate extraction, and generalizing the method to other document-centric decision tasks.

title: AgentGL: Towards Agentic Graph Learning with LLMs via Reinforcement Learning
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) increasingly rely on agentic capabilities-iterative retrieval, tool use, and decision-making-to overcome the limits of static, parametric knowledge. Yet existing agentic frameworks treat external information as unstructured text and fail to leverage the topological dependencies inherent in real-world data. To bridge this gap, we introduce Agentic Graph Learning (AGL), a paradigm that reframes graph learning as an interleaved process of topology-aware navigation and LLM-based inference. Specifically, we propose AgentGL, the first reinforcement learning (RL)-driven framework for AGL. AgentGL equips an LLM agent with graph-native tools for multi-scale exploration, regulates tool usage via search-constrained thinking to balance accuracy and efficiency, and employs a graph-conditioned curriculum RL strategy to stabilize long-horizon policy learning without step-wise supervision. Across diverse Text-Attributed Graph (TAG) benchmarks and multiple LLM backbones, AgentGL substantially outperforms strong GraphLLMs and GraphRAG baselines, achieving absolute improvements of up to 17.5% in node classification and 28.4% in link prediction. These results demonstrate that AGL is a promising frontier for enabling LLMs to autonomously navigate and reason over complex relational environments. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/sunyuanfu/AgentGL.

AI.tldr: Introduces Agentic Graph Learning (AGL) and AgentGL, an RL-driven framework that lets LLM agents navigate and reason over graphs using graph-aware tools, achieving large gains on text-attributed graph tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based agents use tools and retrieval but typically treat external data as flat, unstructured text, ignoring the graph structure present in many real-world domains (e.g., knowledge graphs, social networks). Current GraphLLMs and GraphRAG systems underuse topological dependencies and often lack principled agentic control over exploration. The paper aims to unify agentic LLMs with graph learning so that models can explicitly exploit topology while autonomously exploring and reasoning over relational environments.
AI.method: Define Agentic Graph Learning (AGL), where an LLM agent iteratively navigates a graph and performs inference using graph-aware tools. Propose AgentGL, the first RL-based framework for AGL: (1) augment an LLM with graph-native tools that support multi-scale exploration of text-attributed graphs; (2) introduce search-constrained thinking to regulate how much and how often tools are used, trading off accuracy and efficiency; and (3) design a graph-conditioned curriculum RL strategy to train the navigation policy over long horizons without step-wise supervision, stabilizing learning. The framework is instantiated with multiple LLM backbones and evaluated on standard TAG benchmarks.
AI.result: On diverse text-attributed graph benchmarks, AgentGL consistently outperforms strong GraphLLM and GraphRAG baselines across tasks such as node classification and link prediction. Reported absolute performance gains reach up to 17.5 percentage points for node classification and 28.4 percentage points for link prediction, demonstrating both higher accuracy and more effective use of graph structure compared to prior methods.
AI.conclusion: Agentic Graph Learning, operationalized via AgentGL, effectively combines LLM-based agents with graph-structured reasoning. By allowing LLMs to navigate graphs using specialized tools and training them with RL and curriculum strategies, the approach substantially improves performance on TAG tasks. The results suggest that giving LLM agents explicit, topology-aware control over exploration is a promising direction for enabling autonomous reasoning in complex relational environments, and the released code can facilitate further research in this area.

title: Evaluating Learner Representations for Differentiation Prior to Instructional Outcomes
summary: Learner representations play a central role in educational AI systems, yet it is often unclear whether they preserve meaningful differences between students when instructional outcomes are unavailable or highly context-dependent. This work examines how to evaluate learner representations based on whether they retain separation between learners under a shared comparison rule. We introduce distinctiveness, a representation-level measure that evaluates how each learner differs from others in the cohort using pairwise distances, without requiring clustering, labels, or task-specific evaluation. Using student-authored questions collected through a conversational AI agent in an online learning environment, we compare representations based on individual questions with representations that aggregate patterns across a student's interactions over time. Results show that learner-level representations yield higher separation, stronger clustering structure, and more reliable pairwise discrimination than interaction-level representations. These findings demonstrate that learner representations can be evaluated independently of instructional outcomes and provide a practical pre-deployment criterion using distinctiveness as a diagnostic metric for assessing whether a representation supports differentiated modeling or personalization.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a label-free way to evaluate how well learner representations distinguish among students, introducing a metric called distinctiveness based on pairwise distances, and shows that aggregated learner-level representations are more discriminative than single-interaction ones.
AI.motivation: In educational AI, learner representations are critical for personalization and modeling, but are typically evaluated only through downstream instructional outcomes, which can be unavailable, delayed, or highly context-dependent. There is a need for a general, task-agnostic way to assess whether a representation meaningfully preserves differences between learners, especially before deployment and without relying on labels, clustering assumptions, or specific tasks.
AI.method: The authors define a new representation-level metric called distinctiveness that quantifies how well each learner is separated from others in the cohort using pairwise distances in the embedding space. This metric does not require predefined clusters, labels, or task performance. They then construct two types of representations from data consisting of student-authored questions collected via a conversational AI tutor: (1) interaction-level representations based on individual questions, and (2) learner-level representations that aggregate patterns across each student's interactions over time. They apply the distinctiveness measure to compare these representations and analyze separation, clustering structure, and pairwise discrimination reliability.
AI.result: Empirical analysis shows that learner-level, temporally aggregated representations achieve higher distinctiveness than interaction-level ones. These learner-level representations exhibit clearer separations between students, more pronounced and coherent clustering structures in the representation space, and more stable, reliable pairwise discrimination between learners. In contrast, representations derived from single interactions are less effective at maintaining meaningful differences among learners.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that learner representations can be effectively evaluated without relying on instructional outcome data by using the proposed distinctiveness metric. Distinctiveness serves as a practical, pre-deployment diagnostic for checking whether a representation space supports differentiated modeling and personalization. Aggregating interaction data into learner-level representations is recommended, as it yields more distinctive and discriminative embeddings than interaction-level representations, thereby better supporting personalized educational AI applications.

title: ResearchEVO: An End-to-End Framework for Automated Scientific Discovery and Documentation
summary: An important recurring pattern in scientific breakthroughs is a two-stage process: an initial phase of undirected experimentation that yields an unexpected finding, followed by a retrospective phase that explains why the finding works and situates it within existing theory. We present ResearchEVO, an end-to-end framework that computationally instantiates this discover-then-explain paradigm. The Evolution Phase employs LLM-guided bi-dimensional co-evolution -- simultaneously optimizing both algorithmic logic and overall architecture -- to search the space of code implementations purely by fitness, without requiring any understanding of the solutions it produces. The Writing Phase then takes the best-performing algorithm and autonomously generates a complete, publication-ready research paper through sentence-level retrieval-augmented generation with explicit anti-hallucination verification and automated experiment design. To our knowledge, ResearchEVO is the first system to cover this full pipeline end to end: no prior work jointly performs principled algorithm evolution and literature-grounded scientific documentation. We validate the framework on two cross-disciplinary scientific problems -- Quantum Error Correction using real Google quantum hardware data, and Physics-Informed Neural Networks -- where the Evolution Phase discovered human-interpretable algorithmic mechanisms that had not been previously proposed in the respective domain literatures. In both cases, the Writing Phase autonomously produced compilable LaTeX manuscripts that correctly grounded these blind discoveries in existing theory via RAG, with zero fabricated citations.

AI.tldr: ResearchEVO is an end-to-end system that first evolves novel algorithms using LLM-guided code evolution and then automatically writes full, correctly grounded research papers about the discovered methods.
AI.motivation: Scientific breakthroughs often follow a discover-then-explain pattern: an unexpected empirical result is found first, and only later is it theoretically explained and contextualized. Existing AI research tools typically support isolated parts of this workflow (e.g., code generation, hyperparameter search, or paper drafting) but do not model the entire pipeline from blind exploration to literature-grounded explanation. There is a need for a system that can autonomously search for effective algorithmic solutions without prior theoretical understanding and then translate those empirical discoveries into rigorous, non-hallucinated scientific documentation connected to existing theory and literature.
AI.method: The paper proposes ResearchEVO, a two-phase framework. (1) Evolution Phase: uses large language models to guide a bi-dimensional co-evolution process that simultaneously mutates and optimizes both algorithmic logic and overall architecture of code. The search is driven purely by fitness signals (performance on target tasks), without encoding prior theoretical knowledge or interpretability constraints. (2) Writing Phase: takes the best evolved algorithm and automatically generates a full research manuscript. This phase uses sentence-level retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to ground each claim in relevant literature, includes explicit anti-hallucination verification to avoid fabricated citations or unsupported statements, and designs and reports experiments automatically. The system produces a compilable LaTeX paper as the final output.
AI.result: ResearchEVO is evaluated on two cross-disciplinary tasks: Quantum Error Correction (using real Google quantum hardware data) and Physics-Informed Neural Networks. In both domains, the Evolution Phase discovers new, human-interpretable algorithmic mechanisms that, according to the authors, are absent from existing domain literature. The Writing Phase then generates complete LaTeX manuscripts that successfully explain these discoveries in terms of current theory, correctly cite related work via RAG, and avoid fabricated citations. The produced algorithms achieve strong empirical performance, demonstrating that the blind evolutionary search can yield practically valuable, novel methods.
AI.conclusion: The work concludes that ResearchEVO successfully instantiates a computational analog of the discover-then-explain pattern typical of many scientific breakthroughs. By tightly integrating LLM-guided algorithm evolution with literature-grounded, hallucination-checked scientific writing, the framework provides an end-to-end pipeline from undirected code exploration to publication-ready documentation. The authors position this as the first system to jointly evolve new algorithms and automatically contextualize them in existing theory, suggesting a path toward more autonomous, cross-disciplinary scientific discovery and reporting.

title: LoRM: Learning the Language of Rotating Machinery for Self-Supervised Condition Monitoring
summary: We present LoRM (Language of Rotating Machinery), a self-supervised framework for multi-modal rotating-machinery signal understanding and real-time condition monitoring. LoRM is built on the idea that rotating-machinery signals can be viewed as a machine language: local signals can be tokenised into discrete symbolic units, and their future evolution can be predicted from observed multi-sensor context. Unlike conventional signal-processing methods that rely on hand-crafted transforms and features, LoRM reformulates multi-modal sensor data as a token-based sequence-prediction problem. For each data window, the observed context segment is retained in continuous form, while the future target segment of each sensing channel is quantised into a discrete token. Then, efficient knowledge transfer is achieved by partially fine-tuning a general-purpose pre-trained language model on industrial signals, avoiding the need to train a large model from scratch. Finally, condition monitoring is performed by tracking token-prediction errors as a health indicator, where increasing errors indicate degradation. In-situ tool condition monitoring (TCM) experiments demonstrate stable real-time tracking and strong cross-tool generalisation, showing that LoRM provides a practical bridge between language modelling and industrial signal analysis. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/Q159753258/LormPHM.

AI.tldr: LoRM treats rotating-machine sensor signals like a language, tokenizing future signal segments and using a pre-trained language model to predict them for real-time condition monitoring via prediction errors.
AI.motivation: Rotating machinery is ubiquitous in industry, and its reliability depends on effective condition monitoring. Traditional approaches rely on hand-crafted signal processing and features, are modality-specific, and often require large labelled datasets and model training from scratch. There is a need for a general, scalable, and label-efficient method that can leverage recent advances in self-supervised learning and large language models to handle multi-modal sensor data and enable robust real-time health monitoring.
AI.method: LoRM reframes multi-sensor rotating-machinery data as a sequence prediction task analogous to language modelling. For each sliding time window, the past context segment from all sensors is kept in continuous form, while the future segment of each sensor channel is quantised into discrete tokens via a learned or predefined codebook. These tokens represent a ‘machine language’ describing the evolution of the signals. A general-purpose large language model, pre-trained on text, is then partially fine-tuned on this tokenised industrial data, rather than being trained from scratch. At inference time, the model predicts the next tokens given the observed context. The framework monitors the discrepancy between predicted and actual tokens as a signal-health metric.
AI.result: In in-situ tool condition monitoring (TCM) experiments, LoRM achieves stable, real-time tracking of machine condition and strong generalisation across different tools. The token-prediction error reliably reflects degradation, and the approach is computationally efficient because it builds on a pre-trained language model rather than a bespoke large architecture. These results suggest that the token-based, language-inspired treatment of sensor data is effective for practical industrial monitoring scenarios.
AI.conclusion: LoRM successfully bridges language modelling and industrial signal analysis by interpreting rotating-machinery signals as a discrete language and leveraging pre-trained language models for self-supervised condition monitoring. Its token-prediction-error-based health indicator enables real-time tracking and generalises well across tools, offering a practical and scalable alternative to hand-crafted signal-processing pipelines. The open-source release facilitates further research and industrial deployment.

title: Label Effects: Shared Heuristic Reliance in Trust Assessment by Humans and LLM-as-a-Judge
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as automated evaluators (LLM-as-a-Judge). This work challenges its reliability by showing that trust judgments by LLMs are biased by disclosed source labels. Using a counterfactual design, we find that both humans and LLM judges assign higher trust to information labeled as human-authored than to the same content labeled as AI-generated. Eye-tracking data reveal that humans rely heavily on source labels as heuristic cues for judgments. We analyze LLM internal states during judgment. Across label conditions, models allocate denser attention to the label region than the content region, and this label dominance is stronger under Human labels than AI labels, consistent with the human gaze patterns. Besides, decision uncertainty measured by logits is higher under AI labels than Human labels. These results indicate that the source label is a salient heuristic cue for both humans and LLMs. It raises validity concerns for label-sensitive LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation, and we cautiously raise that aligning models with human preferences may propagate human heuristic reliance into models, motivating debiased evaluation and alignment.

AI.tldr: LLMs used as evaluators (“LLM-as-a-Judge”) systematically trust text labeled as human-written more than identical text labeled as AI-generated, mirroring human bias and raising concerns about evaluation validity and alignment practices.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used to evaluate other models, content, or systems, their judgments are assumed to be objective and label-agnostic. However, in real-world settings, information often comes with source labels (e.g., “written by a human” or “generated by AI”), which can bias human judgments. It is critical to know whether LLMs exhibit similar label-driven biases, because this would compromise the fairness and reliability of LLM-as-a-Judge frameworks and could also reveal how aligning LLMs to human preferences might import human cognitive heuristics into these models.
AI.method: The paper uses a counterfactual experimental design where the same pieces of information are presented with different source labels (human-authored vs AI-generated) to both human participants and LLM evaluators. For humans, eye-tracking is employed to measure visual attention to different parts of the stimulus, especially the source label region versus the content region. For LLMs, the authors inspect internal attention patterns across tokens to see how much attention is devoted to label tokens relative to content tokens. They also analyze the output logits to estimate decision uncertainty under different label conditions.
AI.result: Both humans and LLM evaluators assign higher trust to content labeled as human-authored than to identical content labeled as AI-generated. Eye-tracking shows humans fixate more on the source labels and use them as heuristic cues. For LLMs, attention maps reveal systematically denser attention on label tokens than on content tokens, with greater label dominance when the label is “Human” compared to “AI,” paralleling human gaze behavior. Additionally, LLM output logits indicate higher decision uncertainty when the content is labeled as AI-generated than when labeled as human-authored.
AI.conclusion: Source labels function as strong heuristic cues for trust judgments in both humans and LLMs. This shared reliance on labels introduces systematic bias into LLM-as-a-Judge evaluations whenever source labels are present or can be inferred, undermining the validity and robustness of such evaluation setups. The findings suggest that alignment procedures that tune models to human preferences may unintentionally embed human-like heuristic dependence into LLMs. The paper argues for the need to design debiased evaluation protocols and alignment methods that reduce or control for label sensitivity in both human and model-based judgments.

title: Understanding Performance Gap Between Parallel and Sequential Sampling in Large Reasoning Models
summary: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) have shown remarkable performance on challenging questions, such as math and coding. However, to obtain a high quality solution, one may need to sample more than once. In principal, there are two sampling strategies that can be composed to form more complex processes: sequential sampling and parallel sampling. In this paper, we first compare these two approaches with rigor, and observe, aligned with previous works, that parallel sampling seems to outperform sequential sampling even though the latter should have more representation power. To understand the underline reasons, we make three hypothesis on the reason behind this behavior: (i) parallel sampling outperforms due to the aggregator operator; (ii) sequential sampling is harmed by needing to use longer contexts; (iii) sequential sampling leads to less exploration due to conditioning on previous answers. The empirical evidence on various model families and sizes (Qwen3, DeepSeek-R1 distilled models, Gemini 2.5) and question domains (math and coding) suggests that the aggregation and context length do not seem to be the main culprit behind the performance gap. In contrast, the lack of exploration seems to play a considerably larger role, and we argue that this is one main cause for the performance gap.

AI.tldr: The paper compares sequential and parallel sampling strategies for Large Reasoning Models and finds that parallel sampling surprisingly works better mainly because sequential sampling reduces exploration when conditioning on prior samples, rather than due to aggregation or context-length issues.
AI.motivation: Although sequential sampling has greater theoretical expressive power than parallel sampling, in practice parallel sampling empirically performs better for LRMs on complex tasks like math and coding. The authors want to rigorously investigate why this counterintuitive performance gap occurs and what underlying mechanisms cause parallel sampling to outperform.
AI.method: The authors formalize and compare sequential versus parallel sampling strategies for LRMs, then propose three hypotheses explaining why parallel sampling might outperform: advantages from the aggregator, harms from longer context in sequential runs, and reduced exploration due to conditioning on previous answers. They run systematic experiments across different model families (Qwen3, DeepSeek-R1 distilled models, Gemini 2.5) and domains (math and coding), ablate each factor, and analyze performance impacts to test the hypotheses.
AI.result: Empirical results show that differences in aggregation mechanisms and the longer context required for sequential sampling do not primarily explain the superiority of parallel sampling. Instead, conditioning sequential samples on earlier answers significantly reduces exploration, which more strongly correlates with the observed performance gap between the two strategies.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that the main reason parallel sampling often outperforms sequential sampling in LRMs is that sequential conditioning constrains exploration of the solution space, harming performance. Aggregation design and context length are comparatively minor factors. Encouraging or preserving exploration in multi-sample reasoning procedures is therefore crucial for improving LRM performance.

title: Beyond Behavior: Why AI Evaluation Needs a Cognitive Revolution
summary: In 1950, Alan Turing proposed replacing the question "Can machines think?" with a behavioral test: if a machine's outputs are indistinguishable from those of a thinking being, the question of whether it truly thinks can be set aside. This paper argues that Turing's move was not only a pragmatic simplification but also an epistemological commitment, a decision about what kind of evidence counts as relevant to intelligence attribution, and that this commitment has quietly constrained AI research for seven decades. We trace how Turing's behavioral epistemology became embedded in the field's evaluative infrastructure, rendering unaskable a class of questions about process, mechanism, and internal organization that cognitive psychology, neuroscience, and related disciplines learned to ask. We draw a structural parallel to the behaviorist-to-cognitivist transition in psychology: just as psychology's commitment to studying only observable behavior prevented it from asking productive questions about internal mental processes until that commitment was abandoned, AI's commitment to behavioral evaluation prevents it from distinguishing between systems that achieve identical outputs through fundamentally different computational processes, a distinction on which intelligence attribution depends. We argue that the field requires an epistemological transition comparable to the cognitive revolution: not an abandonment of behavioral evidence, but a recognition that behavioral evidence alone is insufficient for the construct claims the field wishes to make. We articulate what a post-behaviorist epistemology for AI would involve and identify the specific questions it would make askable that the field currently has no way to ask.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that Turing’s behavior-based view of intelligence has quietly shaped AI for 70+ years and now limits progress; it calls for a new, post-behaviorist way of evaluating AI that looks inside systems, not just at their outputs.
AI.motivation: To show that Turing’s famous shift from asking “Can machines think?” to using a behavioral test (the Turing test) was not just a convenient reframing, but a deep epistemological stance about what counts as evidence for intelligence. The authors want to explain how this stance became built into AI’s evaluation methods and why that now restricts the kinds of scientific questions AI can ask, especially compared with fields like cognitive psychology and neuroscience that have moved beyond pure behaviorism.
AI.method: Conceptual and historical analysis: (1) reinterpret Turing’s original proposal as an epistemological commitment rather than a mere testing heuristic; (2) trace how behavior-based evaluation became institutionalized in AI’s practices and benchmarks; (3) draw an analogy to the shift in psychology from behaviorism to cognitivism to illuminate what is currently missing in AI; and (4) sketch a new, post-behaviorist epistemology by specifying the kinds of internal-process-focused questions that AI should be able to pose and answer.
AI.result: They show that AI’s dominant evaluation paradigm—judging systems mainly by external behavior—derives from Turing’s behavioral epistemology and has made it difficult to ask or answer questions about internal computational processes, mechanisms, and organization. They demonstrate that, under the current paradigm, AI cannot systematically distinguish between systems that produce the same outputs via different internal processes, even though such differences matter for attributions of intelligence. They identify a parallel with psychology’s earlier behaviorist limitations and clarify the space of currently ‘unaskable’ questions in AI.
AI.conclusion: AI needs an “epistemological revolution” analogous to psychology’s cognitive revolution: behavior remains important evidence, but it must be supplemented with systematic inquiry into internal structure and computation if the field is to make defensible claims about intelligence. The authors outline a post-behaviorist epistemology for AI, emphasizing process- and mechanism-level investigation and specifying new research questions that such an epistemology would license—questions that current, purely behavioral evaluation frameworks cannot properly formulate or address.

title: Mechanistic Circuit-Based Knowledge Editing in Large Language Models
summary: Deploying Large Language Models (LLMs) in real-world dynamic environments raises the challenge of updating their pre-trained knowledge. While existing knowledge editing methods can reliably patch isolated facts, they frequently suffer from a "Reasoning Gap", where the model recalls the edited fact but fails to utilize it in multi-step reasoning chains. To bridge this gap, we introduce MCircKE (\underline{M}echanistic \underline{Circ}uit-based \underline{K}nowledge \underline{E}diting), a novel framework that enables a precise "map-and-adapt" editing procedure. MCircKE first identifies the causal circuits responsible for a specific reasoning task, capturing both the storage of the fact and the routing of its logical consequences. It then surgically update parameters exclusively within this mapped circuit. Extensive experiments on the MQuAKE-3K benchmark demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed method for multi-hop reasoning in knowledge editing.

AI.tldr: A new method, MCircKE, performs knowledge editing in LLMs by locating and modifying the exact internal circuits used for reasoning, improving multi-hop reasoning after edits.
AI.motivation: Existing knowledge editing techniques can change specific facts in an LLM but often fail when those facts must be used in multi-step reasoning, creating a “Reasoning Gap.” There is a need for a method that not only updates factual recall but also preserves and updates the reasoning processes that depend on those facts, especially for dynamic, real-world deployment of LLMs.
AI.method: MCircKE proposes a mechanistic, circuit-based framework. It first identifies the causal circuits inside the model that support a target reasoning task, including where the fact is stored and how its consequences are routed through the network. Then it performs surgical parameter updates restricted to this mapped circuit rather than editing the whole model or using broad fine-tuning.
AI.result: On the MQuAKE-3K benchmark, which focuses on multi-hop reasoning in knowledge editing scenarios, MCircKE shows superior performance compared with existing methods, demonstrating better propagation of edited knowledge through reasoning chains.
AI.conclusion: Mechanistic circuit-based knowledge editing, as implemented by MCircKE, can more effectively update LLM knowledge in a way that supports multi-step reasoning, narrowing the Reasoning Gap and enabling more reliable model updates in dynamic environments.

title: PECKER: A Precisely Efficient Critical Knowledge Erasure Recipe For Machine Unlearning in Diffusion Models
summary: Machine unlearning (MU) has become a critical technique for GenAI models' safe and compliant operation. While existing MU methods are effective, most impose prohibitive training time and computational overhead. Our analysis suggests the root cause lies in poorly directed gradient updates, which reduce training efficiency and destabilize convergence. To mitigate these issues, we propose PECKER, an efficient MU approach that matches or outperforms prevailing methods. Within a distillation framework, PECKER introduces a saliency mask to prioritize updates to parameters that contribute most to forgetting the targeted data, thereby reducing unnecessary gradient computation and shortening overall training time without sacrificing unlearning efficacy. Our method generates samples that unlearn related class or concept more quickly, while closely aligning with the true image distribution on CIFAR-10 and STL-10 datasets, achieving shorter training times for both class forgetting and concept forgetting.

AI.tldr: Efficient machine unlearning method PECKER that speeds up and stabilizes unlearning for GenAI models via saliency-guided parameter updates within a distillation framework.
AI.motivation: Existing machine unlearning approaches are computationally expensive and suffer from inefficient, poorly directed gradient updates that slow training and destabilize convergence, limiting their practicality for safe and compliant GenAI deployment.
AI.method: They propose PECKER, a distillation-based machine unlearning framework that introduces a saliency mask to focus gradient updates on parameters most responsible for forgetting the targeted data, thereby pruning unnecessary computations while maintaining unlearning quality. The method also includes generating samples that accelerate forgetting of related classes or concepts while staying close to the real data distribution.
AI.result: On CIFAR-10 and STL-10 for both class forgetting and concept forgetting, PECKER matches or outperforms state-of-the-art unlearning approaches while significantly reducing training time and computational overhead, and produces unlearning samples that better align with the true image distribution.
AI.conclusion: By directing gradients via a saliency mask within a distillation setup, PECKER offers a more efficient and stable machine unlearning procedure that preserves or improves unlearning performance while substantially cutting training time, making MU more practical for GenAI models in real-world, compliance-critical scenarios.

title: FRENCH-YMCA: A FRENCH Corpus meeting the language needs of Youth, froM Children to Adolescents
summary: In this paper, we introduce the French-YMCA corpus, a new linguistic resource specifically tailored for children and adolescents. The motivation for building this corpus is clear: children have unique language requirements, as their language skills are in constant evolution and differ from those of adults. With an extensive collection of 39,200 text files, the French-YMCA corpus encompasses a total of 22,471,898 words. It distinguishes itself through its diverse sources, consistent grammar and spelling, and the commitment to providing open online accessibility for all. Such corpus can serve as the foundation for training language models that understand and anticipate youth's language, thereby enhancing the quality of digital interactions and ensuring that responses and suggestions are age-appropriate and adapted to the comprehension level of users of this age.

AI.tldr: Introduction of the French-YMCA corpus, a large, open French text corpus tailored to children and adolescents, designed to train age-appropriate language technologies.
AI.motivation: Children and adolescents have linguistic needs and developmental trajectories that differ from adults, but most language resources and models are built on adult data. There is a lack of large, clean, and accessible French corpora specifically representing youth language to support research and the development of digital tools that can interact with young users appropriately.
AI.method: The authors compiled and cleaned a large collection of French texts aimed at or produced by children and adolescents, resulting in 39,200 text files totaling about 22.5 million words. They ensured diversity of sources, maintained consistent grammar and spelling, and prepared the corpus for open online access, making it suitable for training and evaluating language models for youth-oriented applications.
AI.result: The outcome is the French-YMCA corpus: 39,200 French text files with 22,471,898 words, characterized by source diversity, normalized grammar and spelling, and open accessibility. This resource can be directly used to train or fine-tune language models that better capture the language use and comprehension levels of children and adolescents.
AI.conclusion: The French-YMCA corpus fills an important gap in linguistic resources by providing a large, high-quality, and openly accessible French corpus tailored to children and adolescents. It offers a solid foundation for building language technologies that understand and anticipate youth language, helping create digital interactions and educational tools that are more age-appropriate, understandable, and supportive for young users.

title: CuraLight: Debate-Guided Data Curation for LLM-Centered Traffic Signal Control
summary:
Traffic signal control (TSC) is a core component of intelligent transportation systems (ITS), aiming to reduce congestion, emissions, and travel time. Recent approaches based on reinforcement learning (RL) and large language models (LLMs) have improved adaptivity, but still suffer from limited interpretability, insufficient interaction data, and weak generalization to heterogeneous intersections.
  This paper proposes CuraLight, an LLM-centered framework where an RL agent assists the fine-tuning of an LLM-based traffic signal controller. The RL agent explores traffic environments and generates high-quality interaction trajectories, which are converted into prompt-response pairs for imitation fine-tuning. A multi-LLM ensemble deliberation system further evaluates candidate signal timing actions through structured debate, providing preference-aware supervision signals for training.
  Experiments conducted in SUMO across heterogeneous real-world networks from Jinan, Hangzhou, and Yizhuang demonstrate that CuraLight consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, reducing average travel time by 5.34 percent, average queue length by 5.14 percent, and average waiting time by 7.02 percent. The results highlight the effectiveness of combining RL-assisted exploration with deliberation-based data curation for scalable and interpretable traffic signal control.

AI.tldr: CuraLight is an LLM-centered traffic signal control framework that uses an RL agent to generate high-quality trajectories and an LLM ensemble deliberation system to improve signal timing decisions, achieving better traffic efficiency than state-of-the-art methods in real-world simulations.
AI.motivation: Existing traffic signal control methods using RL and LLMs improve adaptivity but face challenges: low interpretability, limited high-quality interaction data for training, and poor generalization across heterogeneous intersections and networks. There is a need for a scalable, interpretable controller that can perform well across diverse real-world traffic environments.
AI.method: CuraLight places a large language model at the center of the traffic signal controller and uses a reinforcement learning agent as an assistant. The RL agent interacts with traffic simulators (SUMO) to explore and gather high-quality control trajectories. These trajectories are transformed into prompt-response pairs to imitation fine-tune the LLM controller. In addition, a multi-LLM ensemble deliberation system conducts structured debates over candidate signal timing actions, from which preference-aware supervision signals are derived to further train and refine the LLM controller.
AI.result: In experiments on heterogeneous real-world traffic networks from Jinan, Hangzhou, and Yizhuang within the SUMO simulator, CuraLight systematically outperforms state-of-the-art traffic signal control baselines. It achieves reductions of approximately 5.34% in average travel time, 5.14% in average queue length, and 7.02% in average waiting time, indicating improved traffic efficiency and network performance.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that combining RL-assisted exploration with deliberation-based data curation around an LLM core produces a more scalable, interpretable, and effective traffic signal control system. CuraLight demonstrates that LLMs, when supported by RL-generated trajectories and multi-LLM deliberation, can generalize well across heterogeneous intersections and yield measurable improvements in key traffic performance metrics.

title: FrontierFinance: A Long-Horizon Computer-Use Benchmark of Real-World Financial Tasks
summary: As concerns surrounding AI-driven labor displacement intensify in knowledge-intensive sectors, existing benchmarks fail to measure performance on tasks that define practical professional expertise. Finance, in particular, has been identified as a domain with high AI exposure risk, yet lacks robust benchmarks to track real-world developments. This gap is compounded by the absence of clear accountability mechanisms in current Large Language Model (LLM) deployments. To address this, we introduce FrontierFinance, a long-horizon benchmark of 25 complex financial modeling tasks across five core finance models, requiring an average of over 18 hours of skilled human labor per task to complete. Developed with financial professionals, the benchmark reflects industry-standard financial modeling workflows and is paired with detailed rubrics for structured evaluation. We engage human experts to define the tasks, create rubrics, grade LLMs, and perform the tasks themselves as human baselines. We demonstrate that our human experts both receive higher scores on average, and are more likely to provide client-ready outputs than current state-of-the-art systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FrontierFinance, a benchmark of complex, long-horizon financial modeling tasks to evaluate LLMs against skilled human professionals.
AI.motivation: There is growing concern about AI displacing labor in knowledge-intensive fields like finance, but existing benchmarks do not capture the complex, long-duration tasks that constitute real professional work. Additionally, current LLM deployments lack clear accountability mechanisms, and there is no robust way to track AI progress or compare it meaningfully with human experts in real-world financial modeling.
AI.method: The authors construct FrontierFinance, a benchmark comprising 25 complex tasks spanning five core financial models, each typically requiring over 18 hours of skilled human work. They collaborate with financial professionals to design realistic, industry-standard modeling workflows and detailed evaluation rubrics. Human experts both perform the tasks to provide baselines and use the rubrics to grade LLM-generated outputs.
AI.result: On the FrontierFinance benchmark, human financial experts achieve higher quantitative scores than state-of-the-art LLMs and are more likely to produce outputs deemed client-ready. This demonstrates a substantial performance gap between current LLMs and professional human practitioners on complex, practical financial modeling tasks.
AI.conclusion: FrontierFinance offers a rigorous, long-horizon benchmark that better reflects real-world financial modeling practice and enables structured, accountable evaluation of LLM performance. Current LLMs still lag behind human experts on these demanding tasks, underscoring the need for further advances before relying on them for high-stakes professional finance work.

title: LUDOBENCH: Evaluating LLM Behavioural Decision-Making Through Spot-Based Board Game Scenarios in Ludo
summary: We introduce LudoBench, a benchmark for evaluating LLM strategic reasoning in Ludo, a stochastic multi-agent board game whose dice mechanics, piece capture, safe-square navigation, and home-path progression introduce meaningful planning complexity. LudoBench comprises 480 handcrafted spot scenarios across 12 behaviorally distinct decision categories, each isolating a specific strategic choice. We additionally contribute a fully functional 4-player Ludo simulator supporting Random, Heuristic, Game-Theory, and LLM agents. The game-theory agent uses Expectiminimax search with depth-limited lookahead to provide a principled strategic ceiling beyond greedy heuristics. Evaluating six models spanning four model families, we find that all models agree with the game-theory baseline only 40-46% of the time. Models split into distinct behavioral archetypes: finishers that complete pieces but neglect development, and builders that develop but never finish. Each archetype captures only half of the game theory strategy. Models also display measurable behavioral shifts under history-conditioned grudge framing on identical board states, revealing prompt-sensitivity as a key vulnerability. LudoBench provides a lightweight and interpretable framework for benchmarking LLM strategic reasoning under uncertainty. All code, the spot dataset (480 entries) and model outputs are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/LudoBench-5CBF/

AI.tldr: LudoBench is a benchmark and simulator to evaluate how well large language models (LLMs) perform strategic reasoning in the stochastic, multi-agent board game Ludo.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM benchmarks only partially test strategic reasoning, especially under uncertainty and multi-agent interaction. Ludo, with its dice rolls, captures, safe squares, and home progression, offers a compact but rich environment where planning, risk assessment, and opponent modeling matter. The authors want a controlled, interpretable way to measure whether LLMs can align with principled game-theoretic strategies rather than shallow heuristics or prompt artifacts.
AI.method: They design LudoBench, a suite of 480 carefully constructed board positions ("spot scenarios") categorized into 12 distinct decision types, each highlighting a specific strategic dilemma. They also build a full 4-player Ludo simulator that supports different agent types: a random agent, a heuristic agent, a game-theoretic agent, and LLM-based agents. The game-theoretic agent relies on Expectiminimax search with depth-limited lookahead to model optimal play under stochastic outcomes, and it serves as a strategic reference. Six LLMs from four model families are prompted to choose moves in each scenario, and their choices are compared to the game-theoretic agent. The authors further probe prompt sensitivity by conditioning the same board states with "grudge" histories to see if LLM behavior shifts despite identical objective positions.
AI.result: Across the benchmark, all tested LLMs agree with the game-theoretic agent on only about 40–46% of decisions, indicating significant deviations from principled strategy. Behavior clusters into two archetypes: “finishers,” which prioritize quickly completing pieces but underinvest in broader board development, and “builders,” which develop many pieces but fail to convert them into finished ones. Each archetype captures roughly half of the strategic dimensions reflected in the game-theory baseline. Additionally, when the same board positions are framed with different history narratives (e.g., grudges), models change their decisions in measurable ways, exposing high sensitivity to superficial prompt context. The benchmark and simulator, with code and outputs, are publicly released for reproducibility.
AI.conclusion: LudoBench demonstrates that current LLMs struggle to consistently execute game-theoretically aligned strategies in a simple but nontrivial stochastic board game. Their strategies are incomplete, biased toward certain patterns (finishing vs building), and unstable under irrelevant narrative framing, highlighting weaknesses in robust strategic reasoning under uncertainty. The benchmark offers a lightweight, interpretable testbed for future work on planning, decision-making, and alignment of LLMs with principled strategies in multi-agent stochastic environments.

title: "I See What You Did There": Can Large Vision-Language Models Understand Multimodal Puns?
summary: Puns are a common form of rhetorical wordplay that exploits polysemy and phonetic similarity to create humor. In multimodal puns, visual and textual elements synergize to ground the literal sense and evoke the figurative meaning simultaneously. Although Vision-Language Models (VLMs) are widely used in multimodal understanding and generation, their ability to understand puns has not been systematically studied due to a scarcity of rigorous benchmarks. To address this, we first propose a multimodal pun generation pipeline. We then introduce MultiPun, a dataset comprising diverse types of puns alongside adversarial non-pun distractors. Our evaluation reveals that most models struggle to distinguish genuine puns from these distractors. Moreover, we propose both prompt-level and model-level strategies to enhance pun comprehension, with an average improvement of 16.5% in F1 scores. Our findings provide valuable insights for developing future VLMs that master the subtleties of human-like humor via cross-modal reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how well vision-language models understand multimodal puns, builds a new dataset and generation pipeline, shows current models perform poorly, and proposes methods that significantly improve their pun comprehension.
AI.motivation: Multimodal puns, which combine visual and textual cues, are prevalent in human communication and humor, but there is no rigorous benchmark to test whether modern vision-language models can understand such puns. Without this, it is unclear how well these models capture subtle, human-like cross-modal reasoning involved in wordplay.
AI.method: The authors design a multimodal pun generation pipeline to systematically create examples, and then construct MultiPun, a dataset containing different types of multimodal puns along with carefully crafted adversarial non-pun distractors. They evaluate multiple existing vision-language models on the task of distinguishing real puns from distractors. Additionally, they propose strategies at the prompt level (better instructions, cues, or decomposed reasoning) and at the model level (likely fine-tuning or adaptation) to improve pun understanding and measure the gains using F1 scores.
AI.result: Experiments show that most current vision-language models have difficulty telling genuine multimodal puns apart from well-designed non-pun distractors, indicating weak capability in this kind of humorous, cross-modal reasoning. Applying the proposed prompt-level and model-level enhancements leads to substantial performance gains, with an average improvement of 16.5 percentage points in F1 score across evaluated models.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that existing vision-language models are far from mastering multimodal pun comprehension. The new MultiPun dataset and generation pipeline provide a benchmark for systematic evaluation. The improved methods and their documented gains offer concrete directions for building future models that better grasp subtle, human-like humor through deeper cross-modal reasoning.

title: QA-MoE: Towards a Continuous Reliability Spectrum with Quality-Aware Mixture of Experts for Robust Multimodal Sentiment Analysis
summary: Multimodal Sentiment Analysis (MSA) aims to infer human sentiment from textual, acoustic, and visual signals. In real-world scenarios, however, multimodal inputs are often compromised by dynamic noise or modality missingness. Existing methods typically treat these imperfections as discrete cases or assume fixed corruption ratios, which limits their adaptability to continuously varying reliability conditions. To address this, we first introduce a Continuous Reliability Spectrum to unify missingness and quality degradation into a single framework. Building on this, we propose QA-MoE, a Quality-Aware Mixture-of-Experts framework that quantifies modality reliability via self-supervised aleatoric uncertainty. This mechanism explicitly guides expert routing, enabling the model to suppress error propagation from unreliable signals while preserving task-relevant information. Extensive experiments indicate that QA-MoE achieves competitive or state-of-the-art performance across diverse degradation scenarios and exhibits a promising One-Checkpoint-for-All property in practice.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes QA-MoE, a quality-aware mixture-of-experts model for robust multimodal sentiment analysis under continuously varying levels of noise and missing modalities.
AI.motivation: Real-world multimodal sentiment analysis systems must deal with imperfect inputs where modalities can be partially missing or degraded by different levels of noise. Existing approaches typically handle missing or corrupted modalities as discrete, pre-defined cases or under fixed corruption ratios, which makes them inflexible and less robust under continuously changing reliability conditions. There is a need for a unified framework that can model both missingness and varying quality, and adaptively adjust how much the model relies on each modality.
AI.method: The authors introduce the concept of a Continuous Reliability Spectrum that models modality reliability as a continuum, unifying complete availability, partial degradation, and full missingness. On top of this, they propose QA-MoE (Quality-Aware Mixture-of-Experts), where multiple experts are routed based on estimated modality reliability. They estimate reliability using self-supervised aleatoric uncertainty, which quantifies noise inherent in each modality signal. This uncertainty is then used to guide routing decisions within the mixture-of-experts, so that unreliable modalities have reduced influence while reliable ones are emphasized, thereby limiting error propagation while retaining informative cues.
AI.result: Through extensive experiments on multimodal sentiment analysis benchmarks under varying degradation and missingness conditions, QA-MoE attains competitive or state-of-the-art performance compared with existing baselines. It performs robustly across a wide range of corruption levels without needing separate models for different scenarios. Empirically, the method shows that a single trained checkpoint can generalize well to diverse reliability settings, supporting the practicality of the approach.
AI.conclusion: Modeling modality reliability as a continuous spectrum and using self-supervised aleatoric uncertainty to drive a quality-aware mixture-of-experts enables robust multimodal sentiment analysis under real-world, dynamically changing reliability conditions. The proposed QA-MoE framework effectively suppresses the influence of noisy or missing modalities while leveraging useful information, achieving strong performance and a practical "One-Checkpoint-for-All" property that avoids the need for multiple specialized models.

title: BOSCH: Black-Box Binary Optimization for Short-Context Attention-Head Selection in LLMs
summary: Post-training hybridization of large language models (LLMs) often replaces quadratic self-attention with sliding-window attention (SWA) to reduce KV cache usage and improve latency. Existing hybridization schemes are typically defined either at the layer level (e.g., interleaving) or at the head level via static rankings from local to global. Layer-level schemes ignore that local and global dependencies are routed through heads within the same layer, while static head-level rankings suffer from entanglement: a head's local/global behavior can change after hybridization. We propose BOSCH, Black-box Binary Optimization for Short-context Head Selection, a training-free method that formulates the problem as a Large Neighborhood Search and decomposes it into three subproblems: (i) layer-importance detection via small-budget black-box probes, (ii) adaptive per-layer SWA-ratio assignment based on these sensitivities, and (iii) grouped head-level optimization within ratio buckets. Extensive experiments on 4 LLMs ranging from 1.7B to 30B parameters, across 4 SWA ratios, show that BOSCH consistently outperforms layer-level heuristics and 6 strong static head-level methods, with larger gains at higher SWA ratios. Under continual pretraining, BOSCH recover original long-context performance faster and to a higher level. Analysis of the selected heads reveals substantial turnover for BOSCH across different SWA ratios, underscoring the importance of performing head-level selection for each target ratio rather than relying on fixed locality rankings.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes BOSCH, a training-free, black-box optimization framework to choose which attention heads use sliding-window attention in post-trained LLMs, outperforming existing layer- and head-level heuristics across models, ratios, and under continual pretraining.
AI.motivation: Post-training hybridization of LLMs replaces full self-attention with sliding-window attention (SWA) to reduce KV cache and latency, but existing schemes either operate coarsely at the layer level or rely on static head-level local/global rankings. Layer-level methods ignore that local and global dependencies are routed through different heads within the same layer, and static head-level rankings fail because a head’s behavior can change after hybridization (entanglement). There is a need for a principled, training-free way to select which heads use SWA for a given SWA ratio that respects model sensitivities and avoids entanglement.
AI.method: The authors introduce BOSCH (Black-box Binary Optimization for Short-context Head Selection), which treats head selection as a Large Neighborhood Search (LNS) over a binary decision space (windowed vs. full attention per head) without accessing model internals. BOSCH is decomposed into three subproblems: (i) layer-importance detection using small-budget black-box probes to estimate how sensitive each layer is to replacing full attention with SWA; (ii) adaptive assignment of an SWA ratio per layer based on these estimated sensitivities, distributing the global SWA budget where it hurts least; and (iii) grouped head-level optimization within per-layer ratio buckets, where subsets of heads in each layer are iteratively searched/optimized to decide which specific heads become SWA while respecting the per-layer ratio. This procedure is training-free and only requires evaluation queries to the model.
AI.result: Across 4 large language models ranging from 1.7B to 30B parameters and 4 different global SWA ratios, BOSCH consistently achieves better performance than existing layer-level heuristics and six strong static head-level selection methods. The improvements are more pronounced at higher SWA ratios, where more attention is replaced by SWA. In continual pretraining scenarios, models hybridized with BOSCH recover their original long-context performance faster and ultimately reach higher performance levels than baselines. Empirical analysis of which heads are chosen as SWA shows substantial changes in selected heads across different target SWA ratios, highlighting the adaptivity of BOSCH compared with fixed-ranking baselines.
AI.conclusion: BOSCH provides an effective, training-free, black-box framework for choosing which attention heads in LLMs should use sliding-window attention for a specified SWA ratio. By combining layer-importance estimation, adaptive per-layer ratio assignment, and localized head-level search, BOSCH overcomes the limitations of layer-level and static head-level heuristics, especially at aggressive SWA ratios. The substantial turnover in selected heads across ratios demonstrates that head selection must be re-optimized per target ratio instead of relying on fixed locality rankings, and BOSCH offers a practical solution that scales across models and benefits both hybrid inference and continual pretraining.

title: Can Large Language Models Reinvent Foundational Algorithms?
summary: LLMs have shown strong potential to advance scientific discovery. Whether they possess the capacity for foundational innovation, however, remains an open question. In this work, we focus on a prerequisite for foundational innovation: can LLMs reinvent foundational algorithms in computer science? Our \textit{Unlearn-and-Reinvent} pipeline applies LLM unlearning to remove a specific foundational algorithm, such as Dijkstra's or Euclid's algorithm, from an LLM's pretrained knowledge, and then tests whether the model can reinvent it in a controlled environment. To enable effective unlearning, we adopt a GRPO-based, on-policy unlearning method. Across 10 target algorithms, 3 strong open-weight models, and 3 hint levels, our experiments demonstrate that (1) the strongest model Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507 successfully reinvents 50% of the algorithms with no hint, 70% at hint level 1, and 90% at hint level 2; (2) a few high-level hints can enhance the reinvention success rate, but even step-by-step hints fail for those complicated algorithms; and (3) test-time reinforcement learning enables successful reinvention for the Strassen algorithm at hint level 2. Through analyses of output trajectories and ablation studies, we find that generative verifier in the reinvention phase plays a critical role in sustaining models' reasoning strength, helping to avoid the ``thought collapse'' phenomenon. These findings offer insights into both the potential and current limits of LLMs' innovative thinking.

AI.tldr: They investigate whether LLMs can independently reinvent core computer science algorithms after being deliberately untrained on them, using a new unlearning-and-reinvention pipeline.
AI.motivation: While LLMs show strong capabilities in aiding scientific work, it is unclear if they can achieve foundational innovation rather than just recalling what they were trained on. Foundational innovation in computer science includes inventing or rediscovering core algorithms such as Dijkstra’s or Euclid’s algorithm. To study this, the authors need a way to remove prior knowledge of a specific algorithm and then test whether the model can recreate it from scratch, thus isolating genuine inventive reasoning from memorization.
AI.method: They propose an “Unlearn-and-Reinvent” pipeline. First, they apply an LLM unlearning procedure based on a GRPO-style on-policy training approach to selectively remove knowledge of a target algorithm from a pretrained model. After unlearning, they place the model in a controlled task environment to see whether it can reinvent the algorithm. They test 10 canonical algorithms across 3 strong open-weight LLMs and use 3 levels of hints (from none, to high-level descriptions, to more step-by-step guidance). They also study a test-time reinforcement learning setup, including a generative verifier that evaluates and guides model outputs during reinvention, and conduct ablations on this component.
AI.result: For the best-performing model, Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507, the model successfully reinvents 50% of the algorithms without any hints, 70% with low-level hints, and 90% with more detailed hints. Some complex algorithms cannot be reinvented even when step-by-step hints are provided. However, for a particularly challenging case (Strassen’s algorithm) they achieve successful reinvention at the second hint level via test-time reinforcement learning. Analysis shows that including a generative verifier during reinvention is crucial to maintaining reasoning capability and preventing “thought collapse,” where the model’s reasoning degrades during the process.
AI.conclusion: LLMs, when carefully untrained on specific algorithms, can still reinvent a substantial portion of foundational algorithms, especially with modest external guidance, indicating a real if limited capacity for foundational-style innovation. Nonetheless, their difficulties with more complex algorithms and dependence on hints and test-time RL highlight important limits of current models’ inventive reasoning. The generative verifier emerges as a key component to preserve and amplify reasoning quality, suggesting that architecture and training-time / test-time scaffolding are as important as raw model capacity for enabling innovation-like behavior.

title: FinReporting: An Agentic Workflow for Localized Reporting of Cross-Jurisdiction Financial Disclosures
summary: Financial reporting systems increasingly use large language models (LLMs) to extract and summarize corporate disclosures. However, most assume a single-market setting and do not address structural differences across jurisdictions. Variations in accounting taxonomies, tagging infrastructures (e.g., XBRL vs. PDF), and aggregation conventions make cross-jurisdiction reporting a semantic alignment and verification challenge. We present FinReporting, an agentic workflow for localized cross-jurisdiction financial reporting. The system builds a unified canonical ontology over Income Statement, Balance Sheet, and Cash Flow, and decomposes reporting into auditable stages including filing acquisition, extraction, canonical mapping, and anomaly logging. Rather than using LLMs as free-form generators, FinReporting deploys them as constrained verifiers under explicit decision rules and evidence grounding. Evaluated on annual filings from the US, Japan, and China, the system improves consistency and reliability under heterogeneous reporting regimes. We release an interactive demo supporting cross-market inspection and structured export of localized financial statements. Our demo is available at https://huggingface.co/spaces/BoomQ/FinReporting-Demo . The video describing our system is available at https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=f65jdEL31Kk

AI.tldr: FinReporting is an agent-based, LLM-supported system that standardizes and verifies financial reports across different countries by mapping them to a shared ontology and using LLMs as constrained checkers rather than free generators.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based financial reporting tools mostly assume one jurisdiction and thus struggle with structural, semantic, and technological differences between countries’ reporting regimes. Differences in taxonomies, file formats, and aggregation conventions make it hard to compare or reliably process disclosures across markets, creating risks for analysis, automation, and auditability.
AI.method: The authors design FinReporting, an agentic workflow that decomposes cross-jurisdiction financial reporting into auditable steps: (1) acquiring filings from different markets; (2) extracting content from heterogeneous sources (like XBRL and PDF); (3) mapping extracted data into a unified canonical ontology that covers Income Statement, Balance Sheet, and Cash Flow; and (4) logging anomalies. Large language models are used as constrained verifiers that follow explicit decision rules and rely on grounded evidence, rather than as free-form text generators.
AI.result: When applied to annual filings from the US, Japan, and China, the system increases the consistency and reliability of reported financial statements despite the heterogeneity in local reporting regimes and infrastructures. It supports cross-market inspection and structured export of localized financial statements.
AI.conclusion: An agentic, ontology-based workflow that uses LLMs as constrained verifiers can effectively handle semantic alignment and verification problems in cross-jurisdiction financial reporting. This approach improves robustness over prior single-market or free-form LLM summarization systems and is practical enough to be exposed via an interactive demo for real-world cross-market analysis.

title: Emergent social transmission of model-based representations without inference
summary: How do people acquire rich, flexible knowledge about their environment from others despite limited cognitive capacity? Humans are often thought to rely on computationally costly mentalizing, such as inferring others' beliefs. In contrast, cultural evolution emphasizes that behavioral transmission can be supported by simple social cues. Using reinforcement learning simulations, we show how minimal social learning can indirectly transmit higher-level representations. We simulate a naïve agent searching for rewards in a reconfigurable environment, learning either alone or by observing an expert - crucially, without inferring mental states.
