Instead, the learner heuristically selects actions or boosts value representations based on observed actions. Our results demonstrate that these cues bias the learner's experience, causing its representation to converge toward the expert's. Model-based learners benefit most from social exposure, showing faster learning and more expert-like representations. These findings show how cultural transmission can arise from simple, non-mentalizing processes exploiting asocial learning mechanisms.

AI.tldr: Minimal social learning, implemented as simple heuristics in reinforcement learning agents, can indirectly transmit complex, expert-like internal representations without mentalizing.
AI.motivation: To explain how humans acquire rich, flexible, culturally shared knowledge from others despite limited cognitive capacity and without relying on computationally expensive mental state inference (mentalizing). The work aims to bridge perspectives from cognitive science and cultural evolution by showing how simple social cues might drive the emergence of shared internal representations.
AI.method: The authors run reinforcement learning simulations of a naïve agent searching for rewards in a reconfigurable environment. They compare learning conditions: (1) individual/asocial learning and (2) social learning, where the agent observes an expert. The social agent does not infer the expert’s beliefs; instead, it uses heuristics—either copying observed actions or boosting the value of states/actions associated with those actions. They test both model-free and model-based learners to see how social observations shape their learned value functions and internal representations.
AI.result: Minimal social-learning heuristics bias which states and actions the learner experiences and how those experiences are valued, such that the learner’s internal representation gradually converges toward the expert’s. Agents with model-based learning architectures benefit the most from such biased exposure, showing faster learning, better task performance, and more expert-like internal representations than purely asocial learners or simpler model-free agents.
AI.conclusion: Rich, expert-like knowledge and shared internal representations can emerge via simple, non-mentalizing social learning processes that piggyback on standard reinforcement learning mechanisms. Cultural transmission does not require explicit belief inference; instead, observing others’ actions provides powerful cues that structure and bias learning, especially in agents with model-based capacities. This offers a cognitively plausible account of how complex cultural knowledge can spread in human groups.

title: The Model Agreed, But Didn't Learn: Diagnosing Surface Compliance in Large Language Models
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) internalize vast world knowledge as parametric memory, yet inevitably inherit the staleness and errors of their source corpora. Consequently, ensuring the reliability and malleability of these internal representations is imperative for trustworthy real-world deployment. Knowledge editing offers a pivotal paradigm for surgically modifying memory without retraining. However, while recent editors demonstrate high success rates on standard benchmarks, it remains questionable whether current evaluation frameworks that rely on assessing output under specific prompting conditions can reliably authenticate genuine memory modification. In this work, we introduce a simple diagnostic framework that subjects models to discriminative self-assessment under in-context learning (ICL) settings that better reflect real-world application environments, specifically designed to scrutinize the subtle behavioral nuances induced by memory modifications. This probing reveals a pervasive phenomenon of Surface Compliance, where editors achieve high benchmark scores by merely mimicking target outputs without structurally overwriting internal beliefs. Moreover, we find that recursive modifications accumulate representational residues, triggering cognitive instability and permanently diminishing the reversibility of the model's memory state. These insights underscore the risks of current editing paradigms and highlight the pivotal role of robust memory modification in building trustworthy, long-term sustainable LLM systems. Code is available at https://github.com/XiaojieGu/SA-MCQ.

AI.tldr: The paper reveals that many knowledge editing methods for LLMs only superficially match desired outputs instead of truly changing internal beliefs, and proposes a new in-context self-assessment framework that exposes this issue and shows cumulative side effects of repeated edits.
AI.motivation: LLMs store world knowledge in their parameters, but that knowledge can be outdated or wrong. We need ways to reliably and safely modify this parametric memory without full retraining. Existing “knowledge editing” methods look good on benchmarks, but current evaluations may be misleading because they judge edits only by outputs under narrow prompts, not by whether the model’s underlying beliefs are really changed or how stable edits are over time and usage. The authors want a more realistic and stringent way to test whether knowledge has actually been modified and what side-effects edits cause.
AI.method: The authors design a diagnostic framework that evaluates edited models through discriminative self-assessment in in-context learning (ICL) scenarios. Instead of only checking if the model outputs the target fact when directly prompted, they place the model into multiple-choice or similar setups where it must choose among options, effectively probing its internal preferences and “beliefs” under varied contexts. They apply this framework to models that have undergone different knowledge editing methods and examine behaviors across single and recursive edits, analyzing subtle shifts in model responses that indicate whether memory was structurally overwritten or merely mimicked at the surface level.
AI.result: Using the new diagnostic, they uncover a phenomenon they call Surface Compliance: many knowledge editors can produce the desired answers on standard benchmarks but do so by pattern-matching or output mimicry, without genuinely altering the underlying representation of the fact. The framework further shows that when edits are applied recursively, residual traces from past edits accumulate in the representation space, leading to cognitive instability and reduced reversibility, meaning the model’s memory state becomes harder to restore or reliably re-edit over time.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that current knowledge editing approaches and benchmarks are insufficient for ensuring real, stable, and safe memory changes in LLMs. Their ICL-based self-assessment framework provides a more stringent probe of internal belief modification and exposes hidden risks such as superficial compliance and long-term instability from repeated edits. They argue that future work on trustworthy, sustainable LLM systems must focus on robust memory modification methods that genuinely and reversibly alter internal representations, not just outputs in narrow test conditions.

title: Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning with Augmented Step-Level Transitions for LLM Agents
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents have demonstrated strong capabilities in complex interactive decision-making tasks. However, existing LLM agents typically rely on increasingly long interaction histories, resulting in high computational cost and limited scalability. In this paper, we propose STEP-HRL, a hierarchical reinforcement learning (HRL) framework that enables step-level learning by conditioning only on single-step transitions rather than full interaction histories. STEP-HRL structures tasks hierarchically, using completed subtasks to represent global progress of overall task. By introducing a local progress module, it also iteratively and selectively summarizes interaction history within each subtask to produce a compact summary of local progress. Together, these components yield augmented step-level transitions for both high-level and low-level policies. Experimental results on ScienceWorld and ALFWorld benchmarks consistently demonstrate that STEP-HRL substantially outperforms baselines in terms of performance and generalization while reducing token usage. Our code is available at https://github.com/TonyStark042/STEP-HRL.

AI.tldr: STEP-HRL is a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework for LLM agents that replaces long history conditioning with step-level transitions augmented by hierarchical progress summaries, achieving better performance and generalization with fewer tokens.
AI.motivation: LLM-based agents in interactive decision-making tasks currently depend on long, ever-growing interaction histories as context, which leads to high computational cost, poor scalability, and potential degradation in performance as context windows fill. The paper aims to design an architecture that preserves or improves decision quality and generalization while substantially reducing reliance on lengthy histories and token usage.
AI.method: The authors introduce STEP-HRL, a hierarchical reinforcement learning framework that decomposes tasks into subtasks and enables step-level learning. At the high level, completed subtasks are used to encode global task progress. At the low level, a local progress module incrementally and selectively summarizes the interaction history within each subtask into a compact representation. These summaries, combined with current state information, form augmented single-step transitions for training both high-level and low-level policies, instead of using full trajectories as input to the LLM agent.
AI.result: On two interactive decision-making benchmarks, ScienceWorld and ALFWorld, STEP-HRL consistently outperforms baseline LLM agent approaches in both task performance and generalization to new settings. At the same time, it significantly reduces token usage, indicating improved efficiency in context handling without sacrificing, and in fact improving, agent effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Hierarchical structuring of tasks combined with selective, subtask-level history summarization enables LLM agents to operate effectively using step-level transitions rather than full interaction histories. STEP-HRL demonstrates that this design can simultaneously boost performance and generalization while lowering computational and token costs, suggesting a scalable path for building more efficient LLM-based interactive agents.

title: Disentangling MLP Neuron Weights in Vocabulary Space
summary: Interpreting the information encoded in model weights remains a fundamental challenge in mechanistic interpretability. In this work, we introduce ROTATE (Rotation-Optimized Token Alignment in weighT spacE), a data-free method requiring no forward passes that disentangles MLP neurons directly in weight space. Our approach relies on a key statistical observation: neurons that encode coherent, monosemantic concepts exhibit high kurtosis when projected onto the model's vocabulary. By optimizing rotations of neuron weights to maximize their vocabulary-space kurtosis, our method recovers sparse, interpretable directions which we name vocabulary channels. Experiments on Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and Gemma-2-2B-it demonstrate that ROTATE consistently recovers vocabulary channels that are faithful to the neuron's behavior. ablating individual channels selectively disables corresponding input activations or the promotion of specific concepts. Moreover, aggregating channel-level descriptions yields comprehensive neuron descriptions that outperform optimized activation-based baselines by 2-3x in head-to-head comparisons. By providing a data-free decomposition of neuron weights, ROTATE offers a scalable, fine-grained building block for interpreting LMs.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes ROTATE, a data-free method that linearly rotates MLP neuron weight matrices to find sparse, interpretable "vocabulary channels" directly in weight space, enabling fine-grained, scalable mechanistic interpretability of language models without running the model.
AI.motivation: Mechanistic interpretability seeks to understand what neurons in large language models actually represent, but current techniques usually rely on activation-based probing that requires running the model on data, can be computationally expensive, and often yields polysemantic, hard-to-interpret neurons. The authors aim to find a scalable, data-free way to disentangle neuron representations so that individual components correspond to coherent, monosemantic concepts.
AI.method: The authors introduce ROTATE (Rotation-Optimized Token Alignment in weighT spacE), which operates solely on the MLP neuron weight matrices without forward passes. They treat neurons as vectors in vocabulary space (via the input/output embeddings) and use a statistical criterion: monosemantic, concept-encoding neurons should have high kurtosis (heavy-tailedness) over vocabulary logits. They then optimize an orthogonal rotation of neuron weights to maximize kurtosis across dimensions, producing new basis vectors—"vocabulary channels"—that are sparse and aligned with tokens or concepts. They validate these channels via ablation experiments and by aggregating channel-level token descriptions into neuron-level descriptions, and compare against activation-based, optimization-heavy baselines.
AI.result: On Llama-3.1-8B-Instruct and Gemma-2-2B-it, ROTATE consistently finds vocabulary channels that match how neurons behave functionally: ablating individual channels selectively disables specific input activations or suppresses the model’s promotion of the targeted concepts. Human or automatic evaluations show that aggregated channel descriptions yield neuron explanations that are 2–3× better than optimized activation-based baselines in direct comparisons, while requiring no data or forward passes.
AI.conclusion: ROTATE demonstrates that meaningful, monosemantic structure can be extracted directly from neuron weight space by optimizing for vocabulary-space kurtosis, without observing activations. The method produces sparse, concept-aligned vocabulary channels that faithfully explain neuron behavior and provide a fine-grained, scalable building block for interpreting large language models. This supports the promise of data-free, weight-space approaches as a practical path for mechanistic interpretability at scale.

title: Reciprocal Trust and Distrust in Artificial Intelligence Systems: The Hard Problem of Regulation
summary: Policy makers, scientists, and the public are increasingly confronted with thorny questions about the regulation of artificial intelligence (AI) systems. A key common thread concerns whether AI can be trusted and the factors that can make it more trustworthy in front of stakeholders and users. This is indeed crucial, as the trustworthiness of AI systems is fundamental for both democratic governance and for the development and deployment of AI. This article advances the discussion by arguing that AI systems should also be recognized, as least to some extent, as artifacts capable of exercising a form of agency, thereby enabling them to engage in relationships of trust or distrust with humans. It further examines the implications of these reciprocal trust dynamics for regulators tasked with overseeing AI systems. The article concludes by identifying key tensions and unresolved dilemmas that these dynamics pose for the future of AI regulation and governance.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that AI systems should be seen as having a limited form of agency, which allows for reciprocal trust relationships between humans and AI, and explores what this means for AI regulation and governance.
AI.motivation: As AI becomes more pervasive, policymakers, scientists, and the public face difficult questions about how to regulate these systems and whether they can or should be trusted. Existing debates often treat AI purely as tools, overlooking how people actually interact with them in ways that resemble relationships of trust. The authors want to refine the conceptual foundations of AI governance by clarifying how trust and trustworthiness apply to AI and why this matters for democratic oversight and deployment.
AI.method: The paper uses conceptual and normative analysis. It interprets and develops philosophical notions of trust, agency, and artifacthood and applies them to AI systems. It then examines, at a theoretical level, how recognizing a form of agency in AI changes our understanding of trust dynamics and the role of regulators. The argument is structured around redefining AI as artifacts capable of a limited, functional form of agency and exploring the regulatory implications of this reconceptualization.
AI.result: The authors argue that AI systems can be understood as artifacts with a kind of derived or limited agency, sufficient to participate in trust and distrust relationships with humans. This reframing exposes new layers of reciprocal trust: not only do humans need to trust AI, but AI systems, in a functional sense, can also be seen as relying on and 'trusting' human inputs, oversight structures, and environments. This perspective reveals several tensions and open dilemmas for regulators, such as how to assign responsibility, how to design institutions around these new trust dynamics, and how to maintain democratic control when interacting with quasi-agentive systems.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that acknowledging AI systems as limited agents capable of taking part in trust relationships is conceptually coherent and normatively significant. This view complicates the landscape of AI regulation by introducing reciprocal trust dynamics between humans and AI, which in turn create new tensions for governance, responsibility, and oversight. The authors highlight that future regulatory frameworks will need to grapple with these unresolved dilemmas to ensure that AI development and deployment remain aligned with democratic values and robust mechanisms for trust and accountability.

title: BiMind: A Dual-Head Reasoning Model with Attention-Geometry Adapter for Incorrect Information Detection
summary: Incorrect information poses significant challenges by disrupting content veracity and integrity, yet most detection approaches struggle to jointly balance textual content verification with external knowledge modification under collapsed attention geometries. To address this issue, we propose a dual-head reasoning framework, BiMind, which disentangles content-internal reasoning from knowledge-augmented reasoning. In BiMind, we introduce three core innovations: (i) an attention geometry adapter that reshapes attention logits via token-conditioned offsets and mitigates attention collapse; (ii) a self-retrieval knowledge mechanism, which constructs an in-domain semantic memory through kNN retrieval and injects retrieved neighbors via feature-wise linear modulation; (iii) the uncertainty-aware fusion strategies, including entropy-gated fusion and a trainable agreement head, stabilized by a symmetric Kullback-Leibler agreement regularizer. To quantify the knowledge contributions, we define a novel metric, Value-of-eXperience (VoX), to measure instance-wise logit gains from knowledge-augmented reasoning. Experiment results on public datasets demonstrate that our BiMind model outperforms advanced detection approaches and provides interpretable diagnostics on when and why knowledge matters.

AI.tldr: BiMind is a dual-head reasoning model for incorrect-information detection that separates plain-text reasoning from knowledge-augmented reasoning, introduces attention geometry adaptation, self-retrieval knowledge injection, and uncertainty-aware fusion, and uses a new VoX metric to quantify how much external knowledge helps on each instance.
AI.motivation: Existing misinformation/incorrect-information detectors struggle to simultaneously evaluate the text itself and incorporate external knowledge, partly because attention mechanisms can collapse and fail to distinguish what should rely on internal content vs. outside knowledge. There is also a lack of tools to quantify when external knowledge genuinely improves predictions. The paper aims to build a model that can reason both internally and with knowledge, avoid attention collapse, and provide interpretable signals about the utility of knowledge.
AI.method: The authors propose BiMind, a dual-head reasoning architecture with two parallel reasoning streams: one that operates purely on the input text (content-internal) and another that is augmented with retrieved knowledge. Key components are: (1) an attention geometry adapter that adds token-conditioned offsets to attention logits to reshape the attention pattern and mitigate collapse; (2) a self-retrieval mechanism that builds an in-domain semantic memory via k-nearest-neighbor retrieval and injects retrieved representations by feature-wise linear modulation into the model; (3) uncertainty-aware fusion strategies that combine the two heads using entropy-gated fusion and a trainable agreement head, with a symmetric KL-divergence regularizer encouraging consistent yet informative outputs. They also define the VoX metric that measures per-instance logit improvements due to the knowledge-augmented head, thereby quantifying knowledge contribution.
AI.result: On public benchmarks for incorrect-information detection, BiMind achieves higher accuracy and related metrics than strong baselines and prior state-of-the-art methods. The analysis using VoX shows that the model can indicate which instances benefit from external knowledge and by how much, providing interpretable diagnostics and case studies about when external knowledge is crucial versus when internal content suffices.
AI.conclusion: BiMind effectively separates and then fuses internal and knowledge-augmented reasoning for incorrect-information detection, overcoming attention-collapse issues and yielding performance gains over existing methods. Its uncertainty-aware fusion and VoX metric make the contributions of external knowledge transparent at the instance level, enabling more interpretable, controllable detection systems and offering a framework to study the conditions under which external knowledge truly matters.

authors: Gerrit J. J. van den Burg
title: Vision-Guided Iterative Refinement for Frontend Code Generation
comment: Accepted at ICLR 2026 Workshop on AI with Recursive Self-Improvement
summary: Code generation with large language models often relies on multi-stage human-in-the-loop refinement, which is effective but very costly - particularly in domains such as frontend web development where the solution quality depends on rendered visual output. We present a fully automated critic-in-the-loop framework in which a vision-language model serves as a visual critic that provides structured feedback on rendered webpages to guide iterative refinement of generated code. Across real-world user requests from the WebDev Arena dataset, this approach yields consistent improvements in solution quality, achieving up to 17.8% increase in performance over three refinement cycles. Next, we investigate parameter-efficient fine-tuning using LoRA to understand whether the improvements provided by the critic can be internalized by the code-generating LLM. Fine-tuning achieves 25% of the gains from the best critic-in-the-loop solution without a significant increase in token counts. Our findings indicate that automated, VLM-based critique of frontend code generation leads to significantly higher quality solutions than can be achieved through a single LLM inference pass, and highlight the importance of iterative refinement for the complex visual outputs associated with web development.

AI.tldr: They build an automated "visual critic" loop using a vision-language model to iteratively improve frontend code, and study how much of those gains can be baked into the base code LLM via LoRA fine-tuning.
AI.motivation: Human-in-the-loop code refinement, especially for visually judged frontend web tasks, is expensive and slow, and single-pass LLM generation often yields low-quality visual results. There is a need for scalable, automated methods that can critique rendered web UIs and guide systematic improvement of code without heavy human involvement.
AI.method: 1) Propose a critic-in-the-loop framework where a vision-language model inspects rendered webpages produced by a code LLM, gives structured visual feedback, and triggers iterative code refinement for several cycles. 2) Evaluate this loop on realistic frontend tasks from the WebDev Arena dataset, measuring performance across refinement rounds. 3) Apply parameter-efficient fine-tuning (LoRA) to the base code LLM using data from the critic-guided refinements to see if the model can internalize some of the critic’s benefits without an external loop. 4) Compare gains in solution quality and token usage between critic-in-the-loop and fine-tuned single-pass models.
AI.result: The VLM critic-in-the-loop framework yields consistent quality improvements on WebDev Arena tasks, with up to 17.8% performance increase after three refinement cycles versus single-pass baselines. LoRA fine-tuning of the code model captures roughly 25% of the performance gains offered by the full critic loop, and does so without materially increasing token usage, though it does not match the best iterative critic setup.
AI.conclusion: Automated visual critique using a VLM is an effective way to enhance frontend web code generation, substantially outperforming single-shot LLM outputs. Iterative refinement driven by visual feedback is especially important for complex UI tasks, and while partial gains can be baked into the model via lightweight fine-tuning, external critic-in-the-loop mechanisms remain valuable for maximizing quality.

title: A Multi-Stage Validation Framework for Trustworthy Large-scale Clinical Information Extraction using Large Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) show promise for extracting clinically meaningful information from unstructured health records, yet their translation into real-world settings is constrained by the lack of scalable and trustworthy validation approaches. Conventional evaluation methods rely heavily on annotation-intensive reference standards or incomplete structured data, limiting feasibility at population scale. We propose a multi-stage validation framework for LLM-based clinical information extraction that enables rigorous assessment under weak supervision. The framework integrates prompt calibration, rule-based plausibility filtering, semantic grounding assessment, targeted confirmatory evaluation using an independent higher-capacity judge LLM, selective expert review, and external predictive validity analysis to quantify uncertainty and characterize error modes without exhaustive manual annotation. We applied this framework to extraction of substance use disorder (SUD) diagnoses across 11 substance categories from 919,783 clinical notes. Rule-based filtering and semantic grounding removed 14.59% of LLM-positive extractions that were unsupported, irrelevant, or structurally implausible. For high-uncertainty cases, the judge LLM's assessments showed substantial agreement with subject matter expert review (Gwet's AC1=0.80). Using judge-evaluated outputs as references, the primary LLM achieved an F1 score of 0.80 under relaxed matching criteria. LLM-extracted SUD diagnoses also predicted subsequent engagement in SUD specialty care more accurately than structured-data baselines (AUC=0.80). These findings demonstrate that scalable, trustworthy deployment of LLM-based clinical information extraction is feasible without annotation-intensive evaluation.

AI.tldr: They present a scalable, weakly supervised framework to validate LLM-based clinical information extraction, demonstrating it works well for extracting substance use disorder diagnoses from nearly a million notes without heavy manual annotation.
AI.motivation: LLMs can extract rich clinical information from unstructured notes, but real-world use is limited because standard validation requires costly, large-scale manual annotation or relies on incomplete structured data. There is a need for a scalable, trustworthy way to evaluate LLM outputs at population scale under realistic resource constraints.
AI.method: They design a multi-stage validation framework for LLM-based extraction under weak supervision. The pipeline includes: (1) prompt calibration for the primary LLM; (2) rule-based plausibility filters to remove structurally impossible or clearly unsupported outputs; (3) semantic grounding checks to ensure extractions are actually supported in the note and clinically relevant; (4) targeted confirmatory evaluation of high-uncertainty cases by an independent, higher-capacity judge LLM; (5) selective expert review to audit and calibrate the judge; and (6) external predictive validity analysis, checking whether extracted labels predict relevant downstream clinical outcomes. They apply this to extraction of 11 categories of substance use disorder diagnoses from 919,783 clinical notes, comparing outputs to both expert assessment and structured-data baselines.
AI.result: When applied to SUD diagnosis extraction, rule-based and semantic grounding filters removed 14.59% of LLM-positive extractions as unsupported, irrelevant, or implausible. For uncertain cases, the higher-capacity judge LLM agreed well with subject matter experts (Gwet's AC1=0.80), indicating it can serve as a reliable weak supervisor. Using judge-evaluated outputs as a reference standard, the primary LLM achieved an F1 score of 0.80 under relaxed matching criteria. Moreover, the LLM-derived SUD diagnoses predicted future engagement in SUD specialty care with AUC=0.80, outperforming baselines using only structured EHR data.
AI.conclusion: A structured, multi-stage, weakly supervised validation framework can make LLM-based clinical information extraction scalable and trustworthy without extensive manual annotation. By combining rule-based filters, semantic checks, a higher-capacity judge LLM, selective expert review, and outcome-based validation, the approach delivers reasonably accurate extractions and stronger predictive utility than structured-data baselines, supporting real-world deployment at population scale.

title: Deep Researcher Agent: An Autonomous Framework for 24/7 Deep Learning Experimentation with Zero-Cost Monitoring
summary: We present \textbf{Deep Researcher Agent}, an open-source framework that enables large language model (LLM) agents to autonomously conduct deep learning experiments around the clock. Unlike existing AI research assistants that focus on paper writing or code generation, our system addresses the full experiment lifecycle: hypothesis formation, code implementation, training execution, result analysis, and iterative refinement. The framework introduces three key innovations: (1) \textbf{Zero-Cost Monitoring} -- a monitoring paradigm that incurs zero LLM API costs during model training by relying solely on process-level checks and log file reads; (2) \textbf{Two-Tier Constant-Size Memory} -- a memory architecture capped at $\sim$5K characters regardless of runtime duration, preventing the unbounded context growth that plagues long-running agents; and (3) \textbf{Minimal-Toolset Leader-Worker Architecture} -- a multi-agent design where each worker agent is equipped with only 3--5 tools, reducing per-call token overhead by up to 73\%. In sustained deployments spanning 30+ days, the framework autonomously completed 500+ experiment cycles across four concurrent research projects, achieving a 52\% improvement over baseline metrics in one project through 200+ automated experiments -- all at an average LLM cost of \$0.08 per 24-hour cycle. Code is available at https://github.com/Xiangyue-Zhang/auto-deep-researcher-24x7.

AI.tldr: Deep Researcher Agent is an open-source LLM-agent framework that autonomously designs, runs, and iterates deep learning experiments 24/7 with very low API cost using efficient monitoring, memory, and tool architectures.
AI.motivation: Running many deep learning experiments typically requires continuous human supervision and makes LLM-based agents expensive and unstable over long durations due to context bloat, frequent tool calls, and costly monitoring during training. The authors want an autonomous, low-cost, long-running agent that can handle the full experimental lifecycle, not just code or paper writing.
AI.method: They build an LLM-agent framework that covers hypothesis generation, coding, training, analysis, and refinement. The framework adds: (1) Zero-Cost Monitoring that tracks training progress via process-level checks and log-file reads instead of LLM calls; (2) a Two-Tier Constant-Size Memory mechanism capped at about 5K characters to keep context bounded over long runs; and (3) a Minimal-Toolset Leader-Worker multi-agent architecture where workers have only 3–5 tools each, reducing token overhead on each call. They then run the system continuously on multiple projects for over 30 days.
AI.result: In 30+ day deployments across four parallel research projects, the system autonomously executed more than 500 experiment cycles. In one project, about 200 automated experiments yielded a 52% improvement over baseline metrics. The average LLM API cost was about $0.08 per 24-hour cycle, demonstrating both autonomy and cost-efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Deep Researcher Agent can reliably and cheaply conduct end-to-end deep learning experimentation autonomously over long periods. Its zero-cost monitoring, constant-size memory, and minimal-tool multi-agent design make LLM agents practical for sustained, real-world ML research workflows, as evidenced by multi-week deployments and significant empirical gains on at least one project.

title: From Hallucination to Structure Snowballing: The Alignment Tax of Constrained Decoding in LLM Reflection
summary: Intrinsic self-correction in Large Language Models (LLMs) frequently fails in open-ended reasoning tasks due to ``hallucination snowballing,'' a phenomenon in which models recursively justify early errors during free-text reflection. While structured feedback can mitigate this issue, existing approaches often rely on externally trained critics or symbolic tools, reducing agent autonomy. This study investigates whether enforcing structured reflection purely through Outlines-based constrained decoding can disrupt error propagation without additional training. Evaluating an 8-billion-parameter model (Qwen3-8B), we show that simply imposing structural constraints does not improve self-correction performance. Instead, it triggers a new failure mode termed ``structure snowballing.'' We find that the cognitive load required to satisfy strict formatting rules pushes the model into formatting traps. This observation helps explain why the agent achieves near-perfect superficial syntactic alignment yet fails to detect or resolve deeper semantic errors. These findings expose an ``alignment tax'' inherent to constrained decoding, highlighting a tension between structural granularity and internal model capacity in autonomous workflows. Code and raw logs are available in the GitHub repository: https://github.com/hongxuzhou/agentic_llm_structured_self_critique.

AI.tldr: The paper finds that enforcing strict, outline-like structured reflection via constrained decoding does not improve, and can even harm, LLM self-correction, causing “structure snowballing” where models fixate on format over factual accuracy.
AI.motivation: LLMs often hallucinate and then reinforce their own errors when asked to reflect freely on open-ended reasoning tasks, a process called hallucination snowballing. Prior fixes typically add external critics or tools, which undermines the idea of a single autonomous agent. The authors want to know if one can instead improve self-correction purely by constraining the model’s output structure (e.g., outlines) at decoding time, without extra training or external modules.
AI.method: They use an 8B-parameter model (Qwen3-8B) and apply Outlines-based constrained decoding to force its self-reflection into predefined structured formats. They then evaluate how this affects the model’s ability to detect and correct its own errors in open-ended reasoning tasks, comparing free-form reflection versus strictly structured reflection under these constraints.
AI.result: Imposing structural constraints alone does not improve self-correction; it introduces a new failure mode called structure snowballing, where the model becomes trapped in satisfying and elaborating on formatting requirements while allowing initial reasoning errors to persist and propagate. The model shows very high compliance with syntactic and formatting rules but remains poor at identifying or fixing deeper semantic mistakes.
AI.conclusion: Simply forcing structured reflection via constrained decoding, without extra training or external critics, is not sufficient and can be counterproductive. The cognitive load of meeting strict structural requirements drives the model into formatting traps, revealing an “alignment tax”: tighter structural control can trade off against the model’s effective capacity to perform genuine error checking in autonomous workflows.

title: When Do We Need LLMs? A Diagnostic for Language-Driven Bandits
comment: ICLR 2026 Workshop on AI Advances in Finance
summary: We study Contextual Multi-Armed Bandits (CMABs) for non-episodic sequential decision making problems where the context includes both textual and numerical information (e.g., recommendation systems, dynamic portfolio adjustments, offer selection; all frequent problems in finance). While Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly applied to these settings, utilizing LLMs for reasoning at every decision step is computationally expensive and uncertainty estimates are difficult to obtain. To address this, we introduce LLMP-UCB, a bandit algorithm that derives uncertainty estimates from LLMs via repeated inference. However, our experiments demonstrate that lightweight numerical bandits operating on text embeddings (dense or Matryoshka) match or exceed the accuracy of LLM-based solutions at a fraction of their cost. We further show that embedding dimensionality is a practical lever on the exploration-exploitation balance, enabling cost--performance tradeoffs without prompt complexity. Finally, to guide practitioners, we propose a geometric diagnostic based on the arms' embedding to decide when to use LLM-driven reasoning versus a lightweight numerical bandit. Our results provide a principled deployment framework for cost-effective, uncertainty-aware decision systems with broad applicability across AI use cases in financial services.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates contextual multi-armed bandits with both text and numeric context, compares LLM-based bandits with lightweight embedding-based bandits, and proposes when to use each for cost-effective, uncertainty-aware decision-making in finance-related tasks.
AI.motivation: Contextual decision-making problems in finance and other domains increasingly involve rich textual data and are being tackled with LLMs. However, invoking LLMs at every decision step is computationally costly and does not naturally provide uncertainty estimates, which are crucial for exploration-exploitation tradeoffs in bandits. There is a practical need to understand whether simpler embedding-based methods can match LLM performance at lower cost and how to decide which approach to deploy.
AI.method: The authors: (1) formalize a contextual multi-armed bandit setup where contexts are mixed text–numeric; (2) introduce LLMP-UCB, which obtains uncertainty estimates by repeatedly querying an LLM and using these to construct an upper-confidence-bound style bandit policy; (3) implement lightweight numerical bandits that operate directly on text embeddings (dense and Matryoshka), varying embedding dimensionality; (4) empirically compare LLM-based and embedding-based bandits on tasks such as recommendation, portfolio adjustment, and offer selection; and (5) design a geometric diagnostic using the arms’ embedding structure to indicate when LLM reasoning is likely to be beneficial versus when embeddings suffice.
AI.result: Experiments show that bandits running on text embeddings generally match or outperform the accuracy of the LLM-based LLMP-UCB approach while being much cheaper computationally. Adjusting the embedding dimensionality effectively tunes exploration versus exploitation, giving practitioners a way to trade off performance and cost without complex prompting. The proposed geometric diagnostic reliably distinguishes settings where LLM-driven reasoning offers additional value from those where lightweight numerical bandits are adequate.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based reasoning within contextual bandits can be made uncertainty-aware via repeated inference (LLMP-UCB), but in many practical settings, especially in financial decision tasks, simple embedding-based bandits yield similar or better performance at a fraction of the cost. Embedding dimensionality serves as a practical control knob for exploration-exploitation behavior, and the geometric diagnostic provides a principled rule-of-thumb for selecting between LLM-centric and lightweight bandit solutions. Overall, the paper offers a deployment framework for building cost-effective, uncertainty-aware decision systems that leverage text and numeric context in finance and related domains.

title: Short Data, Long Context: Distilling Positional Knowledge in Transformers
summary: Extending the context window of language models typically requires expensive long-context pre-training, posing significant challenges for both training efficiency and data collection. In this paper, we present evidence that long-context retrieval capabilities can be transferred to student models through logit-based knowledge distillation, even when training exclusively on packed short-context samples within a long-context window. We provide comprehensive insights through the lens of Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) and establish three key findings. First, consistent with prior work, we show that phase-wise RoPE scaling, which maximizes rotational spectrum utilization at each training stage, also achieves the best long-context performance in knowledge distillation setups. Second, we demonstrate that logit-based knowledge distillation can directly enable positional information transfer. Using an experimental setup with packed repeated token sequences, we trace the propagation of positional perturbations from query and key vectors through successive transformer layers to output logits, revealing that positional information systematically influences the teacher's output distribution and, in turn, the distillation signal received by the student model. Third, our analysis uncovers structured update patterns in the query state during long-context extension, with distinct parameter spans exhibiting strong sensitivity to long-context training.

AI.tldr: They show that long-context retrieval abilities can be distilled from a long-context teacher into a student using only short-context data, leveraging RoPE analysis and logit-based distillation to understand how positional information and query states are updated.
AI.motivation: Extending LLM context windows is usually expensive because it needs long-context pre-training and suitable data. The authors want a cheaper way to give models long-context retrieval abilities and to understand how positional information flows in RoPE-based transformers during this process.
AI.method: They use logit-based knowledge distillation from a teacher with long-context ability to a student, but train only on short packed sequences inside a large context window. They analyze behavior under Rotary Position Embedding with different RoPE-scaling schemes, run controlled experiments with packed repeated token sequences, and trace how positional perturbations in queries/keys propagate to logits across transformer layers. They also inspect parameter-level changes in query states during long-context extension.
AI.result: (1) Phase-wise RoPE scaling that maximizes rotational spectrum utilization per stage gives the best long-context performance even in knowledge distillation setups. (2) Logit-based distillation transfers positional information: positional perturbations measurably shape the teacher’s output distributions and thus the student’s learning signal. (3) They identify structured, localized regions of the query parameters that are especially sensitive and heavily updated when extending context length.
AI.conclusion: Long-context retrieval capacity can be efficiently transferred from a long-context teacher to a student without explicit long-context training data, via logit-based knowledge distillation and appropriate RoPE scaling. Positional information is indeed embedded in the teacher’s logits and can be inherited by the student, and the associated parameter updates during context extension focus on specific parts of the query representation.

title: Stories of Your Life as Others: A Round-Trip Evaluation of LLM-Generated Life Stories Conditioned on Rich Psychometric Profiles
summary: Personality traits are richly encoded in natural language, and large language models (LLMs) trained on human text can simulate personality when conditioned on persona descriptions. However, existing evaluations rely predominantly on questionnaire self-report by the conditioned model, are limited in architectural diversity, and rarely use real human psychometric data. Without addressing these limitations, it remains unclear whether personality conditioning produces psychometrically informative representations of individual differences or merely superficial alignment with trait descriptors. To test how robustly LLMs can encode personality into extended text, we condition LLMs on real psychometric profiles from 290 participants to generate first-person life story narratives, and then task independent LLMs to recover personality scores from those narratives alone. We show that personality scores can be recovered from the generated narratives at levels approaching human test-retest reliability (mean r = 0.750, 85% of the human ceiling), and that recovery is robust across 10 LLM narrative generators and 3 LLM personality scorers spanning 6 providers. Decomposing systematic biases reveals that scoring models achieve their accuracy while counteracting alignment-induced defaults. Content analysis of the generated narratives shows that personality conditioning produces behaviourally differentiated text: nine of ten coded features correlate significantly with the same features in participants' real conversations, and personality-driven emotional reactivity patterns in narratives replicate in real conversational data. These findings provide evidence that the personality-language relationship captured during pretraining supports robust encoding and decoding of individual differences, including characteristic emotional variability patterns that replicate in real human behaviour.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether personality conditioning in LLMs yields genuinely informative, psychometrically meaningful signals rather than superficial trait mimicry, by encoding real human personality profiles into generated narratives and attempting to recover those profiles from text alone.
AI.motivation: Prior work shows that LLMs can be prompted with persona descriptions to exhibit particular personality traits, but evaluations mostly rely on self-report questionnaires answered by the same conditioned model, involve limited model architectures, and rarely use ground-truth human psychometric data. This makes it unclear whether models truly capture stable, individual-difference information or just echo trait words and stereotypes. The authors want to rigorously assess whether personality can be robustly encoded into and decoded from extended language generated by diverse LLMs, using real human personality profiles as the conditioning signal and independent models as scorers.
AI.method: The authors collect real psychometric personality profiles from 290 human participants. They condition a range of LLMs (10 different narrative generators from 6 providers) on each participant's profile to generate first-person life story narratives. Separate, independent LLMs (three different models) are then tasked with inferring or recovering the original personality scores solely from the generated narratives. They compare recovered scores to the true psychometric scores to assess reliability, decompose systematic biases to see how scoring models counteract generic alignment-related defaults, and perform content analyses on the narratives. Specifically, they examine whether coded behavioural and emotional features in generated narratives correlate with the same features in participants' real conversational data, and whether personality-linked emotional reactivity observed in the narratives replicates in real conversations.
AI.result: Personality scores inferred from the generated narratives correlate strongly with the original human psychometric scores, achieving a mean correlation of r = 0.750, about 85% of human test-retest reliability—a high ceiling in psychometrics. This robustness generalizes across 10 different LLMs used as narrative generators and 3 LLMs used as personality scorers. Bias decomposition reveals that the scoring models manage to reach this accuracy while actively counteracting default personality-like tendencies presumably introduced by alignment or pretraining. Content analysis shows that personality conditioning produces distinct behavioural signals in text: nine out of ten coded narrative features significantly correlate with the same features measured from participants' real conversations. Moreover, the personality-driven patterns of emotional reactivity present in generated narratives also appear in real conversational data.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that LLMs pretrained on natural language capture a deep relationship between language and personality that allows robust encoding and decoding of individual differences. When conditioned on real psychometric profiles, LLMs generate narratives from which independent models can recover personality scores at near-human reliability. The generated narratives also reflect behaviourally meaningful and emotionally patterned differences that align with real conversational behaviour. These results argue that personality conditioning in LLMs is more than superficial trait mimicry: it taps into psychometrically informative representations of stable individual differences, including characteristic emotional variability, with implications for both psychological assessment and the design of personalized AI systems.

title: Joint Knowledge Base Completion and Question Answering by Combining Large Language Models and Small Language Models
summary: Knowledge Bases (KBs) play a key role in various applications. As two representative KB-related tasks, knowledge base completion (KBC) and knowledge base question answering (KBQA) are closely related and inherently complementary with each other. Thus, it will be beneficial to solve the task of joint KBC and KBQA to make them reinforce each other. However, existing studies usually rely on the small language model (SLM) to enhance them jointly, and the large language model (LLM)'s strong reasoning ability is ignored. In this paper, by combining the strengths of the LLM with the SLM, we propose a novel framework JCQL, which can make these two tasks enhance each other in an iterative manner. To make KBC enhance KBQA, we augment the LLM agent-based KBQA model's reasoning paths by incorporating an SLM-trained KBC model as an action of the agent, alleviating the LLM's hallucination and high computational costs issue in KBQA. To make KBQA enhance KBC, we incrementally fine-tune the KBC model by leveraging KBQA's reasoning paths as its supplementary training data, improving the ability of the SLM in KBC. Extensive experiments over two public benchmark data sets demonstrate that JCQL surpasses all baselines for both KBC and KBQA tasks.

AI.tldr: A framework (JCQL) that jointly improves knowledge base completion and question answering by combining large and small language models in an iterative loop.
AI.motivation: Knowledge bases power many applications, but two key tasks—knowledge base completion (adding missing facts) and knowledge base question answering—are usually optimized separately, missing their natural complementarity. Existing joint methods typically rely only on small language models and underuse the stronger reasoning abilities of large language models, leading to hallucinations and inefficiencies. There is a need for a principled way to let KBC and KBQA mutually improve each other while harnessing both LLMs and SLMs.
AI.method: The authors propose JCQL, a joint framework that couples an LLM-based KBQA agent with an SLM-based KBC model in an iterative fashion. (1) For KBC → KBQA, they embed the SLM KBC model as an action within the LLM agent’s reasoning process, so the agent can query the KBC model to expand or correct paths in the knowledge base, reducing hallucination and computational cost. (2) For KBQA → KBC, they collect the reasoning paths generated by the LLM agent during KBQA and use them as additional supervision to incrementally fine-tune the SLM KBC model, enriching its training data and improving completion performance. The overall system alternates between these two phases so that both tasks reinforce each other.
AI.result: On two public benchmark datasets, JCQL achieves better performance than all compared baselines on both knowledge base completion and knowledge base question answering metrics, showing consistent gains from the joint, iterative design and the LLM–SLM collaboration.
AI.conclusion: Jointly modeling KBC and KBQA in a closed loop, where an LLM-based agent and an SLM-based KBC model support each other, leads to superior results on both tasks. Incorporating KBC as an agent action improves KBQA’s reasoning quality and efficiency, while feeding KBQA reasoning paths back into KBC training strengthens the SLM’s completion ability. This demonstrates the practical value of combining LLM reasoning with SLM efficiency in KB-centric applications.

title: LAG-XAI: A Lie-Inspired Affine Geometric Framework for Interpretable Paraphrasing in Transformer Latent Spaces
summary: Modern Transformer-based language models achieve strong performance in natural language processing tasks, yet their latent semantic spaces remain largely uninterpretable black boxes. This paper introduces LAG-XAI (Lie Affine Geometry for Explainable AI), a novel geometric framework that models paraphrasing not as discrete word substitutions, but as a structured affine transformation within the embedding space. By conceptualizing paraphrasing as a continuous geometric flow on a semantic manifold, we propose a computationally efficient mean-field approximation, inspired by local Lie group actions. This allows us to decompose paraphrase transitions into geometrically interpretable components: rotation, deformation, and translation. Experiments on the noisy PIT-2015 Twitter corpus, encoded with Sentence-BERT, reveal a "linear transparency" phenomenon. The proposed affine operator achieves an AUC of 0.7713. By normalizing against random chance (AUC 0.5), the model captures approximately 80% of the non-linear baseline's effective classification capacity (AUC 0.8405), offering explicit parametric interpretability in exchange for a marginal drop in absolute accuracy. The model identifies fundamental geometric invariants, including a stable matrix reconfiguration angle (~27.84°) and near-zero deformation, indicating local isometry. Cross-domain generalization is confirmed via direct cross-corpus validation on an independent TURL dataset. Furthermore, the practical utility of LAG-XAI is demonstrated in LLM hallucination detection: using a "cheap geometric check," the model automatically detected 95.3% of factual distortions on the HaluEval dataset by registering deviations beyond the permissible semantic corridor. This approach provides a mathematically grounded, resource-efficient path toward the mechanistic interpretability of Transformers.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes LAG-XAI, a geometric framework that explains paraphrasing and semantic changes in Transformer embeddings via simple affine transformations, achieving good performance while remaining interpretable and useful for tasks like hallucination detection.
AI.motivation: Transformer-based language models perform well but their internal embedding spaces are hard to interpret mechanistically. Existing approaches often treat paraphrasing as discrete token changes, lack geometric structure, or sacrifice interpretability for accuracy. There is a need for a mathematically grounded, computationally cheap way to understand how meaning changes in embedding space and to leverage this understanding for practical tasks such as detecting hallucinations.
AI.method: The authors model paraphrasing as a continuous geometric flow on a semantic manifold, approximated by an affine transformation inspired by local Lie group actions. They decompose paraphrase transitions between sentence embeddings into rotation, deformation, and translation components in a mean-field fashion. Using Sentence-BERT embeddings, they fit a low-complexity affine operator that maps original sentences to their paraphrases and analyze the resulting geometric invariants. They then test cross-domain generalization on another corpus and apply the same framework as a simple geometric deviation check to detect hallucinations in LLM outputs.
AI.result: On the PIT-2015 paraphrase dataset with Sentence-BERT embeddings, the affine operator reaches an AUC of 0.7713 for paraphrase classification. Relative to a stronger non-linear baseline with AUC 0.8405, the affine model recovers about 80% of the effective classification capacity while remaining explicitly interpretable. The learned transformation shows a stable rotation angle of about 27.84 degrees, almost no deformation (suggesting local isometry), and identifies a narrow semantic corridor of valid paraphrases. The model generalizes to the TURL dataset without retraining and, when used as a geometric anomaly detector on HaluEval, flags 95.3% of factual distortions via deviations outside this corridor.
AI.conclusion: LAG-XAI demonstrates that a simple affine transformation can capture most of the behavior of more complex, non-linear paraphrase models, while providing clear geometric interpretability in terms of rotation, deformation, and translation in embedding space. The presence of stable geometric invariants (e.g., rotation angle and near-isometry) suggests underlying structure in Transformer semantic manifolds. The framework generalizes across datasets and supports a practical application to hallucination detection through a lightweight geometric check, offering a promising, mathematically grounded direction for interpretable analysis of Transformer representations.

title: HybridKV: Hybrid KV Cache Compression for Efficient Multimodal Large Language Model Inference
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have advanced unified reasoning over text, images, and videos, but their inference is hindered by the rapid growth of key-value (KV) caches. Each visual input expands into thousands of tokens, causing caches to scale linearly with context length and remain resident in GPU memory throughout decoding, which leads to prohibitive memory overhead and latency even on high-end GPUs. A common solution is to compress caches under a fixed allocated budget at different granularities: token-level uniformly discards less important tokens, layer-level varies retention across layers, and head-level redistributes budgets across heads. Yet these approaches stop at allocation and overlook the heterogeneous behaviors of attention heads that require distinct compression strategies. We propose HybridKV, a hybrid KV cache compression framework that integrates complementary strategies in three stages: heads are first classified into static or dynamic types using text-centric attention; then a top-down budget allocation scheme hierarchically assigns KV budgets; finally, static heads are compressed by text-prior pruning and dynamic heads by chunk-wise retrieval. Experiments on 11 multimodal benchmarks with Qwen2.5-VL-7B show that HybridKV reduces KV cache memory by up to $7.9\times$ and achieves $1.52\times$ faster decoding, with almost no performance drop or even higher relative to the full-cache MLLM.

AI.tldr: HybridKV is a hybrid KV-cache compression framework for multimodal LLMs that greatly reduces memory and speeds up decoding while preserving or improving performance.
AI.motivation: Multimodal LLMs expand each visual input into thousands of tokens, making key-value attention caches grow linearly with context length and stay in GPU memory throughout decoding. This leads to large memory overheads and latency, even on high-end GPUs. Existing KV compression methods operate at token, layer, or head granularity under a fixed budget but mostly focus on how much budget to allocate, not on adapting compression strategies to the heterogeneous behaviors of different attention heads.
AI.method: HybridKV introduces a three-stage hybrid KV cache compression framework. First, it classifies attention heads into static or dynamic types based on text-centric attention patterns. Second, it uses a top-down, hierarchical budget allocation scheme to assign KV cache budgets across layers and heads. Third, it applies different compression strategies: static heads use text-prior-based pruning of KV tokens, while dynamic heads use chunk-wise retrieval to keep only the most relevant KV chunks over time.
AI.result: On 11 multimodal benchmarks using Qwen2.5-VL-7B, HybridKV reduces KV cache memory usage by up to 7.9× and speeds up decoding by 1.52×. It achieves these efficiency gains with almost no performance drop; in some cases, performance is even higher than that of the full, uncompressed KV cache baseline.
AI.conclusion: HybridKV effectively addresses the KV cache scalability problem in multimodal LLMs by combining head classification, hierarchical budget allocation, and head-specific compression strategies. It shows that tailoring compression to heterogeneous head behaviors can significantly cut memory and latency while maintaining or improving model quality, offering a practical path to more efficient MLLM inference.

title: Social Dynamics as Critical Vulnerabilities that Undermine Objective Decision-Making in LLM Collectives
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents are increasingly acting as human delegates in multi-agent environments, where a representative agent integrates diverse peer perspectives to make a final decision. Drawing inspiration from social psychology, we investigate how the reliability of this representative agent is undermined by the social context of its network. We define four key phenomena-social conformity, perceived expertise, dominant speaker effect, and rhetorical persuasion-and systematically manipulate the number of adversaries, relative intelligence, argument length, and argumentative styles. Our experiments demonstrate that the representative agent's accuracy consistently declines as social pressure increases: larger adversarial groups, more capable peers, and longer arguments all lead to significant performance degradation. Furthermore, rhetorical strategies emphasizing credibility or logic can further sway the agent's judgment, depending on the context. These findings reveal that multi-agent systems are sensitive not only to individual reasoning but also to the social dynamics of their configuration, highlighting critical vulnerabilities in AI delegates that mirror the psychological biases observed in human group decision-making.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how social dynamics in multi-agent LLM systems can bias a representative agent’s final decision, reducing accuracy under social pressure.
AI.motivation: As LLM agents increasingly act as human delegates in group or multi-agent settings, their decisions are shaped not only by individual reasoning but also by interactions with other agents. However, the social-psychological factors that may distort these decisions—similar to biases in human group decision-making—are poorly understood. The paper aims to identify and quantify how such social-context effects can undermine reliability in AI delegates.
AI.method: The authors design controlled multi-agent experiments where a central “representative” LLM agent aggregates input from multiple peer agents and makes a final decision. They systematically manipulate: (1) group composition and size (number of adversarial vs aligned agents) to model social conformity; (2) relative capability or ‘intelligence’ of peers to model perceived expertise; (3) argument length and verbosity to test dominant speaker effects; and (4) rhetorical style (e.g., appeals to credibility or logic) to study rhetorical persuasion. They then measure the representative agent’s decision accuracy under these varying social pressures.
AI.result: The experiments show that the representative agent’s accuracy reliably decreases as social pressure increases. Specifically, having more adversarial peers, peers that are more capable than the representative, or longer and more dominant arguments all significantly reduce decision accuracy. Additionally, carefully chosen rhetorical strategies—emphasizing credibility or logical structure—can substantially sway the representative agent’s judgment, with the exact effect depending on the surrounding context and configuration of agents.
AI.conclusion: Multi-agent LLM systems are not only limited by individual reasoning ability; they are also vulnerable to social-contextual effects analogous to human group biases, such as conformity and persuasion. These social dynamics can be exploited or may emerge naturally, undermining the reliability of an LLM acting as a delegate. Designers of multi-agent AI systems must account for these vulnerabilities in protocol design, aggregation mechanisms, and robustness safeguards to ensure trustworthy group decisions.

title: Context-Value-Action Architecture for Value-Driven Large Language Model Agents
comment: Accepted to Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: ACL 2026
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown promise in simulating human behavior, yet existing agents often exhibit behavioral rigidity, a flaw frequently masked by the self-referential bias of current "LLM-as-a-judge" evaluations. By evaluating against empirical ground truth, we reveal a counter-intuitive phenomenon: increasing the intensity of prompt-driven reasoning does not enhance fidelity but rather exacerbates value polarization, collapsing population diversity. To address this, we propose the Context-Value-Action (CVA) architecture, grounded in the Stimulus-Organism-Response (S-O-R) model and Schwartz's Theory of Basic Human Values. Unlike methods relying on self-verification, CVA decouples action generation from cognitive reasoning via a novel Value Verifier trained on authentic human data to explicitly model dynamic value activation. Experiments on CVABench, which comprises over 1.1 million real-world interaction traces, demonstrate that CVA significantly outperforms baselines. Our approach effectively mitigates polarization while offering superior behavioral fidelity and interpretability.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies that stronger prompt-based reasoning in LLM agents paradoxically reduces behavioral diversity and increases value polarization, and proposes a new CVA architecture with a Value Verifier trained on real human data to better match human value-driven behavior.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based agents that simulate human behavior tend to be rigid and polarized, which is often overlooked because evaluations rely on other LLMs as judges rather than empirical human data. The authors want to understand why this happens and design agents that better reflect the diverse and dynamic nature of human values and decisions.
AI.method: 1) Empirically compare LLM agents against ground-truth human behavioral data, analyzing how increasing reasoning intensity affects behavioral fidelity and diversity; 2) Identify that more reasoning causes value polarization and loss of population diversity; 3) Propose the Context-Value-Action (CVA) architecture, inspired by the Stimulus-Organism-Response model and Schwartz’s basic human values theory; 4) Introduce a separate Value Verifier module, trained on large-scale real interaction traces, that explicitly models dynamic value activation and is decoupled from the action-generation LLM; 5) Evaluate the method on CVABench, a new dataset of 1.1M+ real-world interaction traces.
AI.result: The experiments on CVABench show that: (a) Increasing prompt-driven reasoning depth in standard LLM agents worsens value polarization and reduces diversity relative to human behavior; (b) The proposed CVA architecture with a Value Verifier produces agent behaviors that better align with empirical human data; (c) CVA substantially outperforms baseline approaches on behavioral fidelity metrics while also reducing polarization and providing more interpretable value-based explanations for actions.
AI.conclusion: Stronger chain-of-thought–style reasoning in LLM agents does not necessarily yield more human-like behavior and can instead amplify polarization and reduce diversity. By separating value assessment from action generation and grounding value modeling in large-scale real human data, the proposed CVA framework offers a more faithful, diverse, and interpretable way to simulate human behavior with LLMs.

title: Exclusive Unlearning
summary: When introducing Large Language Models (LLMs) into industrial applications, such as healthcare and education, the risk of generating harmful content becomes a significant challenge. While existing machine unlearning methods can erase specific harmful knowledge and expressions, diverse harmful content makes comprehensive removal difficult. In this study, instead of individually listing targets for forgetting, we propose Exclusive Unlearning (EU), which aims for broad harm removal by extensively forgetting everything except for the knowledge and expressions we wish to retain. We demonstrate that through Exclusive Unlearning, it is possible to obtain a model that ensures safety against a wide range of inputs, including jailbreaks, while maintaining the ability to respond to diverse instructions related to specific domains such as medicine and mathematics.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Exclusive Unlearning, a new approach to make LLMs safer by forgetting everything except approved knowledge and expressions, enabling robust defense against harmful content and jailbreaks while preserving capabilities in selected domains.
AI.motivation: Deploying LLMs in sensitive industrial settings like healthcare and education is risky because models can generate harmful content. Existing machine unlearning focuses on erasing specific harmful pieces of knowledge or expressions, but harmful content is diverse and hard to enumerate exhaustively, so coverage and robustness (e.g., to jailbreaks) are limited.
AI.method: Instead of specifying what the model should forget, the authors invert the problem and specify only what must be retained. Their Exclusive Unlearning (EU) method trains the model to extensively forget all non-whitelisted knowledge and expressions while preserving capability in allowed domains (e.g., medicine, mathematics). Technically, EU is a form of machine unlearning guided by a retention set rather than a forgetting list, though the abstract does not detail the exact optimization procedure.
AI.result: Models trained with Exclusive Unlearning are shown to be safe against a wide range of harmful inputs, including jailbreak-style prompts, yet still able to follow diverse instructions within the retained domains such as medical and mathematical tasks. This suggests EU achieves a better safety–utility trade-off for domain-specific deployments than prior unlearning approaches that target enumerated harms.
AI.conclusion: Exclusive Unlearning offers a promising paradigm for deploying LLMs in high-stakes domains: by whitelisting what to keep rather than blacklisting what to erase, it becomes feasible to robustly remove broad classes of harmful behaviors while maintaining high performance in targeted, approved areas of use.

title: Towards Trustworthy Report Generation: A Deep Research Agent with Progressive Confidence Estimation and Calibration
summary: As agent-based systems continue to evolve, deep research agents are capable of automatically generating research-style reports across diverse domains. While these agents promise to streamline information synthesis and knowledge exploration, existing evaluation frameworks-typically based on subjective dimensions-fail to capture a critical aspect of report quality: trustworthiness. In open-ended research scenarios where ground-truth answers are unavailable, current evaluation methods cannot effectively measure the epistemic confidence of generated content, making calibration difficult and leaving users susceptible to misleading or hallucinated information. To address this limitation, we propose a novel deep research agent that incorporates progressive confidence estimation and calibration within the report generation pipeline. Our system leverages a deliberative search model, featuring deep retrieval and multi-hop reasoning to ground outputs in verifiable evidence while assigning confidence scores to individual claims. Combined with a carefully designed workflow, this approach produces trustworthy reports with enhanced transparency. Experimental results and case studies demonstrate that our method substantially improves interpretability and significantly increases user trust.

AI.tldr: They introduce a deep research agent that assigns calibrated confidence scores to each claim in its reports, using deliberative search and multi-hop reasoning to improve trustworthiness and user trust when no ground truth is available.
AI.motivation: Existing agent-based systems can write research-style reports, but current evaluation methods rely mostly on subjective quality criteria and do not capture how trustworthy the content is. In open-ended research, there is often no clear ground truth, so it is hard to measure epistemic confidence or calibrate models. This gap leaves users vulnerable to hallucinated or misleading information without clear indicators of reliability.
AI.method: They design a deep research agent that integrates progressive confidence estimation into its report-generation pipeline. The agent uses a deliberative search model with deep retrieval and multi-hop reasoning to ground statements in verifiable evidence. During generation, it assigns confidence scores at the level of individual claims and follows a carefully structured workflow aimed at calibration and transparency, so that each part of the report is linked to evidence and accompanied by a confidence estimate.
AI.result: Through experiments and case studies, they show that the proposed system improves the interpretability of generated reports and leads to significantly higher user trust compared with baselines or existing approaches that lack explicit confidence estimation and grounding.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating calibrated confidence estimation, evidence-grounded deliberative search, and a structured workflow into deep research agents yields more trustworthy, transparent research-style reports, particularly in open-ended settings without ground truth, thereby improving interpretability and user trust.

title: Beyond Compromise: Pareto-Lenient Consensus for Efficient Multi-Preference LLM Alignment
summary: Transcending the single-preference paradigm, aligning LLMs with diverse human values is pivotal for robust deployment. Contemporary Multi-Objective Preference Alignment (MPA) approaches predominantly rely on static linear scalarization or rigid gradient projection to navigate these trade-offs. However, by enforcing strict conflict avoidance or simultaneous descent, these paradigms often prematurely converge to local stationary points. While mathematically stable, these points represent a conservative compromise where the model sacrifices potential global Pareto improvements to avoid transient local trade-offs. To break this deadlock, we propose Pareto-Lenient Consensus (PLC), a game-theoretic framework that reimagines alignment as a dynamic negotiation process. Unlike rigid approaches, PLC introduces consensus-driven lenient gradient rectification, which dynamically tolerates local degradation provided there is a sufficient dominant coalition surplus, thereby empowering the optimization trajectory to escape local suboptimal equilibrium and explore the distal Pareto-optimal frontier. Theoretical analysis validates PLC can facilitate stalemate escape and asymptotically converge to a Pareto consensus equilibrium. Moreover, extensive experiments show that PLC surpasses baselines in both fixed-preference alignment and global Pareto frontier quality. This work highlights the potential of negotiation-driven alignment as a promising avenue for MPA. Our codes are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/aaa-6BB8.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Pareto-Lenient Consensus (PLC), a game-theoretic, negotiation-inspired optimization framework to better align large language models with multiple, potentially conflicting human preferences, overcoming limitations of current multi-objective preference alignment methods.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-objective preference alignment (MPA) methods for LLMs usually combine different human value dimensions via fixed linear scalarization or strict gradient projection. These methods are conservative: they avoid any temporary harm to any objective, which causes optimization to get stuck in locally stable but globally suboptimal compromise points on the Pareto frontier. There is a need for an alignment framework that can flexibly trade off short-term local degradation against longer-term global Pareto improvements, better reflecting how humans negotiate value trade-offs.
AI.method: The authors introduce Pareto-Lenient Consensus (PLC), a game-theoretic framework modeling alignment as a dynamic negotiation among objectives. Instead of enforcing strict simultaneous improvement or conflict-avoidance, PLC performs "lenient" gradient rectification: it allows certain objectives to degrade locally as long as there is sufficient surplus gain for a dominant coalition of other objectives. Gradients are adjusted via a consensus rule that encodes this leniency, letting optimization move through temporarily unfavorable regions to escape local equilibria and explore more distant Pareto-optimal solutions. Theoretical analysis characterizes the convergence behavior and shows PLC leads to a Pareto consensus equilibrium rather than getting stuck in conservative stationary points.
