AI.result: Theoretical results show that PLC can escape stalemates caused by strict conflict-avoidance or simultaneous descent and can asymptotically converge to a Pareto consensus equilibrium. Empirically, across multiple benchmarks, PLC outperforms existing MPA baselines both when aligning to a fixed preference configuration and when evaluating the overall quality of the discovered global Pareto frontier, indicating better trade-offs across objectives.
AI.conclusion: Pareto-Lenient Consensus offers a more flexible, negotiation-driven view of multi-objective preference alignment for LLMs, overcoming the over-conservatism of linear scalarization and rigid gradient projection. By tolerating controlled local degradations in some objectives when compensated by sufficient gains in others, PLC enables the optimizer to escape suboptimal stationary points and achieve higher-quality Pareto frontiers. The results suggest that framing alignment as a dynamic negotiation process is a promising direction for robustly aligning models with diverse and potentially conflicting human values.

title: Flowr -- Scaling Up Retail Supply Chain Operations Through Agentic AI in Large Scale Supermarket Chains
summary: Retail supply chain operations in supermarket chains involve continuous, high-volume manual workflows spanning demand forecasting, procurement, supplier coordination, and inventory replenishment, processes that are repetitive, decision-intensive, and difficult to scale without significant human effort. Despite growing investment in data analytics, the decision-making and coordination layers of these workflows remain predominantly manual, reactive, and fragmented across outlets, distribution centers, and supplier networks. This paper introduces Flowr, a novel agentic AI framework for automating end-to-end retail supply chain workflows in large-scale supermarket operations. Flowr systematically decomposes manual supply chain operations into specialized AI agents, each responsible for a clearly defined cognitive role, enabling automation of processes previously dependent on continuous human coordination. To ensure task accuracy and adherence to responsible AI principles, the framework employs a consortium of fine-tuned, domain-specialized large language models coordinated by a central reasoning LLM. Central to the framework is a human-in-the-loop orchestration model in which supply chain managers supervise and intervene across workflow stages via a Model Context Protocol (MCP)-enabled interface, preserving accountability and organizational control. Evaluation demonstrates that Flowr significantly reduces manual coordination overhead, improves demand-supply alignment, and enables proactive exception handling at a scale unachievable through manual processes. The framework was validated in collaboration with a large-scale supermarket chain and is domain-independent, offering a generalizable blueprint for agentic AI-driven supply chain automation across large-scale enterprise settings.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Flowr, an agentic AI framework that automates end-to-end retail supply chain workflows in supermarket chains using specialized AI agents and a central reasoning LLM, with human-in-the-loop oversight to improve efficiency, accuracy, and scalability.
AI.motivation: Retail supermarket supply chains rely on continuous, manual, and labor-intensive workflows—such as demand forecasting, procurement, supplier coordination, and inventory replenishment—that are repetitive, decision-heavy, and hard to scale. Even with data analytics investments, decision-making remains fragmented, reactive, and largely manual across stores, distribution centers, and suppliers. There is a need for a scalable, automated, and coordinated system that can handle these cognitive and coordination tasks more effectively while maintaining accountability and control.
AI.method: The paper proposes Flowr, an agentic AI framework that decomposes end-to-end retail supply chain workflows into specialized AI agents, each with a distinct cognitive role. These agents are powered by a consortium of fine-tuned, domain-specialized large language models coordinated by a central reasoning LLM. The system is orchestrated through a human-in-the-loop model, where supply chain managers can supervise and intervene using an MCP-enabled interface. This structure enforces responsible AI principles, ensures task accuracy, and maintains organizational oversight while automating complex workflows.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation, conducted in collaboration with a large supermarket chain, shows that Flowr substantially reduces manual coordination overhead, improves alignment between demand and supply, and enables proactive exception handling at a scale not feasible with purely manual processes. The results indicate higher operational efficiency and better responsiveness to supply chain disruptions or anomalies.
AI.conclusion: Flowr offers a validated, domain-independent blueprint for automating complex supply chain workflows in large enterprises using agentic AI. By combining specialized LLM-driven agents, a central reasoning model, and human-in-the-loop orchestration, it enables scalable, accurate, and accountable automation of retail supply chain operations, with potential applicability across other large-scale enterprise domains.

title: Epistemic Blinding: An Inference-Time Protocol for Auditing Prior Contamination in LLM-Assisted Analysis
comment: code and LLM skill at: https://github.com/mcuccarese/epistemic-blinding 7 pages 3 figures
summary: This paper presents epistemic blinding in the context of an agentic system that uses large language models to reason across multiple biological datasets for drug target prioritization. During development, it became apparent that LLM outputs silently blend data-driven inference with memorized priors about named entities - and the blend is invisible: there is no way to determine, from a single output, how much came from the data on the page and how much came from the model's training memory. Epistemic blinding is a simple inference-time protocol that replaces entity identifiers with anonymous codes before prompting, then compares outputs against an unblinded control. The protocol does not make LLM reasoning deterministic, but it restores one critical axis of auditability: measuring how much of an output came from the supplied data versus the model's parametric knowledge. The complete target identification system is described - including LLM-guided evolutionary optimization of scoring functions and blinded agentic reasoning for target rationalization - with demonstration that both stages operate without access to entity identity. In oncology drug target prioritization across four cancer types, blinding changes 16% of top-20 predictions while preserving identical recovery of validated targets. The contamination problem is shown to generalize beyond biology: in S&P 500 equity screening, brand-recognition bias reshapes 30-40% of top-20 rankings across five random seeds. To lower the barrier to adoption, the protocol is released as an open-source tool and as a Claude Code skill that enables one-command epistemic blinding within agentic workflows. The claim is not that blinded analysis produces better results, but that without blinding, there is no way to know to what degree the agent is adhering to the analytical process the researcher designed.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces an "epistemic blinding" protocol that hides real entity names from an LLM during reasoning, then compares blinded vs unblinded outputs to quantify how much results depend on the supplied data versus the model’s prior, memorized knowledge.
AI.motivation: LLMs used as reasoning engines over scientific or financial data often mix evidence from the provided datasets with opaque prior knowledge about named entities (e.g., famous genes, well-known companies). From a single output, it is impossible to determine whether a recommendation came from the analysis pipeline or from the model’s memorized associations, which undermines auditability and scientific rigor in applications like drug target discovery or equity screening.
AI.method: The authors propose an inference-time protocol called epistemic blinding: (1) replace all entity identifiers (e.g., gene names, company tickers) with anonymous codes before prompting the LLM; (2) run the analysis and obtain blinded outputs; (3) run an unblinded control where real identifiers are visible; and (4) compare rankings and rationales between blinded and unblinded runs to estimate the influence of parametric (memorized) knowledge versus the supplied data. They integrate this protocol into an agentic system for drug target prioritization, including LLM-guided evolutionary optimization of scoring functions and blinded reasoning for target rationalization, ensuring that both stages can operate without access to entity identity.
AI.result: In oncology target prioritization across four cancer types, applying epistemic blinding changes 16% of the top-20 predictions, yet recovery of known validated targets remains unchanged, indicating that many ranking differences are driven by prior knowledge or bias rather than loss of signal. In S&P 500 equity screening, blinding alters 30–40% of top-20 rankings across five random seeds, revealing strong brand-recognition bias. These experiments demonstrate that contamination from parametric knowledge is substantial and domain-general.
AI.conclusion: Epistemic blinding does not make LLM reasoning deterministic or inherently better, but it restores a key dimension of auditability: the ability to measure the dependence of outputs on provided data versus the model’s internal priors. Without such blinding, researchers cannot reliably assess whether an LLM-based agent is actually following the designed analytical pipeline or merely echoing background knowledge and biases about specific entities. The authors release the protocol as open-source and as a Claude Code skill to facilitate easy adoption in agentic workflows.

title: How LLMs Follow Instructions: Skillful Coordination, Not a Universal Mechanism
summary:
Instruction tuning is commonly assumed to endow language models with a domain-general ability to follow instructions, yet the underlying mechanism remains poorly understood. Does instruction-following rely on a universal mechanism or compositional skill deployment? We investigate this through diagnostic probing across nine diverse tasks in three instruction-tuned models.
  Our analysis provides converging evidence against a universal mechanism. First, general probes trained across all tasks consistently underperform task-specific specialists, indicating limited representational sharing. Second, cross-task transfer is weak and clustered by skill similarity. Third, causal ablation reveals sparse asymmetric dependencies rather than shared representations. Tasks also stratify by complexity across layers, with structural constraints emerging early and semantic tasks emerging late. Finally, temporal analysis shows constraint satisfaction operates as dynamic monitoring during generation rather than pre-generation planning.
  These findings indicate that instruction-following is better characterized as skillful coordination of diverse linguistic capabilities rather than deployment of a single abstract constraint-checking process.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how instruction-tuned language models follow instructions and finds that they rely on coordinated, task-specific skills rather than a single universal instruction-following mechanism.
AI.motivation: Although instruction tuning is believed to give language models a general ability to follow instructions, it is unclear whether this ability comes from one universal mechanism or from composing multiple skills. Clarifying this mechanism can improve our understanding and development of instruction-tuned models.
AI.method: The authors perform diagnostic probing on three instruction-tuned language models across nine varied tasks. They use: (1) general vs. task-specific probes to examine representational sharing; (2) cross-task transfer analysis to see how skills transfer between tasks; (3) causal ablation to test functional dependencies among tasks; (4) layer-wise analysis to study how task complexity manifests across model depth; and (5) temporal analysis during generation to distinguish dynamic monitoring from pre-generation planning.
AI.result: They find that: (1) probes trained across all tasks underperform task-specific probes, suggesting limited shared representations; (2) cross-task transfer is weak and occurs mainly between skill-similar tasks, forming clusters; (3) causal ablation uncovers sparse, asymmetric dependencies among tasks instead of broad shared mechanisms; (4) tasks separate by complexity across layers, with structural constraints encoded in earlier layers and semantic tasks in later ones; and (5) constraint satisfaction functions as ongoing monitoring during token generation rather than as a single pre-planned step.
AI.conclusion: Instruction-following in instruction-tuned language models is best viewed as coordinated deployment of multiple, diverse linguistic skills, not as a single, unified constraint-checking process. This challenges the assumption of a universal instruction-following mechanism and suggests models orchestrate different capabilities dynamically during generation.

title: Artificial Intelligence and the Structure of Mathematics
summary: Recent progress in artificial intelligence (AI) is unlocking transformative capabilities for mathematics. There is great hope that AI will help solve major open problems and autonomously discover new mathematical concepts. In this essay, we further consider how AI may open a grand perspective on mathematics by forging a new route, complementary to mathematical\textbf{ logic,} to understanding the global structure of formal \textbf{proof}\textbf{s}. We begin by providing a sketch of the formal structure of mathematics in terms of universal proof and structural hypergraphs and discuss questions this raises about the foundational structure of mathematics. We then outline the main ingredients and provide a set of criteria to be satisfied for AI models capable of automated mathematical discovery. As we send AI agents to traverse Platonic mathematical worlds, we expect they will teach us about the nature of mathematics: both as a whole, and the small ribbons conducive to human understanding. Perhaps they will shed light on the old question: "Is mathematics discovered or invented?" Can we grok the terrain of these \textbf{Platonic worlds}?

AI.tldr: The paper discusses how AI can provide a new, complementary route to mathematical logic for understanding the global structure of formal proofs, and proposes criteria for AI systems capable of genuine automated mathematical discovery.
AI.motivation: To explore how recent advances in AI can not only solve mathematical problems but also illuminate the foundational structure of mathematics itself, especially the global organization of proofs and the nature of mathematical discovery.
AI.method: Conceptual and theoretical: the authors sketch a formal representation of mathematics using universal proof and structural hypergraphs, analyze foundational questions raised by this representation, and then propose high-level design ingredients and criteria that AI systems should meet to qualify as tools for automated mathematical discovery.
AI.result: They articulate a framework in which formal mathematics is viewed through universal proof and hypergraph structures and present a set of conceptual requirements and desiderata for AI models that aim to autonomously explore and discover in mathematical domains conceived as Platonic worlds.
AI.conclusion: AI has the potential not only to assist with solving specific mathematical problems, but also to reveal new perspectives on the global structure of mathematics and the nature of proofs. By building AI systems that traverse and learn from formal mathematical landscapes, we may gain insight into whether mathematics is discovered or invented and how the vast space of mathematical truths is structured, including the parts that align with human understanding.

title: Claw-Eval: Toward Trustworthy Evaluation of Autonomous Agents
summary: Large language models are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents executing multi-step workflows in real-world software environments. However, existing agent benchmarks suffer from three critical limitations: (1) trajectory-opaque grading that checks only final outputs, (2) underspecified safety and robustness evaluation, and (3) narrow modality coverage and interaction paradigms. We introduce Claw-Eval, an end-to-end evaluation suite addressing all three gaps. It comprises 300 human-verified tasks spanning 9 categories across three groups (general service orchestration, multimodal perception and generation, and multi-turn professional dialogue). Every agent action is recorded through three independent evidence channels (execution traces, audit logs, and environment snapshots), enabling trajectory-aware grading over 2,159 fine-grained rubric items. The scoring protocol evaluates Completion, Safety, and Robustness, reporting Average Score, Pass@k, and Pass^k across three trials to distinguish genuine capability from lucky outcomes. Experiments on 14 frontier models reveal that: (1) trajectory-opaque evaluation is systematically unreliable, missing 44% of safety violations and 13% of robustness failures that our hybrid pipeline catches; (2) controlled error injection primarily degrades consistency rather than peak capability, with Pass^3 dropping up to 24% while Pass@3 remains stable; (3) multimodal performance varies sharply, with most models performing poorer on video than on document or image, and no single model dominating across all modalities. Beyond benchmarking, Claw-Eval highlights actionable directions for agent development, shedding light on what it takes to build agents that are not only capable but reliably deployable.

AI.tldr: Claw-Eval is a comprehensive, trajectory-aware benchmark to evaluate the capability, safety, and robustness of autonomous LLM agents across diverse modalities and tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for LLM-based agents mostly judge only final outputs, provide weak or underspecified safety/robustness assessments, and cover limited modalities and interaction types. This makes it hard to reliably assess whether agents are truly safe, robust, and generally capable for real-world deployment. The authors aim to build a benchmark that directly addresses these gaps and provides richer, more dependable evaluation signals for agent development.
AI.method: The authors design Claw-Eval, an end-to-end evaluation suite of 300 human-verified tasks across 9 categories grouped into: (1) general service orchestration, (2) multimodal perception and generation, and (3) multi-turn professional dialogue. For each task, every agent action is captured through three evidence channels—execution traces, audit logs, and environment snapshots—so evaluators can inspect full trajectories. They define 2,159 fine-grained rubric items and a scoring protocol that assesses Completion, Safety, and Robustness. Metrics include Average Score, Pass@k (best of k trials), and Pass^k (consistency across k trials), run over three trials per model. They then evaluate 14 frontier models under this framework, including experiments with controlled error injections to stress-test robustness and consistency.
AI.result: Using Claw-Eval, the authors find that: (1) traditional trajectory-opaque evaluation that only checks final outputs is unreliable, failing to detect 44% of safety violations and 13% of robustness failures that the trajectory-aware pipeline identifies; (2) when they inject controlled errors, models’ consistency degrades significantly—Pass^3 can drop by up to 24%—even though peak capability, captured by Pass@3, stays roughly unchanged; (3) multimodal performance is highly uneven: most models handle documents and images better than videos, and no single model performs best across all modalities and task types.
AI.conclusion: Claw-Eval provides a richer and more reliable way to evaluate LLM agents by making their trajectories observable, explicitly scoring safety and robustness, and covering a wide range of modalities and interaction patterns. The findings show that current evaluation practices systematically underestimate safety and robustness problems, and that models can appear capable under best-case metrics while being inconsistent and brittle under perturbations. The benchmark thus offers concrete guidance and a standardized testbed for developing agents that are not just powerful but also safe, robust, and suitable for real-world deployment.
